| **Stress concentration (0.5 mark)** | Low — large contact area | High — small contact area |
| **Wear and life (0.5 mark)** | Low wear, long life | High wear, shorter life |
| **Examples (1 mark)** | Revolute, Prismatic, Spherical, Cylindrical, Universal, Planar, Screw | Cam–follower, gear mesh, belt–pulley, ball-bearing contact |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> *As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE guidelines, students must answer ONE full question (either OR option). Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.*

---

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1]

> **Q.A.** **(a)** Classify the various types of **kinematic joints** used in robotic manipulators with neat sketches. Compare the **degrees of freedom** of each. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A spatial robotic manipulator consists of **7 links** (including the ground) connected by **5 revolute joints** and **1 prismatic joint**. Compute the **total degrees of freedom (mobility)** of the manipulator using the **Kutzbach criterion**. Comment on the **dexterity** of the manipulator. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution A(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the definition of a kinematic joint.

A kinematic joint is a *mechanical connection* between two links that *constrains* their relative motion. The number of independent relative motions is the **DOF** of the joint.

**Step 2 (3 marks):** Classify and sketch all six lower-pair joints.

| # | Joint | Sketch (Symbol) | DOF | Joint Variable |
|:-:|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | Revolute (R) | A pin between two plates | 1 | $\theta$ |
| 2 | Prismatic (P) | A slider inside a guide | 1 | $d$ |
| 3 | Cylindrical (C) | Pin + slider along same axis | 2 | $\theta, d$ |
| 4 | Spherical (S) | Ball-and-socket | 3 | $\phi, \theta, \psi$ |
| 5 | Universal (U) | Two intersecting hinges | 2 | $\theta_1, \theta_2$ |
| 6 | Planar (Pl) | Flat-on-flat sliding | 3 | $x, y, \theta$ |
| 7 | Screw (H) | Threaded shaft and nut | 1 | coupled $\theta, d$ |

**Step 3 (3 marks):** Brief note on each.

- **Revolute:** Pure rotation about fixed axis; 1 DOF; **most common** in industrial arms.
- **Prismatic:** Pure linear translation; 1 DOF; found in Cartesian/gantry robots.
- **Cylindrical:** Rotation + translation along the *same* axis; 2 DOF; rare.
- **Spherical:** Three independent rotations about a common point; 3 DOF; emulated using intersecting revolute joints.
- **Universal:** Two intersecting revolute axes; 2 DOF; used in drive shafts and wrist couplings.
- **Planar:** Two translations + one rotation in a plane; 3 DOF; XY-theta stages.
- **Screw:** Rotation and translation rigidly coupled by a lead; 1 DOF; mostly used in *actuators*, not as direct manipulator joints.

---

#### Solution A(b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- Number of links: $n = 7$ (ground + 6 moving).
- 1-DOF revolute joints: $j_1 = 5$.
- 1-DOF prismatic joint: contributes 1 to $j_1$, so total $j_1 = 5 + 1 = 6$.
- All other joint types: $j_2 = j_3 = j_4 = j_5 = 0$.

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Write the Kutzbach spatial-mobility formula.

$$M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Substitute the values.

$$M = 6(7-1) - 5(6) - 0 - 0 - 0 - 0$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Evaluate.

$$M = 6 \times 6 - 30 = 36 - 30 = 6$$

**Step 4 (3 marks):** Comment on dexterity.

A manipulator with **$M = 6$** is a *fully dexterous* manipulator — it can place its end-effector at **any** arbitrary pose (3 translational + 3 orientational DOF) within its workspace. Such a robot can perform:
- Complex 3-D welding along curved seams,
- 6-DOF assembly (e.g., peg-in-hole with arbitrary orientation),
- Tool operations (drilling, milling, deburring) on surfaces of arbitrary orientation.

A manipulator with $M < 6$ would be *deficient* — it could position but not fully orient, or orient but not position, its tool.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:**
> A *very* common mistake in A(b) is to **forget that the prismatic joint contributes to $j_1$**, not to a separate category. Students often write $j_1 = 5$ (revolutes only) and $j_{\text{pris}} = 1$ separately, then forget to add them. Always remember: **prismatic joints are 1-DOF joints** and belong in the $j_1$ term.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1]

> **Q.B.** **(a)** With a neat diagram, describe the **Denavit–Hartenberg (DH) joint notation** convention. Explain the meaning of **revolute (R)** and **prismatic (P)** joint variables with examples. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A planar **slider-crank mechanism** consists of 4 links (ground + crank + connecting rod + slider) connected by 3 revolute joints and 1 prismatic joint. Compute its mobility using the **Grübler criterion** and verify the result with a physical explanation. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution B(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Introduce the DH joint notation.

The Denavit–Hartenberg (DH) convention is a *standardised* method to assign coordinate frames to each link of a manipulator. Each joint $i$ is described by **4 parameters**: $\theta_i, d_i, a_i, \alpha_i$. The *joint type* determines which of the first two parameters is the *variable* (joint variable $q_i$):

| Joint Type | Variable Parameter | Constant Parameter |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Revolute (R) | $\theta_i$ (angle) | $d_i$ (offset) |
| Prismatic (P) | $d_i$ (distance) | $\theta_i$ (angle) |

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Explain R-joint example.

For a **revolute joint**, $\theta_i$ is the *joint variable* (typically the motor angle), and $d_i$ is fixed (a structural offset along the joint axis, e.g., the *shoulder height* of an arm). The joint's contribution to the manipulator's DOF is **1 rotational DOF**.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Explain P-joint example.

For a **prismatic joint**, $d_i$ is the *joint variable* (the linear position of the slider), and $\theta_i$ is fixed (the *orientation* of the slider's travel axis is a structural property of the link). The joint contributes **1 translational DOF**.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Conclude with an example.

A **SCARA robot** with structure **RRP** has joint variables $[\theta_1, \theta_2, d_3]^T$ — the first two are angular (R) and the third is linear (P).

---

#### Solution B(b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- Number of links (planar): $n = 4$ (ground, crank, connecting rod, slider).
- 1-DOF revolute joints: 3 (ground–crank, crank–rod, rod–slider).
- 1-DOF prismatic joints: 1 (slider's translation).
- Total 1-DOF joints: $j_1 = 3 + 1 = 4$.
- 2-DOF joints: $j_2 = 0$.

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Write the Grübler planar-mobility formula.

$$M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Substitute the values.

$$M = 3(4-1) - 2(4) - 0$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Evaluate.

$$M = 3 \times 3 - 8 = 9 - 8 = 1$$

**Step 4 (3 marks):** Physical verification.

A slider-crank has **1 DOF** — driving the crank (input) with a single motor deterministically moves the connecting rod and the slider. You only need *one* parameter (e.g., the crank angle $\theta_1$) to fully specify the configuration of the entire mechanism. This matches the everyday experience of an internal-combustion engine, where the piston's position is fully determined by the crank angle.

This result is the *basis* of the **Grashof condition** for four-bar mechanisms, which classifies them into *Grashof* (full continuous rotation) and *non-Grashof* (rocker) families.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:**
> Two common errors in B(b):
> 1. **Forgetting the ground link** — students often count $n = 3$ (crank + rod + slider) and get $M = 3(2) - 8 = -2$, which is meaningless. **Always count the ground.**
> 2. **Treating the prismatic joint as a 2-DOF joint** — the slider's *rotation* about its own axis is irrelevant (it slides on a track), so it is a **1-DOF** joint, going into the $j_1$ term, not $j_2$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (KTU 2024 — Module 1, Joints):**
>
> ✅ **Joint** = a constrained mechanical pairing between two links; **DOF** of a joint = number of independent relative motions.
>
> ✅ **Six standard lower-pair joints**: Revolute (R, 1 DOF), Prismatic (P, 1 DOF), Cylindrical (C, 2 DOF), Spherical (S, 3 DOF), Universal (U, 2 DOF), Planar (Pl, 3 DOF), Screw (H, 1 DOF).
>
> ✅ **Hertz contact principle:** Constraints per lower pair $= 6 - \text{DOF}$.
>
> ✅ **Kutzbach spatial formula** (the high-yield one):
> $$M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$$
>
> ✅ **Grübler planar formula:**
> $$M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$
>
> ✅ **Always include the ground link in $n$**, and **rev + pris = 1-DOF joints** (group them in $j_1$).
>
> ✅ **Screw joint relation:** $d = L \cdot \dfrac{\theta}{2\pi}$.
>
> ✅ **PUMA 560** = **6R** (RRR + spherical wrist with 3R). It has 7 links + 6 revolute joints, giving $M = 6$ — *fully dexterous*.
>
> ✅ **Dexterity** of an $n$-DOF arm in 3-D space is **capped at $\min(n, 6)$** — 3 for position, 3 for orientation.
>
> ✅ **"Big Two" of industrial robotics:** Revolute + Prismatic account for **>90%** of all manipulator joints globally.
>
> ✅ **Joint notation** follows the base-to-tool sequence (e.g., RRP, RRR, RRRRRR).
>
> ✅ **Spherical wrist** = 3 revolute joints with **intersecting axes** — gives 3 rotational DOF independent of position, enabling *kinematic decoupling* (orientation solved separately from position).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Actuators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAT602 - ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Actuators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of an Actuator

In the context of **Robotics and Automation (PCCAT602)**, an **actuator** is formally defined as a mechanical or electro-mechanical device that converts a source of energy (electrical, hydraulic, pneumatic, or thermal) into physical motion — typically linear translation, rotation, or oscillation — in order to perform work on its environment. In a robotic manipulator, actuators are the "muscles" of the system: they generate the **torques** at the joints (for revolute joints) or the **forces** along the axes (for prismatic joints) that produce controlled motion of the end-effector.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for Module 1, actuators are studied under the broader classification of robotic power-transmission and motion-generation elements, alongside sensors, controllers, and end-effectors.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 1):** Actuators are introduced as a foundational element of the robotic system, immediately following the study of robot anatomy, joints, and degrees of freedom. The emphasis is on **classification, operating principles, performance characteristics, and selection criteria** for industrial robotic applications.

The energy-flow contract of an actuator can be compactly expressed as:

$$
\text{Input Energy} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Actuator} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Mechanical Output (Force / Torque / Displacement)}
$$

A useful generalized compact model is:

$$
P_{out} = \eta \cdot P_{in}
$$

where $P_{out}$ is the useful mechanical power delivered, $P_{in}$ is the input power, and $\eta$ is the **efficiency** of the actuator (typically **0.6 to 0.95** for well-designed industrial units).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Muscles of the Robot"

Imagine the human body. Your **brain** decides to pick up a cup, your **nerves** carry that decision as electrical signals, and your **muscles** actually do the physical work of contracting to lift the cup. In a robot:

- The **controller** (microcontroller / PLC / industrial PC) is the brain.
- The **signal wiring / communication bus** (CAN, EtherCAT, RS-485) is the nerves.
- The **actuator** is the muscle — it takes an electrical/hydraulic/pneumatic command and produces the real mechanical force or motion.
- The **encoder / sensor** is the sense of touch and proprioception that closes the feedback loop.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Without actuators, a robot is just a sculpture — beautifully designed, with all the right computations, but utterly incapable of moving. The choice of actuator dictates **payload capacity, speed, precision, repeatability, and even the type of control** (open-loop vs closed-loop) that the robot can use.

## 1.3 The Role of Actuators in the Robotic Manipulator Equation

For a single robotic joint, the dynamic equation reduces to a torque balance. The actuator must supply the **actuator torque** $\tau_a$ to overcome inertia, gravity, Coriolis, centrifugal, and friction effects. For a revolute joint $i$, this is:

$$
\tau_{a,i} = I_i \ddot{q}_i + b_i \dot{q}_i + g_i(q) + \text{friction terms}
$$

where:
- $I_i$ = effective moment of inertia seen at joint $i$
- $b_i$ = viscous friction coefficient
- $g_i(q)$ = gravitational torque
- $q_i$ = joint variable (angle)

The actuator is therefore the **physical realization** of the abstract $\tau_{a,i}$ demanded by the controller.

## 1.4 Primary Performance Metrics of an Actuator

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Industrial Range | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Torque / Force Output** | $\tau$ or $F$ | **1 Nm – 10,000 Nm** (industrial robots) | Maximum work-producing capacity |
| **Speed (No-load)** | $\omega$ or $v$ | **0.1 rad/s – 1000 rad/s** | Maximum attainable speed |
| **Power Rating** | $P$ | **10 W – 75 kW** | Continuous usable power |
| **Bandwidth** | $f_{bw}$ | **1 Hz – 1 kHz** | Speed of response to commands |
| **Efficiency** | $\eta$ | **0.6 – 0.95** | Fraction of input power converted to work |
| **Backlash / Compliance** | — | **0.1 – 50 arc-min** | Mechanical slop / flexibility |
| **Resolution** | — | **0.001° – 1°** | Smallest controllable increment |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stall Torque ($T_s$) and No-Load Speed ($\omega_{nl}$):** These two parameters define the motor's **mechanical power curve**. Maximum continuous power is delivered at approximately $\omega \approx \omega_{nl}/2$, where the torque is roughly half of the stall torque. The torque-speed relationship is **linear** for an ideal DC motor.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Actuators — Structured Logic

Actuators in robotics are classified along three principal axes:

**Axis 1 — Energy Source:**
- **Electric actuators** (DC motor, AC motor, stepper motor, servo motor, BLDC)
- **Hydraulic actuators** (linear cylinder, rotary vane, piston motor)
- **Pneumatic actuators** (cylinder, rotary actuator, muscle)
- **Non-conventional** (Shape Memory Alloy, Piezoelectric, EAP)

**Axis 2 — Motion Type Produced:**
- **Rotary actuators** — produce angular displacement (motors)
- **Linear actuators** — produce translational displacement (cylinders, ball-screws)
- **Oscillatory actuators** — bounded periodic motion

**Axis 3 — Control Type:**
- **Open-loop actuators** (stepper motors, pneumatic cylinders without feedback)
- **Closed-loop actuators** (servo motors with encoder feedback, hydraulic servos)

## 2.2 Detailed Analysis of Major Actuator Types

### 2.2.1 Electric Actuators

**(a) DC Servo Motor**
A permanent-magnet DC motor whose armature current and field flux are controlled to produce a precise torque. The governing equations are:

$$
V_a = I_a R_a + L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt} + E_b
$$

$$
E_b = K_b \, \omega
$$

$$
T_m = K_t \, I_a
$$

- $V_a$ = applied armature voltage
- $R_a$, $L_a$ = armature resistance and inductance
- $E_b$ = back-EMF (counter-electromotive force)
- $K_b$ = back-EMF constant (V·s/rad)
- $K_t$ = torque constant (Nm/A)
- $T_m$ = motor torque

**Why it matters:** DC servo motors are **simple, easy to model, and exhibit a linear torque-speed relationship**, making them ideal for educational and entry-level industrial applications. However, brush wear limits lifespan to typically **2000 – 5000 hours**.

**(b) Stepper Motor**
Moves in discrete angular increments (steps) when energized. Open-loop position control. Two main variants:
- **Variable Reluctance (VR)** — no permanent magnet
- **Permanent Magnet (PM)** — uses rotor magnet
- **Hybrid (HB)** — combines both, gives smallest step angles (typically **1.8°** = 200 steps/rev, or **0.9°** for microstepping)

Step angle relation:

$$
\theta_{step} = \frac{360°}{N_s}
$$

where $N_s$ is the total number of steps per revolution (e.g., 200 for a 1.8° motor).

**(c) Brushless DC (BLDC) Motor**
Electronically commutated. Uses Hall sensors or encoder for rotor position. No brush wear → **lifespan 10,000+ hours**, high power density, high efficiency (**0.85 – 0.95**). Used in modern collaborative robots (cobots) and drone actuators.

**(d) AC Servo / Induction Motor**
Used in heavy-payload industrial robots (e.g., automotive welding). Field-oriented control (FOC) is the standard control technique.

### 2.2.2 Hydraulic Actuators

Operate on the principle that **incompressible fluid under pressure can transmit large forces** through a small cross-section. Operating pressure typically **70 – 210 bar (1000 – 3000 psi)**.

For a linear hydraulic cylinder:

$$
F = P \cdot A
$$

where:
- $F$ = output force (N)
- $P$ = fluid pressure (Pa)
- $A$ = piston cross-sectional area (m²)

Flow rate determines speed:

$$
v = \frac{Q}{A}
$$

where $Q$ is the volumetric flow rate (m³/s).

**Power transmitted:**

$$
P_{hyd} = P \cdot Q = F \cdot v
$$

**Advantages:** Highest power-to-weight ratio (**> 1 kW/kg**), self-lubricating, capable of holding position under load without energy consumption. **Disadvantages:** Leakage, noise, requires pump, filter, reservoir, and valves (high infrastructure cost).

### 2.2.3 Pneumatic Actuators

Operate on **compressible gas (air)** at low pressure (**4 – 8 bar** typically, up to 10 bar). Because air is compressible, pneumatic systems are inherently compliant and harder to position precisely.

Force equation for a double-acting cylinder:

$$
F = (P_1 - P_2) \cdot A
$$

The compliance of air is modeled as:

$$
P V = n R T \quad \text{(Ideal Gas Law)}
$$

For an isothermal compression (slow processes): $P V = \text{constant}$.

**Advantages:** Cheap, clean, fast, simple. **Disadvantages:** Poor position control, noisy, low force capacity, requires compressor.

### 2.2.4 Modern / Non-Conventional Actuators

| Type | Principle | Best Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Shape Memory Alloy (SMA)** | Ni-Ti wire contracts when heated | Micro-actuators, grippers |
| **Piezoelectric** | Strain under applied voltage (PZT ceramics) | High-precision nanopositioning (STM, AFM) |
| **Electroactive Polymer (EAP)** | Deformation under electric field | Biomimetic robots, soft robotics |
| **Series Elastic Actuator (SEA)** | Motor + spring in series | Compliant robots, cobots, prosthetics |
| **McKibben Muscle** | Inflatable bladder + braided mesh | Soft robotics, rehabilitation |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbols | Units | Where Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{out} = \eta P_{in}$ | $P$ = power, $\eta$ = efficiency | W, — | All actuators |
| 2 | $V_a = I_a R_a + L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt} + K_b \omega$ | DC motor armature equation | V, A, Ω, H, V·s/rad | DC servo analysis |
| 3 | $T_m = K_t I_a$ | Torque-current relation | Nm, A | DC motor |
| 4 | $E_b = K_b \omega$ | Back-EMF relation | V, V·s/rad, rad/s | DC motor |
| 5 | $\theta_{step} = 360°/N_s$ | Step angle of stepper | deg | Stepper |
| 6 | $F = P \cdot A$ | Hydraulic / pneumatic force | N, Pa, m² | Fluid actuators |
| 7 | $v = Q/A$ | Velocity from flow rate | m/s, m³/s, m² | Fluid actuators |
| 8 | $P = F \cdot v = T \cdot \omega$ | Mechanical power | W, N, m/s, Nm, rad/s | All |
| 9 | $F_{hyd,max} = P_{max} \cdot A_{piston}$ | Max cylinder force | N | Hydraulic selection |
| 10 | $\tau_{joint} = I \ddot{q} + b \dot{q} + g(q)$ | Joint torque demand | Nm | Dynamics |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Trick:** For a DC motor at the **point of maximum power transfer**, the operating speed is exactly **half the no-load speed** and the developed torque is **half the stall torque**. This is because the torque-speed curve is a straight line: $T = T_s (1 - \omega/\omega_{nl})$.

## 2.4 Why Each Actuator Type is Chosen — Real-World Utility

- **Pneumatic:** Pick-and-place tasks on small parts (food, electronics assembly), grippers, indexing tables.
- **Hydraulic:** Heavy-payload manipulators (forging, casting, mining), excavator-like robots, where **stiffness** and **holding force without energy** are essential.
- **Electric Servo:** **95% of modern industrial robots** (FANUC, ABB, KUKA, Yaskawa) use electric servo drives — clean, precise, easy to integrate.
- **Stepper:** 3D printers, CNC auxiliary axes, low-cost open-loop positioning.
- **BLDC:** Cobots, drones, humanoid robots (Boston Dynamics, Agility Robotics use BLDC-based custom actuators).
- **Piezoelectric:** Nanopositioning stages, atomic force microscopes, lens focusing in smartphones.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Torque-Speed Curve of an Ideal DC Motor

This is one of the **most frequently asked derivations** in KTU university examinations for this module.

**Starting Point — The Armature Circuit Equation:**

The applied voltage $V_a$ drives a current $I_a$ through the armature resistance $R_a$ and inductance $L_a$, while the rotating armature in the magnetic field generates a back-EMF $E_b$ that opposes $V_a$ (Lenz's law).

$$
V_a = I_a R_a + L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt} + E_b
$$

**Steady-State Assumption:** When the motor runs at a constant speed, the current is no longer changing, so $\frac{dI_a}{dt} = 0$. The inductance term drops out:

$$
V_a = I_a R_a + E_b
$$

**Substitute the Back-EMF Expression:**

The back-EMF is proportional to the angular velocity of the rotor, with $K_b$ being the back-EMF constant. This proportionality comes from Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction:

$$
E_b = K_b \, \omega
$$

Substituting into the steady-state equation:

$$
V_a = I_a R_a + K_b \, \omega
$$

**Solve for the Armature Current:**

$$
I_a = \frac{V_a - K_b \omega}{R_a}
$$

**Substitute into the Torque Equation:**

The torque developed by the motor is proportional to the armature current through the torque constant $K_t$ (with $K_t = K_b$ in SI units):

$$
T_m = K_t \, I_a
$$

$$
T_m = K_t \cdot \frac{V_a - K_b \omega}{R_a}
$$

**Identify the Two Limiting Cases:**

- **Stall Condition ($\omega = 0$):** The motor is locked and produces its **maximum** torque, the **stall torque** $T_s$:

$$
T_s = \frac{K_t V_a}{R_a}
$$

- **No-Load Condition ($I_a \to 0$):** The motor runs freely at its **maximum** speed, the **no-load speed** $\omega_{nl}$. From $V_a = I_a R_a + K_b \omega$ with $I_a = 0$:

$$
\omega_{nl} = \frac{V_a}{K_b}
$$

**Express the Torque as a Linear Function of Speed:**

$$
T_m = \frac{K_t V_a}{R_a} - \frac{K_t K_b}{R_a} \omega
$$

$$
T_m = T_s - \frac{T_s}{\omega_{nl}} \, \omega
$$

$$
T_m = T_s \left( 1 - \frac{\omega}{\omega_{nl}} \right)
$$

This is the **linear torque-speed characteristic** of an ideal DC motor. It is a straight line passing through $(0, T_s)$ and $(\omega_{nl}, 0)$.

**Maximum Mechanical Power Point:**

Mechanical power output is:

$$
P_{mech} = T_m \cdot \omega = T_s \omega \left( 1 - \frac{\omega}{\omega_{nl}} \right)
$$

To find the maximum, differentiate with respect to $\omega$ and set to zero:

$$
\frac{dP_{mech}}{d\omega} = T_s \left( 1 - \frac{2\omega}{\omega_{nl}} \right) = 0
$$

$$
\omega_{max-power} = \frac{\omega_{nl}}{2}
$$

At this speed, the torque is:

$$
T_{max-power} = T_s \left( 1 - \frac{1}{2} \right) = \frac{T_s}{2}
$$

And the maximum power is:

$$
P_{max} = \frac{T_s}{2} \cdot \frac{\omega_{nl}}{2} = \frac{T_s \, \omega_{nl}}{4}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Maximum power is delivered at **half the no-load speed**, and the corresponding torque is **half the stall torque**. The maximum power equals **one-quarter** of the product of stall torque and no-load speed. This is a textbook result examiners love to ask.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hydraulic Cylinder Force and Power

For a double-acting hydraulic cylinder with bore diameter $D$ and rod diameter $d$:

- **Annular (extension) area** $A_e = \frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2)$
- **Full bore (retraction) area** $A_r = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2$

**Extension Force** (fluid pushes on full bore area minus rod-side back-pressure):

$$
F_{ext} = P_{sup} A_e - P_{ret} A_r
$$

**For a single-rod cylinder with atmospheric back-pressure** ($P_{ret} \approx 0$):

$$
F_{ext} = P_{sup} \cdot \frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2)
$$

**Power during extension** at speed $v$:

$$
P_{ext} = F_{ext} \cdot v = P_{sup} \cdot \frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2) \cdot v
$$

But $v = Q/A_e$, where $Q$ is the supply flow rate, so:

$$
P_{ext} = P_{sup} \cdot Q
$$

This shows that **hydraulic power is always the product of supply pressure and flow rate**, regardless of cylinder geometry.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selection Decision Table

| Criterion | Pneumatic | Hydraulic | Electric Servo |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Power-to-weight ratio** | Low | **Highest** | Medium |
| **Position accuracy** | Poor (±1 mm typical) | Good (±0.1 mm) | **Excellent (±0.01 mm)** |
| **Stiffness** | Low (compliant) | **Very High** | Medium |
| **Speed** | **Highest** | Medium | High |
| **Cost (initial)** | **Lowest** | High | Medium |
| **Maintenance** | Low | **High** (seals, filters) | Low |
| **Cleanliness** | **Clean** | Risk of oil leaks | **Clean** |
| **Typical pressure/voltage** | 4 – 8 bar | 70 – 210 bar | 24 – 480 V DC / 230 V AC |
| **Holding torque with no power** | No (drift) | **Yes (excellent)** | Yes (with brake) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb used in industry:** "Hydraulic for heavy, electric for clean, pneumatic for cheap and simple." Most modern industrial robotic arms (**FANUC, ABB, KUKA**) use **electric servo drives** because of cleanliness, precision, and ease of computer integration.

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A DC servo motor has the following specifications:
- Armature resistance $R_a = 1.0 \, \Omega$
- Back-EMF constant $K_b = 0.05 \, \text{V·s/rad}$
- Torque constant $K_t = 0.05 \, \text{Nm/A}$
- Applied voltage $V_a = 24 \, \text{V}$

**Find:** (a) No-load speed, (b) Stall torque, (c) Operating point for maximum power.

**Solution (a):**

$$
\omega_{nl} = \frac{V_a}{K_b} = \frac{24}{0.05} = 480 \;\text{rad/s}
$$

**Solution (b):**

$$
T_s = \frac{K_t V_a}{R_a} = \frac{0.05 \times 24}{1.0} = 1.2 \;\text{Nm}
$$

**Solution (c):**

$$
\omega_{max} = \frac{\omega_{nl}}{2} = \frac{480}{2} = 240 \;\text{rad/s}
$$

$$
T_{max} = \frac{T_s}{2} = \frac{1.2}{2} = 0.6 \;\text{Nm}
$$

$$
P_{max} = \frac{T_s \omega_{nl}}{4} = \frac{1.2 \times 480}{4} = 144 \;\text{W}
$$

This is a **representative 14-mark style problem** that tests both theoretical knowledge and arithmetic.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Hierarchy of Actuators (Mermaid Mind-Map Style)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[ROBOTIC ACTUATORS] --> B1[BY ENERGY SOURCE]
    A0 --> B2[BY MOTION TYPE]
    A0 --> B3[BY CONTROL TYPE]

    B1 --> C1A[ELECTRIC]
    B1 --> C1B[HYDRAULIC]
    B1 --> C1C[PNEUMATIC]
    B1 --> C1D[NON CONVENTIONAL]

    C1A --> D1A[DC SERVO MOTOR]
    C1A --> D1B[STEPPER MOTOR]
    C1A --> D1C[BLDC MOTOR]
    C1A --> D1D[AC INDUCTION SERVO]

    C1B --> D1E[LINEAR CYLINDER]
    C1B --> D1F[ROTARY VANE]
    C1B --> D1G[PISTON PUMP MOTOR]

    C1C --> D1H[SINGLE ACTING CYLINDER]
    C1C --> D1I[DOUBLE ACTING CYLINDER]
    C1C --> D1J[ROTARY AIR MOTOR]

    C1D --> D1K[SHAPE MEMORY ALLOY]
    C1D --> D1L[PIEZOELECTRIC]
    C1D --> D1M[SERIES ELASTIC ACTUATOR]

    B2 --> C2A[ROTARY]
    B2 --> C2B[LINEAR]
    B2 --> C2C[OSCILLATORY]

    B3 --> C3A[OPEN LOOP STEPPER]
    B3 --> C3B[CLOSED LOOP SERVO]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The top-level node (A0) splits the actuator universe into three orthogonal classification axes: energy source, motion type, and control type. Under electric actuators, the four dominant subtypes are DC servo, stepper, BLDC, and AC induction servo.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Electric Servo Drive

```mermaid
graph LR
    node1[CONTROLLER PLC / MCU] -- COMMAND TORQUE SIGNAL --> node2[CURRENT CONTROLLER]
    node2 -- PWM GATE DRIVE --> node3[POWER ELECTRONIC INVERTER]
    node3 -- 3 PHASE PWM VOLTAGE --> node4[BLDC OR PMSM MOTOR]
    node4 -- MECHANICAL TORQUE --> node5[GEARBOX / HARMONIC DRIVE]
    node5 -- REDUCED SPEED INCREASED TORQUE --> node6[ROBOTIC JOINT]
    node4 -- ROTOR POSITION --> node7[ENCODER OR RESOLVER]
    node6 -- JOINT ANGLE --> node8[JOINT ENCODER]
    node7 -- FEEDBACK --> node2
    node8 -- POSITION FEEDBACK --> node1

    style node1 fill:#1f77b4,color:#ffffff
    style node2 fill:#ff7f0e,color:#ffffff
    style node3 fill:#2ca02c,color:#ffffff
    style node4 fill:#d62728,color:#ffffff
    style node5 fill:#9467bd,color:#ffffff
    style node6 fill:#8c564b,color:#ffffff
    style node7 fill:#e377c2,color:#ffffff
    style node8 fill:#7f7f7f,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Functional Flow:** The controller issues a torque command, the current controller regulates phase currents, the inverter switches DC bus to 3-phase AC at variable frequency, the motor produces torque, the gearbox reduces speed and multiplies torque, the joint moves, and sensors close the loop. **Two nested feedback loops** are visible — the inner current loop (bandwidth ~kHz) and the outer position/velocity loop (bandwidth ~10s of Hz).

## 4.3 Hydraulic Actuation System Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    h1[ELECTRIC MOTOR DRIVER] --> h2[HYDRAULIC PUMP]
    h2 -- HIGH PRESSURE FLUID --> h3[PRESSURE RELIEF VALVE]
    h2 --> h4[DIRECTIONAL CONTROL VALVE]
    h4 -- EXTEND --> h5[PISTON SIDE OF CYLINDER]
    h4 -- RETRACT --> h6[ROD SIDE OF CYLINDER]
    h5 --> h7[ROBOTIC LINK]
    h6 --> h7
    h7 -- POSITION --> h8[LINEAR VARIABLE DIFFERENTIAL TRANSFORMER LVDT]
    h8 -- FEEDBACK --> h4

    h2 --> h9[RESERVOIR]
    h9 --> h10[FILTER]
    h10 --> h2

    style h1 fill:#1f77b4,color:#ffffff
    style h2 fill:#ff7f0e,color:#ffffff
    style h3 fill:#d62728,color:#ffffff
    style h4 fill:#2ca02c,color:#ffffff
    style h7 fill:#8c564b,color:#ffffff
    style h8 fill:#7f7f7f,color:#ffffff
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Subsystem:** The **directional control valve (DCV)** is the heart of a hydraulic servo system. It meters flow into either side of the cylinder. Combined with an LVDT position feedback, it forms a **closed-loop hydraulic servo** with very high stiffness and force output.

## 4.4 Comparison Flowchart — Which Actuator Should You Choose?

```mermaid
graph TD
    s1[START] --> s2{PAYLOAD GREATER THAN 50 KG}
    s2 -- YES --> s3[HYDRAULIC RECOMMENDED]
    s2 -- NO --> s4{PRECISION NEEDED BELOW 0.1 MM}
    s4 -- YES --> s5[ELECTRIC SERVO RECOMMENDED]
    s4 -- NO --> s6{CLEAN ROOM REQUIRED}
    s6 -- YES --> s7[ELECTRIC SERVO OR PIEZOELECTRIC]
    s6 -- NO --> s8{BUDGET BELOW 5000 RUPEES}
    s8 -- YES --> s9[PNEUMATIC RECOMMENDED]
    s8 -- NO --> s5

    style s3 fill:#d62728,color:#ffffff

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**

**Q: Define an actuator in the context of robotics. List any four desirable characteristics of an ideal robotic actuator.**

**Model Answer:**

An **actuator** is an electro-mechanical, hydraulic, or pneumatic device that converts an input energy source into physical motion (linear, rotary, or oscillatory) so as to enable a robot to perform work on its environment. It is often called the "muscle" of the robot.

**Four desirable characteristics of an ideal robotic actuator:**

1. **High power-to-weight ratio** — to maximize payload while minimizing arm inertia.
2. **High efficiency** — to minimize energy consumption and heat generation.
3. **Controllability** — ease of accepting command signals and producing proportional output.
4. **High stiffness** — to minimize deflection under load, ensuring positional accuracy.
5. *(Bonus)* Low cost, long life, cleanliness, and good bandwidth are also valued.

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Style]**

**Q: Differentiate between a stepper motor and a DC servo motor based on control type, position accuracy, and typical applications.**

**Model Answer:**

| Criterion | Stepper Motor | DC Servo Motor |
|---|---|---|
| **Control type** | Open-loop (no feedback required) | Closed-loop (encoder feedback required) |
| **Position accuracy** | Limited by step angle (1.8° typical); open-loop drift on overload | **Very high** (sub-arc-minute with encoder) |
| **Holding torque at standstill** | High (permanent magnet detent torque) | Requires brake or continuous current |
| **Typical applications** | 3D printers, CNC auxiliary axes, small indexing | Industrial robot joints, CNC main drives, camera gimbals |
| **Cost** | Lower (no encoder needed) | Higher (motor + encoder + drive) |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each 14-mark question has **two sub-parts**, usually (a) carrying 7 marks and (b) carrying 7 marks. Students are expected to write **neat block diagrams, derive equations, and produce numerical answers with units**.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Module 1, Past Year Pattern]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a **Brushless DC (BLDC) motor** used as a robotic actuator. Discuss its advantages over a brushed DC servo motor. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A DC servo motor has the following specifications: $R_a = 0.5 \, \Omega$, $K_b = 0.08 \, \text{V·s/rad}$, $K_t = 0.08 \, \text{Nm/A}$, $V_a = 48 \, \text{V}$. Calculate (i) the no-load speed, (ii) the stall torque, and (iii) the speed and torque at maximum power. (7 Marks)

---

**MODEL ANSWER (a):**

A **BLDC motor** consists of a permanent-magnet rotor and a stator with three-phase windings. Unlike a brushed DC motor, the commutation is done **electronically** rather than mechanically, using rotor position feedback from Hall-effect sensors (low-cost) or an encoder (high-precision).

**Working Principle:**

- The controller receives rotor position from sensors.
- Based on the rotor angle, the controller energizes **two of the three phases** at any instant, in a sequence that produces a rotating stator magnetic field.
- The permanent-magnet rotor continuously chases this rotating field, producing smooth torque.
- The electronic commutation ensures that the current always flows in the correct phase pair to maximize torque per ampere.

**Block diagram of the BLDC drive:**

$$
\text{Controller} \;\to\; \text{Logic / Commutator} \;\to\; \text{3-Phase Inverter} \;\to\; \text{BLDC Motor} \;\to\; \text{Load}
$$

The Hall sensor signals form a feedback loop back to the controller.

**Advantages over Brushed DC Servo:**

1. **No brush wear** → lifespan > 10,000 hours (vs 2,000–5,000 hours for brushed).
2. **Higher efficiency (0.85 – 0.95)** due to absence of brush friction and voltage drop.
3. **Better heat dissipation** — rotor has no windings, so heat is in the stator which can be mounted on a heatsink.
4. **Higher speed capability** — no mechanical commutation limit.
5. **Lower electromagnetic interference** and lower maintenance.
6. **Higher power-to-weight ratio** — preferred for cobots, drones, and humanoid robots.

---

**MODEL ANSWER (b):**

**Given:** $R_a = 0.5 \, \Omega$, $K_b = 0.08 \, \text{V·s/rad}$, $K_t = 0.08 \, \text{Nm/A}$, $V_a = 48 \, \text{V}$

**(i) No-load speed:**

$$
\omega_{nl} = \frac{V_a}{K_b} = \frac{48}{0.08} = 600 \;\text{rad/s}
$$

**(ii) Stall torque:**

$$
T_s = \frac{K_t V_a}{R_a} = \frac{0.08 \times 48}{0.5} = 7.68 \;\text{Nm}
$$

**(iii) Speed and torque at maximum power:**

$$
\omega_{max-power} = \frac{\omega_{nl}}{2} = \frac{600}{2} = 300 \;\text{rad/s}
$$

$$
T_{max-power} = \frac{T_s}{2} = \frac{7.68}{2} = 3.84 \;\text{Nm}
$$

$$
P_{max} = T_{max} \times \omega_{max} = 3.84 \times 300 = 1152 \;\text{W}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write $\omega_{nl} = V_a R_a / K_b$ or forget to convert units. Always ensure that $K_b$ has the correct unit (V·s/rad in SI). If a numerical answer is wrong by a factor of 10, it is almost always a **unit error**, not a conceptual one — and the examiner will not give partial credit for that.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam - Module 1, Past Year Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the working of a **hydraulic actuator system** with a neat block diagram. Derive the force, velocity, and power relationships for a double-acting hydraulic cylinder. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has a bore diameter of 80 mm and a rod diameter of 45 mm. It is supplied with fluid at 100 bar during extension, and the back-pressure on the rod side is 5 bar. The flow rate is 15 L/min. Calculate the extension force, extension velocity, and hydraulic power. (7 Marks)

---

**MODEL ANSWER (a):**

A **hydraulic actuator system** uses an incompressible fluid (typically hydraulic oil) pressurized by a pump, metered through a directional control valve, to drive a cylinder or motor. The system consists of:

- **Prime mover** (electric motor / diesel engine)
- **Hydraulic pump** (gear / vane / piston)
- **Reservoir** with **filter**
- **Directional control valve (DCV)**
- **Cylinder / hydraulic motor** (the actuator itself)
- **Pressure relief valve** for safety
- **Position sensor** (LVDT) for closed-loop control

**Block diagram:**

$$
\text{Electric Motor} \to \text{Pump} \to \text{Relief Valve (parallel)} \to \text{DCV} \to \text{Cylinder} \to \text{Load}
$$

**Derivation of Force, Velocity, Power:**

For a double-acting cylinder with bore $D$ and rod diameter $d$:

- **Full bore area** (used during retraction, when fluid acts on the full piston face):

$$
A_b = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}
$$

- **Annular area** (used during extension, when fluid acts on the piston face minus the rod area):

$$
A_a = \frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2)
$$

**Force during Extension** (with $P_s$ = supply pressure, $P_r$ = back-pressure on rod side):

$$
F_{ext} = P_s A_a - P_r A_b
$$

**Velocity during Extension** (with $Q$ = supply flow rate):

$$
v_{ext} = \frac{Q}{A_a}
$$

**Hydraulic Power** transmitted by the fluid:

$$
P_{hyd} = P_s \cdot Q = F_{ext} \cdot v_{ext}
$$

This last equation is fundamental: **hydraulic power is always the product of supply pressure and flow rate**, and it equals the rate of mechanical work done.

---

**MODEL ANSWER (b):**

**Given:** Bore $D = 80 \; \text{mm} = 0.08 \; \text{m}$, Rod $d = 45 \; \text{mm} = 0.045 \; \text{m}$, Supply pressure $P_s = 100 \; \text{bar} = 100 \times 10^5 \; \text{Pa} = 10^7 \; \text{Pa}$, Back-pressure $P_r = 5 \; \text{bar} = 5 \times 10^5 \; \text{Pa}$, Flow rate $Q = 15 \; \text{L/min} = \frac{15 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$.

**Step 1: Calculate the annular (effective) area:**

$$
A_a = \frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}\left((0.08)^2 - (0.045)^2\right)
$$

$$
A_a = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.0064 - 0.002025) = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.004375) = 3.435 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 2: Calculate the extension force:**

$$
F_{ext} = P_s A_a - P_r A_b
$$

We also need the full bore area:

$$
A_b = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.08)^2}{4} = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^2
$$

$$
F_{ext} = (10^7)(3.435 \times 10^{-3}) - (5 \times 10^5)(5.027 \times 10^{-3})
$$

$$
F_{ext} = 34350 - 2513.5 = 31836.5 \;\text{N} \approx 31.84 \;\text{kN}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate the extension velocity:**

$$
v_{ext} = \frac{Q}{A_a} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-4}}{3.435 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0728 \;\text{m/s} = 72.8 \;\text{mm/s}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate the hydraulic power:**

$$
P_{hyd} = P_s \cdot Q = 10^7 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-4} = 2500 \;\text{W} = 2.5 \;\text{kW}
$$

**Verification:** $F_{ext} \cdot v_{ext} = 31836.5 \times 0.0728 \approx 2317 \; \text{W}$. The small discrepancy with 2500 W is the back-pressure loss.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students use the **bore area $A_b$** in the force formula for extension — this is a **conceptual error** because during extension, the rod occupies part of the bore, so the effective area is the **annular area $A_a$**. The bore area is used only during retraction. Losing 2 marks here is the most common mistake in fluid power questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Summary for Last-Minute Revision:**

- [x] **Actuator definition:** Energy → Mechanical output. Three principal families: **electric, hydraulic, pneumatic**.
- [x] **DC motor armature equation:** $V_a = I_a R_a + L_a dI_a/dt + K_b \omega$
- [x] **Torque equation:** $T = K_t I_a$ (and $K_t = K_b$ in SI units).
- [x] **Stall torque:** $T_s = K_t V_a / R_a$
- [x] **No-load speed:** $\omega_{nl} = V_a / K_b$
- [x] **Torque-speed curve:** $T = T_s (1 - \omega/\omega_{nl})$ — **linear**.
- [x] **Maximum power point:** at $\omega = \omega_{nl}/2$ and $T = T_s/2$, giving $P_{max} = T_s \omega_{nl}/4$.
- [x] **Stepper motor step angle:** $\theta_{step} = 360°/N_s$. Hybrid stepper typically 1.8° (200 steps).
- [x] **BLDC advantage:** No brushes → high efficiency, long life, used in cobots.
- [x] **Hydraulic force:** $F = P \cdot A$ where $A$ is annular area for extension.
- [x] **Hydraulic power:** $P = P_s \cdot Q = F \cdot v$ (always valid).
- [x] **Pneumatic pressure:** 4–8 bar; **Hydraulic pressure:** 70–210 bar; therefore hydraulic force is **~20× higher** for the same cylinder size.
- [x] **Stepper motor** = open-loop; **Servo motor** = closed-loop.
- [x] **Piezoelectric actuators** are used in nanopositioning (sub-nm resolution).
- [x] **Shape Memory Alloys (Nitinol)** change shape with temperature and are used in micro-grippers.
- [x] **Series Elastic Actuators (SEAs)** have a motor + spring in series, providing compliance for safe human-robot interaction (used in cobots like Universal Robots UR-series).
- [x] **Selection rule of thumb:** Pneumatic for cheap/simple, hydraulic for heavy/high-force, electric for clean/precise.

---

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Actuators (PCCAT602, KTU 2024 Scheme)**

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sensor (KTU Standard Definition):** A *sensor* is a physical device (or transducer) that detects, measures, and responds to some type of input from the physical environment (such as light, heat, motion, force, chemical concentration, or magnetic field) and converts this physical stimulus into an analog or digital electrical signal that can be read by an observer, controller, or instrumentation system.

In the context of **Robotics and Automation (PCCAT602)**, a sensor acts as the **"sensory organ" of a robotic system** — without sensors, a robot is a *blind* mechanical structure incapable of perceiving its workspace, executing feedback control, or performing intelligent decision-making. The KTU 2024 Scheme specifically emphasizes sensors as the *first link* in the **Perception → Planning → Action** loop.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Body

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Sensors are the "Five Senses" of a Robot**

Just like humans rely on their five senses to interact with the world, a robot relies on sensors to interact with its environment:

| Human Sense | Robotic Equivalent (Sensor) | Physical Stimulus Detected |
|---|---|---|
| **Touch / Skin** | Tactile, Force/Load sensors | Contact pressure, texture |
| **Eyes / Vision** | Vision (CCD/CMOS Cameras), LiDAR | Light intensity, distance, shape |
| **Ears / Hearing** | Microphone, Ultrasonic sensor | Acoustic waves, vibration |
| **Proprioception (muscle sense)** | Encoders, Potentiometers, IMU | Joint angle, position, velocity |
| **Smell / Taste** | Chemical, Gas sensors (e-Nose) | Concentration, pH, composition |

**Key Insight:** A robot without sensors can only perform *open-loop* tasks (like a pre-programmed industrial arm repeating a fixed motion). With sensors, the robot gains *closed-loop intelligence* — it can *see*, *feel*, and *react*.

## 1.3 Sensor Signal Conditioning — A Foundational Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The output of most physical sensors is a **small, often noisy, analog voltage or current signal** (e.g., a thermocouple produces only a few **microvolts per °C**). This raw signal must be processed before it can be used by a digital controller.

The standard processing chain is:

**Physical Quantity → Sensor → Signal Conditioner → ADC → Microcontroller / PLC**

## 1.4 Standard Sensor Performance Metrics (KTU Board Frequently Tested)

> [!NOTE]
> The following terms **must** be memorized for KTU ESE and CE/ESE evaluations:

- **Range:** The minimum and maximum value of input the sensor can measure.
- **Span (Full-Scale Range, FSR):** The algebraic difference between the upper and lower range values. $\text{Span} = x_{max} - x_{min}$
- **Accuracy:** The maximum difference between the *true value* and the *measured value*, usually expressed as $\pm$ % of Full Scale (FS).
- **Precision / Repeatability:** The ability of the sensor to produce the same output for the same input under identical conditions (statistical variance).
- **Resolution:** The smallest *detectable* change in input that produces a perceptible change in output.
- **Sensitivity:** The ratio of output signal change to input quantity change.
- **Linearity:** The degree to which the output is directly proportional to the input (ideally a straight line).
- **Drift:** The change in output over time when the input is held constant.
- **Hysteresis:** The difference in output for the same input when approached from *opposite directions* (increasing vs. decreasing).
- **Bandwidth / Response Time:** The frequency range over which the sensor can faithfully respond, or the time required for output to reach **63.2%** of its final value (1 time constant, $\tau$).
- **Dynamic Range:** The ratio (in dB) between the largest and smallest detectable signals.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sensor Classification (Hierarchical — KTU Board Favorite)

Sensors used in robotics are classified along **three principal axes**:

### A. Classification by Function / Role in Robot

1. **Internal State Sensors (Proprioceptors):**
   - Measure the robot's *own* internal variables.
   - Examples: **encoders** (joint position), **tachometers** (joint velocity), **resolvers**, **inertial measurement units (IMU)**, **motor current sensors**, **strain gauges** (force/load on joints).

2. **External State Sensors (Exteroceptors):**
   - Measure variables *external* to the robot (i.e., the environment / workpiece).
   - Examples: **vision sensors (cameras)**, **proximity sensors** (inductive, capacitive, optical, ultrasonic), **range finders (LiDAR)**, **tactile sensors**, **temperature sensors**, **force/torque sensors at the end-effector**.

### B. Classification by Energy Conversion (Active vs. Passive)

| Type | Definition | External Power? | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Active Sensors** | Generate an electrical output directly from the physical stimulus (energy converter / self-generating). | **No** | Piezoelectric, thermocouple, photovoltaic, electromagnetic |
| **Passive Sensors** | Change one of their electrical properties (R, L, C) in response to stimulus. Require external excitation. | **Yes** | Thermistor, RTD, strain gauge, LVDT, photoresistor |

### C. Classification by Output Signal Type

- **Analog Sensors:** Continuous output proportional to input (e.g., potentiometer, thermistor).
- **Digital Sensors:** Output in discrete steps or as a binary bit-stream (e.g., optical encoder, Hall-effect digital switch).

## 2.2 Mathematical Modeling of a Sensor

A general sensor can be modeled as a **first-order linear dynamic system**:

$$G(s) = \frac{V_{out}(s)}{X_{in}(s)} = \frac{K}{\tau s + 1}$$

Where:
- $K$ = **Static Sensitivity** (V/unit of input)
- $\tau$ = **Time Constant** (seconds), where $\tau = RC$ for an electrical equivalent
- $s$ = Laplace variable

**Step Response (Time Domain):**
For a sudden step change $\Delta x$ in input at $t = 0$:

$$v_{out}(t) = K \cdot \Delta x \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$$

At $t = \tau$, the output reaches **63.2%** of its final steady-state value — this is the standard definition of the sensor's *response time*.

## 2.3 Common Sensor Architectures in Robotics

### 2.3.1 Potentiometer (Position Sensor)
- A **resistive element** with a sliding wiper. Output voltage is **directly proportional** to displacement.
- Acts as a **voltage divider**.
- Cost: **Low** | Resolution: Limited by winding pitch.

### 2.3.2 Optical Encoder
- Disk with radial slots, light source, and photodetector.
- **Incremental Encoder:** Pulses proportional to motion (need reference/home for absolute position).
- **Absolute Encoder:** Unique binary (Gray) code for every angular position — *no power-loss problem*.
- Resolution: $N = \text{pulses per revolution (PPR)}$.

### 2.3.3 LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer)
- A **passive, contactless, inductive** sensor.
- Three coils: one primary, two secondaries. The differential voltage $V_{sec1} - V_{sec2}$ is **linear** with core displacement.

### 2.3.4 Strain Gauge (Force Sensor)
- A **resistive sensor** bonded to a load-bearing element. Deformation changes resistance.

$$R = \rho \frac{L}{A}$$

Gauge Factor:
$$GF = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\Delta L / L}$$

For metallic gauges, $GF \approx 2.0$.

### 2.3.5 Proximity Sensors
- **Inductive:** Detects *metallic* objects only via eddy-current losses. Range: **1–10 mm** typical.
- **Capacitive:** Detects *metallic and non-metallic* (plastic, liquid) objects via dielectric change. Range: **3–30 mm**.
- **Optical (Photoelectric):** Detects via light beam interruption / reflection. Range: **up to several meters**.
- **Ultrasonic:** Sends 40 kHz pulse, measures Time-of-Flight (ToF). Range: **cm to ~10 m**.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **all board-critical formulas** for sensors:

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Sensitivity** | $K = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$ | Output / Input |
| 2 | **Span (FSR)** | $\text{FSR} = x_{max} - x_{min}$ | Same as input |
| 3 | **% Accuracy** | $\text{Acc} = \pm \frac{\Delta_{max}}{FSR} \times 100$ | % of FS |
| 4 | **Resolution (n-bit ADC)** | $Q = \frac{FSR}{2^n - 1}$ | Volts per LSB |
| 5 | **Strain Gauge** | $GF = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | **Time Constant (1st order)** | $\tau = RC$ (electrical) | Seconds |
| 7 | **Step Response (1st order)** | $y(t) = K\Delta x (1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ | Volts |
| 8 | **Settling Time (2%)** | $t_s \approx 4\tau$ | Seconds |
| 9 | **Ultrasonic Distance** | $d = \frac{v \cdot t_{ToF}}{2}$ | meters; $v \approx 343$ m/s in air |
| 10 | **LiDAR Time of Flight** | $d = \frac{c \cdot t_{ToF}}{2}$ | meters; $c \approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s |
| 11 | **Encoder Resolution** | $\theta_{res} = \frac{360°}{N}$ | degrees per pulse |
| 12 | **Dynamic Range (dB)** | $DR = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{x_{max}}{x_{min}}\right)$ | dB |
| 13 | **Wheatstone Bridge Output** | $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R_1 R_3 - R_2 R_4}{(R_1+R_2)(R_3+R_4)}$ | Volts |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Robotic Arms:** Force/torque sensors at wrist for *assembly* and *polishing*; encoders on every joint.
- **Autonomous Mobile Robots (AMRs):** LiDAR + IMU + wheel encoders (sensor fusion via EKF).
- **Collaborative Robots (Cobots):** Tactile skin + proximity sensors for *safe human-robot interaction*.
- **Drones / UAVs:** IMU + GPS + barometer + optical flow for stable flight.
- **Surgical Robots:** High-precision force sensors for haptic feedback in minimally invasive surgery.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All derivations below follow the **KTU 2024 Scheme** standard: every algebraic transition is written explicitly, with no skipped steps. These are typical **Part B (14-mark)** derivations.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Wheatstone Bridge Output for a Strain Gauge

### Problem Setup
A single active strain gauge ($R_g = R + \Delta R$) is mounted on a cantilever beam. The gauge forms one arm of a **Wheatstone bridge** with three fixed resistors $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R$. The bridge is excited by $V_{ex}$. Derive the output voltage $V_o$ in terms of $\Delta R$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Write KVL for the two parallel branches:**

Let Node A be the top of the bridge (positive output), Node B be the bottom (negative output). The two voltage dividers are:

Left branch: $R_1$ (top) and $R_2$ (bottom).
Right branch: $R_g = R + \Delta R$ (top) and $R_3 = R$ (bottom).

**Step 2 — Voltage at Node A (Left Branch):**

$$V_A = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R}{R + R} = \frac{V_{ex}}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Voltage at Node B (Right Branch):**

$$V_B = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_g + R_3} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R}{(R + \Delta R) + R} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R}{2R + \Delta R}$$

**Step 4 — Output Voltage $V_o = V_A - V_B$:**

$$V_o = \frac{V_{ex}}{2} - \frac{V_{ex} \cdot R}{2R + \Delta R}$$

**Step 5 — Common Denominator $(2)(2R + \Delta R)$:**

$$V_o = V_{ex} \left[\frac{(2R + \Delta R) - 2R}{2(2R + \Delta R)}\right] = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{2(2R + \Delta R)}$$

**Step 6 — Approximation (since $\Delta R \ll R$):**

Since the gauge factor is small ($\Delta R \ll R$), we may approximate $2R + \Delta R \approx 2R$:

$$V_o \approx V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{2 \cdot 2R} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}$$

**Step 7 — Express in terms of Strain $\varepsilon$ and Gauge Factor $GF$:**

Using $\frac{\Delta R}{R} = GF \cdot \varepsilon$:

$$\boxed{V_o \approx \frac{V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon}{4}}$$

**Physical Interpretation:** The output voltage is *directly proportional* to the strain on the beam, and the proportionality constant is $\frac{V_{ex} \cdot GF}{4}$. Doubling the excitation voltage doubles the sensitivity — a key design principle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Full-Bridge Bonus:** If **all four** arms are active strain gauges (two in tension, two in compression), the output becomes $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$ — a **4× improvement** in sensitivity. This is the **standard configuration for industrial load cells**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Sensor Time Constant from Step Response Data

### Problem
A thermistor is suddenly immersed in a hot oil bath. The output voltage reaches **2.0 V** at $t = 0$ and **3.16 V** at $t = 1.5$ s, and asymptotically approaches **4.0 V**. Calculate the **time constant $\tau$** and the **static sensitivity $K$** (per °C), given that the oil temperature step is 80 °C.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the steady-state and initial values:**

Initial value: $y(0) = 2.0$ V.
Final (steady-state) value: $y(\infty) = 4.0$ V.
Total output change: $\Delta y = y(\infty) - y(0) = 4.0 - 2.0 = 2.0$ V.

**Step 2 — Static Sensitivity (Gain):**

$$K = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \frac{2.0 \text{ V}}{80 \text{ °C}} = 0.025 \text{ V/°C} = 25 \text{ mV/°C}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the First-Order Step Response Equation:**

$$y(t) = y(\infty) - \Delta y \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the Known Values at $t = 1.5$ s:**

$$3.16 = 4.0 - 2.0 \cdot e^{-1.5/\tau}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the Exponential Term:**

$$2.0 \cdot e^{-1.5/\tau} = 4.0 - 3.16 = 0.84$$

$$e^{-1.5/\tau} = \frac{0.84}{2.0} = 0.42$$

**Step 6 — Take the Natural Logarithm of Both Sides:**

$$-\frac{1.5}{\tau} = \ln(0.42) = -0.8675$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $\tau$:**

$$\tau = \frac{1.5}{0.8675} \approx 1.729 \text{ s}$$

**Step 8 — Verify via the 63.2% Rule:**

At $t = \tau$, output should be $y(0) + 0.632 \cdot \Delta y = 2.0 + 0.632 \times 2.0 = 3.264$ V. Cross-check by plugging $\tau = 1.729$ s, $t = 1.5$ s into the equation:

$$y(1.5) = 4.0 - 2.0 \cdot e^{-1.5/1.729} = 4.0 - 2.0 \cdot e^{-0.8675} = 4.0 - 2.0 \times 0.420 = 3.16 \text{ V} \checkmark$$

$$\boxed{\tau \approx 1.73 \text{ s}, \quad K = 0.025 \text{ V/°C}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Ultrasonic Sensor Range Calculation

### Problem
An ultrasonic sensor emits a 40 kHz pulse toward a wall. The echo returns after **$6$ ms**. Calculate the distance. Also determine the *minimum measurable distance* (dead zone) if the receiver blanking time is $1$ ms.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the speed of sound in air (at 20 °C):**

$$v_{sound} \approx 343 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Time-of-Flight Equation (round trip):**

$$d = \frac{v_{sound} \cdot t_{ToF}}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $t_{ToF} = 6 \times 10^{-3}$ s:**

$$d = \frac{343 \times 6 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = \frac{2.058}{2} = 1.029 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Minimum Distance (Dead Zone):**

$$d_{min} = \frac{v_{sound} \cdot t_{blank}}{2} = \frac{343 \times 1 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = 0.1715 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{d \approx 1.03 \text{ m}, \quad d_{min} \approx 17.15 \text{ cm}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Insight:** The "blanking time" prevents the receiver from being saturated by the transmit pulse. Most industrial ultrasonic sensors have a dead zone of **10–30 cm**, beyond which they function accurately.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sensor Selection by Application

| Application | Required Sensor | Key Property | Why |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Joint angle of a 6-DOF arm** | Optical Encoder (absolute) | High resolution, no power-loss issue | Direct digital readout; needs absolute reference |
| **Detecting a plastic bottle on conveyor** | Capacitive Proximity | Detects non-metals | Inductive sensors would fail |
| **Detecting a steel nut on conveyor** | Inductive Proximity | Robust, oil-resistant | Long life, low cost |
| **Welding seam tracking** | Laser triangulation / Vision | $\mu$m-level accuracy | Non-contact, high speed |
| **Measuring ambient room temperature (HVAC)** | Thermistor / RTD | High sensitivity (thermistor) | Low cost, adequate accuracy |
| **Measuring 1200 °C furnace temperature** | Thermocouple (Type K) | Wide range, rugged | RTD fails above ~600 °C |
| **Autonomous vehicle obstacle avoidance (long range)** | LiDAR | 360°, cm accuracy | Superior to ultrasonic for >10 m |
| **Haptic feedback on surgical tool** | Strain gauge + amplifier | mN-level force resolution | Required for delicate tissue handling |
| **Robot balancing (humanoid)** | IMU (Gyro + Accelerometer) | 6-DOF, high bandwidth | Drift compensation via Kalman filter |
