# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define an end effector. List any four types of end effectors used in industrial robots.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> An end effector is a device attached to the wrist of a robotic manipulator that enables the robot to perform its designated task by interacting with the environment or workpiece.
>
> Four types of end effectors are:
> 1. **Mechanical Grippers** — for grasping and holding workpieces.
> 2. **Vacuum Grippers** — for handling smooth, flat objects like glass sheets.
> 3. **Welding Torches** — for spot or arc welding in automotive assembly.
> 4. **Spray Painting Nozzles** — for uniform coating applications.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a parallel-jaw gripper and an angular-jaw gripper.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Parallel-Jaw Gripper | Angular-Jaw Gripper |
|---------|---------------------|---------------------|
| Jaw motion | Linear (translatory) | Rotary (pivoting) |
| Gripping orientation | Constant | Varies with opening |
| Best suited for | Cuboidal, prismatic parts | Cylindrical, irregular parts |
| Mechanical complexity | Higher | Lower |
| Self-locking risk | No | Possible (over-center toggle) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B questions carry **14 marks each** and offer internal choice. Each question typically has sub-parts **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Solve both sub-parts completely to score full marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Modified]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working of a 2-finger pneumatic parallel-jaw gripper. List its main components.**

**Model Solution:**

A 2-finger pneumatic parallel-jaw gripper uses compressed air to actuate two opposing jaws that translate linearly to grasp an object.

**Main Components (4 marks):**
1. **Pneumatic Cylinder** (double-acting type): Converts air pressure into linear piston motion.
2. **Four-Bar Linkage** (or wedge mechanism): Transmits and synchronizes the piston's motion to both jaws equally.
3. **Finger Jaws (2 Nos.)**: The contacting members, usually T-slotted to allow finger pad interchangeability.
4. **Finger Pads**: Removable contact surfaces, often made of rubber, polyurethane, or textured steel.
5. **Limit Switches / Position Sensors**: Detect fully open and fully closed positions.
6. **FRL Unit**: Filters, regulates, and lubricates the incoming compressed air.
7. **Solenoid Valves** (5/2 or 3/2): Electrically control airflow direction.
8. **Mounting Flange**: Bolts to the robot wrist, conforms to ISO 9409 standard.

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
When the robot controller sends a **grip signal**, the solenoid valve energizes and admits compressed air into the **blank end** of the pneumatic cylinder. The piston moves forward, pushing the linkage. The linkage converts this linear motion into **equal and opposite linear motion** of the two jaws, causing them to close onto the workpiece. Friction between finger pads and the object holds it. To release, the controller de-energizes the valve, venting the blank end and pressurizing the **rod end**, retracting the piston and opening the jaws.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**A 2-finger mechanical gripper is required to lift a payload of 15 kg. The coefficient of friction between the rubber finger pads and the object surface is 0.3. The gripper experiences an upward acceleration of 2 m/s² during the lift. Using a safety factor of 2.5, determine: (i) the gripping force per finger, and (ii) the minimum pneumatic cylinder bore diameter if the supply pressure is 5 bar and the linkage mechanical advantage is 1.5.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Mass $m = 15$ kg
- $\mu = 0.3$
- $a = 2$ m/s² (upward)
- $k = 2.5$
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- $P_{supply} = 5$ bar $= 5 \times 10^5$ N/m²
- $M.A. = 1.5$

**Step 1: Calculate the weight of the payload.**

$$W = m \cdot g = 15 \times 9.81 = 147.15 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2: Apply the gripping force formula under acceleration.**

$$N = \frac{k \cdot W \cdot (g + a)}{2\mu \cdot g}$$

$$N = \frac{2.5 \times 147.15 \times (9.81 + 2)}{2 \times 0.3 \times 9.81}$$

$$N = \frac{2.5 \times 147.15 \times 11.81}{5.886}$$

$$N = \frac{4344.85}{5.886} = 738.2 \text{ N}$$

> **Answer (i): Gripping force per finger = 738.2 N**

**Step 3: Calculate the total gripping force.**

$$F_{total} = 2N = 2 \times 738.2 = 1476.4 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4: Calculate the required actuator force using mechanical advantage.**

$$F_{actuator} = \frac{F_{total}}{M.A.} = \frac{1476.4}{1.5} = 984.27 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5: Calculate the minimum cylinder bore diameter.**

The force produced by a pneumatic cylinder is:

$$F_{actuator} = P \cdot A = P \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

Solving for $D$:

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{4 \cdot F_{actuator}}{P \cdot \pi}}$$

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 984.27}{5 \times 10^5 \times \pi}}$$

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{3937.08}{1.5708 \times 10^6}}$$

$$D = \sqrt{2.5064 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.05006 \text{ m}$$

$$D = 50.06 \text{ mm}$$

> **Answer (ii): Minimum cylinder bore diameter ≈ 50 mm**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Most common mistake:** Students forget to multiply by the safety factor $k = 2.5$ — losing 2 marks.
> 2. **Second common mistake:** Using $W$ instead of $m(g+a)$ for accelerating systems. The effective weight **increases** under upward acceleration.
> 3. **Third common mistake:** Forgetting to convert bar to N/m² (multiply by $10^5$) before using in the cylinder bore formula.
> 4. **Unit discipline:** Always carry units through the calculation. KTU examiners specifically check this.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023 - Modified]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the different drive mechanisms used in mechanical grippers. Compare pneumatic, hydraulic, and electric grippers on the basis of gripping force, speed, control, and typical applications.**

**Model Solution:**

**Drive Mechanisms (3 marks):**
1. **Pneumatic Drive** — Uses compressed air (typically 4–6 bar) acting on a piston.
2. **Hydraulic Drive** — Uses pressurized oil (typically 50–200 bar) acting on a piston.
3. **Electric Drive** — Uses DC/Stepper/Servo motors driving lead screws, racks, or cams.
4. **Spring / Mechanical Drive** — Uses preloaded springs; gripper closes when released.

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Pneumatic | Hydraulic | Electric (Servo) |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|------------------|
| Gripping Force | Medium (up to ~2000 N) | Very High (up to 50,000 N) | High (up to 10,000 N) |
| Speed | Very Fast (open/close in 0.1–0.5 s) | Medium (0.5–1 s) | Medium (0.5–1 s) |
| Position Control | Binary (open/close) | Binary (open/close) | Continuous, precise |
| Force Control | Limited (via pressure regulator) | Limited | Excellent (servo-loop) |
| Sensor Integration | Difficult | Difficult | Easy (built-in encoders) |
| Cleanliness | Very clean (only air) | Risk of oil leaks | Very clean |
| Cost | Low | High (pump, hoses) | High (motors, drivers) |
| Typical Application | Pick-and-place, packaging, food industry | Heavy forging, casting handling | Precision assembly, electronics |

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**A robot gripper with 3 fingers is used to hold a smooth cylindrical shaft of mass 8 kg. The coefficient of friction is 0.25. If the safety factor is 2, calculate: (i) the gripping force per finger, and (ii) the total gripping force exerted by the gripper. Comment on why a 3-finger gripper is preferred for cylindrical workpieces.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $m = 8$ kg
- $\mu = 0.25$
- $k = 2$
- $n = 3$ (number of fingers)

**Step 1: Calculate the weight.**

$$W = 8 \times 9.81 = 78.48 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2: Calculate gripping force per finger using 3-finger formula.**

$$N = \frac{k \cdot W}{3\mu} = \frac{2 \times 78.48}{3 \times 0.25} = \frac{156.96}{0.75} = 209.28 \text{ N}$$

> **Answer (i): Gripping force per finger = 209.28 N**

**Step 3: Calculate total gripping force.**

$$F_{total} = 3N = 3 \times 209.28 = 627.84 \text{ N}$$

> **Answer (ii): Total gripping force = 627.84 N**

**Step 4: Comparison with 2-finger case.**

For a 2-finger gripper holding the same load:

$$N_{2-finger} = \frac{k \cdot W}{2\mu} = \frac{2 \times 78.48}{2 \times 0.25} = 313.92 \text{ N per finger}$$

$$F_{2-finger,total} = 627.84 \text{ N (same total)}$$

**Comment (2 marks):**
Although the **total** gripping force is the same, the 3-finger gripper distributes it across 3 contact points, giving:
1. **Self-centering** of the cylindrical workpiece (no slip sideways, no rotation about vertical axis).
2. **Lower stress concentration** on the workpiece surface (important for delicate parts).
3. **33% lower per-finger force** reduces wear on finger pads and prevents surface damage to soft parts.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1, End Effectors and Mechanical Grippers**

- **End Effector Definition:** Device mounted on robot wrist to interact with environment (4 marks question every year).
- **Three Major Functions of Grippers:** Grasp, Hold, Release.
- **Five Types of End Effectors to memorize:** Mechanical, Vacuum, Magnetic, Adhesive, Tool-type.
- **Friction Law:** $F_{friction} = \mu N$ — this is the **single most important equation** in the module.
- **Base Gripping Force Formula (2-finger, static):** $F = kW / \mu$ (total) or $N = kW / 2\mu$ (per finger).
- **Acceleration-Adjusted Formula:** $F = kW(g+a) / (2\mu g)$ — required for **every dynamic problem**.
- **3-Finger Formula:** $N = kW / 3\mu$ (per finger).
- **Always include Safety Factor $k$ (typically 2 to 3)** in KTU answers; examiners deduct marks otherwise.
- **Common $\mu$ values to memorize:** Rubber on steel = **0.7–0.9**, steel on steel (dry) = **0.5–0.8**, steel on steel (oily) = **0.1–0.2**, rubber on glass = **0.5–0.6**.
- **Drive Mechanism Comparison:** Pneumatic = fast & clean, Hydraulic = high force, Electric = precise & sensor-rich.
- **Mechanical Advantage Formula:** $M.A. = F_{out} / F_{in}$ — used to size actuators.
- **Pneumatic Cylinder Force:** $F = P \times (\pi D^2 / 4)$ — derive bore diameter from required force.
- **Free Body Diagrams** are mandatory in KTU 14-mark answers and carry **3–4 marks by themselves**.
- **Industrial Examples to mention:** Pneumatic grippers in car assembly, vacuum grippers in glass handling, magnetic grippers in steel plate handling.
- **Standards to mention (bonus marks):** ISO 9409 for mechanical flange mounting.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Special Tools
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAT602 - ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Special Tools

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Special Tools in Robotics

In the context of industrial robotics, **Special Tools** (also referred to as **end-effectors**, **end-of-arm tooling (EOAT)**, or **manipulator tools**) are the specialized devices mounted at the **wrist** or **flange** of a robotic manipulator. These tools enable the robot to perform a specific, often non-gripping, industrial task. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus for *PCCAT603 / PCCAT602 – Robotics and Automation*, special tools extend a robot's functional capability beyond mere pick-and-place operations, allowing it to interact with the work environment via processes such as **welding, painting, drilling, deburring, heat sealing, and assembly**.

Formally, the **tool coordinate frame** $\{T\}$ is attached to the tool's tip, and its position/orientation relative to the **end-effector** is described by the **tool transformation matrix** $^B_TT$, which is essential for accurate task-level programming.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> The syllabus explicitly distinguishes between:
> - **Grippers** (mechanical handling tools) – covered separately.
> - **Special Tools** (process tools) – the focus of this module, which perform a manufacturing operation *on* the workpiece rather than transporting it.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **human hand holding different instruments** — a surgeon uses a scalpel, a carpenter uses a drill, and a painter uses a spray gun. The "arm" of the person is the robot, and the **instrument in the hand is the "special tool."** Just as a surgeon cannot operate without the right scalpel, an industrial robot cannot weld, paint, or drill without being equipped with the correct special tool. Each tool transforms the robot from a "mover" into a "doer."

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (Board Examiner's Perspective):**
> A **Special Tool** is any end-effector that performs an active manufacturing process (joining, coating, cutting, etc.) on the workpiece, as opposed to a **gripper** which merely holds and transports the workpiece.

## 1.3 Classification of Special Tools

The KTU 2024 scheme broadly classifies special tools into the following categories:

| Sl. No | Category | Example Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Welding Tools** | MIG/MAG/TIG torches for arc welding |
| 2 | **Painting/Coating Tools** | Spray guns for automotive painting |
| 3 | **Material Removal Tools** | Drills, grinders, deburring spindles |
| 4 | **Joining/Sealing Tools** | Heat sealing jaws, ultrasonic welders |
| 5 | **Sensor-Integrated Tools** | Force-torque probes, tool-center-point (TCP) calibration probes |
| 6 | **Hybrid/Quick-Change Tools** | Tool changers holding multiple special tools |

> [!TIP]
> The standard payload of an industrial robot (e.g., **6 kg**, **10 kg**, **165 kg** for a Fanuc M-2000iA) must exceed the **combined mass of the special tool + workpiece + process forces**. This is a **frequently asked 3-mark question**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principles of Common Special Tools

### 2.1.1 Robotic Arc Welding Tool
The **MIG/MAG welding torch** is the most common special tool. Key operating parameters include:
- **Wire Feed Rate (WFR):** typically **2–15 m/min**.
- **Welding Current (I):** **50–500 A** (DC).
- **Arc Voltage (V):** **15–35 V**.
- **Travel Speed (v):** **0.2–1.5 m/min**.

The robot must maintain a constant **stick-out distance** (the distance between the contact tip and the workpiece), usually **10–25 mm**, and a constant **torch angle** (typically **15°–30°** from vertical — the *push angle* or *drag angle*).

> **Why this matters:** Small deviations in the torch angle or stick-out cause porosity, undercuts, and lack of fusion — common **welding defects** examined in the KTU theory paper.

### 2.1.2 Robotic Spray Painting Tool
The **electrostatic/air-spray gun** atomizes paint into fine droplets using compressed air at **3–6 bar** pressure. Key metrics:
- **Flow rate (Q):** **50–500 mL/min**.
- **Atomization pressure (P_a):** **0.2–0.4 MPa**.
- **Stand-off distance (d):** **150–300 mm**.
- **Gun speed (v_g):** **0.5–1.0 m/s** for uniform film thickness.

The **film thickness** $t_f$ deposited on the surface is governed by:

$$t_f = \dfrac{Q}{v_g \cdot w \cdot \rho_p}$$

where $w$ is the spray fan width and $\rho_p$ is the paint density.

### 2.1.3 Robotic Drilling / Deburring Tool
Drilling spindles (pneumatic or electric) with rotational speeds up to **40,000 RPM** are used. The **cutting force** $F_c$ on the drill is given by the classical Kienzle model:

$$F_c = K_c \cdot A_c$$

where $K_c$ is the **specific cutting force** (N/mm²) and $A_c$ is the **chip cross-sectional area** (mm²).

### 2.1.4 Heat Sealing Tool
Used in plastic packaging. Two heated jaws (typically at **160 °C–220 °C**) apply pressure (**0.2–0.5 MPa**) for a dwell time of **0.5–3 seconds** to fuse thermoplastic films.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **high-yield formula reference** for Special Tools questions. Memorize these — they appear in KTU board papers every year.

| # | Parameter / Equation | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tool Center Point (TCP) | $P_{TCP}$ | mm | Defines the working point of the tool |
| 2 | Payload equation | $m_p \le m_{rated} - m_{tool} - m_{safety}$ | kg | Robot selection |
| 3 | Spray film thickness | $t_f = \dfrac{Q}{v_g \cdot w \cdot \rho_p}$ | $\mu m$ | Paint coverage analysis |
| 4 | Kienzle cutting force | $F_c = K_c \cdot A_c$ | N | Drilling/milling force |
| 5 | Spindle power | $P_s = F_c \cdot v_c$ | W | Tool motor sizing |
| 6 | Heat seal dwell time | $t_d = \dfrac{E}{\dot{Q}_{in}}$ | s | Sealing cycle time |
| 7 | Welding travel speed | $v = \dfrac{I \cdot 60}{A_{weld} \cdot \rho_{filler} \cdot k}$ | mm/s | Weld bead geometry |
| 8 | Wrist torque | $\tau_w = F_{tool} \cdot L_{tool}$ | N·m | Joint 5/6 load check |
| 9 | Tool changer repeatability | $\Delta_{repeat}$ | $\pm 0.02$ mm | Quick-change tool systems |
| 10 | TCP offset transformation | $^B_TT = \, ^B_WT \cdot \, ^W_TT$ | homogeneous matrix | Kinematic chain |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do not confuse** $\vert P_{TCP} \vert$ (magnitude) with the **TCP definition vector** $\begin{bmatrix} x & y & z \end{bmatrix}^T$. The pipe character has been intentionally written as $\vert \cdot \vert$ to keep the LaTeX isolated.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Over **90%** of car body welding is done by robotic MIG torches (BMW, Tesla, Tata Motors production lines).
- **Aerospace:** Robotic drilling of **CFRP** and **aluminium** fuselage panels (Airbus A350, Boeing 787) using special drilling spindles.
- **Electronics Manufacturing:** Robotic **dispensing** and **soldering** tools for PCB assembly.
- **Food & Pharma:** Robotic **heat-sealing** and **labeling** tools in aseptic packaging lines.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Tool Center Point (TCP) Calibration

The TCP is the **point on the tool that performs the actual task** (e.g., the tip of the welding wire, the nozzle of the spray gun). The KTU board routinely asks: *"Define TCP and explain any one method of TCP calibration."* Below is the **complete, exhaustive derivation** of the **4-point TCP calibration method** — students must reproduce this step-by-step for full marks.

### Step 1 — Define the Reference Point
Let a fixed reference point $P_{ref}$ in the **base frame** $\{B\}$ be approached by the tool tip from **four different orientations** of the robot. The joint configurations are $\mathbf{q}^{(1)}, \mathbf{q}^{(2)}, \mathbf{q}^{(3)}, \mathbf{q}^{(4)}$.

### Step 2 — Forward Kinematics
Using the **Denavit-Hartenberg (DH)** forward kinematics, the position of the **end-effector (flange)** in the base frame is:

$$
p_{flange}^{(i)} = f_{FK}(\mathbf{q}^{(i)})
$$

for $i = 1, 2, 3, 4$.

### Step 3 — Tool Transformation
The TCP position is related to the flange position by the **tool offset vector** $t_{tool}$ expressed in the **tool frame**:

$$
P_{TCP}^{(i)} = p_{flange}^{(i)} + R_{flange}^{(i)} \cdot t_{tool}
$$

where $R_{flange}^{(i)}$ is the **3×3 rotation matrix** of the flange orientation.

### Step 4 — Equating to Fixed Reference
Since the TCP touches the same fixed point in space for all four configurations:

$$
P_{ref} = p_{flange}^{(i)} + R_{flange}^{(i)} \cdot t_{tool}, \quad \forall i \in \{1,2,3,4\}
$$

### Step 5 — Subtracting Pairs
Subtracting the equation for $i=1$ from $i=2$, $i=3$, and $i=4$ eliminates $P_{ref}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 &= \left[p_{flange}^{(2)} - p_{flange}^{(1)}\right] + \left[R_{flange}^{(2)} - R_{flange}^{(1)}\right] t_{tool} \\
0 &= \left[p_{flange}^{(3)} - p_{flange}^{(1)}\right] + \left[R_{flange}^{(3)} - R_{flange}^{(1)}\right] t_{tool} \\
0 &= \left[p_{flange}^{(4)} - p_{flange}^{(1)}\right] + \left[R_{flange}^{(4)} - R_{flange}^{(1)}\right] t_{tool}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Matrix Form
Rearranging gives a **linear system** $\mathbf{A} \, t_{tool} = \mathbf{b}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{A} &= \begin{bmatrix} R_{flange}^{(2)} - R_{flange}^{(1)} \\ R_{flange}^{(3)} - R_{flange}^{(1)} \\ R_{flange}^{(4)} - R_{flange}^{(1)} \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{(a } 9 \times 3 \text{ matrix)} \\
\mathbf{b} &= \begin{bmatrix} p_{flange}^{(1)} - p_{flange}^{(2)} \\ p_{flange}^{(1)} - p_{flange}^{(3)} \\ p_{flange}^{(1)} - p_{flange}^{(4)} \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7 — Least-Squares Solution
The over-determined system is solved using the **Moore-Penrose pseudo-inverse**:

$$
t_{tool} = \left(\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{A}\right)^{-1} \mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{b}
$$

This yields the **TCP offset** $t_{tool} = \begin{bmatrix} t_x & t_y & t_z \end{bmatrix}^T$ in the tool frame.

> **Why this works:** Each configuration gives 3 equations; with 4 configurations, we get 9 equations in 3 unknowns, which is over-determined — hence the least-squares minimization reduces calibration error.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Spray Painting Film Thickness

Given a robotic spray gun with flow rate $Q = 250 \, \text{mL/min}$, gun speed $v_g = 0.6 \, \text{m/s}$, fan width $w = 80 \, \text{mm}$, and paint density $\rho_p = 1.1 \, \text{g/cm}^3$, find the film thickness.

### Step 1 — Unit Conversion
Convert all quantities to **SI base units**:
- $Q = 250 \, \text{mL/min} = 250 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}^3 / 60 \, \text{s} = 4.167 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$
- $v_g = 0.6 \, \text{m/s}$
- $w = 80 \, \text{mm} = 0.08 \, \text{m}$
- $\rho_p = 1.1 \, \text{g/cm}^3 = 1100 \, \text{kg/m}^3$

### Step 2 — Apply the Film Thickness Formula
$$
t_f = \dfrac{Q}{v_g \cdot w \cdot \rho_p}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute
$$
t_f = \dfrac{4.167 \times 10^{-6}}{0.6 \times 0.08 \times 1100}
$$

### Step 4 — Simplify the Denominator
$$
0.6 \times 0.08 = 0.048
$$

$$
0.048 \times 1100 = 52.8
$$

### Step 5 — Final Calculation
$$
t_f = \dfrac{4.167 \times 10^{-6}}{52.8} = 7.89 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{m} = 78.9 \, \mu m
$$

> **Result:** The deposited film thickness is approximately **$\mathbf{79 \, \mu m}$**, which lies within the typical industrial automotive paint range of **60–120 μm**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Special Tool vs. Application

| Selection Criterion | Welding | Painting | Drilling | Heat Sealing |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Tool mass** | 5–15 kg | 1–3 kg | 3–10 kg | 2–6 kg |
| **Cycle time** | 30–90 s | 5–30 s | 2–10 s | 1–3 s |
| **Reach envelope req.** | Large | Large | Small | Small |
| **Repeatability needed** | $\pm 0.1$ mm | $\pm 1$ mm | $\pm 0.05$ mm | $\pm 0.5$ mm |
| **Workspace hazard** | Arc UV, sparks | VOC fumes, mist | Chips, coolant | Heat, fumes |
| **Robot DOF required** | 6 | 6 (often 7) | 5 or 6 | 4 |
| **Process force** | 10–50 N | < 5 N | 200–2000 N | 100–500 N |

## 4.3 Process Flow — TCP Calibration Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Mount the Special Tool on Flange] --> S2[Step 1: Identify a Fixed Reference Point]
    S2 --> S3[Step 2: Move Tool Tip to Reference - Orientation 1 - Record FK]
    S3 --> S4[Step 3: Repeat for Orientations 2 3 4]
    S4 --> S5[Step 4: Build the A and b Matrices]
    S5 --> S6[Step 5: Solve Least Squares for t_tool]
```
