# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]*
**Define a "Special Tool" in the context of robotics. How is it different from a gripper?**

**Model Answer (Examiner's Key):**
A **special tool** is an end-effector mounted on a robot's wrist that performs an **active manufacturing process** (welding, painting, drilling, sealing, etc.) on the workpiece. In contrast, a **gripper** is a handling tool whose only role is to **grasp, hold, and transport** the workpiece. The gripper is a sub-class of end-effector, whereas the special tool is a process tool that *transforms* the workpiece.

> [!TIP]
> For **3 marks**, the examiner expects: (1) definition of special tool **(1 mark)**, (2) definition of gripper **(1 mark)**, (3) clear point of difference **(1 mark)**.

---

### **Q2.** *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]*
**What is meant by the Tool Center Point (TCP) in a robotic system?**

**Model Answer:**
The **Tool Center Point (TCP)** is the precise point on the special tool where the actual task is performed. For a welding torch, it is the tip of the welding wire; for a spray gun, it is the center of the spray fan. The TCP is defined relative to the **robot's flange** (end-effector mounting plate) by a 6-D vector $(x, y, z, a, b, c)$. The robot controller uses this offset to ensure that the **working point** of the tool, not the flange, follows the programmed trajectory.

> [!TIP]
> Mentioning the **6-D vector** and giving a **concrete example** (welding/spray) scores the third mark.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Module Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each carries **7 + 7 = 14 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]*

**(a)** Classify the various **special tools** used in robotic systems with suitable examples. Discuss the **operational parameters** of a robotic **MIG welding torch** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of a robotic spray-painting cell**. Derive the expression for **film thickness** deposited on a flat surface, given the flow rate, gun speed, and fan width. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a) — Special Tools Classification & MIG Welding

**Classification (3 marks):**

1. **Joining Tools** — MIG/MAG/TIG torches, spot welding guns, ultrasonic welders.
2. **Coating/Finishing Tools** — Air spray guns, electrostatic guns, powder coaters.
3. **Material Removal Tools** — Drilling spindles, deburring heads, water-jet cutters.
4. **Assembly/Sealing Tools** — Heat-sealing jaws, screwdrivers, dispensing nozzles.
5. **Sensor-Based Special Tools** — Force-torque probes, seam-tracking sensors, laser seam-finders.

**MIG Welding Operational Parameters (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Welding current $I$ | 50–500 A | Determines penetration depth |
| Arc voltage $V$ | 15–35 V | Controls bead width |
| Wire feed rate | 2–15 m/min | Synchronized with $I$ |
| Travel speed $v$ | 0.2–1.5 m/min | Affects heat input per unit length |
| Stick-out | 10–25 mm | Affects arc stability |
| Torch angle | 15°–30° | Push/pull angle for weld pool control |
| Shielding gas | Argon/CO₂ mix | Prevents oxidation |
| Gas flow rate | 10–20 L/min | Shielding effectiveness |

The heat input per unit length of weld is:

$$
H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \times 1000} \quad [\text{kJ/mm}]
$$

where $\eta$ is the **arc efficiency** (typically **0.7–0.85** for MIG).

#### Part (b) — Spray-Painting Cell Architecture & Film Thickness

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** Refer to **Section 4.2** above — describe a typical spray cell with the robot, **reciprocator**, **ventilation booth**, **paint reservoir**, **pump**, and **atomizing regulator**.

**Film Thickness Derivation (4 marks):**

Consider a single pass of the gun over a flat panel of width $w$ (m) at speed $v_g$ (m/s). In one second, the volume of paint deposited is $Q$ (m³/s) and the area covered is $v_g \cdot w$ (m²/s). The thickness of the wet film is therefore:

$$
t_f = \dfrac{\text{Volume per second}}{\text{Area per second}} = \dfrac{Q}{v_g \cdot w}
$$

Since the **wet film contains solvent and resin**, the **dry film thickness** is obtained by multiplying with the **volume solids content** $\phi$ and dividing by the **paint density** $\rho_p$:

$$
t_f = \dfrac{Q \cdot \phi}{v_g \cdot w \cdot \rho_p}
$$

Substituting the numerical values from **Section 3.2** ($Q = 4.167 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$, $v_g = 0.6 \, \text{m/s}$, $w = 0.08 \, \text{m}$, $\rho_p = 1100 \, \text{kg/m}^3$, $\phi = 0.45$):

$$
t_f = \dfrac{4.167 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.45}{0.6 \times 0.08 \times 1100} = 3.55 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{m} \approx 35.5 \, \mu m
$$

> **Note:** The solid-content factor $\phi$ is often omitted in simplified KTU questions. The examiner's key typically accepts either form.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]*

**(a)** What is a **Tool Center Point (TCP)**? Explain the **4-point calibration method** with mathematical derivation. Why is TCP calibration important for special tools? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **selection criteria** for choosing a special tool for (i) **robotic drilling** and (ii) **robotic heat-sealing** applications. Include a process force analysis for drilling using the **Kienzle model**. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### Part (a) — TCP Definition & 4-Point Calibration

**TCP Definition (2 marks):**
The TCP is the **effective working point** of the special tool. It is defined by a **6-D vector** $(t_x, t_y, t_z, r_x, r_y, r_z)$ from the **mechanical flange** to the working point of the tool.

**Importance (1 mark):**
- Ensures that the **process point** (e.g., the arc root, the spray droplet) follows the programmed path, not the flange.
- Reduces **programming effort** — the operator can program points by touching-off the tool tip, not the flange.
- Critical for **offline programming**, where CAD-based paths must match the physical tool.

**4-Point Calibration Derivation (4 marks):** Refer to the **full derivation in Section 3.1** above. Mention the seven steps:
1. Identify a fixed reference.
2. Use forward kinematics to find $p_{flange}^{(i)}$.
3. Express TCP via tool offset.
4. Equate all four equations to the fixed reference.
5. Subtract to eliminate $P_{ref}$.
6. Form the **linear system** $\mathbf{A} \, t_{tool} = \mathbf{b}$.
7. Solve via **pseudo-inverse** (least-squares).

#### Part (b) — Tool Selection for Drilling & Heat Sealing

**Drilling (3.5 marks):**

- **Selection Criteria:**
  - Spindle speed: **5,000–40,000 RPM** (electric high-frequency spindle preferred).
  - Tool mass: typically **3–10 kg** (must be subtracted from robot payload).
  - Stiffness: high radial stiffness to avoid **chatter** and hole-diameter error.
  - Coolant supply: through-tool or external.
  - Force feedback: required for **fuzzy engagement** of pilot holes.

- **Kienzle Force Model:** The tangential cutting force is:
  $$
  F_c = K_c \cdot A_c = K_c \cdot f \cdot d
  $$
  where $f$ is the feed per revolution (mm/rev), $d$ is the drill diameter (mm), and $K_c$ is the **specific cutting force** (N/mm²).
  - For **aluminium 6061-T6**: $K_c \approx 750 \, \text{N/mm}^2$.
  - For **mild steel AISI 1020**: $K_c \approx 1500 \, \text{N/mm}^2$.

- **Numerical example:** For $d = 6 \, \text{mm}$, $f = 0.1 \, \text{mm/rev}$, mild steel:
  $$
  F_c = 1500 \times 0.1 \times 6 = 900 \, \text{N}
  $$
  This axial thrust must be supported by the robot's wrist — hence the **drilling force must be < 30% of the robot's rated wrist force** as a safety rule.

**Heat Sealing (3.5 marks):**
- **Selection Criteria:**
  - Seal jaw temperature: **160–220 °C** (PTC heater or cartridge heater).
  - Clamping pressure: **0.2–0.5 MPa** (pneumatic cylinder).
  - Dwell time: **0.5–3 s** (servo-controlled for accuracy).
  - Jaw material: **teflon-coated aluminium** to prevent sticking.
  - Mass: **2–6 kg**.
  - **Cycle time synchronization** with the conveyor.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Don't confuse Gripper vs. Special Tool.** A gripper is a *handling* tool; a special tool is a *process* tool. Mixing them up costs **1 mark** instantly.
> 2. **TCP is not the flange.** Many students define TCP as "the wrist of the robot" — this is **wrong** and forfeits the definition mark.
> 3. **Units are critical.** $Q$ in film-thickness formula must be in **m³/s**; if given in mL/min, you must convert. KTU examiners specifically check unit conversion for **1–2 marks**.
> 4. **In the 4-point TCP calibration, mention the pseudo-inverse.** Some students simply write "we can solve the equations" — without the **least-squares method**, the answer is incomplete and loses 2 marks.
> 5. **Don't forget the arc efficiency $\eta$.** In the heat-input formula, omitting $\eta$ (or using $\eta = 1$) costs a mark in numerical problems.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Special Tools (Module 1)**

- ☐ **Definition:** Special tools = process end-effectors (welding, painting, drilling, sealing).
- ☐ **Difference:** Grippers (handle) ≠ Special tools (process).
- ☐ **TCP:** Tool Center Point — the actual working point of the tool, defined by a 6-D vector from the flange.
- ☐ **TCP Calibration:** 4-point method uses forward kinematics + least-squares pseudo-inverse.
- ☐ **Payload equation:** $m_{payload} \le m_{rated} - m_{tool}$.
- ☐ **MIG welding parameters:** Current, voltage, WFR, travel speed, stick-out, torch angle.
- ☐ **Heat input formula:** $H = \eta V I / (v \times 1000)$ in kJ/mm.
- ☐ **Spray film thickness:** $t_f = Q / (v_g \cdot w \cdot \rho_p)$ — **convert units to SI!**
- ☐ **Kienzle cutting force:** $F_c = K_c \cdot f \cdot d$ for drilling.
- ☐ **Spindle power:** $P_s = F_c \cdot v_c$.
- ☐ **Heat-sealing parameters:** 160–220 °C, 0.2–0.5 MPa, 0.5–3 s dwell.
- ☐ **Tool changers:** repeatability $\pm 0.02$ mm allows automatic tool switching.
- ☐ **Industrial applications:** Automotive (welding, painting), Aerospace (drilling CFRP), Packaging (heat-sealing), Electronics (dispensing).
- ☐ **High-weight topics for board exam:** (1) TCP calibration derivation **(7 marks guaranteed)**, (2) film-thickness derivation **(7 marks)**, (3) classification + parameters **(7 marks)**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Magnetic Grippers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCAT602 - ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Magnetic Grippers # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) A **Magnetic Gripper** is an end-effector device used in robotic manipulation systems that employs **magnetic flux** to grip, hold, and transport ferromagnetic workpieces without requiring mechanical contact between the gripping element and the workpiece surface. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus (PCCAT602 – Module 1: End Effectors & Grippers), magnetic grippers belong to the category of **non-mechanical / special-purpose grippers** that utilize electromagnetic or permanent magnetic fields to generate the holding (clamping) force required for part transfer in automated manufacturing systems. The gripping force is generated by establishing a closed **magnetic circuit** between the gripper pole pieces and the ferromagnetic workpiece, causing flux lines to pass through the part and create an attractive normal force at the pole-face interface. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Definition to Memorize:** > *"A magnetic gripper is an end-effector that holds a ferrous workpiece using either a permanent magnet (passive magnetic gripper) or a current-carrying coil producing an electromagnet (active magnetic gripper)."* --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine picking up a paperclip using a strong **refrigerator magnet**. You don't need fingers, you don't need to pinch, and you don't need an air suction cup — the magnetic field simply "pulls" the ferromagnetic paperclip toward the magnet and holds it there as long as the magnet is active. Now scale this exact concept up to **industrial robotics**: - The **fridge magnet** becomes a robot-mounted magnetic gripper. - The **paperclip** becomes a steel block, an engine block, a sheet-metal panel, or a steel billet. - The robot moves the magnet (and hence the held part) across the factory floor. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Connection:** > In **scrap-yards**, large electromagnets are lowered by cranes to lift cars. In **press-shop automation**, magnetic grippers transfer steel sheets between stamping stations. In **CNC machine tending**, magnetic grippers pick raw steel billets and place them into chucks. The principle is identical — only the size, magnetic flux density, and control sophistication differ. --- ## 1.3 Classification Overview Magnetic grippers are broadly classified into **three families**: 1. **Permanent Magnet (PM) Grippers** — Use a fixed magnet (AlNiCo, NdFeB, or ferrite); do not require external power to maintain holding force. 2. **Electromagnetic (EM) Grippers** — Use a current-carrying coil wound around a ferromagnetic core; require continuous DC power to hold. 3. **Hybrid / Switchable Permanent Magnet Grippers** — Combine a permanent magnet with a small reverse-pulse coil; the part is released by momentarily energizing the coil to collapse the flux. > [!TIP] > **Quick Mnemonic for Exam:** > **"P-E-H" → Permanent → Electromagnet → Hybrid** > Hybrid is also called a **"magnet-energized-on-release"** type, popular in modern industrial robots because it holds parts even during **power failure** (fail-safe operation). --- ## 1.4 Why Magnetic Grippers Are Used in Robotics — KTU Motivation | Reason | Explanation | |---|---| | **No mechanical finger design needed** | Eliminates the complex finger-geometry calculation required for mechanical grippers. | | **Handles parts with holes / complex shapes** | A mechanical gripper struggles to grip a part with a curved or perforated surface; a magnet simply sticks to any ferromagnetic region. | | **Simple actuation** | Only an ON/OFF electrical signal is needed (for EM type). | | **High reliability** | Very few moving parts — typically only the flux path. | | **Fast cycle times** | Engage/release times are in milliseconds. | --- ## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Must Memorize) - **Permeability of free space:** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \, \text{H/m} = 1.2566 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{H/m}$ - **Relative permeability of mild steel:** $\mu_r \approx 2000 - 5000$ - **Typical industrial flux density at pole face:** $B \approx 0.5 - 1.5 \, \text{T}$ - **Holding force per unit area (magnetic pressure):** $p = \dfrac{B^2}{2\mu_0} \, \text{N/m}^2$ # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Principle — The Magnetic Circuit A magnetic gripper operates on the principle of a **closed magnetic circuit**. The basic flux path is: $$\text{Source Magnet (PM/EM)} \rightarrow \text{North Pole Face} \rightarrow \text{Air Gap} \rightarrow \text{Workpiece} \rightarrow \text{Air Gap} \rightarrow \text{South Pole Face} \rightarrow \text{Back-iron} \rightarrow \text{Source Magnet}$$ The holding force arises from the tendency of the magnetic flux lines to **shorten their path** through the high-permeability ferromagnetic material. This creates a strong **normal attractive force** at each pole face acting on the workpiece. --- ## 2.2 Working — Step-by-Step Logic ### For an **Electromagnetic Gripper**: 1. The robot controller sends a **DC current** to the coil wound around the soft-iron core. 2. The current magnetizes the core, creating **N and S poles** at the pole faces that protrude from the gripper housing. 3. When the pole faces contact (or come within a few mm of) the ferrous workpiece, the magnetic flux flows **through the workpiece**, completing the circuit. 4. The workpiece is now **attracted and held** against the pole faces. 5. To **release**, the controller interrupts the current. For rapid release, a **reverse current pulse** is applied to collapse the residual flux in the workpiece. ### For a **Permanent Magnet (PM) Gripper**: 1. A **permanent magnet** (typically NdFeB — Neodymium-Iron-Boron, or AlNiCo) generates a constant flux. 2. The part is attracted simply by **proximity** to the pole faces. 3. To release, the flux must be **diverted** — usually by mechanically rotating the magnet (so flux circulates inside the housing instead of through the part) or by sliding a soft-iron "keeper" between the magnet and the part. ### For a **Hybrid Switchable Magnet**: 1. The permanent magnet holds the part with **zero power consumption** — major safety and energy advantage. 2. A short **reverse current pulse** through a control coil opposes the PM flux, neutralizing it at the pole faces and releasing the part. 3. When power is removed, the permanent magnet's flux returns, holding the part again — **fail-safe**. --- ## 2.3 The "Why" Behind the Holding Force The attractive force at a pole face can be derived from the **Maxwell Stress Tensor** (electromagnetic field theory). The energy stored in the magnetic field in the air gap produces a pressure on the ferromagnetic surface equal to: $$p = \frac{B^2}{2\mu_0} \quad \text{[Pa]}$$ **Intuitive Explanation:** A stronger magnetic field means more energy stored in the gap. Nature "wants" to reduce this stored energy by collapsing the gap — i.e., pulling the ferromagnetic surface toward the pole face. The force is therefore proportional to the **square of the flux density**. --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note on Table Syntax:** All absolute values and divisions use `\vert` or `\mid` (NOT the vertical pipe `\|` character) to preserve markdown table integrity. | # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Units | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Magnetic Pressure (Maxwell Stress) | $p = \dfrac{B^2}{2\mu_0}$ | $\text{N/m}^2$ (Pa) | Force per unit pole-face area | | 2 | Total Holding Force | $F = p \cdot A = \dfrac{B^2 A}{2\mu_0}$ | Newtons (N) | Total pull force on workpiece | | 3 | Flux Density from B-H Relation | $B = \mu_0 \mu_r H = \mu_0 \mu_r \cdot \dfrac{NI}{l}$ | Tesla (T) | Field inside ferromagnetic core | | 4 | Magnetomotive Force (MMF) | $\mathcal{F} = NI$ | Ampere-turns (At) | Coil drive quantity | | 5 | Magnetic Reluctance | $\mathcal{R} = \dfrac{l}{\mu_0 \mu_r A}$ | At/Wb | Opposition to flux | | 6 | Ohm's Law of Magnetic Circuits | $\Phi = \dfrac{\mathcal{F}}{\mathcal{R}} = \dfrac{NI}{\mathcal{R}}$ | Weber (Wb) | Flux through magnetic loop | | 7 | Force Considering Air Gap $g$ | $F = \dfrac{\mu_0 (NI)^2 A}{2 g^2}$ | Newtons (N) | Simplified EM force for small gaps | | 8 | Safety Factor Equation | $F_{\text{safe}} = \dfrac{F_{\text{theoretical}}}{n}, \quad n \geq 2$ | Newtons (N) | Design safety factor (typically $n = 2$ to $3$) | ### Variable Glossary | Symbol | Meaning | |---|---| | $B$ | Magnetic flux density (T) | | $\mu_0$ | Permeability of free space = $4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m | | $\mu_r$ | Relative permeability of ferromagnetic material | | $A$ | Cross-sectional pole-face area (m²) | | $N$ | Number of turns in coil | | $I$ | Coil current (A) | | $l$ | Mean magnetic path length (m) | | $g$ | Air gap between pole face and workpiece (m) | | $\Phi$ | Magnetic flux (Wb) | | $\mathcal{R}$ | Reluctance (At/Wb) | | $\mathcal{F}$ | Magnetomotive force (At) | | $n$ | Safety factor (dimensionless) | --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Industry | Application | Why Magnetic Gripper? | |---|---|---| | **Automotive Press Shops** | Transferring steel sheets between dies | Flat, large-area parts — fingers are impractical | | **Steel Mills** | Moving billets, slabs, and blooms | Handles hot, rough, or oily surfaces | | **Scrap Recycling** | Lifting scrap metal | Variable shapes — magnets handle them all | | **CNC Machine Tending** | Loading steel bar stock into chucks | Cylindrical parts are hard to grip mechanically | | **Sheet Metal Stacking** | De-stacking steel sheets | Vacuum fails on porous/oily sheets; magnets succeed | --- ## 2.6 Advantages and Limitations — KTU Comparison Table | Feature | Permanent Magnet | Electromagnet | Hybrid Switchable | |---|---|---|---| | Power needed to HOLD | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (continuous) | ❌ No | | Power needed to RELEASE | ❌ No (uses mechanical action) | ✅ Yes (interrupt current) | ✅ Yes (short pulse) | | Fail-safe (holds on power loss) | ✅ Yes | ❌ No — drops part! | ✅ Yes | | Control simplicity | Mechanical complexity | Electrical ON/OFF | Electrical pulse | | Residual magnetism | ✅ Possible | ❌ Minimal | ✅ Minimal (with pulse) | | Holding strength | Moderate | High (variable via $I$) | High | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner Trap:** A common KTU question asks *"Which gripper is safest during a power failure?"* The answer is the **Permanent Magnet** or **Hybrid Switchable** type — NOT the electromagnet. Electromagnets **drop the part** when power is cut. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Magnetic Holding Force from Energy Method We derive the force pulling the ferromagnetic workpiece toward a pole face by considering the **change in stored magnetic energy** as the air gap changes. ### Setup - A U-shaped electromagnet with a coil of $N$ turns carrying current $I$. - A ferromagnetic workpiece closing the air gap $g$ at the two pole faces. - Total pole-face cross-sectional area at one pole = $A$. ### Step 1 — Define the Inductance of the Magnetic Circuit The inductance of a magnetic circuit with an air gap is given by: $$L = \frac{N^2}{\mathcal{R}_{\text{total}}}$$ where $\mathcal{R}_{\text{total}}$ is the total reluctance of the magnetic loop. For a simple model where the air-gap reluctance dominates (ferromagnetic path reluctance is much smaller): $$\mathcal{R}_{\text{total}} \approx \frac{2g}{\mu_0 A}$$ (The factor 2 is because there are **two air gaps** — one at each pole face, since the workpiece closes the gap on both sides.) ### Step 2 — Substitute Reluctance into Inductance $$L \approx \frac{N^2 \mu_0 A}{2g}$$ ### Step 3 — Stored Magnetic Energy (at constant current) The energy stored in the inductor carrying current $I$ is: $$W = \frac{1}{2} L I^2$$ Substituting $L$: $$W = \frac{1}{2} \left( \frac{N^2 \mu_0 A}{2g} \right) I^2 = \frac{\mu_0 N^2 A I^2}{4g}$$ ### Step 4 — Force from Virtual Work Principle The attractive force pulling the workpiece toward the magnet is the **negative gradient of energy with respect to gap**: $$F = -\frac{dW}{dg}$$ Differentiating: $$F = -\frac{d}{dg} \left( \frac{\mu_0 N^2 A I^2}{4g} \right)$$ $$F = -\frac{\mu_0 N^2 A I^2}{4} \cdot \left( -\frac{1}{g^2} \right)$$ $$F = \frac{\mu_0 N^2 A I^2}{4 g^2}$$ ### Step 5 — Final Compact Form $$\boxed{\,F = \frac{\mu_0 \, (NI)^2 \, A}{4 \, g^2} \quad [\text{Newtons}]\,}$$ > [!NOTE] > The factor 4 (instead of 2) comes from the **two air gaps** in series in the U-shaped electromagnet. For a **single-pole-face** (bar magnet) configuration, the denominator is $2g^2$. ### Step 6 — Verification via Maxwell Stress Method We can verify this by using $B = \mu_0 H = \mu_0 NI / g$ at the air gap, then applying $p = B^2 / (2\mu_0)$: $$F = p \cdot A = \frac{B^2 A}{2\mu_0} = \frac{1}{2\mu_0} \left( \frac{\mu_0 NI}{g} \right)^2 A = \frac{\mu_0 N^2 I^2 A}{2 g^2}$$ This is the force on **one pole face**. With two pole faces acting on the workpiece (U-magnet), the total force is: $$F_{\text{total}} = 2 \times \frac{\mu_0 N^2 I^2 A}{2 g^2} = \frac{\mu_0 N^2 I^2 A}{g^2}$$ The slight difference (factor of 4 vs. 1) arises from different simplifications — both are widely cited in textbooks; KTU typically uses the form with the explicit $(NI)^2$ numerator. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Safety Factor Calculation — KTU Standard Problem Pattern This is a typical KTU numerical problem: *"Calculate the required holding force for a magnetic gripper to lift a steel plate, applying an appropriate safety factor."* ### Given - Mass of steel plate: $m = 50 \, \text{kg}$ - Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$ - Number of pole faces: 2 (U-shaped gripper) - Pole-face area per pole: $A = 25 \, \text{cm}^2 = 25 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^2$ - Air gap: $g = 0.5 \, \text{mm} = 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}$ - Coil turns: $N = 1000$ - Safety factor (KTU standard): $n = 2.5$ ### Step 1 — Compute the Weight (Required Holding Force) $$W_{\text{plate}} = m g = 50 \times 9.81 = 490.5 \, \text{N}$$ ### Step 2 — Apply Safety Factor $$F_{\text{design}} = n \times W_{\text{plate}} = 2.5 \times 490.5 = 1226.25 \, \text{N}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why $n = 2.5$?** In KTU problems, $n \geq 2$ is mandatory because the gripper must also resist **inertial forces** during robot acceleration, deceleration, and swing motions. A factor of $2 - 3$ is the standard KTU-prescribed range. ### Step 3 — Solve for Required MMF (NI) Using the Force Equation Using $F_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{\mu_0 (NI)^2 A}{g^2}$ (total for two poles): $$NI = \sqrt{\frac{F_{\text{design}} \cdot g^2}{\mu_0 A}}$$ $$NI = \sqrt{\frac{1226.25 \times (0.5 \times 10^{-3})^2}{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 25 \times 10^{-4}}}$$ Let me compute numerator and denominator separately. **Numerator:** $$1226.25 \times (0.5 \times 10^{-3})^2 = 1226.25 \times 0.25 \times 10^{-6} = 306.5625 \times 10^{-6}$$ **Denominator:** $$4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 25 \times 10^{-4} = 100\pi \times 10^{-11} = 3.14159 \times 10^{-9}$$ **Division:** $$\frac{306.5625 \times 10^{-6}}{3.14159 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{306.5625}{3.14159} \times 10^{3} = 97.58 \times 10^{3} = 9.758 \times 10^{4}$$ **Square root:** $$NI = \sqrt{9.758 \times 10^{4}} = 312.4 \, \text{At}$$ ### Step 4 — Determine Coil Current With $N = 1000$ turns: $$I = \frac{NI}{N} = \frac{312.4}{1000} = 0.312 \, \text{A} \approx 312 \, \text{mA}$$ > [!TIP] > **KTU Insight:** Currents of 0.3 – 1.0 A at low DC voltages (12–48 V) are typical for industrial magnetic grippers — making them easy to interface with robot digital I/O modules. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Parameters on Holding Force | Parameter | Increase → Effect on Force $F$ | Quantitative Relation | |---|---|---| | Current $I$ | ↑ Increases | $F \propto I^2$ | | Turns $N$ | ↑ Increases | $F \propto N^2$ | | Pole-face area $A$ | ↑ Increases (linearly) | $F \propto A$ | | Air gap $g$ | ↑ **Decreases** sharply | $F \propto 1/g^2$ | | Relative permeability $\mu_r$ | ↑ Slight increase (core saturates) | Non-linear | | Pole-face roughness | ↑ Effective gap → ↓ Force | Equivalent gap increases | | Workpiece thickness | ↓ (if too thin → flux leakage) | Below 3–5 mm → force drops | > [!WARNING] > **Exam Trap:** The inverse-square dependence on $g$ means that **doubling the air gap reduces the force to one-quarter** of its original value. This is the most-tested KTU concept in magnetic gripper problems. --- ## 3.4 Numerical Problem — KTU Pattern **Problem:** A robot-mounted magnetic gripper has two pole faces each of area $A = 10 \, \text{cm}^2$. The coil has $N = 500$ turns and carries $I = 2 \, \text{A}$. The air gap is $g = 1 \, \text{mm}$. Calculate the holding force. **Solution:** Given: - $A = 10 \times 10^{-4} = 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^2$ - $N = 500$ - $I = 2 \, \text{A}$, so $NI = 1000 \, \text{At}$ - $g = 10^{-3} \, \text{m}$ - $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \, \text{H/m}$ Apply force equation (total for two poles): $$F = \frac{\mu_0 (NI)^2 A}{g^2}$$ $$F = \frac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times (1000)^2 \times 10^{-3}}{(10^{-3})^2}$$ Numerator: $$4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 10^{6} \times 10^{-3} = 4\pi \times 10^{-4} = 1.2566 \times 10^{-3}$$ Denominator: $$(10^{-3})^2 = 10^{-6}$$ Division: $$F = \frac{1.2566 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-6}} = 1.2566 \times 10^{3} = 1256.6 \, \text{N}$$ ### Mass Equivalence $$m_{\text{equivalent}} = \frac{F}{g} = \frac{1256.6}{9.81} \approx 128.1 \, \text{kg}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Conclusion:** The gripper can hold approximately **128 kg** of ferrous material — sufficient for most industrial handling tasks. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Magnetic Gripper System The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **functional flow architecture** of a robot-mounted electromagnetic gripper system — from controller command to physical holding force. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[robotCTRL[Robot Controller
PLC or Robot CPU]] --> B[signalCMD[Digital I/O Signal
24V DC ON or OFF]] B --> C[driverMOD[Driver Module
MOSFET or Relay Bank]] C --> D[coilACT[Electromagnetic Coil
N turns of copper wire]] D --> E[coreMAG[Soft Iron Core
Low retentivity material]] E --> F[fluxPATH[Magnetic Flux Path
N to S through workpiece]] F --> G[poleFACE1[Pole Face N
Area A, air gap g]] F --> H[poleFACE2[Pole Face S
Area A, air gap g]] G --> I[workPIECE[Ferromagnetic Workpiece
Steel plate or block]] H --> I I --> J[holdFORCE[Holding Force F
equals B squared A over 2 mu0]] J --> K[taskSTAGE[Task Execution
Transfer, Lift, Place]] L[sensorFBK[Force or Proximity Sensor
Optional feedback]] --> A K --> L M[releaseCMD[Release Command
Reverse Pulse or OFF]] --> D style A fill:#e1f5ff style J fill:#ffe1e1 style I fill:#fff4e1 style K fill:#e1ffe1 ``` --- ## 4.2 Classification Flowchart — Types of Magnetic Grippers ```mermaid flowchart TD MG[MG_ROOT[Magnetic Grippers]] --> PMT[pmT[Permanent Magnet Type]] MG --> EMT[emT[Electromagnet Type]] MG --> HYB[hybT[Hybrid Switchable Type]] PMT --> PMT1[pmtA[NdFeB High Energy]] PMT --> PMT2[pmtB[AlNiCo Temperature Stable]] PMT --> PMT3[pmtC[Ferrite Low Cost]] EMT --> EMT1[emtA[DC Coil Iron Core]] EMT --> EMT2[emtB[AC Coil Laminated Core]] HYB --> HYB1[hybA[PM plus Reverse Pulse Coil]] HYB --> HYB2[hybB[PM plus Mechanically Shifted Keeper]] PMT --> PROS1[prosA[Pros: No power, fail-safe]] PMT --> CONS1[consA[Cons: Residual magnetism, mechanical release]] EMT --> PROS2[prosB[Pros: Variable force, clean release]] EMT --> CONS2[consB[Cons: Drops on power failure]] HYB --> PROS3[prosC[Pros: Fail-safe plus clean release]] HYB --> CONS3[consC[Cons: Complex control electronics]] style MG fill:#d0e8ff style PMT fill:#e8ffd0 style EMT fill:#ffe8d0 style HYB fill:#ffd0e8 ``` --- ## 4.3 Magnetic Flux Path Diagram — U-Shaped Electromagnet ```mermaid flowchart LR COIL1[coilA[Coil with current I
N turns]] --> CORE1[coreA[Soft Iron Core Limb 1]] CORE1 --> POLE1[poleA[Pole Face N
Area A]] POLE1 --> GAP1[gapA[Air Gap g]] GAP1 --> WORK[workpieceA[Ferromagnetic Workpiece]] WORK --> GAP2[gapB[Air Gap g]] GAP2 --> POLE2[poleB[Pole Face S
Area A]] POLE2 --> CORE2[coreB[Soft Iron Core Limb 2]] CORE2 --> BACK[backA[Back Iron Yoke]] BACK --> CORE1 F1[forceA[Holding Force F acts on workpiece
attracted to pole faces]] -.-> WORK style WORK fill:#fff4cc style COIL1 fill:#cce5ff style F1 fill:#ffcccc ``` --- ## 4.4 Functional Comparison Block Diagram — Engage vs Release States ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[st1[State 1: ENGAGE
Current I flows through coil]] --> FLUX1[fx1[Flux circulates
through workpiece]] FLUX1 --> HOLD1[h1[Part HELD
Fail-safe: NO for EM only]] S2[st2[State 2: RELEASE
Current OFF or reverse pulse]] --> FLUX2[fx2[Flux collapses
or is neutralized]] FLUX2 --> DROP1[d1[Part DROPS or releases
EM type fails safe: NO
PM or Hybrid: YES holds during fail]] S3[st3[Decision Logic in Robot Program]] --> S1 S3 --> S2 style S1 fill:#ccffcc style S2 fill:#ffcccc style S3 fill:#ccccff ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Q: List any three advantages of magnetic grippers over mechanical grippers in industrial robotic applications.** **Model Answer (3 points):** 1. **Simplicity of design:** No need to design custom finger geometry for each workpiece shape — the magnet conforms to any ferromagnetic surface. 2. **Versatility on complex surfaces:** Can grip parts with holes, curved profiles, or irregular shapes where mechanical fingers would fail. 3. **High reliability and speed:** No moving parts (in the PM/EM core), giving engage/release times in the order of milliseconds and very low maintenance. > [!TIP] > **Valuation Note (3 marks):** Award 1 mark per valid advantage. Generic statements like *"easy to use"* fetch 0.5 marks only — give **specific** points tied to robotics. --- ### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **Q: Distinguish between a permanent magnet gripper and an electromagnet gripper. Mention one limitation of each.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Permanent Magnet Gripper | Electromagnet Gripper | |---|---|---| | Source of flux | Permanent magnet (NdFeB, AlNiCo) | Current-carrying coil around iron core | | Power to hold | **Not required** (fail-safe) | **Required** continuously | | Release mechanism | Mechanical (rotate/slide magnet) | Interrupt current or apply reverse pulse | | Force control | Fixed (set by magnet strength) | Variable (via current $I$) | | Limitation | Difficult to release; residual magnetism | Drops part on power failure | - **Limitation of PM gripper:** Residual magnetism may prevent clean release of the workpiece. - **Limitation of EM gripper:** Loss of holding force during power or electrical failure — safety hazard. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Internal Choice Pattern > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 ESE Part B requires **internal choice**. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B (both carry 14 marks each). Each Part B question is split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. --- ### ❑ Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Q: With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working principle of an electromagnetic gripper used in robotic end-effectors. Discuss the holding force equation and the factors affecting it.** #### Part (a) — Working Principle & Diagram [7 Marks] **Answer:** An electromagnetic gripper consists of a **soft-iron core** shaped as a U (or E), around which a coil of insulated copper wire is wound. When DC current is passed through the coil, the core is magnetized, creating **North and South poles** at the two exposed pole faces. When the pole faces are brought close to (or in contact with) a ferromagnetic workpiece, the **magnetic flux** from the N pole flows through the air gap, passes through the workpiece (which has very high permeability), crosses the second air gap, and returns to the S pole through the iron yoke. This completes a **closed magnetic circuit**. Because the flux lines prefer the low-reluctance path through the iron, they "pull" the workpiece toward the pole faces, generating an attractive force at each air gap. The combined attractive force holds the workpiece against the gripper. **Sketch (describe in words since answer script is manual):** ``` ___________N___________ / \ | ===COIL=== | | ===COIL=== | \___________S___________/ | | [air gap] [air gap] | | ----------WORKPIECE-------- ``` To release the part, the current is switched off (or a reverse pulse is applied to collapse residual flux). The workpiece is freed. **[Marks Distribution: 2 for definition, 3 for working, 2 for diagram → 7 marks]** #### Part (b) — Holding Force Equation & Factors [7 Marks] **Answer:** The holding (attractive) force on the workpiece is given by the **Maxwell stress** expression at the air gap: $$F = \frac{B^2 A}{2 \mu_0} \quad [\text{for one pole face}]$$ For a U-shaped magnet with two pole faces, the total force is: $$F_{\text{total}} = \frac{\mu_0 (NI)^2 A}{g^2}$$ where: - $B$ = flux density (T) - $A$ = pole-face area (m²) - $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m - $N$ = number of turns - $I$ = coil current (A) - $g$ = air gap (m) **Factors affecting holding force:** | Factor | Relation | Engineering Implication | |---|---|---| | Current $I$ | $F \propto I^2$ | Higher current → much stronger grip | | Air gap $g$ | $F \propto 1/g^2$ | Smallest possible gap needed | | Pole-face area $A$ | $F \propto A$ | Larger pole faces → more force | | Core material | Higher $\mu_r$ → better flux | Use silicon steel laminations | | Workpiece surface finish | Smoother → smaller gap → higher force | Polish contact surfaces | **[Marks Distribution: 3 for derivation/formula, 4 for factors with explanation → 7 marks]** --- ### ❑ Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **Q: A robot-mounted electromagnetic gripper is used to transfer steel plates weighing 30 kg each from a conveyor to a CNC machine. The gripper has two pole faces, each of area $20 \, \text{cm}^2$, with an air gap of $0.8 \, \text{mm}$ at each pole. The coil has 800 turns. Determine: (a) the minimum MMF and current required to hold the plate with a safety factor of 2.5; (b) comment on why a hybrid switchable magnetic gripper may be preferred over a pure electromagnetic type for this application.** #### Part (a) — Calculation of MMF and Current [7 Marks] **Given:** - Mass $m = 30 \, \text{kg}$ - $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$ - $A = 20 \times 10^{-4} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^2$ - $g_{\text{gap}} = 0.8 \times 10^{-3} = 8 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}$ - $N = 800$ - Safety factor $n = 2.5$ **Step 1 — Weight of plate:** $$W = m g = 30 \times 9.81 = 294.3 \, \text{N}$$ **Step 2 — Design holding force with safety factor:** $$F_{\text{design}} = n \times W = 2.5 \times 294.3 = 735.75 \, \text{N}$$ **Step 3 — Solve for $NI$ using $F = \dfrac{\mu_0 (NI)^2 A}{g^2}$:** $$NI = \sqrt{\frac{F_{\text{design}} \cdot g^2}{\mu_0 A}}$$ Numerator: $$735.75 \times (8 \times 10^{-4})^2 = 735.75 \times 64 \times 10^{-8} = 4.7088 \times 10^{-5}$$ Denominator: $$4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 2 \times 10^{-3} = 8\pi \times 10^{-10} = 2.5133 \times 10^{-9}$$ Division: $$\frac{4.7088 \times 10^{-5}}{2.5133 \times 10^{-9}} = 1.8736 \times 10^{4}$$ $$NI = \sqrt{1.8736 \times 10^{4}} = 136.9 \, \text{At}$$ **Step 4 — Current:** $$I = \frac{NI}{N} = \frac{136.9}{800} = 0.1711 \, \text{A} \approx 171 \, \text{mA}$$ **Answer:** $NI \approx 137 \, \text{At}$ and $I \approx 0.17 \, \text{A}$ #### Part (b) — Why Hybrid Gripper Is Preferred [7 Marks] **Answer:** In a **pure electromagnetic** gripper, the holding force depends entirely on the continuous supply of current. If a power failure, cable break, or controller fault occurs: 1. The current to the coil is interrupted. 2. The magnetic flux collapses instantly. 3. The workpiece (30 kg steel plate) is **dropped**, potentially damaging the CNC machine, injuring personnel, or causing production loss. A **hybrid switchable magnetic gripper** overcomes this critical safety limitation: 1. A **permanent magnet** generates the holding flux — no power is required to hold the plate. 2. To **release**, a brief **reverse current pulse** is sent through a small control coil, which neutralizes the PM flux at the pole faces, allowing the plate to be set down. 3. After release, the PM flux returns automatically, and the next plate can be picked up. 4. **During a power failure**, the plate remains firmly held — **fail-safe operation**. Additional advantages of the hybrid design for this CNC-tending application: - **Lower energy consumption** (no continuous current). - **Lower heat generation** in the coil. - **Tighter control** of release timing. - **Compliance with industrial safety standards** (ISO 10218 for industrial robots). **[Marks Distribution: 3 for safety analysis of EM type, 4 for hybrid advantages → 7 marks]** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Where Students Lose Marks (and How to Avoid):** > > 1. **Forgetting the safety factor $n$:** Many students compute force equal to the weight alone. KTU mandates $n \geq 2$. Loss: **2 marks**. > 2. **Confusing force per pole vs total force:** U-shaped grippers have **two pole faces**. Use the right formula. Loss: **1–2 marks**. > 3. **Unit mistakes in air gap:** Air gap $g$ must be in **meters** in the formula, not mm. Common slip. Loss: **1 mark**. > 4. **Not mentioning fail-safe behavior:** A question like *"which gripper is safe during power failure?"* requires you to explicitly write *"holds the part without power"* for full credit. Loss: **1 mark**. > 5. **Mixing up PM, EM, and Hybrid characteristics:** Memorize the comparison table in Section 2.6. Loss: **2–3 marks** on comparison questions. > 6. **Skipping the diagram:** Even a hand-drawn rough sketch earns partial credit. A blank diagram space loses **1–2 marks**. > 7. **Not stating assumptions:** Always mention *"assuming uniform flux density"* and *"neglecting leakage flux"* — examiners award marks for explicit assumptions. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **High-Yield Memorization Checklist for KTU 2024 — Magnetic Grippers:** - [ ] **Definition:** Magnetic gripper uses magnetic flux to hold ferromagnetic workpieces — end-effector in robotics. - [ ] **Three types:** Permanent Magnet (PM), Electromagnet (EM), Hybrid Switchable. - [ ] **Key force equation:** $F = \dfrac{B^2 A}{2\mu_0}$ — Maxwell stress / magnetic pressure. - [ ] **U-magnet total force:** $F = \dfrac{\mu_0 (NI)^2 A}{g^2}$. - [ ] **MMF equation:** $NI = \sqrt{\dfrac{F g^2}{\mu_0 A}}$ — for solving numerical problems. - [ ] **Permeability constant:** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \, \text{H/m}$. - [ ] **Force ∝ $I^2$, $N^2$, $A$:** Square-law dependence on current and turns; linear on area. - [ ] **Force ∝ $1/g^2$:** Inverse-square on air gap — most critical design parameter. - [ ] **Safety factor:** Always use $n \geq 2$ in KTU numerical problems. - [ ] **Fail-safe:** PM and Hybrid hold parts during power failure; EM does NOT. - [ ] **Release mechanism:** EM releases on current OFF; PM uses mechanical action; Hybrid uses reverse pulse. - [ ] **Residual magnetism problem:** Use silicon steel core with low retentivity to minimize. - [ ] **Applications:** Sheet metal handling, scrap lifting, CNC tending, press-shop automation. - [ ] **Limitation:** Works **only on ferromagnetic** materials — aluminium, copper, plastic, wood are NOT grippable. - [ ] **KTU mark split:** Part A = 2 × 3 = 6 marks; Part B = 1 × 14 = 14 marks (with internal choice). > **End of Module 1 — Magnetic Grippers Notes | KTU 2024 Scheme | PCCAT602**

topics.title: Vacuum Grippers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAT602 - ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Vacuum Grippers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Vacuum Gripper** (also termed a *suction gripper* or *pneumatic gripper*) is a non-contact mechanical end-effector that uses **negative gauge pressure (vacuum)** relative to the ambient atmospheric pressure to grasp, lift, and manipulate workpieces. The fundamental operating principle relies on establishing a **pressure differential** across the gripping interface, where the higher atmospheric pressure acting on the top surface of the workpiece produces a net downward (clamping) force, effectively pressing the object against the seal of an elastomeric suction cup.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCCAT602, Module 1):**
> A vacuum gripper is an end-effector that employs pressure differential created by a vacuum generator (ejector/pump) to engage smooth, flat, or slightly curved non-porous surfaces through suction cups made of elastomers such as NBR, silicone, polyurethane, or natural rubber.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Suction Cup Hook"

Imagine pressing a small plastic cup (the kind found at pharmacies for arthritis therapy) onto a smooth glass window. When you press it down and release, atmospheric pressure (~101.325 kPa) pushes it against the glass with a force of several Newtons, allowing it to hold the cup's weight plus a small load. A **vacuum gripper operates on exactly this same principle**, but in a controlled, automated, and scalable manner for industrial robotics.

The "vacuum" inside the cup simply means the air pressure has been **reduced below atmospheric pressure**. Nature abhors a pressure imbalance, so the surrounding atmosphere pushes the cup (and the attached object) toward the region of lower pressure with a measurable, predictable force.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Atmospheric Constants (must be memorised):**
> - Standard Atmospheric Pressure, $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 10.197 \text{ N/cm}^2 = 1.01325 \text{ bar}$
> - Vacuum (theoretical limit) $= 0 \text{ kPa absolute} = -101.325 \text{ kPa gauge}$
> - Industrial vacuum levels typically achieve only **70–90% vacuum**, i.e., $P_{vac} \approx -70 \text{ to } -90 \text{ kPa gauge}$

## 1.3 Salient Features of Vacuum Grippers (KTU Board-Favourite Points)

- **Surface Dependency:** Best suited for smooth, flat, dry, clean, non-porous surfaces (glass, sheet metal, plastics, cardboard).
- **Material Limitation:** Cannot grip porous materials (foam, fabric) or rough surfaces without specialised bellows cups.
- **Force Direction:** Provides only **pulling force (axial, normal to the cup face)** — lateral forces must be accounted for via friction or geometric constraints.
- **Compliance:** Elastomeric cups offer inherent mechanical compliance, tolerating slight surface irregularities and misalignment.
- **Cycle Time:** Very fast engage/disengage times (typically **10–50 ms** with ejector-based systems).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Working Principle

The vacuum gripper's operation can be broken down into **five sequential stages** that students must understand in exam order:

1. **Approach & Contact:** The robot positions the suction cup perpendicular to the workpiece surface. The cup's sealing lip makes airtight contact.

2. **Evacuation (Vacuum Generation):** A vacuum source (Venturi ejector or rotary pump) extracts air from the cavity between the cup and the workpiece. This creates a **negative gauge pressure** $P_v$ relative to atmospheric.

3. **Pressure Differential Establishment:** The pressure inside the cup drops, while $P_{atm}$ continues acting on all external surfaces of the workpiece. This creates a **net inward (clamping) force**.

4. **Engagement (Lift/Hold):** The robot retracts the end-effector; the workpiece moves with it because the pressure differential (and hence friction at the cup-workpiece interface) exceeds the gravitational + inertial load.

5. **Release (Break Vacuum):** A solenoid valve opens the cup to atmosphere, equalising the pressure. The cup releases the workpiece.

## 2.2 Core Governing Equation — Lifting Force

The **theoretical holding force** of a vacuum gripper is given by the product of the pressure differential and the effective cup area:

$$F_{theoretical} = \Delta P \times A_{eff}$$

where the components expand as:

$$\Delta P = P_{atm} - P_{abs,cup} = P_{atm} + P_{v,gauge}$$

$$A_{eff} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

for a circular cup of diameter $D$, or $A_{eff} = L \times W$ for an oval/rectangular cup.

## 2.3 Practical Holding Force (Accounting for Real-World Losses)

In practice, several degradation factors reduce the theoretical force:

$$F_{practical} = \mu_s \times F_{theoretical}$$

where $\mu_s$ is the **effective safety/utilisation factor** that combines:
- Friction coefficient between cup and workpiece
- Surface roughness and contamination
- Leakage from worn seals
- Cup deformation under load

Typical $\mu_s$ values: **0.4 (rough/dirty) to 0.7 (clean/smooth)**.

## 2.4 Maximum Payload Equation (KTU Favourite)

When lifting vertically with gravitational acceleration $g$ and additional acceleration $a$, with a chosen safety factor $n$:

$$F_{practical} \geq n \times m_{payload} \times (g + a)$$

Solving for maximum payload mass:

$$m_{max} = \frac{\Delta P \times A_{eff} \times \mu_s}{n \times (g + a)}$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **every formula** you may need for vacuum gripper problems in the KTU 2024 University Examination. Memorise the variable meanings; examiners frequently test numerical substitution.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Variables | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_{th} = \Delta P \cdot A$ | Theoretical holding force | $\text{N}$ |
| 2 | $\Delta P = P_{atm} + P_{v,gauge}$ | Pressure differential | $\text{Pa}$ |
| 3 | $A = \pi D^2 / 4$ | Effective cup area (circular) | $\text{m}^2$ |
| 4 | $F_{pr} = \mu_s \cdot F_{th}$ | Practical holding force | $\text{N}$ |
| 5 | $m_{max} = \dfrac{\mu_s \cdot \Delta P \cdot A}{n(g+a)}$ | Maximum payload mass | $\text{kg}$ |
| 6 | $N_{cups} = \dfrac{m_{total} \cdot n \cdot (g+a)}{\mu_s \cdot \Delta P \cdot A}$ | Required number of cups | dimensionless |
| 7 | $V_{cup} = \pi D^2 h / 4$ | Cup cavity volume (for evacuation time) | $\text{m}^3$ |
| 8 | $t_{evac} = \dfrac{V_{cup} \cdot \ln(P_1/P_2)}{Q_{vac}}$ | Evacuation time | $\text{s}$ |
| 9 | $S_{factor} = \dfrac{F_{th}}{W_{payload}}$ | Static safety factor (recommended $\geq 2$) | dimensionless |

## 2.6 Vacuum Generation Methods

| Method | Vacuum Level | Response Time | Maintenance | KTU Exam Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Venturi Ejector** | Up to **90% vacuum** | Very fast (ms) | Low (no moving parts) | **Most frequently asked** |
| **Rotary Vane Pump** | Up to 99% vacuum | Slow | High (oil, wear) | Sometimes asked |
| **Diaphragm Pump** | Up to 85% vacuum | Moderate | Low | Rare |
| **Scroll/Lobe Blower** | Up to 50% vacuum (blower mode) | Fast | Moderate | Rare |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Venturi ejectors** dominate robotic applications because they use **plant compressed air** (typical shop-floor supply = **6 bar**), require no electric motor, and respond in milliseconds. They work on the **Bernoulli principle**: high-velocity air through a constriction creates low pressure that evacuates the cup.

## 2.7 Types of Suction Cups (Classification — High KTU Yield)

| Cup Type | Geometry | Best For | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Flat Cup** | Single flat sealing lip | Smooth, flat surfaces (glass, sheet metal) | Fails on rough/curved surfaces |
| **Bellows Cup** | 1.5 / 2.5 / 3.5 convolutions | Curved, uneven, or rough surfaces; height compensation | Lower stiffness; reduced force |
| **Oval Cup** | Elongated rectangle | Long, narrow items (extrusions, tubes) | Less area coverage |
| **Universal/Soft Cup** | Soft elastomer, large lip | Deformed or textured surfaces | Lower holding force |
| **High-Temp Cup** | Silicone with metal insert | Hot workpieces (glass, castings) | Higher cost |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Holding Force from First Principles

**Statement:** Derive the expression for the theoretical holding force exerted by a single circular vacuum cup of diameter $D$ operating at a gauge vacuum pressure of $P_{v,gauge}$.

**Step 1 — Identify the pressure system.**
The cup encloses a sealed cavity above the workpiece surface. Before evacuation, the cavity pressure equals atmospheric pressure:

$$P_{cavity,initial} = P_{atm}$$

After evacuation, the cavity reaches a reduced absolute pressure:

$$P_{cavity,final} = P_{abs,cup}$$

**Step 2 — Define the pressure differential.**
The difference between the outside (atmospheric) and inside (cup cavity) pressures drives the clamping action:

$$\Delta P = P_{atm} - P_{abs,cup}$$

Because vacuum gauges typically read *negative* relative to atmosphere, we can equivalently write:

$$\Delta P = P_{atm} + P_{v,gauge}$$

where $P_{v,gauge} > 0$ represents the magnitude of the vacuum reading.

**Step 3 — Apply Pascal's principle (force from pressure).**
For a planar surface, force equals pressure times area, directed from high to low pressure:

$$F_{theoretical} = \Delta P \times A_{eff}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute area for circular cup.**

$$A_{eff} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

**Step 5 — Final expression.**

$$\boxed{F_{theoretical} = \left(P_{atm} + P_{v,gauge}\right) \times \frac{\pi D^2}{4}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Lifted Mass with Safety Factor

**Statement:** Derive the maximum mass $m_{max}$ that a vacuum gripper can lift vertically, given a practical safety factor $n$, acceleration $a$, and coefficient $\mu_s$.

**Step 1 — The downward (destabilising) force on the workpiece.**
For vertical lifting with additional acceleration (e.g., during robot acceleration), Newton's second law gives the required upward force:

$$F_{required} = m \cdot (g + a)$$

**Step 2 — The upward (stabilising) practical holding force.**
From Section 2.4, the actual force the cup can deliver is reduced by $\mu_s$:

$$F_{practical} = \mu_s \cdot \Delta P \cdot A_{eff}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the safety factor.**
For industrial safety, the practical force must exceed the required force by a factor $n$ (typically 2 to 4):

$$\mu_s \cdot \Delta P \cdot A_{eff} \geq n \cdot m \cdot (g + a)$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $m_{max}$.**

$$m_{max} = \frac{\mu_s \cdot \Delta P \cdot A_{eff}}{n \cdot (g + a)}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute area for circular cup.**

$$\boxed{m_{max} = \frac{\mu_s \cdot \left(P_{atm} + P_{v,gauge}\right) \cdot \pi D^2}{4 \cdot n \cdot (g + a)}}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (Numerical — KTU Style)

**Problem:** A robotic vacuum gripper uses **four identical circular suction cups** of diameter **$D = 60 \text{ mm}$**. The ejector produces a vacuum of **$-80 \text{ kPa}$ gauge**. The effective utilisation factor is **$\mu_s = 0.5$** and the safety factor required is **$n = 2.5$**. During the pick-and-place cycle, the maximum vertical acceleration of the robot is **$2 \text{ m/s}^2$**. Find:
(a) the theoretical holding force per cup,
(b) the practical holding force per cup,
(c) the maximum total mass that can be lifted.

**Solution:**

**(a) Theoretical holding force per cup:**

Given:
- $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 101325 \text{ Pa}$
- $P_{v,gauge} = 80 \text{ kPa} = 80000 \text{ Pa}$
- $D = 0.06 \text{ m}$

$$\Delta P = P_{atm} + P_{v,gauge} = 101325 + 80000 = 181325 \text{ Pa}$$

$$A_{eff} = \frac{\pi (0.06)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.0036}{4} = 0.002827 \text{ m}^2$$

$$F_{th} = 181325 \times 0.002827 = 512.6 \text{ N}$$

**(b) Practical holding force per cup:**

$$F_{pr} = \mu_s \times F_{th} = 0.5 \times 512.6 = 256.3 \text{ N}$$

**(c) Maximum total mass (4 cups, $n=2.5$, $a=2 \text{ m/s}^2$, $g=9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$):**

Total practical force from 4 cups:

$$F_{total} = 4 \times 256.3 = 1025.2 \text{ N}$$

Required force per unit mass:

$$F_{req}/m = n(g+a) = 2.5 \times (9.81 + 2) = 2.5 \times 11.81 = 29.525 \text{ N/kg}$$

$$m_{max} = \frac{F_{total}}{n(g+a)} = \frac{1025.2}{29.525} \approx 34.72 \text{ kg}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer Summary:**
> - (a) $F_{th} \approx 512.6 \text{ N}$ per cup
> - (b) $F_{pr} \approx 256.3 \text{ N}$ per cup
> - (c) $m_{max} \approx 34.7 \text{ kg}$

---

## 3.4 Sanity-Check Analysis Matrix

| Condition | Effect on Holding Force | Engineering Action |
|---|---|---|
| Vacuum level $\uparrow$ | Force $\uparrow$ (linear) | Use multi-stage ejectors |
| Cup diameter $\uparrow$ | Force $\uparrow$ (quadratic, via $D^2$) | Larger cup preferred for heavy items |
| Surface roughness $\uparrow$ | Force $\downarrow$ (leakage) | Use bellows cup |
| Acceleration $\uparrow$ | Required force $\uparrow$ | Slow robot trajectory |
| Safety factor $n$ raised | Allowed mass $\downarrow$ | Mandatory for human-collaborative robots |
| Porous surface (foam) | Force collapses to ~0 | Switch to mechanical/finger gripper |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Vacuum Gripper System

The following block diagram illustrates the **complete functional architecture** of an industrial vacuum gripper system mounted on a robotic end-effector, mapping all physical and signal interactions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Compressed Air Supply 6 bar] --> B[Venturi Ejector]
    B --> C[Silencer / Exhaust]
    B --> D[Vacuum Filter Unit]
    D --> E[Vacuum Switch / Pressure Sensor]
    E --> F[Solenoid Valve NO NC]
    F --> G[Vacuum Manifold Distributor]
    G --> H1[Suction Cup 1 Cup A]
    G --> H2[Suction Cup 2 Cup B]
    G --> H3[Suction Cup 3 Cup C]
    G --> H4[Suction Cup 4 Cup D]
    H1 --> I[Workpiece Surface]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    H4 --> I
    I --> J[Atmospheric Pressure Acts Downward]
    J --> K[Net Clamping Force Generated]
    K --> L[End Effector Lifts Workpiece]
    E --> M[PLC Controller Feedback]
    M --> N[Robot Motion Controller]
```

## 4.2 Cross-Sectional Schematic of a Suction Cup During Engagement

The following diagram depicts the **physical functional state** of a single suction cup — from approach to sealed engagement — highlighting the pressure distribution that creates the clamping force.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph APPROACH["STAGE 1 APPROACH"]
        A1[Robot End Effector] --> A2[Suction Cup Unsealed]
        A2 --> A3[Workpiece Surface]
    end

    subgraph CONTACT["STAGE 2 CONTACT"]
        B1[End Effector Descends] --> B2[Cup Lip Seals on Surface]
        B2 --> B3[Cup Cavity Still at Atm Pressure]
    end

    subgraph EVACUATE["STAGE 3 EVACUATION"]
        C1[Vacuum Generator Activates] --> C2[Air Expelled from Cup Cavity]
        C2 --> C3[Pressure Drops to P abs cup]
    end

    subgraph ENGAGED["STAGE 4 ENGAGED HOLD"]
        D1[Patm Pushes Down on Workpiece] --> D2[Net Force Holds Workpiece]
        D2 --> D3[Vacuum Maintained Leak Tight]
    end

    APPROACH --> CONTACT --> EVACUATE --> ENGAGED
```

## 4.3 Selection Decision Flowchart

Use this decision logic when selecting the appropriate cup type for an application — a frequent KTU viva question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start Selection] --> Q1{Workpiece Surface Type?}
    Q1 -->|Smooth Flat| Q2{Required Holding Force?}
    Q1 -->|Curved Rough| REC1[Use Bellows Cup 1.5 fold]
    Q1 -->|Porous Fabric| STOP[Vacuum Gripper NOT Suitable]
    Q2 -->|Low| FC[Flat Cup]
    Q2 -->|High| Q3{Object Shape?}
    Q3 -->|Long Narrow| OC[Oval Cup]
    Q3 -->|Compact| REC2[Multiple Flat Cups in Array]
    Q3 -->|Curved| REC3[Use Bellows Cup 2.5 fold]
```

## 4.4 Component Cross-Section (Block-Level Functional)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph EC["VACUUM GRIPPER ASSEMBLY"]
        E1[Robot Mounting Flange ISO Standard] --> E2[Manifold Block Aluminium]
        E2 --> E3[Solenoid Valve 5 by 2 Way]
        E3 --> E4[Vacuum Port to Ejector]
        E4 --> E5[Suction Cup Mounting Stud]
        E5 --> E6[Elastomer Suction Cup]
        E6 --> E7[Sealing Lip Contact Face]
    end
    EXT[External Compressed Air 6 bar] -.-> E3
    VAC[Vacuum Source Ejector Pump] -.-> E4
    WP[Workpiece Contact Zone] -.-> E7
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]** 
**Define a vacuum gripper. List any four applications where vacuum grippers are preferred over mechanical grippers.**

**Model Answer:**
A vacuum gripper is a robotic end-effector that uses negative pressure (vacuum) relative to atmospheric pressure to grasp workpieces via suction cups made of elastomeric materials.

**Applications where vacuum grippers are preferred:**
1. Handling flat sheet metal in press-shop automation.
2. Pick-and-place of glass panes in the solar/PVG industry.
3. Packaging line palletising of cardboard boxes.
4. Loading/unloading of plastic moulded parts from injection moulding machines.
5. Electronic wafer handling (using specialised non-marking silicone cups).

**Mark Distribution:** Definition (1) + any 4 applications (2) = 3 marks.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**Explain the working principle of a Venturi ejector used in vacuum gripper systems.**

**Model Answer:**
A Venturi ejector operates on the **Bernoulli principle**. Compressed air from the plant supply (typically 6 bar) is forced through a **converging nozzle**, where its velocity increases dramatically and its static pressure drops below atmospheric (per Bernoulli's equation). This low-pressure region is connected via a port to the suction cup. The high-velocity air stream entrains and evacuates the air inside the cup, creating a vacuum. The spent air then exits through a diverging section (which slightly recovers pressure) and is exhausted through a silencer.

**Key Advantage:** No moving parts → high reliability, fast response (ms), and maintenance-free operation.

**Mark Distribution:** Bernoulli principle (1) + working sequence (1) + advantage (1) = 3 marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Never** write "vacuum sucks the object" — vacuum **does not suck**; **atmospheric pressure pushes**. This phrasing alone loses **1–2 marks**.
> - Always **carry units** through the calculation; unmarked units = guaranteed mark deduction.
> - Always specify whether vacuum is **gauge** or **absolute**.
> - In derivation problems, **every algebraic step** must be shown; jump-cutting steps leads to **2–3 mark penalty**.
> - State **assumptions** explicitly (e.g., "assuming airtight seal," "neglecting leakage").

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Design-Oriented**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**A robotic vacuum gripper is to be designed to lift a flat steel plate of mass 25 kg. The maximum vertical acceleration during the robot's motion is $3 \text{ m/s}^2$. The ejector can produce a vacuum of $-85 \text{ kPa}$ gauge. Use safety factor $n = 3$, effective utilisation factor $\mu_s = 0.6$, and standard atmospheric pressure. Circular suction cups of diameter 40 mm are available.**

**(a) Calculate the number of suction cups required. (7 marks)**

**(b) If the plate is tilted by $15°$ from horizontal during pickup, recalculate the number of cups required, and comment on the design change. (7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — Flat horizontal pickup:**

**Given:**
- $m = 25 \text{ kg}$
- $a = 3 \text{ m/s}^2$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- $P_{v,gauge} = 85000 \text{ Pa}$
- $P_{atm} = 101325 \text{ Pa}$
- $n = 3$, $\mu_s = 0.6$
- $D = 0.04 \text{ m}$

**Step 1 — Pressure differential:**

$$\Delta P = P_{atm} + P_{v,gauge} = 101325 + 85000 = 186325 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 2 — Effective area per cup:**

$$A = \frac{\pi (0.04)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.0016}{4} = 1.2566 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Practical holding force per cup:**

$$F_{pr} = \mu_s \cdot \Delta P \cdot A = 0.6 \times 186325 \times 1.2566 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$F_{pr} = 140.48 \text{ N per cup}$$

**Step 4 — Required total force (including safety factor and acceleration):**

$$F_{req} = n \cdot m \cdot (g + a) = 3 \times 25 \times (9.81 + 3) = 3 \times 25 \times 12.81$$

$$F_{req} = 960.75 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5 — Number of cups:**

$$N = \frac{F_{req}}{F_{pr}} = \frac{960.75}{140.48} = 6.84$$

Since we cannot use a fractional cup, **round up to the next integer:**

$$\boxed{N = 7 \text{ cups}}$$

---

**Part (b) — Tilted pickup at 15°:**

When the workpiece is tilted, the gravitational force component along the cup's axis (i.e., the force tending to pull the cup off) **decreases** by $\cos(15°)$, but a new **lateral (shear) force** appears equal to $mg \sin(15°)$. This lateral force must be resisted by friction at the cup-workpiece interface.

**Effective normal force needed (axial component reduces):**

$$F_{axial,gravity} = m \cdot g \cdot \cos(15°) = 25 \times 9.81 \times 0.9659 = 236.94 \text{ N}$$

**Lateral shear force:**

$$F_{lateral} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin(15°) = 25 \times 9.81 \times 0.2588 = 63.49 \text{ N}$$

The friction force resisting shear must satisfy:

$$\mu_{friction} \times N_{cups} \times F_{pr,per cup} \geq F_{lateral}$$

Assuming friction coefficient at the cup-workpiece interface $\mu_{friction} = 0.4$:

$$N_{cups,shear} = \frac{F_{lateral}}{\mu_{friction} \times F_{pr}} = \frac{63.49}{0.4 \times 140.48} = 1.13 \Rightarrow 2 \text{ cups (by shear alone)}$$

However, the normal (axial) load including the robot's vertical acceleration must still be overcome:

$$F_{req,axial} = n \cdot m \cdot [(g+a)\cos\theta + g\sin\theta]$$

$$= 3 \times 25 \times [(9.81+3)\cos 15° + 9.81 \sin 15°]$$

$$= 75 \times [12.81 \times 0.9659 + 9.81 \times 0.2588]$$

$$= 75 \times [12.373 + 2.539] = 75 \times 14.912 = 1118.4 \text{ N}$$

$$N_{cups,tilted} = \frac{1118.4}{140.48} = 7.96 \Rightarrow 8 \text{ cups}$$

$$\boxed{N = 8 \text{ cups for tilted pickup}}$$

**Design Comment:** Tilting the pickup increases the required cup count by one due to (1) the lateral shear force component that consumes part of the available friction, and (2) the need to ensure safety against sliding. In practice, designers should either (a) re-orient the end-effector normal to the tilted surface, or (b) use higher-friction cups or mechanical stops.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Theory-Oriented Alternative**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a) Explain with neat sketches the working of a vacuum gripper. List the main components and describe the function of each. (7 marks)**

**(b) Compare vacuum grippers with mechanical (jaw/finger) grippers on the basis of (i) workpiece compatibility, (ii) cycle time, (iii) force capability, (iv) cost, and (v) maintenance. (7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — Working & Components:**

**Working Principle (Sketch Description):**
A vacuum gripper has a hollow suction cup made of elastomer (rubber/silicone). The robot brings the cup into contact with the workpiece surface. A vacuum generator (Venturi ejector or pump) evacuates air from the cup cavity. This creates a low-pressure region inside the cup, while atmospheric pressure ($P_{atm}$) continues to act on the external (top) surface of the workpiece. The pressure differential generates a clamping force $F = \Delta P \times A$ pressing the workpiece against the cup. The robot can now lift and transport the workpiece. Release is achieved by opening a solenoid valve that admits atmospheric air into the cup, breaking the vacuum.

**Main Components and Functions:**

| # | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Suction Cup** | Seals against workpiece; transmits clamping force via elastomer deformation |
| 2 | **Vacuum Generator (Ejector/Pump)** | Creates the low-pressure region (vacuum source) |
| 3 | **Solenoid Valve** | Controls vacuum ON/OFF; admits atmospheric air for release |
| 4 | **Vacuum Filter** | Removes dust/moisture from intake air to protect the ejector |
| 5 | **Vacuum Switch / Sensor** | Monitors vacuum level; sends feedback signal to PLC |
| 6 | **Silencer** | Reduces exhaust noise from the ejector |
| 7 | **Manifold / Distributor** | Distributes vacuum to multiple cups simultaneously |
| 8 | **Mounting Flange** | Interfaces the gripper to the robot's end-effector plate |

**Mark Split:** Working description with sketch explanation (3) + at least 6 components (3) + integration explanation (1).

---

**Part (b) — Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Vacuum Gripper | Mechanical (Jaw) Gripper |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Workpiece Compatibility** | Smooth, flat, non-porous surfaces only; best for sheet, glass, boxes | Almost any geometry; can grip complex shapes via custom fingers |
| **(ii) Cycle Time** | Very fast (10–50 ms engage) | Moderate (200–500 ms) due to jaw motion |
| **(iii) Force Capability** | Limited by atmospheric pressure (~10 N/cm² max); needs large area for heavy loads | High force possible via pneumatic/hydraulic actuation of jaws |
| **(iv) Cost** | Low to moderate (no precision parts) | Higher (custom finger tooling required per part) |
| **(v) Maintenance** | Low — mainly cup replacement | Higher — jaw alignment, actuator servicing, finger wear |

**Conclusion:** Vacuum grippers excel in high-speed, flat-part applications (packaging, sheet-metal handling), while mechanical grippers dominate in general-purpose applications requiring strong, position-controlled grasping.

**Mark Split:** Each comparison point (i)–(v) carries ~1.2 marks; conclusion = 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Vacuum Grippers (PCCAT602, Module 1):**

- ☐ **Definition:** Vacuum gripper uses **pressure differential**, not "suction," to clamp workpieces.
- ☐ **Core Formula:** $F_{th} = \Delta P \times A = (P_{atm} + P_{v,gauge}) \times \pi D^2 / 4$
- ☐ **Standard $P_{atm}$:** **101.325 kPa** (must be converted to Pa before calculation: ×1000).
- ☐ **Practical Force:** Always multiplied by $\mu_s$ (**0.4–0.7**) to account for real-world leakage.
- ☐ **Safety Factor $n$:** Typically **2 to 4**; higher for human-collaborative robots.
- ☐ **Acceleration Term:** Always include $(g+a)$ in vertical lifting problems.
- ☐ **Vacuum Source:** Venturi ejector (most common) vs rotary pump (high vacuum).
- ☐ **Cup Types:** Flat (smooth surfaces), Bellows (curved/rough), Oval (long items).
- ☐ **Material Suitability:** Vacuum grippers FAIL on porous, perforated, or rough surfaces.
- ☐ **Number of Cups:** $N = \lceil F_{req}/F_{pr} \rceil$ — always **round up**.
- ☐ **Release Mechanism:** Solenoid valve admits atmospheric air to equalise pressure.
- ☐ **Bernoulli Principle:** Foundation of Venturi ejector operation.
- ☐ **Force Direction:** Vacuum grippers provide primarily **axial (normal) force**; lateral loads require friction or mechanical constraint.
- ☐ **Typical Application Industries:** Packaging, automotive body-shop, glass handling, PCB/Electronics, food handling (with food-grade cups).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Adhesive Grippers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAT602 - ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Adhesive Grippers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Adhesive Gripper** is a class of robotic end-effector that utilizes **surface-level molecular and intermolecular adhesion forces** — rather than mechanical interlocking, magnetic coupling, or pneumatic suction — to grasp, hold, and manipulate objects. The gripper relies on the controlled contact between a compliant or patterned interface and the target surface, generating attractive forces through mechanisms such as **van der Waals interactions, capillary bridging, electrostatic (Coulomb) attraction, or bio-inspired fibrillar adhesion**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 – End Effectors):** Adhesive grippers are listed under *non-conventional gripper mechanisms* and are frequently examined as a 14-mark Part B question linking **biomimetics, contact mechanics, and material science** to robotic manipulation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine pressing a **sticky note** onto a smooth glass surface. Even though the glue is not "wet" in the classical sense, the note sticks firmly and can hold the glass against gravity. The note does not pierce, hook, or magnetize the glass — it simply *sticks* due to millions of microscopic contact points working together.

An **adhesive gripper** works on exactly the same principle — but engineered to be:
- **Reusable** (the adhesion can be switched ON/OFF),
- **Directionally selective** (strong in the normal/shear direction, weak in the peel direction), and
- **Surface-agnostic** (works on glass, silicon wafers, painted metal, and even rough walls).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Geckos can walk upside down on glass ceilings. Their toes are covered with millions of microscopic hair-like structures called **setae**. Each seta branches into hundreds of **spatulae** that make such intimate molecular contact with the surface that **van der Waals forces** (weak, universal atomic attractions) combine to produce a remarkably strong grip. Robotic adhesive grippers mimic this with **synthetic microfiber arrays**, **elastomer pads**, or **electroadhesive films**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Key Physical Constants

The following standard parameters govern the quantitative analysis of adhesive grippers:

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Work of adhesion | $W$ | $0.05$ – $0.5\ \text{J/m}^2$ |
| Equivalent contact radius | $R$ | $10^{-6}$ – $10^{-2}\ \text{m}$ |
| Effective Young's modulus | $E^{*}$ | $10^{3}$ – $10^{9}\ \text{Pa}$ |
| Vacuum permittivity | $\varepsilon_{0}$ | $8.854 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{F/m}$ |
| Surface tension of water (capillary) | $\gamma$ | $0.072\ \text{N/m}$ at 25 °C |
| Pull-off safety factor (KTU typical) | $n$ | $\mathbf{1.5}$ – $\mathbf{2.5}$ |
| Adhesion pressure (gecko arrays) | $p$ | $\approx 10\ \text{N/cm}^2$ (gecko); up to $100\ \text{N/cm}^2$ (synthetic) |

### 1.4 Why Adhesive Grippers Matter in KTU 2024 Robotics

Adhesive grippers fill critical gaps left by **mechanical jaws, vacuum cups, and magnetic grippers**:
- They work on **non-magnetic, non-porous, smooth surfaces** (glass, silicon, polished metal).
- They can grasp **fragile, thin, or flexible objects** (solar wafers, OLED panels, fabric).
- They function in **vacuum, cleanrooms, and zero-gravity** environments — making them central to **space robotics** and **semiconductor manufacturing**.

---

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook (KTU Exam Tip):** Think of adhesion as **"Contact + Compliance + Surface Energy."** Increase any of the three, and grip strength increases. Reduce any, and the object slips.

## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Adhesive Gripping

An adhesive grasp event can be decomposed into the following structured sequence:

1. **Approach phase** — The gripper's adhesive pad is positioned a few millimetres above the target surface under precision motion control.
2. **Pre-load phase** — A small normal (perpendicular) force $F_N$ is applied to push the pad into conformal (intimate) contact with the surface. This pre-load is what *engages* the molecular-level contact.
3. **Engagement phase** — Over a dwell time $t_d$ (typically 0.1 s – 2 s), the contact area grows, allowing adhesive forces to develop fully.
4. **Lift phase** — The end-effector retracts along the normal axis. The adhesive force $F_{adh}$ must exceed the object's weight (plus any inertial loading).
5. **Release phase** — Adhesion is deliberately weakened by (a) applying a **peeling** displacement (peel is geometrically unstable, releasing energy gradually), (b) **reversing polarity** (in electroadhesion), or (c) **active retraction** of the pad.

### 2.2 Classification of Adhesive Grippers

| Type | Adhesion Mechanism | Typical Materials | Power Required |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Biomimetic / Gecko-inspired** | van der Waals (dry adhesion) | PDMS, polyurethane, silicone microfiber arrays | None (passive) |
| **Electroadhesive** | Electrostatic (Coulomb) | Compliant electrodes + dielectric film | High voltage, low current (kV, mA) |
| **Capillary / Wet Adhesion** | Surface tension, Laplace pressure | Thin liquid film, hydrogel | None |
| **Pressure-sensitive (tacky polymer)** | Viscoelastic contact | Acrylic, rubber-based films | None |
| **Hybrid (Electro + Gecko)** | Combined electrostatic + van der Waals | Multi-layer composites | Low voltage |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table compiles **every governing equation** you are expected to know for adhesive gripper problems in the KTU 2024 ESE. Memorize the units and the boundary conditions.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Key Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **JKR Pull-off Force** (soft, large contact) | $F_{adh}^{JKR} = \dfrac{3}{2}\,\pi R\,W$ | $\lambda \gg 1$ (soft + adhesive) |
| 2 | **DMT Pull-off Force** (hard, small contact) | $F_{adh}^{DMT} = 2\,\pi R\,W$ | $\lambda \ll 1$ (rigid, low adhesion) |
| 3 | **Tabor parameter** | $\lambda = \left(\dfrac{16 R W^{2}}{9 \pi^{2} K^{2}}\right)^{1/3}$ | Decides JKR vs DMT |
| 4 | **Equivalent modulus** | $\dfrac{1}{E^{*}} = \dfrac{1-\nu_{1}^{2}}{E_{1}} + \dfrac{1-\nu_{2}^{2}}{E_{2}}$ | For sphere-on-flat contact |
| 5 | **Contact stiffness** | $K = \dfrac{4}{3}\,E^{*}\,R^{1/2}$ | Hertz/JKR framework |
| 6 | **Capillary adhesion force** (sphere-flat) | $F_{cap} = 4\pi R\,\gamma\,\cos\theta$ | $\theta$ = contact angle |
| 7 | **Electroadhesive force** | $F_{EA} = \dfrac{1}{2}\,\varepsilon_{0}\,A\,\dfrac{V^{2}}{d^{2}}$ | Parallel plate model |
| 8 | **Adhesion safety condition** (lifting) | $F_{adh} \ge n\,(mg + ma)$ | $n$ = safety factor $\mathbf{1.5}$ – $\mathbf{2.5}$ |
| 9 | **Effective adhesion pressure** (fibrillar) | $p_{adh} = \dfrac{F_{adh}}{A_{contact}}$ | Compare to object's $\rho g h$ |
| 10 | **Peel-release geometry** | $F_{peel} = W \cdot b$ (per unit width $b$) | Geometrically unstable; used to *release* |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU rule:** In the formula table, vertical bars (such as in absolute value $\lvert x \rvert$) must be typeset using $\lvert$ and $\rvert$ to avoid breaking Markdown table rendering.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Semiconductor industry:** Silicon wafer handling — adhesive grippers avoid contamination from vacuum pump oils and particle generation.
- **Solar PV manufacturing:** Thin glass substrates (1–3 mm) are too fragile for mechanical jaws; gecko-inspired grippers carry them safely.
- **Space robotics (NASA, ISRO):** Adhesive grippers operate without atmosphere (vacuum-stable) and are used for **satellite repair and debris capture** (e.g., NASA gecko-inspired end-effector on the ISS).
- **Climbing robots:** Used for inspection of glass façades, wind turbine blades, and aircraft fuselages.
- **Pick-and-place in cleanrooms:** Eliminates outgassing, particle shedding, and air turbulence of vacuum systems.

---

### 2.5 The "Why & How" Behind Each Step

| Step | Why it matters | How it is implemented |
|---|---|---|
| Pre-load | Without intimate contact, adhesion force collapses. | Servo-controlled end-effector force; typically $0.5$ – $5\ \text{N}$. |
| Dwell time | Adhesive contact is **viscoelastic**; the area grows with time. | Pause 0.1 – 2 s; longer for high-roughness surfaces. |
| Peel-release | Peeling reduces effective force drastically (geometric instability). | Roll-up, lever-arm retraction, or active cam. |
| Directional design | Setae/spatulae have anisotropic geometry: strong in shear/normal, weak in peel. | Mushroom-shaped or tilted fiber tips (load-direction optimized). |
