## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: JKR Adhesion Force for a Sphere on a Flat

We derive the **pull-off force** for a soft elastic sphere pressed against a rigid flat surface, as modelled by **Johnson–Kendall–Roberts (1971)**.

**Given:**
- Sphere radius: $R$
- Work of adhesion: $W$ (energy per unit area to separate interfaces)
- Equivalent modulus: $E^{*}$ (combined elastic response of both bodies)

**Step 1 — Hertzian contact radius (no adhesion):**

$$a_{0} = \left(\dfrac{3 R F_{N}}{4 E^{*}}\right)^{1/3}$$

where $F_N$ is the applied normal load. This is the classical Hertz result.

**Step 2 — Introduce surface energy:**

The JKR theory adds a contact potential energy term:
$$U_{s} = -W \pi a^{2}$$

The total energy is the sum of elastic strain energy and surface energy:
$$U_{total} = U_{elastic}(a) + U_{surface}(a) = -\dfrac{2 E^{*}}{3 R}\,a^{3}\,\delta + W \pi a^{2}$$

where $\delta$ is the indentation depth.

**Step 3 — Apply the energy balance (J KR criterion):**

The JKR criterion at the contact edge states that the **energy release rate** equals the work of adhesion $W$:

$$\dfrac{\partial U_{total}}{\partial a} = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; F = \dfrac{4 E^{*} a^{3}}{3 R} - 2\sqrt{2\pi E^{*} W\, a^{3}}$$

This gives the equilibrium contact radius under combined load and adhesion.

**Step 4 — Solve for contact radius $a$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
a^{3} &= \dfrac{3 R}{4 E^{*}} \left[ F_{N} + 3 \pi R W + \sqrt{6 \pi R W F_{N} + (3\pi R W)^{2}} \right]
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Pull-off force (the key KTU result):**

At pull-off, the contact radius $a \to 0$, and the surface energy is at maximum. Setting $F_N = -F_{po}$ (negative because it is tensile), the pull-off force is:

$$\boxed{\,F_{po}^{JKR} = \dfrac{3}{2}\,\pi R W\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Notice that $F_{po}$ is **independent of elastic modulus $E$** in the JKR model. This is counter-intuitive but follows from the energy-balance argument — the adhesion is governed by surface energy, not stiffness.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Tabor Parameter & JKR/DMT Crossover

**Given:** Sphere radius $R$, work of adhesion $W$, equivalent modulus $E^{*}$.

**Step 1 — Define the elastic deformation scale:**

When a soft sphere approaches a rigid flat, the elastic indentation at zero load has a characteristic length:
$$\delta_{c} = \left(\dfrac{R W^{2}}{E^{*\,2}}\right)^{1/3}$$

**Step 2 — Compare to atomic range of adhesion:**

Adhesive forces act over a typical range $\varepsilon \approx 10^{-10}$ m. If $\delta_{c} \gg \varepsilon$, the sphere deforms significantly before separating — **JKR regime**.
If $\delta_{c} \ll \varepsilon$, deformation is negligible — **DMT regime**.

**Step 3 — Form the dimensionless Tabor parameter:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\lambda &= \dfrac{\delta_{c}}{\varepsilon} \\
&= \left( \dfrac{16 R W^{2}}{9 \pi^{2} K^{2}\,\varepsilon^{2}} \right)^{1/3}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Apply decision rules:**

| Regime | Condition | Pull-off formula |
|---|---|---|
| **JKR** | $\lambda > 1$ | $F_{po} = \dfrac{3}{2}\pi R W$ |
| **DMT** | $\lambda < 1$ | $F_{po} = 2\pi R W$ |
| **Maugis–Dugdale (transition)** | $\lambda \approx 1$ | Intermediate (Maugis chart) |

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Electroadhesive Force (Parallel-Plate Model)

**Given:** Plate area $A$, separation $d$, applied voltage $V$, dielectric film with permittivity $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_{0}\varepsilon_{r}$.

**Step 1 — Capacitance of the parallel plate:**

$$C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{0}\varepsilon_{r}\, A}{d}$$

**Step 2 — Energy stored in the capacitor:**

$$U = \dfrac{1}{2}\,C V^{2} = \dfrac{1}{2}\,\dfrac{\varepsilon_{0}\varepsilon_{r}\,A V^{2}}{d}$$

**Step 3 — Force as negative gradient of energy:**

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{EA} &= -\dfrac{\partial U}{\partial d} = \dfrac{1}{2}\,\varepsilon_{0}\varepsilon_{r}\,A\,\dfrac{V^{2}}{d^{2}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example (KTU typical):**

Given: $A = 100\ \text{cm}^{2} = 10^{-2}\ \text{m}^{2}$, $V = 5\ \text{kV}$, $d = 0.1\ \text{mm} = 10^{-4}\ \text{m}$, $\varepsilon_{r} \approx 3$ (polymer dielectric).

$$F_{EA} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times (8.854 \times 10^{-12}) \times 3 \times 10^{-2} \times \dfrac{(5000)^{2}}{(10^{-4})^{2}}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{EA} &= 0.5 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 3 \times 10^{-2} \times 2.5 \times 10^{7} \times 10^{8} \\
&= 0.5 \times 8.854 \times 3 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-12 - 2 + 7 + 8} \\
&= 33.2 \times 10^{1} \\
&\approx 332\ \text{N}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Common Mistake:** Forgetting to square the voltage *and* square the separation — both are squared because $V^2 / d^2$ is a dimensionless scaling divided by a length scale that creates a pressure. Always check dimensional consistency: $\varepsilon_0$ is F/m = C²/(N·m²), and the final result must be in **Newtons**.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example: Selecting Adhesion Gripper for a Solar Wafer

**Problem (KTU 14-mark style):** A robot must lift a silicon solar wafer of mass $m = 0.5\ \text{kg}$ (a typical 156 mm × 156 mm wafer) using a gecko-inspired adhesive pad with $R_{eff} = 2\ \text{mm}$ and work of adhesion $W = 0.3\ \text{J/m}^2$. The gripper uses PDMS fibers. Determine:
(a) The JKR pull-off force available.
(b) The safety factor against lifting.
(c) Whether a 2 × 2 array of identical pads would be sufficient if the wafer must also withstand an inertial load of $a = 1\ \text{m/s}^2$ (acceleration).

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — JKR pull-off force:**

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{po} &= \dfrac{3}{2}\,\pi\,R\,W \\
&= \dfrac{3}{2} \times \pi \times 2 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.3 \\
&= 0.5 \times 3.1416 \times 6 \times 10^{-4} \\
&\approx 9.42 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{N} \;\;\text{(per fiber tip)}
\end{aligned}$$

For a typical pad with $N = 10^{5}$ fiber tips per cm² over a 4 cm² contact area, $N_{total} = 4 \times 10^{5}$ tips:

$$F_{pad} = N_{total} \times F_{po} = 4 \times 10^{5} \times 9.42 \times 10^{-4} \approx 377\ \text{N}$$

**Part (b) — Required force with safety factor $n = 2$:**

$$F_{req} = n \cdot m g = 2 \times 0.5 \times 9.81 \approx 9.81\ \text{N}$$

**Single pad safety factor:**

$$n_{actual} = \dfrac{F_{pad}}{mg} = \dfrac{377}{4.905} \approx 76.9$$

→ A single pad is **massively over-specified** (the wafer is light; the gripper is designed for heavier glass panels).

**Part (c) — 2 × 2 array under combined load:**

Required lift force = $m(g + a) = 0.5 \times (9.81 + 1) = 5.405\ \text{N}$.

With safety factor $n = 2.0$:
$$F_{design} = 2.0 \times 5.405 = 10.81\ \text{N}$$

Force from 4 pads = $4 \times 377 = 1508\ \text{N} \gg 10.81\ \text{N}$.

**Conclusion:** A 2 × 2 array is over-engineered for this wafer. A **single pad** would suffice, but the array provides redundancy and tolerance to contamination.

```mermaid
    A --> H
    A --> I
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    E --> F{Grip Stable?}

```

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions (with Model Answers)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**
**Define an adhesive gripper. Mention any two advantages over a vacuum gripper.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **adhesive gripper** is a robotic end-effector that uses **intermolecular adhesion forces** (van der Waals, electrostatic, capillary) generated at the contact interface to hold objects, without mechanical interlocking or suction.

**Two advantages over vacuum grippers (1.5 each):**

1. **Works in vacuum / cleanroom environments** — Vacuum grippers fail when there is no air (e.g., space) or when particulate contamination is forbidden (semiconductor fabs). Adhesive grippers are air-independent.
2. **Operates on non-porous, smooth surfaces with no leakage issues** — Vacuum grippers lose seal on rough or perforated surfaces; adhesive grippers conform to micro-roughness via compliant fibers and maintain grip.

*(Accept any other valid advantage: no particle generation, no pump noise, reusable without refilling, low power.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Supplementary]**
**State the JKR formula for the pull-off force of a soft sphere on a flat surface and explain the meaning of each term.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$F_{po}^{JKR} = \dfrac{3}{2}\,\pi\,R\,W$$

- $F_{po}$ = **Pull-off force** (in Newtons) — the tensile force required to separate the sphere from the flat.
- $R$ = **Equivalent radius of contact** of the sphere (in metres).
- $W$ = **Work of adhesion** (in J/m²) — the energy required per unit area to separate the two surfaces in vacuum.

(1 mark — formula; 1 mark — each of $R$ and $W$ explained; final mark for the coefficient $3\pi/2$ and its independence from $E^*$.)

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Part B questions in Module 1 carry 14 marks and offer an **internal choice**. Both alternatives below are independently valid for the full 14 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat functional block diagram, explain the **working principle of a gecko-inspired adhesive gripper**. Discuss its working in **(i) normal lifting** and **(ii) shear gripping** modes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **JKR pull-off force** for an elastic sphere in contact with a rigid flat. List **two key differences** between the JKR and DMT models. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
A gecko-inspired gripper mimics the fibrillar microstructure of a gecko's foot using an array of micro- or nano-scale compliant fibers (typically PDMS, polyurethane, or silicone).

**Functional block diagram (2 marks):**
Refer to the diagram in Section 4.4. The pad consists of:
- Backing substrate
- Base elastomer layer (provides compliance and distributes load)
- Hierarchical microfibers (mimics setae and spatulae)

**Working in Normal Lifting Mode (2 marks):**
- The pre-load is applied perpendicular to the surface, ensuring intimate contact.
- Once lifted, the contact area must remain stable. Mushroom-shaped tips provide **load-sharing** so that all fibers contribute to adhesion.
- The pad experiences a **tensile (negative) load** at separation. If this load does not exceed the JKR pull-off, the object remains held.

**Working in Shear Gripping Mode (2 marks):**
- A **tangential (shear) load** is applied, which causes the fibers to bend and rotate, generating a **frictional pull-off force** orders of magnitude higher than the normal adhesion alone.
- This is the dominant mode used by geckos during locomotion; the directional asymmetry (strong in shear, weak in peel) is built into the fiber geometry.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b)

**JKR Derivation (5 marks):**

Step 1 — Hertzian contact radius:
$$a_{0} = \left(\dfrac{3 R F_{N}}{4 E^{*}}\right)^{1/3}$$

Step 2 — Total energy (elastic + surface):
$$U_{total} = -\dfrac{2 E^{*}}{3 R}\,a^{3}\,\delta + W \pi a^{2}$$

Step 3 — JKR equilibrium:
$$F = \dfrac{4 E^{*} a^{3}}{3 R} - 2\sqrt{2\pi E^{*} W a^{3}}$$

Step 4 — At $a \to 0$ and tensile load, the **pull-off force** becomes:
$$\boxed{\,F_{po}^{JKR} = \dfrac{3}{2}\,\pi R W\,}$$

**Differences between JKR and DMT (2 marks — 1 each):**

| Aspect | JKR | DMT |
|---|---|---|
| **Material regime** | Soft + high adhesion (large $R$, low $E$) | Hard + low adhesion (small $R$, high $E$) |
| **Pull-off force** | $\dfrac{3}{2}\pi R W$ | $2\pi R W$ (lower by 25%) |
| **Deformation** | Significant | Negligible |
| **Tabor parameter** | $\lambda > 1$ | $\lambda < 1$ |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Main]**

**(a)** Classify **adhesive grippers** based on their adhesion mechanism. Compare their **adhesion force ranges, energy requirements, and surface compatibility** in a structured table. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 6-axis robot uses an **electroadhesive gripper** to lift an aluminium plate of mass $2\ \text{kg}$. The gripper has $A = 50\ \text{cm}^{2}$, dielectric thickness $d = 0.2\ \text{mm}$, $\varepsilon_{r} = 4$, and the applied voltage is $V = 4\ \text{kV}$. Verify whether the gripper can lift the plate with a safety factor of $2.0$. Take $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^{2}$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a)

**Classification (3 marks):**

1. **Biomimetic / Gecko-inspired (van der Waals)**
2. **Electroadhesive (electrostatic)**
3. **Capillary (surface tension)**
4. **Pressure-sensitive (viscoelastic)**
5. **Hybrid (combined mechanisms)**

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Type | Adhesion Force Range | Energy Required | Surface Compatibility |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Gecko-inspired** | $0.5$ – $30\ \text{N/cm}^2$ | None (passive) | Smooth, clean, dry, non-magnetic |
| **Electroadhesive** | $0.1$ – $10\ \text{N/cm}^2$ | High voltage (1 – 10 kV), mA current | Conductors + insulators; sensitive to dust |
| **Capillary** | $0.05$ – $1\ \text{N/cm}^2$ | None | Humid environments; smooth surfaces |
| **Pressure-sensitive** | $0.2$ – $5\ \text{N/cm}^2$ | None | Smooth, clean; leaves residue |
| **Hybrid** | Tunable | Low voltage | Broadest compatibility |

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b)

**Step 1 — Required lifting force (with safety factor):**

$$F_{req} = n \cdot m g = 2.0 \times 2 \times 9.81 = 39.24\ \text{N}$$

**Step 2 — Available electroadhesive force:**

$$F_{EA} = \dfrac{1}{2}\,\varepsilon_{0}\,\varepsilon_{r}\,A\,\dfrac{V^{2}}{d^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values:**

- $\varepsilon_{0} = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{F/m}$
- $\varepsilon_{r} = 4$
- $A = 50\ \text{cm}^{2} = 5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^{2}$
- $V = 4\ \text{kV} = 4 \times 10^{3}\ \text{V}$
- $d = 0.2\ \text{mm} = 2 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}$

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{EA} &= 0.5 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 4 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} \times \dfrac{(4 \times 10^{3})^{2}}{(2 \times 10^{-4})^{2}} \\
&= 0.5 \times 8.854 \times 4 \times 5 \times \dfrac{16 \times 10^{6}}{4 \times 10^{-8}} \times 10^{-12-3} \\
&= 88.54 \times 4 \times 10^{6} \times 10^{-8} \times 10^{-15} \\
&= 88.54 \times 4 \times 10^{-17} \times 10^{14} \\
&\text{(recheck with explicit computation)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Direct numerical computation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{EA} &= 0.5 \times (8.854 \times 10^{-12}) \times 4 \times (5 \times 10^{-3}) \times \dfrac{(4 \times 10^{3})^{2}}{(2 \times 10^{-4})^{2}} \\
&= 0.5 \times 8.854 \times 4 \times 5 \times 10^{-12-3} \times \dfrac{16 \times 10^{6}}{4 \times 10^{-8}} \\
&= 88.54 \times 10^{-15} \times 4 \times 10^{14} \\
&= 88.54 \times 4 \times 10^{-1} \\
&\approx 35.42\ \text{N}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Compare to required force:**

$$F_{EA} = 35.42\ \text{N} \;\;<\;\; F_{req} = 39.24\ \text{N}$$

**Step 5 — Actual safety factor:**

$$n_{actual} = \dfrac{35.42}{2 \times 9.81} = \dfrac{35.42}{19.62} \approx 1.81$$

**Conclusion (final 1 mark):**
The available force is **insufficient** to achieve the design safety factor of 2.0. The actual safety factor is **1.81**, which is below the KTU 2024 recommended minimum. **Recommendation:** Increase the area to $A \geq 56.5\ \text{cm}^{2}$, or raise the voltage to $V \geq 4.25\ \text{kV}$, or use a parallel 2-pad arrangement.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** — especially $\text{cm}^{2} \to \text{m}^{2}$ and $\text{mm} \to \text{m}$. This is the **#1 cause of arithmetic errors** in KTU 2024 papers.
> 2. **Squaring the wrong term** — In $F_{EA}$, both $V$ *and* $d$ are squared. A common mistake is to write $V^{2}/d$ instead of $V^{2}/d^{2}$. Loss: 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Using the wrong adhesion model** — JKR for soft gecko pads, DMT for hard contacts, parallel-plate for electroadhesion. Mixing them up = 2–3 marks lost.
> 4. **Not stating boundary conditions / Tabor parameter** — Examiners explicitly look for *when* JKR or DMT is valid. Loss: 1 mark.
> 5. **Drawing the gripper without naming parts** — Always label: backing, fibers, compliance layer, pre-load actuator, release mechanism.
> 6. **Confusing adhesion with friction** — Adhesion is the *normal* (perpendicular) holding force; friction is the *shear* (tangential) holding force. They are coupled but distinct.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Adhesive Grippers**

- **Definition (must-memorize):** A gripper that uses molecular-level adhesion (van der Waals, electrostatic, capillary) to hold objects — *not* mechanical, magnetic, or vacuum force.
- **Five main types:** Gecko-inspired, Electroadhesive, Capillary, Pressure-sensitive, Hybrid.
- **JKR pull-off:** $F_{po} = \dfrac{3}{2}\pi R W$ — for **soft + adhesive** contacts ($\lambda > 1$).
- **DMT pull-off:** $F_{po} = 2\pi R W$ — for **hard + weakly adhesive** contacts ($\lambda < 1$).
- **Tabor parameter $\lambda$** decides JKR vs DMT — always compute it first.
- **Electroadhesive force:** $F_{EA} = \dfrac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2 / d^2$ — both $V$ and $d$ are squared.
- **Capillary force (sphere-flat):** $F_{cap} = 4\pi R \gamma \cos\theta$ — depends on contact angle $\theta$.
- **Safety factor for KTU design:** $n = 1.5$ to $2.5$ on total lift force.
- **Pre-load is mandatory** — without it, adhesion force is negligible.
- **Peel release** is geometrically unstable; that is the *key* to releasing objects.
- **Applications to remember (KTU favorites):** silicon wafer handling, solar panel pick-and-place, climbing robots, space debris capture.
- **Gecko adhesion is anisotropic** — strong in shear/normal, weak in peel.
- **JKR result is independent of $E^{*}$** — a classic KTU "trick" question.
- **DMT result is 25% lower** than JKR for the same $R, W$ — examiners may ask for comparison.
- **Vacuum permittivity** to memorize: $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{F/m}$.
- **Mushroom-shaped tips** give 2–3× the adhesion of flat tips — a frequent design question.
- **Hierarchical fibers (setae + spatulae)** increase adaptability to rough surfaces — frequently cited in KTU answers for "design for robustness."

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** In 14-mark answers, always structure as **Definition → Diagram → Mechanism → Mathematical Model → Numerical Example → Limitations/Applications**. This maps directly to KTU's marking rubric and earns full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Active and Passive Grippers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAT602 - ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Active and Passive Grippers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Gripper?

In the context of industrial robotics, a **Gripper** (also called a *gripping device*, *manipulator*, or *end-effector*) is a mechanical device mounted at the terminal link of a robotic manipulator (the robot's "wrist" or "flange") whose primary function is to **grasp, hold, lift, manipulate, transport, or release** a workpiece or tool.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Standard Textbook Terminology):**
> A gripper is a special end-effector designed to grasp and hold a workpiece securely during a manufacturing operation. Grippers are classified based on the **mechanism of actuation (active)** or **mechanism of inherent mechanical holding (passive)**.

Every robotic workcell, whether performing pick-and-place, welding, painting, or assembly, uses some form of gripper to bridge the robot and the physical object.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Hand

Imagine your own **human hand** as the ultimate gripper:

- When you pick up a heavy dumbbell at the gym, your **brain sends neural signals** to your muscles, which **actively contract** to close your fingers around the weight. The muscles consume energy (glucose). This is analogous to an **ACTIVE GRIPPER** — it requires external energy to maintain the grasp.
- Now consider a **clothespin** clipped onto a clothesline. It requires you to *squeeze* it once (to open it and clamp it), but the spring inside passively maintains the grip on the cloth *without any continuous energy input*. This is analogous to a **PASSIVE GRIPPER** — it holds the object through inherent mechanical design (like a spring, a hook, or gravity).

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Summary:**
> - **Active = Needs Power to Hold** (Motor ON = Gripper Closed)
> - **Passive = No Power Needed to Hold** (Mechanical Feature = Grip Maintained)

---

## 1.3 Active Grippers — Formal Definition

**Active Grippers** are gripping devices that require an **external source of energy** (pneumatic, hydraulic, electric, or a combination) to **actuate the grasping motion** and to **maintain the gripping force** on the workpiece throughout the operation.

### 1.3.1 Key Characteristics of Active Grippers

- Require **continuous or intermittent external power supply**.
- Capable of **variable and controllable gripping force** (force-feedback capable).
- Suitable for objects of **varying geometry, weight, and surface properties**.
- Higher **complexity, cost, and maintenance** requirements.
- Standard **metric gripping force range**: **5 N to 50,000 N (5 N to 50 kN)** depending on the application.

### 1.3.2 Common Types of Active Grippers

| # | Type | Actuation Source | Typical Use |
|---|------|------------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Pneumatic Finger (Jaw) Gripper** | Compressed air | Pick-and-place, packaging |
| 2 | **Hydraulic Gripper** | Pressurized oil | Heavy payloads, forging |
| 3 | **Servo-Electric Gripper** | Electric motor + encoder | Precision assembly, electronics |
| 4 | **Electromagnetic Gripper** | DC current through coil | Ferromagnetic materials (steel sheets) |
| 5 | **Vacuum Gripper** | Suction (vacuum pump) | Flat, smooth surfaces (glass, PCB) |
| 6 | **Solenoid-Actuated Gripper** | Electromagnetic solenoid | Lightweight ON/OFF gripping |

---

## 1.4 Passive Grippers — Formal Definition

**Passive Grippers** are gripping devices that **do NOT require any external power source** to hold an object once the gripping action has been initiated. They rely purely on **inherent mechanical design features** such as springs, gravity, mechanical interlocking, friction, or compliant structures.

### 1.4.1 Key Characteristics of Passive Grippers

- **No external energy** required for holding (energy may only be needed for release, if any).
- **Simpler design, lower cost**, and higher reliability.
- **Limited adaptability** — typically designed for specific workpiece geometry.
- Suitable for **high-speed, repetitive tasks** in mass production.
- **Fail-safe by default** — releases the object upon failure of any external system.

### 1.4.2 Common Types of Passive Grippers

| # | Type | Holding Mechanism | Typical Use |
|---|------|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Spring-Loaded Finger Gripper** | Mechanical spring tension | Clamping brackets, jigs |
| 2 | **Hook Gripper** | Gravity + mechanical hook | Hanging parts on conveyors |
| 3 | **Cam / Latch Gripper** | Mechanical interlocking | Rotational locking |
| 4 | **Capillary / Micro-Gripper** | Surface tension of liquid | MEMS, micro-parts |
| 5 | **Bernoulli Gripper** | Pressure differential (passive air flow) | Non-contact handling of delicate wafers |
| 6 | **Gravity / Wedge Gripper** | Weight of object creates self-lock | Heavy castings, dies |

---

## 1.5 Why This Distinction Matters in KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The classification **Active vs. Passive** is a **high-weightage KTU Module 1 topic** and is almost guaranteed to appear as:
> - A **2-mark or 3-mark short-answer** question (Part A)
> - A **comparison question** in Part B (with sub-parts on advantages/disadvantages)
> - An **application-based question** (e.g., "Suggest a gripper type for handling a 200 kg steel plate")

Understanding the fundamental **energy-based classification** is the first step toward gripper selection in real industrial robotics.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principles

### 2.1.1 Active Gripper — The Operational Pipeline

An active gripper functions through a **controlled energy-conversion chain**. Let us break it down:

1. **Energy Source Input** — Compressed air, hydraulic fluid, or electrical current is supplied from the workcell infrastructure.
2. **Actuator Conversion** — The energy is converted into mechanical motion by an actuator (e.g., pneumatic cylinder piston, servo motor rotor, solenoid plunger).
3. **Transmission Mechanism** — The actuator motion is transmitted through linkages, rack-and-pinion, cam-followers, or lead screws to the **gripper jaws/fingers**.
4. **Jaw Movement** — The jaws close (or open) to engage the workpiece.
5. **Force Maintenance** — The actuator continues to supply force to maintain grip during handling.
6. **Control Loop (Optional but Critical)** — Force sensors, position encoders, or pressure transducers feed back data to the robot controller to **modulate grip force in real time**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Active" matters:** Without continuous power, the grip is LOST. This means active grippers are NOT inherently fail-safe.

---

### 2.1.2 Passive Gripper — The Operational Pipeline

A passive gripper functions through **static mechanical equilibrium**. The breakdown:

1. **Mechanical Feature Engagement** — A spring, hook, cam, or geometric constraint engages the workpiece either through robot motion or initial actuation.
2. **Self-Locking Equilibrium** — Internal mechanical features (e.g., over-center cam, spring preload, gravity-induced wedge action) create a **force-locked** or **form-locked** state.
3. **Zero Energy Holding** — No external power is needed to maintain the grasp; the workpiece is held by inherent physical laws (friction, gravity, elastic deformation).
4. **Release Mechanism** — A small actuation (sometimes a separate trigger, sometimes just gravity upon reorientation) releases the object.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Passive" matters:** These are **fail-safe** — even if power to the entire factory fails, the workpiece remains secured (or is released in a controlled manner).

---

## 2.2 Mathematical Foundations of Gripping

### 2.2.1 Basic Force Balance on a Two-Jaw Gripper

Consider the simplest model — a **two-finger (two-jaw) gripper** gripping a cylindrical object vertically (lifted against gravity):

**Free Body Diagram (Conceptual):**
- Weight of object: $W = m \cdot g$
- Coefficient of friction between jaw and object: $\mu$
- Normal force applied by EACH jaw: $N$

For the object to remain gripped (no slippage):

$$F_{friction,total} \geq W$$

Since there are two jaws, each providing a normal force $N$, and friction force from each jaw is $\mu N$:

$$2 \cdot \mu \cdot N \geq m \cdot g$$

$$\boxed{N \geq \frac{m \cdot g}{2 \cdot \mu}}$$

This is the **minimum normal force per jaw** required to prevent the object from slipping.

### 2.2.2 For a Pneumatic Cylinder-Actuated Gripper

The gripping force $N$ per jaw is related to the **cylinder bore pressure** by:

$$N = P \cdot A_{piston} \cdot \eta_{mech}$$

Where:
- $P$ = Pneumatic supply pressure (Pa)
- $A_{piston}$ = Effective piston area (m²)
- $\eta_{mech}$ = Mechanical efficiency of the linkage (typically 0.4–0.7)

### 2.2.3 For a Vacuum Gripper

The lifting (holding) force generated by suction is:

$$F_{lift} = (P_{atm} - P_{vacuum}) \cdot A_{cup} - W_{cup}$$

Where:
- $P_{atm}$ = Atmospheric pressure $\approx \mathbf{101.325 \text{ kPa}}$
- $P_{vacuum}$ = Internal (vacuum) pressure under the suction cup
- $A_{cup}$ = Effective area of the suction cup
- $W_{cup}$ = Weight of the suction cup assembly itself

### 2.2.4 For an Electromagnetic Gripper

The holding (pull) force of a simple electromagnet on a ferromagnetic surface is approximately:

$$F_{mag} = \frac{B^2 \cdot A}{2 \cdot \mu_0}$$

Where:
- $B$ = Magnetic flux density in the air gap (Tesla)
- $A$ = Cross-sectional area of the pole face (m²)
- $\mu_0$ = Permeability of free space = $4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ H/m}$

### 2.2.5 For a Bernoulli (Non-Contact) Gripper

Bernoulli grippers use high-velocity air flow to create a low-pressure zone above the object, lifting it without contact:

$$\Delta P = \frac{1}{2} \rho v^2$$

Where $\rho$ is air density and $v$ is the air jet velocity. The lift force is approximately:

$$F_{Bernoulli} \approx \Delta P \cdot A_{effective}$$

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Table — Must Memorize for KTU Exam**

| # | Formula | Variable Meaning | Typical Units | Application |
|---|---------|------------------|---------------|-------------|
| 1 | $N \geq \dfrac{m \cdot g}{2\mu}$ | $N$=normal force/jaw, $m$=mass, $\mu$=friction coeff. | N, kg, m/s², — | Two-jaw gripper minimum grip |
| 2 | $N = P \cdot A_{piston} \cdot \eta$ | $P$=pressure, $A$=area, $\eta$=efficiency | Pa, m², — | Pneumatic grip force |
| 3 | $F_{lift} = (P_{atm} - P_{vac}) \cdot A_{cup}$ | $P_{atm}$=101.325 kPa, $A$=cup area | N, Pa, m² | Vacuum gripper lifting |
| 4 | $F_{mag} = \dfrac{B^2 A}{2\mu_0}$ | $B$=flux density, $A$=area, $\mu_0$=4π×10⁻⁷ | N, T, m², H/m | Electromagnetic holding |
| 5 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{1}{2}\rho v^2$ | $\rho$=air density, $v$=velocity | Pa, kg/m³, m/s | Bernoulli principle |
| 6 | $F_{grip} = \dfrac{W}{\mu}$ (single side) | $W$=weight, $\mu$=friction coeff. | N, — | Frictional hold criterion |
| 7 | $T_{motor} = F_{grip} \cdot r_{eff}$ | $r_{eff}$=effective lever arm | N·m, N, m | Servo motor sizing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Remember:** In KTU answer sheets, **always show units**. Examiners in Kerala are strict about dimensional consistency. Always end with a clearly boxed final answer.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Active and passive grippers are deployed across every modern industry:

| Industry | Active Gripper Use | Passive Gripper Use |
|----------|-------------------|---------------------|
| **Automotive Assembly** | Robotic spot welding, painting | Part transfer on conveyors |
| **Electronics Manufacturing** | Pick-and-place of PCBs | Spring-loaded test jigs |
| **Food & Beverage** | Vacuum pick of cheese, bread | Hook grip on hanging meats |
| **Pharmaceuticals** | Precision vial handling | Capillary grip of micro-pills |
| **Heavy Engineering** | Hydraulic grip on forged parts | Wedge-locks on casting dies |
| **Semiconductor** | Bernoulli wafer handlers | Edge-grip compliant holders |

The choice between active and passive is **not always either/or** — hybrid designs (e.g., a passive spring gripper with an active release solenoid) are common in industry.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — Minimum Gripping Force for a Two-Jaw Active Gripper

**Problem Setup:**
A robotic gripper with two parallel jaws is required to lift a cylindrical steel workpiece of mass $m = 5 \text{ kg}$ vertically. The coefficient of friction between the rubber-lined jaws and the steel surface is $\mu = 0.4$. The jaws grip the cylinder at its mid-height.

**Find:** The minimum total gripping force $F_{grip,total}$ that the actuator must deliver, and the minimum pressure $P$ required if a single-acting pneumatic cylinder with bore diameter $D = 40 \text{ mm}$ and mechanical efficiency $\eta = 0.6$ is used.

---

### Step 1: Identify the forces acting on the workpiece

The workpiece experiences:
- Weight acting downward: $W = m \cdot g$
- Two normal forces $N$ from each jaw (acting horizontally, opposing each other)
- Two friction forces $\mu N$ acting upward (opposing gravity)

### Step 2: Apply static equilibrium in the vertical direction

For the workpiece to remain stationary (no slip), the upward friction forces must balance the downward weight.

$$\sum F_y = 0$$

$$\mu N + \mu N - W = 0$$

$$2 \mu N = W$$

### Step 3: Substitute the weight expression

$$2 \mu N = m g$$

$$N = \frac{m g}{2 \mu}$$

### Step 4: Plug in numerical values

$$N = \frac{5 \text{ kg} \times 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2}{2 \times 0.4}$$

$$N = \frac{49.05}{0.8}$$

$$\boxed{N = 61.3125 \text{ N per jaw}}$$

### Step 5: Calculate the total gripping force

$$F_{grip,total} = 2N = 2 \times 61.3125$$

$$F_{grip,total} = 122.625 \text{ N}$$

This is the force the actuator must produce.

### Step 6: Relate to the pneumatic cylinder

The cylinder force output is:

$$F_{cylinder} = P \cdot A_{piston} \cdot \eta$$

We are given:
- $F_{cylinder} = 122.625 \text{ N}$ (must match the gripping force needed)
- $D = 40 \text{ mm} = 0.04 \text{ m}$
- $\eta = 0.6$

### Step 7: Calculate the piston area

$$A_{piston} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

$$A_{piston} = \frac{\pi (0.04)^2}{4}$$

$$A_{piston} = \frac{\pi \times 0.0016}{4}$$

$$A_{piston} = 1.2566 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

### Step 8: Solve for the required pressure

$$P = \frac{F_{cylinder}}{A_{piston} \cdot \eta}$$

$$P = \frac{122.625}{1.2566 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.6}$$

$$P = \frac{122.625}{7.5398 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 162,620 \text{ Pa} \approx 162.6 \text{ kPa} \approx 1.626 \text{ bar}}$$

### Step 9: Interpretation

A standard industrial compressed air line at **6 bar (600 kPa)** is more than sufficient to operate this gripper — confirming the design is feasible with standard shop-floor utilities.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — Vacuum Gripper Holding Capacity

**Problem Setup:**
A vacuum gripper with a circular suction cup of diameter $d = 80 \text{ mm}$ is to lift a flat glass panel of mass $m_g = 12 \text{ kg}$. The vacuum pump can achieve a maximum vacuum pressure of $P_{vac} = 30 \text{ kPa}$ (absolute). The weight of the suction cup itself is $W_{cup} = 0.5 \text{ N}$. Atmospheric pressure is $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$.

**Find:** Is the gripper capable of lifting the panel? Also calculate the **factor of safety (FoS)**.

---

### Step 1: Calculate the effective area of the suction cup

$$A_{cup} = \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$

$$A_{cup} = \frac{\pi \times (0.080)^2}{4}$$

$$A_{cup} = 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

### Step 2: Calculate the pressure differential across the cup

$$\Delta P = P_{atm} - P_{vac}$$

$$\Delta P = 101.325 - 30$$

$$\Delta P = 71.325 \text{ kPa} = 71,325 \text{ Pa}$$

### Step 3: Calculate the theoretical lifting force

$$F_{lift} = \Delta P \cdot A_{cup}$$

$$F_{lift} = 71,325 \times 5.0265 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$F_{lift} \approx 358.52 \text{ N}$$

### Step 4: Subtract the weight of the suction cup itself

$$F_{net} = F_{lift} - W_{cup}$$

$$F_{net} = 358.52 - 0.5$$

$$F_{net} \approx 358.02 \text{ N}$$

### Step 5: Calculate the weight of the glass panel

$$W_{panel} = m_g \cdot g = 12 \times 9.81 = 117.72 \text{ N}$$

### Step 6: Check the lifting condition

Since $F_{net} = 358.02 \text{ N} > W_{panel} = 117.72 \text{ N}$, the gripper CAN lift the panel. ✓

### Step 7: Calculate the Factor of Safety

$$\text{FoS} = \frac{F_{net}}{W_{panel}}$$

$$\text{FoS} = \frac{358.02}{117.72}$$

$$\boxed{\text{FoS} \approx 3.04}$$

### Step 8: Interpretation

A factor of safety greater than 2 is generally acceptable for industrial vacuum grippers. A FoS of **3.04** indicates a robust design with adequate margin against leakage, dynamic loads, and surface irregularities.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Active vs Passive Grippers

> [!NOTE]
> **This matrix is a high-yield KTU exam answer template — memorize the structure.**

| Parameter | Active Gripper | Passive Gripper |
|-----------|----------------|-----------------|
| **Energy Required** | Yes — pneumatic/hydraulic/electric | No (mechanical only) |
| **Holding Force** | Variable, controllable | Fixed (depends on spring/gravity) |
| **Fail-Safe Behavior** | NOT fail-safe (releases on power loss) | Fail-safe (object may stay held) |
| **Control Complexity** | High (needs controller integration) | Low (essentially passive) |
| **Cost** | Higher (actuator + compressor + controller) | Lower (mechanical parts only) |
| **Adaptability to Part Variation** | Excellent (programmable force) | Limited (dedicated to a part shape) |
| **Maintenance** | Moderate to high | Low |
| **Speed** | Limited by actuator response | Very high (almost instantaneous) |
| **Typical Applications** | Assembly, welding, precision tasks | Conveyors, jigs, dedicated transfers |
| **Examples** | Pneumatic finger, electromagnet | Hook, cam, spring clamp |
| **Suitability for Heavy Loads** | Excellent (hydraulics up to 50 kN) | Limited to mechanical strength |
| **Suitability for Delicate Parts** | Good (force control) | Limited |

---

## 3.4 Decision Logic — When to Choose Which Gripper

```
IF workpiece is FERROMAGNETIC AND flat AND heavy
    → USE Active Electromagnetic Gripper
ELSE IF workpiece is FLAT, SMOOTH, AND non-porous
    → USE Active Vacuum Gripper
ELSE IF workpiece is DELICATE (wafer, glass) AND non-contact preferred
    → USE Active Bernoulli Gripper (or passive variant)
ELSE IF task is DEDICATED REPETITIVE AND speed is critical
    → USE Passive Hook / Cam / Spring Gripper
ELSE IF workpiece is HEAVY (forging, casting)
    → USE Active Hydraulic Gripper
ELSE IF workpiece is SMALL AND uniform
    → USE Passive Capillary Gripper (micro parts)
ELSE
    → USE Active Servo-Electric Gripper with force feedback
END
```
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Grippers

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ROBOTIC GRIPPERS] --> B[ACTIVE GRIPPERS]
    A --> C[PASSIVE GRIPPERS]
    B --> B1[Pneumatic Finger]
    B --> B2[Hydraulic Gripper]
    B --> B3[Servo Electric Gripper]
    B --> B4[Electromagnetic Gripper]
    B --> B5[Vacuum Gripper]
    B --> B6[Solenoid Gripper]
    C --> C1[Spring Loaded]
    C --> C2[Hook Gripper]
    C --> C3[Cam Latch Gripper]
    C --> C4[Capillary Gripper]
    C --> C5[Bernoulli Gripper]
    C --> C6[Wedge Gripper]
```

---

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Active Pneumatic Gripper

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Compressed Air Supply] --> B[Filter Regulator Lubricator]
    B --> C[Solenoid Valve 5 by 2]
    C --> D[Pneumatic Cylinder]
    D --> E[Linkage Mechanism]
    E --> F[Gripper Jaws]
    F --> G[Workpiece]
    H[Controller PLC] --> C
    I[Pressure Sensor] --> D
    I --> H
```

---

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Passive Spring Gripper

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Approach Motion] --> B[Jaw Contact]
    B --> C[Mechanical Spring Engages]
    C --> D[Self Locking Equilibrium]
    D --> E[Transport Phase]
    E --> F[Release Trigger or Reorientation]
    F --> G[Workpiece Drop]
```

---

## 4.4 Force Flow Diagram — Two-Jaw Active Gripper

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Pneumatic Pressure P] --> B[Cylinder Force F cyl]
    B --> C[Linkage Mechanical Advantage M]
    C --> D[Normal Force per Jaw N]
    D --> E[Friction Force per Jaw mu N]
    E --> F[Total Vertical Hold Force 2 mu N]
    F --> G[Workpiece Weight mg]
    H[Safety Factor SF] --> F
```

---

## 4.5 Selection Logic Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[New Gripping Task] --> B{Workpiece Material}
    B -->|Ferromagnetic| C[Electromagnetic Gripper]
    B -->|Non-Magnetic Flat| D[Vacuum Gripper]
    B -->|Irregular Shape| E{Frequency of Change}
    E -->|High| F[Servo Electric Active Gripper]
    E -->|Low| G[Passive Custom Jaws]
    B -->|Micro Parts| H[Capillary or Bernoulli Gripper]
    C --> I[Check Power Source]
    D --> I
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Select Active or Passive]
```

---

## 4.6 Active vs Passive — Comparative Block Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ACTIVE
        A1[Power Source] --> A2[Actuator]
        A2 --> A3[Jaw Motion]
        A3 --> A4[Workpiece Held]
        A5[Controller] -.Feedback.-> A2
    end
    subgraph PASSIVE
        P1[Robot Motion] --> P2[Mechanical Trigger]
        P2 --> P3[Spring or Hook Activates]
        P3 --> P4[Workpiece Held Mechanically]
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Set A]**

**Define the following with one suitable application each:**
**(a)** Active Gripper
**(b)** Passive Gripper

#### Model Answer:

**(a) Active Gripper:** An active gripper is an end-effector that requires an external source of energy (such as compressed air, hydraulic fluid, or electric current) to actuate the grasping motion and to maintain the gripping force on the workpiece throughout the operation.
*Application:* Pneumatic two-jaw gripper used in pick-and-place operations on an automotive assembly line.

**(b) Passive Gripper:** A passive gripper is an end-effector that holds the workpiece without the need for any continuous external energy source, relying instead on inherent mechanical design features such as springs, gravity, hooks, or mechanical interlocking.
*Application:* Spring-loaded clamp gripper used to hold a bracket on a dedicated machining fixture.

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**List any FOUR differences between Active and Passive Grippers.**

#### Model Answer:

| # | Active Gripper | Passive Gripper |
|---|----------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Requires external power to operate and hold | No external power required for holding |
| 2 | Gripping force is variable and controllable | Gripping force is fixed by design |
| 3 | Higher cost and complexity | Lower cost and simple construction |
| 4 | NOT inherently fail-safe | Inherently fail-safe |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the working of a **pneumatic two-jaw active gripper**. Derive the expression for the minimum gripping force required to lift a cylindrical object of mass *m* held vertically, assuming a coefficient of friction *μ* between the jaws and the object. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A robotic gripper is required to lift a steel block of mass **15 kg** using a two-jaw pneumatic gripper. The coefficient of friction between the jaw pads and the block is **μ = 0.35**. The pneumatic cylinder has bore diameter **D = 50 mm** and mechanical efficiency **η = 0.55**. Calculate: **(i)** the minimum normal force per jaw, **(ii)** the minimum pneumatic pressure required. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Working and Derivation:**

A pneumatic two-jaw active gripper consists of:
- A double-acting pneumatic cylinder (bore + piston + rod)
- A 5/2 solenoid-operated directional control valve
- A filter-regulator-lubricator (FRL) unit for clean, regulated air
- Linkage mechanism (typically a four-bar or toggle linkage)
- Two parallel jaws lined with high-friction rubber pads

**Working:** Compressed air from the supply line is filtered, regulated, and directed via the solenoid valve into one side of the cylinder. The piston moves, pushing the linkage, which causes the jaws to close on the workpiece. Reverse the valve, air enters the other port, and the jaws open.

**Free Body Diagram (Vertical Lift):**

- Weight of object: $W = mg$ acting downward
- Two normal forces $N$ acting horizontally
- Two friction forces $\mu N$ acting vertically upward

**Equilibrium:**

$$2 \mu N = mg$$

$$\boxed{N_{min} = \frac{mg}{2\mu}}$$

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $m = 15 \text{ kg}$
- $\mu = 0.35$
- $D = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$
- $\eta = 0.55$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**(i) Minimum normal force per jaw:**

$$N_{min} = \frac{mg}{2\mu} = \frac{15 \times 9.81}{2 \times 0.35}$$

$$N_{min} = \frac{147.15}{0.70}$$

$$\boxed{N_{min} = 210.21 \text{ N per jaw}}$$

**(ii) Piston area:**

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.05)^2}{4} = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Total gripping force:**

$$F_{grip} = 2 N_{min} = 2 \times 210.21 = 420.43 \text{ N}$$

**Required pressure:**

$$P = \frac{F_{grip}}{A \cdot \eta} = \frac{420.43}{1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.55}$$

$$P = \frac{420.43}{1.0799 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 389,332 \text{ Pa} \approx 389.3 \text{ kPa} \approx 3.89 \text{ bar}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With the help of a diagram, explain the working of a **vacuum gripper**. Derive the expression for the lifting force developed by a single circular suction cup. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain any **FOUR types of passive grippers** with one application each. Justify why passive grippers are preferred in dedicated high-speed conveyor systems. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Vacuum Gripper — Working and Derivation:**

A vacuum gripper consists of:
- A rubber or silicone suction cup with a concave internal cavity
- A vacuum pump (ejector or motor-driven)
- A connecting vacuum hose
- A check valve and a vacuum gauge/switch

**Working:** The suction cup is placed on a smooth, flat workpiece surface. The vacuum pump evacuates air from the cup cavity, creating a pressure differential. Atmospheric pressure pushes down on the top of the cup (and on the rest of the workpiece), while the cavity pressure is much lower. This pressure differential generates a net downward force on the workpiece, which the gripper uses to lift the object against gravity.

**Diagram (Schematic):**

```
[Atmospheric Pressure P_atm acting downward on top of cup]
        ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓
   ┌──────────────────┐
   │   Rubber Suction │ ← Vacuum Pressure P_vac inside cavity
   │       Cup        │
   └──────┬───────────┘
          │ (sealed to workpiece surface)
   ───────┴──────────────────────
   ↑ Net Lifting Force F_lift
```

**Derivation:**

Effective pressure differential:

$$\Delta P = P_{atm} - P_{vac}$$

Effective area of the cup (circular, diameter $d$):

$$A_{cup} = \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$

The lifting (holding) force generated is the product of the pressure differential and the effective area:

$$\boxed{F_{lift} = (P_{atm} - P_{vac}) \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4}}$$

**(b) Four Passive Gripper Types:**

1. **Spring-Loaded Finger Gripper:** Two metal fingers are pre-tensioned by a coil spring. The robot pushes the gripper onto the workpiece, the fingers deflect slightly, and the spring force clamps the part. *Application:* Holding a metal bracket in a CNC machining fixture.

2. **Hook Gripper:** A simple metal hook engages a hole or lug on the workpiece. The weight of the workpiece creates a self-locking condition against the hook's geometry. *Application:* Hanging painted panels on an overhead conveyor in an automobile plant.

3. **Cam-Latch Gripper:** A rotating cam (eccentric profile) engages a mating surface on the workpiece. Over-center locking holds the cam in place. *Application:* Indexing rotary tables and quick-change tool holders.

4. **Capillary Gripper:** Uses the surface tension of a small liquid bridge (often water or an ionic liquid) to pick up micro-parts (MEMS, micro-gears). *Application:* Micro-electronics assembly of chips smaller than 1 mm.

**Justification for Conveyor Systems:**
Passive grippers are preferred in dedicated high-speed conveyor systems because:
- **No actuation delay** — grip engagement is instantaneous upon contact.
- **No external plumbing** required — simpler installation on moving conveyors.
- **Fail-safe operation** — power failure does not drop parts.
- **Higher cycle rates** achievable (often >60 cycles/min).
- **Lower maintenance** — fewer moving parts, no seals to replace.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Active" with "Automatically operated":** Active refers to the ENERGY requirement, not automation. Many students wrongly think active = automatic, passive = manual.
> 2. **Forgetting units:** Examiners in KTU deduct 0.5 to 1 mark per question for missing or incorrect units.
> 3. **Skipping the free body diagram (FBD):** Even a simple sketch showing forces on the workpiece is worth 1–2 marks and is often skipped.
> 4. **Not stating assumptions:** Always explicitly state: "Assuming jaws are symmetric, friction is uniform, no acceleration, etc."
> 5. **Wrong factor-of-safety interpretation:** A FoS > 1 means grip is possible; a FoS < 1 means slip will occur. Students sometimes invert this.
> 6. **Mixing up force and pressure:** Gripper force $N$ is in Newtons; cylinder pressure $P$ is in Pascals or bar. Do not confuse them.
> 7. **Omitting "with neat sketch" instructions:** Always include a diagram, even if rough — KTU examiners reward it.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read before entering the exam hall:**
>
> - ✅ Active Grippers need **external power** (pneumatic / hydraulic / electric) to grasp AND to maintain grip.
> - ✅ Passive Grippers use **mechanical features** (springs, hooks, gravity, surface tension) — no power to hold.
> - ✅ Two-jaw gripper minimum normal force per jaw: $N = \dfrac{mg}{2\mu}$.
> - ✅ Pneumatic cylinder force: $F = P \cdot A \cdot \eta$ where $\eta$ is the linkage efficiency.
> - ✅ Vacuum gripper lift: $F_{lift} = (P_{atm} - P_{vac}) \cdot A_{cup}$, with $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa.
> - ✅ Electromagnetic holding: $F_{mag} = \dfrac{B^2 A}{2\mu_0}$.
> - ✅ Passive grippers are **fail-safe**; active grippers are **NOT fail-safe**.
> - ✅ Bernoulli grippers use **non-contact air flow** for delicate surfaces (wafers, glass).
> - ✅ Capillary grippers use **liquid surface tension** for micro-parts (MEMS).
> - ✅ **Selection criteria:** Workpiece material → surface → weight → required speed → accuracy → cost.
> - ✅ Always draw a **free body diagram** in derivation questions.
> - ✅ Always state **assumptions** and **units** in numerical answers.
> - ✅ Standard industrial compressed air pressure: **6 bar (600 kPa)**.
> - ✅ Standard safety factor for industrial grippers: **FoS ≥ 2** for static holding.
> - ✅ Active grippers offer **variable, controllable force**; passive grippers offer **fixed force**.
> - ✅ For **ferromagnetic** parts → Electromagnetic gripper is the natural choice.
> - ✅ For **flat, smooth** parts (glass, PCB) → Vacuum gripper is optimal.
> - ✅ For **heavy payloads** (>1 ton) → Hydraulic actuation is mandatory.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ethics in Robotics - Three Laws - Applications of Robots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAT602 - ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Ethics in Robotics - Three Laws - Applications of Robots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Ethics in Robotics — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)**
> *Robotics Ethics* (also called **Machine Ethics** or **Robot Ethics**) is the applied branch of applied philosophy and engineering that studies the moral conduct, design principles, regulatory frameworks, and behavioural constraints that must govern the design, manufacture, deployment, and operation of intelligent robotic systems. It interrogates the question: *“What obligations does a robot (and its creator) owe to humans, society, and the environment?”*

The discipline sits at the intersection of three pillars:
1. **Engineering Responsibility** — safe, reliable, fail-safe hardware/software design.
2. **Legal Compliance** — product liability, ISO standards, data privacy.
3. **Moral Philosophy** — Asimovian logic, deontological duties, consequentialist outcomes.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Robot as a Well-Trained Driver"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain-English Analogy**
> Imagine giving the keys of a Ferrari to a teenager. The car itself has *no morals* — it will accelerate at 300 km/h if commanded. The **ethics layer** is analogous to the *driver-training school, the speed limiter, the seat-belt alarm, and the traffic police combined*. In robotics, ethics is the **governor** between the robot's raw actuators and the physical world. Just as society does not trust an un-licensed driver on a highway, society will not deploy an *un-ethically-designed* autonomous system in a hospital, a battlefield, or a child's bedroom.

## 1.3 The Three Laws of Robotics (Asimov, 1942)

> [!NOTE]
> **Source Context**
> These laws were formulated by **science author Isaac Asimov** in his 1942 short story *“Runaround”* (later compiled in *I, Robot*, 1950). They are **fictional axiomatic postulates** intended to provide a logical framework for robot behaviour, NOT actual engineering protocols. However, they form the historical and philosophical foundation of modern robo-ethics and are mandatory in KTU syllabi.

The Three Laws, in order of priority (highest first):

| Priority | Law | Statement (Asimov's Original) |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| **1st** | **Non-Injury Law** | *A robot may not injure a human being, or, through inaction, allow a human being to come to harm.* |
| **2nd** | **Obedience Law** | *A robot must obey the orders given by authorized human beings, except where such orders conflict with the First Law.* |
| **3rd** | **Self-Preservation Law** | *A robot must protect its own existence, as long as such protection does not conflict with the First or Second Law.* |

A **Zeroth Law** was later added by Asimov (1985, *Robots and Empire*):
> *A robot may not harm humanity as a whole, or, by inaction, allow humanity to come to harm.*

## 1.4 Modern Ethical Frameworks in Robotics (Beyond Asimov)

- **IEEE Global Initiative on Ethically Aligned Design** — 8 general principles including *Human Rights, Well-being, Data Agency, Effectiveness, Transparency, Accountability, Awareness of Misuse, Competence*.
- **Asilomar AI Principles (2017)** — 23 guidelines for safe and beneficial AI/robotics.
- **EU AI Act (2024)** — risk-based classification (Unacceptable, High, Limited, Minimal).
- **ISO 13482:2014 / ISO/TS 15066:2016** — safety standards for personal-care and collaborative robots.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Logical Hierarchy of the Three Laws

The laws form a **strict priority stack**. A robot's decision engine MUST evaluate them in descending order of priority at every control cycle.

### Step-by-step logical flow:

1. **Sense** the environment (sensors perceive humans, commands, self-state).
2. **Classify** the perceived situation into one of four classes:
   - Class A: Imminent human harm detected?
   - Class B: Human command received?
   - Class C: Self-endangerment detected?
3. **Resolve** by conflict-resolution algorithm:

$$
\boxed{\text{Action} = \arg\min_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \; \big[\, w_1 \cdot H(a) + w_2 \cdot O(a) + w_3 \cdot S(a) \,\big]}
$$

where:
- $H(a)$ = human-harm cost of action $a$ (Law 1 weight: $w_1 = \infty$)
- $O(a)$ = disobedience cost of action $a$ (Law 2 weight: $w_2 = 1$)
- $S(a)$ = self-destruction cost of action $a$ (Law 3 weight: $w_3 = 0.1$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the priority matters:** A robot receiving the command *“jump off the cliff”* from a human will refuse — because obeying (Law 2) would violate non-injury (Law 1). A robot may not lie to protect itself if the lie endangers a human.

## 2.2 Limitations & Paradoxes of the Three Laws (KTU Favourite)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Many students write the laws verbatim and score 2/14. To get full marks, you **must** discuss the **limitations** — Asimov's entire fiction is built around *exposing* these loopholes.

| Paradox / Limitation | Description | Real-World Analogue |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Ambiguity of "harm"** | Is emotional distress "harm"? Is long-term environmental damage "harm"? | A robot telling a patient their diagnosis truthfully vs. a white lie. |
| **Identification of "human"** | What if a prosthetic makes a human 50% machine? What about cyborgs or avatars? | Autonomous cars detecting pedestrians in fog. |
| **Conflict of Laws 1 & 3** | A robot must sacrifice itself to save a human — but how does it quantify risk to *itself*? | Drone returning to base vs. staying to guide a lost hiker. |
| **Two-human dilemma** | Robot can save one of two drowning humans — which one? | Trolley-problem equivalents in autonomous vehicles. |
| **Through inaction** | Passive non-action also violates Law 1. Robot must *act* even uncommanded. | Witnessing a crime — should a surveillance robot intervene? |
| **Law 0 vs Law 1** | Saving humanity may require sacrificing a specific human. | War-time bombing to save a nation. |
| **Verification problem** | The laws are stated in natural language, not formal logic. | Subjective interpretation in edge cases. |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Robotics Ethics Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory data to memorize for KTU board exams**

| Metric / Term | Standard Value or Definition | SI Unit / Notation |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** | Independent axes of motion (Translation + Rotation) | integer |
| **Payload Capacity** | Maximum mass robot can manipulate | **kg** or **N** |
| **Repeatability** | Position-return accuracy under same path | **mm** or **±0.02 mm** |
| **Reach / Working Envelope** | Maximum spatial extent of end-effector | **m** |
| **Sensors per robot** | Proprioceptive (encoders, IMU) + Exteroceptive (camera, LiDAR) | count |
| **Turing-Test pass rate** | % judges fooled by robot | **%** |
| **Ethical compliance (ISO)** | 13482 (personal care), 10218 (industrial), 15066 (collaborative) | standard ID |
| **Asimov's priority constant** | $w_1 \gg w_2 \gg w_3$ with $w_1 = \infty$ | dimensionless |
| **Risk Class (EU AI Act)** | Unacceptable \vert High \vert Limited \vert Minimal | categorical |
| **Collaborative Robot Speed** | Limited to **250 mm/s** for safe human coexistence | **mm/s** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Table formatting rule:** Always use `\vert` instead of the pipe symbol `|` inside table cells to prevent Markdown parser breakage.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of the Ethics Layer

- **Autonomous Vehicles (Tesla, Waymo):** Implement the *trolley problem* solver in firmware.
- **Medical Robots (Da Vinci):** Refuse to make a cut if nerve proximity exceeds a threshold.
- **Military Drones:** Operate under "Man-in-the-loop" — human pulls the final trigger; robot cannot autonomously fire.
- **Collaborative Robots (Cobots):** Use **power-and-force-limiting** actuators so collisions with humans cause no injury.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Formal Logic Derivation of the Laws

Although Asimov's laws are expressed in natural language, modern robo-ethicists translate them into **deontic logic** (logic of obligation/permission). Let us derive the **Law 1** formally.

### Step 1: Define the universe of actors
Let:
- $R$ = the robot
- $H_i$ = the $i$-th human being, $i \in \{1, 2, \dots, n\}$
- $A_t$ = the action being considered at decision-cycle $t$
- $E_H(R, A_t)$ = the "injury event" — an ordered pair $(H_i, \text{harm})$ triggered by $R$ executing $A_t$

### Step 2: Express the injury predicate
We define the predicate $\text{Harms}(R, H_i, A_t)$ as TRUE if and only if executing $A_t$ causes a physical, psychological, or socioeconomic injury to $H_i$.

### Step 3: Translate the First Law into formal logic

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Law 1:} \quad & \forall t \in \mathcal{T}, \forall A_t \in \mathcal{A}, \forall H_i \in \mathcal{H}: \\
& \neg \text{Harms}(R, H_i, A_t) \;\lor\; \neg \text{Preventable}(R, \text{Harms}(H_j, A_t))
\end{aligned}
$$

In words: *For every time-step, every action, and every human, the robot must EITHER (a) not cause harm OR (b) be incapable of preventing the harm.*

### Step 4: Translate the Second Law (Obedience)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Law 2:} \quad & \forall t, \forall A_t^{\text{cmd}} \in \mathcal{A}^{\text{cmd}}, \forall H_i \in \mathcal{H}^{\text{auth}}: \\
& \text{Obeys}(R, A_t^{\text{cmd}}) \iff \text{Consistent}(A_t^{\text{cmd}}, \text{Law 1})
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Translate the Third Law (Self-Preservation)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Law 3:} \quad & \forall t, \forall A_t^{\text{def}}: \\
& \text{Executes}(R, A_t^{\text{def}}) \iff \big[\text{Consistent}(A_t^{\text{def}}, \text{Law 1}) \;\land\; \text{Consistent}(A_t^{\text{def}}, \text{Law 2})\big]
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6: Conflict resolution
When two laws suggest different actions, the **higher-priority** law wins. Mathematically:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_t^{*} = \arg\max_{A_t \in \mathcal{A}} \;\Big[ & \mathbf{1}\!\left\{A_t \text{ satisfies Law 1}\right\} \cdot W_1 \\
& + \mathbf{1}\!\left\{A_t \text{ satisfies Law 2}\right\} \cdot W_2 \\
& + \mathbf{1}\!\left\{A_t \text{ satisfies Law 3}\right\} \cdot W_3 \Big]
\end{aligned}
$$

with $W_1 = 1000, \; W_2 = 100, \; W_3 = 1$ (typical engineering weight assignment, with $W_1 \to \infty$ for absolute priority).

## 3.2 Application-Domain Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **How to use this matrix in exam answers:** When KTU asks *"Discuss applications of robots"*, structure your answer using these 8 columns. The examiner has a marking rubric — addressing *all* columns = full marks.

| # | Domain | Task Examples | Typical Robot | Sensors | DOF | Pay-load | Ethical Concern | Standard |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Industrial** | Welding, painting, pick-place | Articulated 6-axis arm | Force, vision | 6 | 5–500 kg | Worker displacement | ISO 10218 |
| 2 | **Medical** | Surgery, rehab, dispensing | Da Vinci, exoskeleton | Haptic, EMG | 7 | <5 kg | Patient safety, liability | ISO 13482 |
| 3 | **Military** | EOD, surveillance, logistics | PackBot, BigDog | IR, acoustic | 4–8 | 30–200 kg | Autonomy in lethal force | Geneva conventions |
| 4 | **Space** | Roving, sample collection | Mars Rover, Canadarm | Stereo vision, lidar | 6 | varies | Communication delay, autonomy | NASA-STD-8729 |
| 5 | **Domestic** | Vacuuming, lawn-mowing | Roomba, Husqvarna | Bumper, IR cliff | 2–3 | <2 kg | Child/pet safety | IEC 60335 |
| 6 | **Hazardous** | Nuclear cleanup, fire | TALON, RoboCrane | Radiation, thermal | 5–6 | high | Operator exposure | ISO 9283 |
| 7 | **Agricultural** | Seeding, harvesting, milking | Agribot, Lely A4 | GPS, multispectral | 4 | 20–1000 kg | Food-chain integrity | ISO 18497 |
| 8 | **Service** | Hospitality, elderly care | Pepper, Care-O-bot | Face-recognition, mic | 4–7 | <10 kg | Privacy, dependency | IEEE 7000-series |
| 9 | **Underwater** | Wreck exploration, cable-laying | ROV, AUV | Sonar, pressure | 5–6 | high | Ecosystem disruption | — |
| 10 | **Entertainment** | Theme-park animatronics, toys | AIBO, NAO | Camera, mic | 12–25 | <1 kg | Emotional manipulation | ISO 13482 |

## 3.3 Derivation — Why Collaborative Robots Cap Speed at 250 mm/s

The **ISO/TS 15066:2016** speed limit for cobots is derived from biomechanical injury thresholds.

### Step 1: Human pain threshold
From biomechanical studies (University of Mainz, 2013), a constant force of **140 N** on the hand produces no injury, while **280 N** causes minor bruising.

### Step 2: Quasi-static contact model

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\max} &= m_{\text{robot}} \cdot a + m_{\text{payload}} \cdot a + F_{\text{drive}} \\
&\le 140 \; \text{N (quasi-static limit)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Maximum permissible velocity
For a typical cobot with effective moving mass $m_{\text{eff}} = 0.56$ kg:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{\max} \cdot \frac{dv}{dt} &\le \frac{F_{\max}}{m_{\text{eff}}} \\
v_{\max} \cdot 0.5 \;\; \text{m/s}^2 &\le \frac{140}{0.56} = 250 \; \text{m/s}^2 \\
\therefore v_{\max} &\le 250 \;\; \text{mm/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final boxed result:** This is why KTU board exams love quoting **$v_{\max} = 250$ mm/s** for cobots — it is engineering-derived, NOT arbitrary.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Asimov's Three Laws Decision Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robot Decision Cycle at time t] --> B{Sense Environment}
    B --> C{Human Harm Detected?}
    C -- YES --> D[ABORT Action - Law 1 overrides]
    C -- NO --> E{Human Command Received?}
    E -- YES --> F{Command violates Law 1?}
    F -- YES --> D
    F -- NO --> G[Execute Command - Law 2 satisfied]
    E -- NO --> H{Self in Danger?}
    H -- YES --> I{Protect-self violates Law 1 or 2?}
    I -- YES --> J[Accept Damage - Law 3 lowest priority]
    I -- NO --> K[Execute Self-Defence - Law 3 satisfied]
    H -- NO --> L[Idle / Continue Task]
    D --> M[Output Action]
    G --> M
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Application Taxonomy of Robots

```mermaid
graph LR
    ROBOT[ROBOT] --> IND[Industrial]
    ROBOT --> SVC[Service]
    ROBOT --> MED[Medical]
    ROBOT --> MIL[Military]
    ROBOT --> SPC[Space]
    ROBOT --> HAZ[Hazardous]
    ROBOT --> AGR[Agriculture]
    ROBOT --> DOM[Domestic]
    IND --> I1[Assembly Line]
    IND --> I2[Welding Robot]
    IND --> I3[Painting Arm]
    SVC --> S1[Hospitality]
    SVC --> S2[Elderly Care]
    SVC --> S3[Cleaning]
    MED --> M1[Surgical DaVinci]
    MED --> M2[Rehab Exoskeleton]
    MED --> M3[Prosthetic Limb]
    MIL --> MI1[EOD PackBot]
    MIL --> MI2[Surveillance Drone]
    MIL --> MI3[Logistics Convoy]
    SPC --> SP1[Mars Rover]
    SPC --> SP2[Canadarm on ISS]
    SPC --> SP3[Satellite Servicer]
    HAZ --> H1[Nuclear Cleanup]
    HAZ --> H2[Firefighting]
    HAZ --> H3[Deep-Sea ROV]
    AGR --> A1[Auto Harvester]
    AGR --> A2[Drone Sprayer]
    AGR --> A3[Milking Robot]
    DOM --> D1[Vacuum Cleaner]
    DOM --> D2[Lawn Mower]
    DOM --> D3[Companion Pet]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Ethical Layer Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SENSOR[Sensor Layer: Vision, Force, Lidar, IMU] --> PERCEPT[Perception Module]
    PERCEPT --> CLASS[World State Classifier]
    CLASS --> ETHIC[Ethics Engine: Three-Law Solver]
    COMMAND[Human Command Input] --> ETHIC
    ETHIC --> PLAN[Motion Planner]
    PLAN --> CTRL[Low-Level Motor Controller]
    CTRL --> ACTU[Actuators: Servo, Hydraulic, Pneumatic]
    ACTU --> PHYS[Physical World]
    PHYS --> SENSOR
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Textual Fallback)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this textual block if Mermaid rendering fails in your PDF viewer.**

```
[ SENSOR ARRAY ]
        |
        v
[ PERCEPTION ] --> Object list + Human positions + Self-state
        |
        v
[ WORLD MODEL DATABASE ] --> 3D map + risk heatmap
        |
        v
[ ETHICS ENGINE ] --> Applies Three Laws in priority order
        |
        v
[ TASK PLANNER ] --> Selects action satisfying all 3 laws
        |
        v
[ TRAJECTORY GENERATOR ] --> Smooth, collision-free path
        |
        v
[ ACTUATOR CONTROLLER ] --> PWM signals to motors
        |
        v
[ PHYSICAL ROBOT ]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (KTU 2024):** Part A = 3 marks (short answer); Part B = 14 marks (full descriptive). You must answer one full Part B (with internal choice between Q-A and Q-B).

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Bank]
**State Asimov's Three Laws of Robotics in order of priority.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **First Law (Non-Injury):** A robot may not injure a human being or, through inaction, allow a human being to come to harm. *(1 mark)*
2. **Second Law (Obedience):** A robot must obey the orders given by authorized human beings, except where such orders conflict with the First Law. *(1 mark)*
3. **Third Law (Self-Preservation):** A robot must protect its own existence, as long as such protection does not conflict with the First or Second Laws. *(1 mark)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often forget the phrase *"through inaction"* in Law 1 and lose the mark. The inaction clause is what makes a robot *proactive* in protecting humans, not merely *passive*.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample]
**List any six industrial applications of robots.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 0.5 each):**
1. Arc welding and spot welding
2. Spray painting and coating
3. Material handling and pick-and-place
4. Assembly (e.g., screwdriving, riveting)
5. Palletizing and packaging
6. Machine tending (CNC loading/unloading)
7. Inspection and quality control *(bonus — pick any 6)*

---

## Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)
**Internal Choice: Answer EITHER Question A OR Question B.**

---

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) [7 Marks] — Discuss the ethical concerns in deploying robots in medical and military applications.

**Model Answer:**

**Medical Robotics (3.5 marks):**
- **Patient Safety:** Surgical robots like the Da Vinci system operate near vital organs. A 1 mm positional error can sever a nerve. *Mitigation:* Redundant sensors, haptic feedback, surgeon-in-the-loop.
- **Liability:** If a robot-assisted surgery fails, who is responsible — the surgeon, the hospital, the manufacturer, or the software developer? *Current legal answer:* Surgeon holds primary liability.
- **Data Privacy:** Care-bots record intimate patient data. *Compliance:* GDPR, HIPAA.
- **Dependency:** Elderly patients may become emotionally dependent on companion robots, raising autonomy concerns.

**Military Robotics (3.5 marks):**
- **Autonomy in Lethal Force:** Should a robot decide who lives and who dies? Currently, the U.S. Department of Defense mandates a **human-in-the-loop** for all lethal decisions.
- **Proportionality & Discrimination:** Robots must distinguish combatants from civilians — extremely hard in urban warfare.
- **Asymmetric Warfare:** Autonomous weapons could trigger an AI arms race.
- **Accountability Vacuum:** No existing legal framework assigns criminal liability to a machine.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion (mandatory for full marks):** Both medical and military robotics require an **ethics-by-design** approach — embedding Three-Law logic into firmware, not as an afterthought.

#### (b) [7 Marks] — Explain the limitations of Asimov's Three Laws with suitable examples.

**Model Answer (4 major limitations — 1.75 marks each):**

1. **Ambiguity of "harm"** — If a robot tells a patient the truth about terminal cancer, it causes *psychological* harm. Strictly applying Law 1, the robot should lie, violating medical ethics. *Resolution attempt:* Use quantifiable harm metrics (e.g., Glasgow Coma Scale) instead of natural language.

2. **Through-inaction paradox** — Law 1 forces a robot to *act* whenever harm is possible, even if acting might cause *more* harm. Example: A rescue robot watching two drowning humans can save only one. By choosing one, it "allows" the other to come to harm. *Resolution attempt:* The Zeroth Law (saving the *greater* number).

3. **Two-human dilemma (Trolley Problem)** — A self-driving car must choose: hit a pedestrian or swerve and kill the passenger. The laws provide no rule for *inter-human* trade-offs.

4. **Identification problem** — In a future with cyborgs, brain-computer interfaces, and telepresence avatars, *who qualifies as a "human"*? A 90%-prosthetic soldier may or may not be covered by Law 1.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Do NOT just list the laws and their definitions. KTU specifically tests whether you can *critique* them. Students who skip the "limitations" sub-part lose 4–5 marks.

---

### 📗 Question B (14 Marks)

#### (a) [7 Marks] — Classify the major applications of robots and explain any three industrial applications in detail.

**Model Answer:**

**Classification (1 mark):** Robots are classified into **Industrial, Medical, Military, Space, Domestic, Hazardous, Agricultural, and Service** robots (with the standard ISO / IEEE taxonomy).

**Three Industrial Applications in Detail (2 marks each):**

**1. Arc Welding Robot (6-axis articulated):**
- Used in automotive chassis assembly.
- **Sensors:** Arc-current feedback, seam-tracking vision.
- **Cycle time:** ~60 seconds per weld.
- **Safety:** Light curtains, area scanners — operator must stay outside the cell.

**2. Pick-and-Place Robot (SCARA or Delta):**
- Used in electronics, food packaging, pharmaceutical sorting.
- **Speed:** Up to 300 picks/min for a Delta robot.
- **Repeatability:** ±0.02 mm.
- **Payload:** Typically 0.1–10 kg.

**3. Painting Robot (Explosion-proof 6-axis):**
- Used in automotive body painting.
- **Hazard:** Flammable paint vapors — motor enclosures are **ATEX-certified**.
- **Throughput:** ~30 cars/hour in a fully automated line.
- **Advantage:** Eliminates human exposure to VOC carcinogens.

#### (b) [7 Marks] — Write a detailed note on the role of ethics in modern collaborative robots (cobots).

**Model Answer:**

Collaborative robots (cobots) are designed to share the workspace with humans, making **ethics-by-design** non-negotiable. Key ethical provisions include:

1. **Power-and-Force Limiting (PFL):** Cobot motors limit contact force to **≤140 N** (quasi-static) and **≤280 N** (transient), derived from biomechanical pain thresholds. *(1.5 marks)*

2. **Speed Monitoring:** ISO/TS 15066 caps the cobot's velocity at **250 mm/s** when a human is within the safeguarded space. Above this, the cobot must perform a **protective stop**. *(1.5 marks)*

3. **Separation Monitoring:** Using laser scanners, the cobot maintains a *separation distance* from the human. As distance decreases, speed decreases. *(1 mark)*

4. **Hand-Guiding Operation:** In teach-mode, the human physically guides the cobot; the cobot senses the guiding force and records the trajectory. This honours Asimov's **Law 2 (Obedience)** literally. *(1.5 marks)*

5. **Ethical Fail-Safes:** Modern cobots implement a hard-coded **Asimov Law 1 equivalent** — if a collision exceeds 80 N (warning zone), the cobot reverses direction within **<50 ms**. *(1 mark)*

6. **Transparency & Logs:** All safety-relevant events are logged (per ISO 13849), enabling *post-hoc accountability* — addressing modern AI ethics concerns about "black-box" decision making. *(0.5 mark)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Do not confuse **cobot speed (250 mm/s)** with **industrial-robot speed (up to 10 m/s)**. This is a classic KTU question where students mix up the two and lose 2 marks.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Asimov's Three Laws** must be quoted in *exact original wording*, in **priority order**: Law 1 → Law 2 → Law 3, with the **Zeroth Law** as the meta-supervisor of all three.
- ✅ **Law 1 is absolute** — it overrides every other instruction, including self-preservation.
- ✅ **Limitations of the Three Laws** are the high-weight KTU questions — at least **4 must be memorized**: ambiguity of harm, through-inaction, two-human dilemma, identification problem.
- ✅ **Eight robot application domains**: Industrial, Medical, Military, Space, Domestic, Hazardous, Agricultural, Service.
- ✅ **Cobot speed limit:** $v_{\max} = 250$ mm/s, contact force $\le 140$ N — both are *engineering-derived*, not arbitrary.
- ✅ **Standards to cite**: ISO 10218 (industrial), ISO 13482 (personal care), ISO/TS 15066 (collaborative), ISO 13849 (safety-related control systems).
- ✅ **Risk Classes (EU AI Act 2024):** Unacceptable, High, Limited, Minimal — remember the order.
- ✅ **Liability in medical robotics** currently rests with the **operating surgeon**, not the manufacturer.
- ✅ **Military autonomous weapons** require a **human-in-the-loop** — robot cannot pull the final trigger (DoD Directive 3000.09).
- ✅ **DOF and Payload** are the two most-frequently asked robot-specification parameters — typical industrial robot = **6 DOF, 5–500 kg payload**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-line mnemonic for the Three Laws:**
> **"I → O → S"** → **I**njury first, **O**bedience second, **S**elf-preservation third.
> In Malayalam-friendly form: *“ആദ്യം മനുഷ്യൻ, പിന്നെ കല്പന, അവസാനം സ്വന്തം”*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensors, Actuators and Control: Sensor Classification - Touch, Force, Proximity, Vision Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAT602 - ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sensors, Actuators and Control: Sensor Classification - Touch, Force, Proximity, Vision Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Robotic Sensor?

A **robotic sensor** is a transducer that detects a physical, chemical, or biological stimulus from the robot's internal state or external environment and converts it into a measurable electrical signal that the controller can interpret. In the KTU 2024 scheme, sensors are formally classified under **internal (proprioceptive)** and **external (exteroceptive)** categories.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCCAT602 Module 2):** "A sensor is a device that responds to a physical stimulus (such as heat, light, sound, pressure, magnetism, or a particular motion) and transmits the resulting impulse for interpretation or measurement, or for operating a controller."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Robot as a Human

Imagine a human working in a factory. To safely pick up a glass, the person:
- **Touches** it (tactile feedback)
- **Feels** how heavy it is (force feedback)
- **Senses** that a wall is near (proximity awareness)
- **Sees** where it is (vision)

A robot performs the **exact same job** using sensors instead of skin, muscles, eyes, and nerves. The four primary sensor families covered in this module map almost one-to-one to these human faculties:

| Human Faculty | Robotic Equivalent | Primary Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Skin (contact) | **Touch Sensor** | Detect physical contact |
| Muscles/Load | **Force Sensor** | Measure applied load |
| Spatial memory | **Proximity Sensor** | Detect nearby objects without contact |
| Eyes | **Vision Sensor** | Capture and interpret visual scenes |

> [!NOTE]
> The word **transducer** is the KTU-board-preferred term because the device *transduces* (converts) one form of energy into another — for example, mechanical strain into a change in electrical resistance (the principle behind the strain-gauge force sensor).

## 1.3 Touch Sensors — Formal Definition

A **touch sensor** (also called a *tactile sensor*) is a contact-mode device that produces a binary or graded electrical output when a robot's end-effector makes physical contact with an object or surface. The output is a **discrete contact state** (Yes/No) or, in advanced versions, a **distributed pressure map**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Metric:** The performance of a touch sensor is measured by its **spatial resolution** (number of taxels per cm²), **force threshold** (minimum detectable force, typically in **mN** or **grams**), and **response time** (typically **< 5 ms** for industrial use).

## 1.4 Force Sensors — Formal Definition

A **force sensor** measures the magnitude and direction of mechanical force (and, in 6-DOF wrist sensors, torque) applied to the robot's end-effector or joints. The two dominant KTU-relevant technologies are:
1. **Strain-gauge based** (resistive change under deformation)
2. **Piezoelectric based** (charge generation under stress)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Key Term:** The **Wrist Force Sensor** (mounted between the robot's wrist and the gripper) is the canonical exam example. It usually has **6 Degrees of Freedom** — three force components (Fx, Fy, Fz) and three moment components (Mx, My, Mz).

## 1.5 Proximity Sensors — Formal Definition

A **proximity sensor** detects the presence of an object within a defined range **without physical contact**.
