It emits a field or beam (electromagnetic, electrostatic, acoustic, or optical) and senses changes caused by an approaching target. Detection ranges vary from **1 mm to several meters**, depending on the principle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "no contact"?** Proximity sensors are essential in **high-speed pick-and-place**, **obstacle avoidance**, and **safety light curtains** where contact would damage the part, the robot, or both.

## 1.6 Vision Sensors — Formal Definition

A **vision sensor** (machine-vision system) uses a 2D or 3D camera (CCD or CMOS) to capture an image, which is then processed by algorithms to extract features such as edges, blobs, holes, colours, or 3D point clouds. The system provides **non-contact**, **high information-density** feedback — typically **megabytes per frame**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:** A **Vision Sensor** (single-purpose, integrated lighting + lens + processor) is different from a **Vision System** (multi-component PC-based setup). The KTU syllabus treats them as one family but examiners will award marks for explicitly noting the distinction.

## 1.7 Master Classification Flow (Sense → Output)

The complete KTU 2024 sensor taxonomy covered in this module can be represented as:

- **Internal (Proprioceptive):** Encoders, resolvers, tachometers, joint position sensors
- **External (Exteroceptive):** Touch, Force/Torque, Proximity, Vision (this module's focus)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why is Classification Important? (The Engineering "Why")

A robotics engineer must classify sensors because **each physical principle responds to a different physical variable**. A capacitive proximity sensor cannot see colour. An inductive proximity sensor cannot detect a wooden block. A vision sensor cannot feel weight. The classification framework below tells you *which sensor to deploy for which task* — a question worth 7 to 14 marks on every KTU exam.

## 2.2 Hierarchical Classification Logic

Following the KTU 2024 module structure, sensors are first split into:

1. **Contact vs Non-Contact** (operational)
2. **Passive vs Active** (energy requirement)
3. **Digital vs Analog** (output form)

The next sub-section breaks each of the four target sensor families into these same three lenses.

## 2.3 Detailed Family-Wise Analysis

### 2.3.1 Touch Sensors — Operating Principle

A **resistive touch sensor** works on the principle that contact between two conductive layers reduces the resistance of the contact region. Current can then flow between top and bottom electrodes, and a controller detects a voltage drop.

A **capacitive touch sensor** detects the change in capacitance caused by the proximity (or contact) of a finger/object to a charged plate. The human body acts as one plate of a capacitor; the sensor's conductive pad is the other.

- **Typical operating voltage:** **3.3 V or 5 V DC**
- **Typical force threshold:** **0.1 N to 1.0 N**
- **Spatial density:** Modern tactile arrays reach **> 100 taxels/cm²**

### 2.3.2 Force Sensors — Operating Principle

A **strain-gauge force sensor** is built from a metallic foil pattern bonded to a flexible beam (the *flexure*). When force is applied, the beam deforms, the foil stretches or compresses, and the electrical resistance changes. This change is detected by a **Wheatstone bridge** circuit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** A single strain gauge has very low sensitivity. That is why KTU-board-favourite force sensors use a **full Wheatstone bridge** with 4 active gauges — this *cancels temperature drift* and *doubles the output signal* compared to a single gauge.

### 2.3.3 Proximity Sensors — Four Sub-Principles

| Sub-Type | Field/Beam Used | Detects | Typical Range | Comment |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Inductive** | Electromagnetic field (coil) | Metallic objects only | **1 – 10 mm** | Immune to dust/oil |
| **Capacitive** | Electrostatic field | Metals, plastics, liquids, humans | **1 – 40 mm** | Detects non-metals |
| **Optical (through-beam / retro-reflective / diffuse)** | Light beam (LED/laser) | Any opaque object | **mm to 50 m** | Long range, precise |
| **Ultrasonic** | Sound wave (20 kHz–200 kHz) | Almost any material, surfaces | **0.1 – 10 m** | Used in mobile robots |
| **Magnetic / Hall-effect** | Magnetic field | Permanent magnets / ferromagnetic | **1 – 50 mm** | Very robust |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Point:** Inductive sensors **cannot** detect wood, plastic, or any non-metallic object. If the exam asks "which sensor detects a wooden block on a conveyor?", the answer is **capacitive or optical** — never inductive.

### 2.3.4 Vision Sensors — Operating Principle

A camera consists of a **lens system** that focuses light onto a **photosensitive sensor array** (CCD or CMOS). Each element in the array (*pixel*) accumulates charge proportional to incident light intensity. After a fixed integration (exposure) time, the charge is read out, digitised by an **A/D converter**, and stored as a 2D array of intensity values — a *digital image*.

- **CCD** (Charge-Coupled Device): High uniformity, low noise, slower readout
- **CMOS** (Complementary Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor): Lower power, faster readout, on-chip processing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Resolution vs Frame Rate Trade-off:** Higher spatial resolution (e.g., **4096 × 3072** pixels) means more detail but **slower frame rate** and **more data** to process. Industrial vision systems balance this carefully.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Memorize these equations, their variable definitions, and units. KTU paper setters frequently test the **strain gauge equation** and the **camera FOV equation**.

| # | Concept | Equation | Variable Definitions | Engineering Use |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Strain-gauge sensitivity (Gauge Factor)** | $GF = \dfrac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$ | $GF$ = gauge factor (unitless, **≈ 2.0** for metal, **≈ 100–200** for semiconductor), $\Delta R$ = change in resistance ($\Omega$), $R$ = original resistance ($\Omega$), $\varepsilon$ = strain (unitless) | Converts a small mechanical deformation into a measurable resistance change |
| 2 | **Hooke's Law (for flexure)** | $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ | $\sigma$ = stress (Pa), $E$ = Young's modulus (Pa), $\varepsilon$ = strain | Relates the applied force to the resulting strain on the flexure |
| 3 | **Force from strain** | $F = \sigma \cdot A = E \cdot A \cdot \varepsilon$ | $F$ = force (N), $A$ = cross-section area (m²) | Gives the load on a flexure from measured strain |
| 4 | **Wheatstone bridge output** | $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R / R}{4}$ (for full bridge with one varying arm, **× 1** for full active bridge) | $V_{out}$ = differential output (V), $V_{ex}$ = excitation voltage (V) | Converts resistance change into voltage |
| 5 | **Camera Field of View (FOV)** | $FOV = 2 \cdot \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{d}{2 f}\right)$ | $d$ = sensor dimension (mm), $f$ = focal length (mm) | Determines how much of the scene the camera sees |
| 6 | **Image magnification** | $m = \dfrac{h_i}{h_o} = \dfrac{d_i}{d_o}$ | $h_i, h_o$ = image, object height (m); $d_i, d_o$ = image, object distance (m) | Relates object size to pixel size |
| 7 | **Object distance (thin lens)** | $\dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{d_o} + \dfrac{1}{d_i}$ | $f$ = focal length (m), $d_o$ = object distance (m), $d_i$ = image distance (m) | Used to focus the camera |
| 8 | **Inductive proximity — effective range** | $s_r \approx 0.81 \cdot s_n$ (for standard targets) | $s_r$ = real switching distance (mm), $s_n$ = nominal switching distance (mm) | Real-world de-rating factor |
| 9 | **Ultrasonic time-of-flight** | $d = \dfrac{c \cdot t}{2}$ | $d$ = distance (m), $c$ = speed of sound in air ($\approx$ **343 m/s** at 20 °C), $t$ = round-trip time (s) | Range measurement by ultrasonic proximity |
| 10 | **Touch-sensor threshold detection** | $V_{touch} \ge V_{ref}$ | $V_{touch}$ = voltage at sense node (V), $V_{ref}$ = reference threshold (V) | Comparator output logic |

> [!WARNING]
> **Board Examiner's Note:** Always include **units** in the final numerical answer. Marks are deducted for unitless numbers in KTU 2024 scheme valuation.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Touch sensors** — Assembly line part detection, prosthetic hand feedback, robotic surgery (da Vinci system)
- **Force sensors** — Peg-in-hole assembly, deburring, polishing (constant-force control), electronic weighing
- **Inductive proximity** — Detecting metal cans on a bottling line, end-of-travel limit on CNC tools
- **Capacitive proximity** — Detecting plastic pellets, liquid level in a non-metallic tank
- **Optical proximity** — Counting packages on a conveyor at high speed
- **Ultrasonic proximity** — Mobile-robot obstacle avoidance, parking sensors in cars
- **Vision sensors** — Quality inspection (crack detection), barcode reading, bin picking, SLAM

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Strain Gauge to Force Conversion

**Goal:** Derive the relationship between an applied force $F$ on a flexure and the differential output voltage $V_{out}$ of a full-bridge strain-gauge force sensor.

**Step 1 — Strain from applied force.**

The flexure is a uniform beam of cross-section $A$ and Young's modulus $E$. By definition of stress $\sigma$ and the Hooke's Law relation $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$, the strain $\varepsilon$ produced by an axial force $F$ is:

$$\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A}$$

$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{\sigma}{E} = \dfrac{F}{A \cdot E}$$

*Text:* We first apply the definition of mechanical stress (force per unit area), then rearrange Hooke's Law to solve for the resulting longitudinal strain. The strain is the fractional change in length, which is what the strain gauge actually measures.

**Step 2 — Resistance change from strain.**

Using the gauge-factor definition $GF = \dfrac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$, the fractional change in resistance is:

$$\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = GF \cdot \varepsilon = GF \cdot \dfrac{F}{A \cdot E}$$

*Text:* The gauge factor is a constant of the strain-gauge material (≈ 2.0 for constantan foil). We use it to convert the mechanical strain into the electrical resistance change that the bridge circuit will detect.

**Step 3 — Bridge output voltage.**

For a **full active Wheatstone bridge** (all 4 arms active), the differential output is:

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$$

Substituting Step 2:

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{GF \cdot F}{A \cdot E}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the force.**

Rearranging to make $F$ the subject of the formula:

$$F = \dfrac{V_{out} \cdot A \cdot E}{V_{ex} \cdot GF}$$

*Text:* This is the **fundamental force-sensor equation** in robotics. The controller measures $V_{out}$, knows $V_{ex}$, the geometry ($A$), the material property ($E$), and the constant $GF$ — and can therefore compute the applied force in real time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Sanity Check:** If $V_{out}$ doubles, $F$ doubles (linear). If the excitation $V_{ex}$ is increased, the same force produces a larger output, improving signal-to-noise ratio. Increasing $E$ (using a stiffer flexure) *reduces* the output for a given force, but increases the *load range* of the sensor.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Camera Field of View from First Principles

**Goal:** Derive the FOV equation from similar triangles in the thin-lens model.

**Step 1 — Geometry setup.**

A thin convex lens of focal length $f$ is placed at distance $d_o$ from the object. Light from the object's top edge enters the lens and is refracted to form an image at distance $d_i$ from the lens. The image height is $h_i$ and the object height is $h_o$.

**Step 2 — Apply the thin-lens formula.**

The thin-lens formula (derived from Snell's law under paraxial approximation) is:

$$\dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{d_o} + \dfrac{1}{d_i}$$

*Text:* This equation relates the focal length of the lens to the object and image distances. It is a fundamental result of geometric optics and is the basis of all camera focusing systems.

**Step 3 — Magnification relation.**

From similar triangles on either side of the lens:

$$m = \dfrac{h_i}{h_o} = \dfrac{d_i}{d_o}$$

*Text:* Because the object and the image subtend the same angle at the lens, the ratio of their heights equals the ratio of their distances from the lens.

**Step 4 — Derive the FOV equation.**

For a camera sensor of width $d$ (along one axis), the maximum imageable object height at a given distance is $2 h_o$ when $h_i = d / 2$. Substituting $h_i = d / 2$ and $h_o = d \cdot d_o / (2 d_i)$ (from Step 3) into the angle-of-view definition $FOV = 2 \cdot \arctan(h_o / d_o)$:

$$FOV = 2 \cdot \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{d}{2 f}\right)$$

*Text:* This is the standard FOV formula used in vision-sensor datasheets. Note that **FOV increases as focal length decreases** (wide-angle lens) and **FOV decreases as the sensor gets smaller** for a fixed focal length.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Example (KTU Favourite):** A camera with a **6 mm** focal-length lens and a **1/3-inch sensor** (≈ 4.8 mm horizontal) gives:
>
> $FOV_h = 2 \cdot \arctan(4.8 / (2 \cdot 6)) = 2 \cdot \arctan(0.4) \approx 43.6°$.
>
> This kind of calculation appears in nearly every KTU 2024 paper.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Ultrasonic Distance from Time of Flight

**Goal:** Derive the distance equation for an ultrasonic proximity sensor.

**Step 1 — Wave equation.**

The ultrasonic sensor emits a burst of sound waves of frequency $f$ (typically **40 kHz**), which propagate through air at speed $c$ (≈ **343 m/s** at 20 °C). The wave travels to the object, reflects, and returns.

**Step 2 — Round-trip path length.**

If the round-trip time is $t$, the total path length traversed is:

$$L = c \cdot t$$

*Text:* The wave travels at a known speed, so multiplying by the measured time gives the total path length. The factor of 2 is missing — we account for it in the next step.

**Step 3 — One-way distance.**

Because the wave travels to the object *and back*, the one-way distance is half the total path:

$$d = \dfrac{c \cdot t}{2}$$

*Text:* This is the canonical ultrasonic ranging equation. The factor of 2 appears because of the round-trip nature of the measurement.

> [!NOTE]
> **Speed of Sound Variation:** $c = 331 + 0.6 \cdot T$ (m/s) where $T$ is the air temperature in °C. The KTU syllabus expects students to remember $c \approx 343$ m/s at standard conditions.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sensor Selection Decision Table

The matrix below is a KTU exam-style summary that consolidates when to choose which sensor. Memorize it for direct 7-mark questions.

| Application Scenario | Best Sensor Choice | Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Detect presence of a steel nut on a conveyor | **Inductive proximity** | Metal target, short range, robust to oil |
| Detect transparent plastic bottle | **Capacitive or optical (retro-reflective)** | Inductive fails on non-metal; capacitive detects dielectrics |
| Detect a wooden block at 5 m range | **Ultrasonic or optical** | Inductive fails; capacitive has too short a range |
| Measure weight of an object in a gripper | **Strain-gauge force sensor** | Direct force measurement with high accuracy |
| Quality check (cracks on a circuit board) | **Vision sensor (2D camera)** | Visual detail required |
| Part recognition by colour | **Vision sensor (colour camera)** | Only vision can perceive colour |
| Detect robot collision with a human | **Touch / capacitive skin** | Binary contact signal; redundant with proximity |
| Robot mapping an unknown room | **Vision + Ultrasonic fusion** | Vision = rich landmarks, ultrasonic = range under varied lighting |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Master Sensor Classification (KTU Module 2 Scope)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Robotic Sensors Module 2]:::root
    A --> B[Touch Sensors]
    A --> C[Force Sensors]
    A --> D[Proximity Sensors]
    A --> E[Vision Sensors]

    B --> B1[Resistive Tactile]
    B --> B2[Capacitive Tactile]
    B --> B3[Piezoelectric Tactile]

    C --> C1[Strain Gauge Force]
    C --> C2[Piezoelectric Force]
    C --> C3[Wrist F T Sensor 6 DOF]

    D --> D1[Inductive]
    D --> D2[Capacitive]
    D --> D3[Optical]
    D --> D4[Ultrasonic]
    D --> D5[Magnetic Hall Effect]

    E --> E1[CCD Camera]
    E --> E2[CMOS Camera]
    E --> E3[Line Scan Camera]
    E --> E4[3D Stereo Camera]

    classDef root fill:#0F4C81,color:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px;
```

> [!NOTE]
> The colour-coded block diagram above is the **single most-reproduced figure** in KTU robotics exam answers. Reproduce it whenever a question says "classify the sensors used in robotics".

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Strain-Gauge Force Sensor Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    F1[Applied Force F on Flexure]:::input --> F2[Flexure Deformation]
    F2 --> F3[Strain epsilon in Foil]
    F3 --> F4[Resistance Change delta R]
    F4 --> F5[Full Wheatstone Bridge]
    F5 --> F6[Differential Voltage Vout]
    F6 --> F7[Amplifier and ADC]
    F7 --> F8[Controller Computes F]
    F8 --> F9[Force Output Display or Control Action]:::output

    classDef input fill:#2E86AB,color:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000000;
    classDef output fill:#A23B72,color:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000000;
```

**Reading the flow:** A mechanical input (force) is converted to strain, which is converted to a resistance change, which the bridge converts to a voltage, which is digitised and finally computed back into a force value.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Vision Sensor Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    V1[Scene and Object]:::scene --> V2[Illumination Source LED or Laser]
    V2 --> V3[Optical Lens System]
    V3 --> V4[Image Sensor CCD or CMOS]
    V4 --> V5[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    V5 --> V6[Image Buffer RAM]
    V6 --> V7[Image Processor DSP or FPGA]
    V7 --> V8[Feature Extraction Edges Blobs Colours]
    V8 --> V9[Decision Output Pass Fail Coordinates]

    classDef scene fill:#1B998B,color:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000000;
```

**Reading the flow:** Light from the scene (often actively illuminated) is focused by the lens onto the image sensor, digitised, buffered, and then processed by algorithms to extract features, ending in a decision or coordinate output for the robot controller.

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Proximity Sensor Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    P0[Choose Proximity Sensor]:::root --> P1{Target Material?}
    P1 -- Metallic --> P2{Inductive? OK}
    P1 -- Non-Metallic --> P3{Need to Sense Through Dielectric?}
    P3 -- Yes --> P4[Capacitive Proximity]
    P3 -- No --> P5{Range more than 100 mm?}
    P5 -- Yes --> P6[Ultrasonic or Optical]
    P5 -- No --> P7[Optical Diffuse]
    P1 -- Magnetic --> P8[Hall Effect Sensor]

    classDef root fill:#0F4C81,color:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000000;
```

**Reading the flow:** This decision tree is a **direct answer template** to any KTU question of the form *"Suggest a suitable proximity sensor for …"*. The decision branches are based on the *target material* and the *required sensing range*.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]`

**Q: Classify sensors used in industrial robots. Differentiate between internal and external sensors with two examples each.**

**Model Answer (target: 3 marks):**

A **robotic sensor** is a device that detects a physical stimulus and converts it into a measurable electrical signal. Sensors are broadly classified into two categories:

1. **Internal (Proprioceptive) Sensors:** Measure the robot's *own* internal state.
   - *Example 1:* Optical encoder — measures joint angle.
   - *Example 2:* Resolver or potentiometer — measures joint position/velocity.

2. **External (Exteroceptive) Sensors:** Measure the state of the *environment* around the robot.
   - *Example 1:* Vision camera — measures object position/shape.
   - *Example 2:* Force/torque sensor — measures contact force from environment.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mark Split:** 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the two categories, 1 mark for the two examples per category.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]`

**Q: Briefly explain the working principle of an inductive proximity sensor. State one limitation.**

**Model Answer (target: 3 marks):**

An inductive proximity sensor consists of a coil carrying an alternating current, generating an alternating electromagnetic field at its face. When a **ferrous or non-ferrous metal target** enters this field, **eddy currents** are induced in the target, which draw energy from the oscillator circuit. This causes a measurable drop in the oscillation amplitude. A detector circuit senses the drop and switches the output state from OFF to ON, indicating the target is present.

**Limitation:** It can detect **only metallic targets** and is ineffective for non-metallic materials such as wood, plastic, ceramics, or liquids.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Two questions of 14 marks each from the module. The student must answer **ONE** full question. The second question is the *internal choice*. We provide both below.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a strain-gauge based force sensor used in robotic assembly. Derive the relationship between applied force and the bridge output voltage. (14 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Block Diagram and Working (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Introduction (1 mark).**
A strain-gauge force sensor measures the mechanical force applied to a robot's end-effector (e.g., during assembly, polishing, or peg-in-hole tasks). It is the most widely used force-sensing technology in industrial robotics.

**Step 2 — Construction (2 marks).**
The sensor consists of a **metallic flexure** (a precisely machined elastic element) on which **four foil strain gauges** are bonded — two along the direction of the load (longitudinal) and two perpendicular to it (transverse). The four gauges are connected to form a **full Wheatstone bridge**, with excitation voltage $V_{ex}$ applied across the bridge.

**Step 3 — Working (3 marks).**
- When force $F$ is applied to the flexure, the longitudinal gauges stretch (tension) and the transverse gauges compress.
- This deformation changes the resistance of each gauge: $R \to R + \Delta R$ for tensioned gauges, $R \to R - \Delta R$ for compressed gauges.
- The full-bridge configuration produces a differential output voltage $V_{out}$ proportional to $\Delta R / R$, which in turn is proportional to the applied force.
- After amplification and A/D conversion, the controller computes the magnitude and direction of $F$.

**Step 4 — Block diagram (1 mark).**
The block flow was shown in Section 4.2: Force → Flexure → Strain → Resistance change → Wheatstone bridge → Differential voltage → ADC → Controller.

#### Part (b) — Derivation of Force vs Voltage (7 marks)

Starting from the strain $\varepsilon = F / (A \cdot E)$ (from Section 3.1) and the gauge factor $GF = \dfrac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$:

$$\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = GF \cdot \varepsilon = \dfrac{GF \cdot F}{A \cdot E}$$

For a **full active Wheatstone bridge**, the differential output voltage is:

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{GF \cdot F}{A \cdot E}$$

Solving for the force:

$$F = \dfrac{V_{out} \cdot A \cdot E}{V_{ex} \cdot GF}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Split for derivation:** 2 marks for strain formula, 2 marks for gauge factor substitution, 2 marks for bridge equation, 1 mark for final rearrangement and a brief numerical-style example.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Choice Q]`

**Q: With suitable diagrams, explain the following proximity sensors and their typical applications: (i) Inductive, (ii) Capacitive, (iii) Ultrasonic, (iv) Optical. Compare them in a table. (14 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Inductive and Capacitive (7 marks)

**Inductive Proximity Sensor (3.5 marks).**
The sensor has a coil that produces an AC electromagnetic field. When a metallic target enters the field, eddy currents are induced in the target, which absorb energy from the oscillator. The amplitude drop is detected and the output switches. **Applications:** Detecting metal parts on conveyors, end-of-stroke limit sensing on CNC tools, position verification in hydraulic/pneumatic cylinders.

**Capacitive Proximity Sensor (3.5 marks).**
The sensor has an oscillator whose frequency depends on the capacitance of a sensing plate relative to a reference. When any object (metal, plastic, wood, liquid, human body) approaches, the capacitance changes, shifting the oscillator frequency. A detector circuit senses this shift. **Applications:** Detecting plastic pellets in hoppers, liquid level in non-metallic tanks, sheet-feed control in printing presses, mobile-robot bumpers.

#### Part (b) — Ultrasonic and Optical + Comparison Table (7 marks)

**Ultrasonic Proximity Sensor (2.5 marks).**
The sensor emits a burst of **40 kHz** sound waves and listens for the echo. Time-of-flight $t$ gives the distance $d = c \cdot t / 2$. **Applications:** Mobile-robot obstacle avoidance, reversing car alarms, fluid-level measurement in open tanks.

**Optical Proximity Sensor (2.5 marks).**
A photoemitter (LED or laser diode) sends out a light beam; a photodetector measures the received light. Three configurations exist:
- *Through-beam:* Emitter and receiver are separate; object breaks the beam.
- *Retro-reflective:* Emitter and receiver in one unit; beam reflects off a reflector.
- *Diffuse reflective:* Object itself reflects the beam back to the receiver.
**Applications:** Bottling line cap detection, label counting, long-range object detection up to 50 m.

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Inductive | Capacitive | Ultrasonic | Optical |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Detects | Metals only | Most materials | Most materials | Opaque objects |
| Typical Range | 1–10 mm | 1–40 mm | 0.1–10 m | mm to 50 m |
| Affected by | None (robust) | Humidity, temperature | Air turbulence, soft surfaces | Dust, smoke, transparency |
| Cost | Low | Low | Medium | Medium–High |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Before Writing the Exam:**
> 1. **Don't confuse the gauge factor with Poisson's ratio.** They are not the same. $GF$ relates $\Delta R / R$ to axial strain; Poisson's ratio relates transverse strain to axial strain.
> 2. **Inductive proximity sensors CANNOT detect non-metals.** A common 3-mark trap.
> 3. **Always state the excitation voltage $V_{ex}$ in a force-sensor equation.** Skipping it costs 1 mark.
> 4. **For ultrasonic distance, remember to divide by 2.** The wave travels to the target *and back* — students who forget the divisor lose 2 marks.
> 5. **Do not write "camera = eye".** It is a *black-box analogy* only. Examiners prefer formal definitions.
> 6. **In a "suggest a sensor" question, justify your choice** with one reason (target material, range, environment). Marks are awarded for the *reason*, not just the name.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Read this just before entering the exam hall.**

- [x] A **sensor** is a *transducer*; it converts one form of energy into an electrical signal.
- [x] Sensors are classified as **internal (proprioceptive)** or **external (exteroceptive)**.
- [x] **Touch sensors** detect contact; key types are *resistive*, *capacitive*, *piezoelectric*.
- [x] **Force sensors** use a *strain-gauge on a flexure* and a *Wheatstone bridge*; the key equation is $F = V_{out} \cdot A \cdot E / (V_{ex} \cdot GF)$.
- [x] **Strain-gauge gauge factor** $GF \approx 2$ for metallic foil, $\approx 100$–$200$ for semiconductor.
- [x] **Proximity sensors** are *non-contact*; four main types — *inductive* (metals only), *capacitive* (most materials, short range), *ultrasonic* (long range, distance by time-of-flight), *optical* (very long range, line-of-sight).
- [x] **Ultrasonic distance** = $c \cdot t / 2$, with $c \approx 343$ m/s at 20 °C.
- [x] **Vision sensors** are CCD or CMOS cameras; key equation is $FOV = 2 \cdot \arctan(d / 2f)$.
- [x] **Thin-lens equation** $1 / f = 1 / d_o + 1 / d_i$ governs focusing.
- [x] In a KTU answer, always **state the principle, draw a block diagram, and give one application** — this guarantees full marks for a 7-mark sub-question.
- [x] **Units matter.** Always write Newtons, volts, metres, mm — never bare numbers in the final answer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Suggested Self-Test Order (in 1 hour):**
> 1. Re-draw the master classification diagram from memory (10 min).
> 2. Re-derive the strain-gauge force equation (10 min).
> 3. Compute the FOV for a 8 mm lens on a 1/2-inch sensor (10 min).
> 4. Write a 3-sentence answer for "inductive vs capacitive proximity" (10 min).
> 5. Solve one full 14-mark past-paper question end-to-end (20 min).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Internal Sensors - Position Sensors, Velocity Sensors, Acceleration Sensors, Force Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAT602 - ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Internal Sensors - Position Sensors, Velocity Sensors, Acceleration Sensors, Force Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Internal Sensors in Robotics?

**Formal KTU Definition:**
> *Internal sensors* are measurement devices mounted *within* the robot's own structure (joints, links, end-effector, or base) that monitor the **internal state** of the manipulator. They quantify quantities such as joint position, angular/linear velocity, acceleration, and the contact force/torque exchanged with the environment. They form the feedback backbone of the robot's **proprioceptive control loop**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Proprioception vs. Exteroception:** Internal sensors are *proprioceptive* (they sense the robot's own body), in contrast to *exteroceptive* sensors (vision, range finders) that perceive the outside world. KTU Module-2 deals exclusively with the **proprioceptive** family.

**Intuitive Analogy — The "Human Body" Mapping**

| Robot Component | Human Body Equivalent | Sensor Function |
|---|---|---|
| Joint position sensors | Stretch receptors in muscles | Know where my arm is, even with eyes closed |
| Velocity sensors | Muscle spindles (rate of stretch) | Know how fast my hand is moving |
| Acceleration sensors | Vestibular system (inner ear) | Sense sudden jerks, falls, tilts |
| Force/torque sensors | Nerve endings in skin & joints | Feel whether I'm pushing a wall or holding a feather |

When you close your eyes and still know your arm is bent at 90°, that's proprioception — and that is *exactly* what internal sensors give a robot.

## 1.2 Classification of Internal Sensors (KTU Module-2 Scope)

The four canonical families are:

1. **Position Sensors** — measure linear or angular displacement.
2. **Velocity Sensors** — measure rate of change of position.
3. **Acceleration Sensors** — measure rate of change of velocity (inertial).
4. **Force/Torque Sensors** — measure the mechanical interaction force at the wrist or joint.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this order matters in a KTU exam:** These four families follow a natural kinematic chain — *Position → Velocity → Acceleration* (the three time-derivatives of motion), with *Force* being the dynamic cause of acceleration via Newton's second law. Examiners love questions that link these via $\tau = I\alpha$ or $F = ma$.

**Key Performance Metrics (must be memorized for KTU):**
- **Resolution:** smallest detectable change $\Delta x_{\min}$
- **Accuracy:** closeness to true value
- **Repeatability:** ability to give the same reading under identical conditions
- **Linearity:** deviation from ideal straight-line output
- **Bandwidth:** operating frequency range (Hz)
- **Hysteresis:** output difference for increasing vs. decreasing input
- **Operating range (full-scale):** maximum measurable input
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Position Sensors

### 2.1.1 Potentiometric Position Sensor

A **potentiometer** is a variable resistor with a wiper that slides along a resistive element. Output voltage is a linear function of wiper position.

**Operating Principle:**
- A reference DC voltage $V_{ref}$ is applied across the full resistive track.
- The wiper taps off a fraction of the voltage proportional to displacement.
- Three types: **linear (translation)**, **rotary (single-turn)**, **multi-turn**.

**Why and How It Works:**
- The resistive element has uniform resistance per unit length $\rho$ ($\Omega$/m).
- The portion of the element from the reference end to the wiper has resistance $R_x = \rho \cdot x$.
- The full track resistance is $R_{total} = \rho \cdot L$.
- The output is therefore a **voltage divider** in real time.

**KTU Formula (Voltage Divider Law):**

$$V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{R_x}{R_{total}} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{x}{L}$$

For a rotary potentiometer measuring joint angle $\theta$ over a full sweep of $\theta_{max}$:

$$V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{\theta}{\theta_{max}}$$

**Limitations:** Mechanical wear of the wiper, limited resolution (finite wire turns), contact noise, finite bandwidth, friction-induced loading error.

### 2.1.2 Optical Encoders (Incremental & Absolute)

**Incremental Encoder:**
- A disc with alternating transparent/opaque radial lines rotates between a light source and a photodetector.
- Each line crossing produces a pulse; pulses are counted by an up/down counter to give relative position.

**Absolute Encoder:**
- Uses a Gray-coded disc (multi-track, multi-bit).
- Each angular position has a **unique binary code word**; position is read directly without a homing cycle.

**KTU Formulas:**

$$N_{pulses} = N_{lines/rev} \times N_{revolutions}$$

$$\theta_{resolution} = \frac{360°}{N_{lines/rev}} = \frac{2\pi}{N_{lines/rev}} \text{ (rad)}$$

$$\theta_{position} = \frac{N_{counted}}{N_{lines/rev}} \times 360°$$

Quadrature decoding (using two channels A and B phase-shifted by 90°) gives **4× the resolution**:

$$\theta_{resolution,\,quad} = \frac{360°}{4 \cdot N_{lines/rev}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Trick Question Alert:** "Why are absolute encoders used in robot joints?" — Because they retain position information even after a power loss, eliminating the need for a homing routine — a critical feature for industrial safety.

### 2.1.3 LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer)

A **mutual-inductance** device with one primary coil and two secondary coils wound on a common hollow former. A movable soft-iron core changes the magnetic coupling.

**Operating Principle:**
- Primary is excited with AC ($V_p$, typically 1–10 kHz, 1–24 V rms).
- The two secondaries are connected in **series-opposition**.
- Output voltage is the **difference** of the two induced EMFs.
- When core is centered → output is nominally zero (null position).
- Core displacement unbalances the coupling → output amplitude is proportional to displacement, phase indicates direction.

**KTU Formula:**

$$V_{out} = K_{LVDT} \cdot (x_1 - x_2)$$

where $x_1, x_2$ are the displacements of the core from the centers of the two secondaries, and $K_{LVDT}$ is the sensitivity (V/mm). For a symmetric coil arrangement with core displacement $x$ from null:

$$V_{out} = K_{LVDT} \cdot x$$

**Advantages (often asked as 3-mark question):**
- **Frictionless** measurement (no contact between core and coils).
- **Infinite resolution** (limited only by detector).
- **Excellent linearity** ($\pm 0.1\%$ typical).
- **Robust** — survives harsh environments (sealed, used in hydraulic actuators).

### 2.1.4 Resolvers and Synchros

A **resolver** is a rotary transformer with one rotor winding (excited by AC) and two stator windings placed 90° apart. The induced stator voltages are:

$$V_{s1} = V_r \cos\theta \cdot \sin(\omega t)$$

$$V_{s2} = V_r \sin\theta \cdot \sin(\omega t)$$

The angle $\theta$ is recovered by **arctangent demodulation**:

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{V_{s2}}{V_{s1}}\right)$$

**Synchro:** A 3-stator version used for remote angular transmission (legacy in servos).

**Why used in robots:** Rugged, works in **high-temperature / high-vibration** environments (e.g., aerospace, military) where optical encoders fail.

## 2.2 Velocity Sensors

### 2.2.1 Tachogenerator (DC and AC)

A **tachogenerator** is a small permanent-magnet DC/AC generator coupled to the rotating shaft. The generated EMF is **directly proportional to angular velocity** (Faraday's law).

**KTU Formula:**

$$E = K_e \cdot \omega$$

where $E$ is the induced EMF (V), $K_e$ is the generator constant (V·s/rad), and $\omega$ is the angular velocity (rad/s).

For a DC tachogenerator:

$$V_{out} = K_e \cdot \omega \quad \text{(linear, ideal)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Error:** Students often write $V = K \cdot n$ where $n$ is rpm. While dimensionally it works, the **canonical KTU form** uses $\omega$ in rad/s. Convert using $\omega = 2\pi n / 60$.

### 2.2.2 Velocity from Encoders (Differentiation Method)

Since a digital encoder gives position as a discrete count, velocity is obtained by **numerical differentiation** over a fixed time window $T$:

$$\omega = \frac{\Delta N}{\Delta t} \cdot \frac{2\pi}{N_{lines/rev}}$$

**Two common KTU methods:**

| Method | Formula | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **M-method** (frequency) | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi f_{pulse}}{N_{lines/rev}}$ | High speeds |
| **T-method** (period) | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi}{N_{lines/rev} \cdot T_{pulse}}$ | Low speeds |

## 2.3 Acceleration Sensors (Accelerometers)

### 2.3.1 Seismic-Mass Accelerometer (General Principle)

A **proof mass** $m$ is suspended on a spring $k$ with damper $c$ inside the sensor housing. When the housing accelerates with $a$, the mass experiences an inertial force $F_{inertia} = -m a$ relative to the case. Relative displacement $x$ of the mass w.r.t. housing is measured.

**Equation of Motion (Newton's Second Law on the mass):**

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = -m a_{case}$$

In **quasi-static** (low frequency, $\omega \ll \omega_n$) limit, inertial force balances spring force:

$$kx \approx -m a \quad \Rightarrow \quad a \approx -\frac{k}{m} x$$

So the **relative displacement is proportional to acceleration** — and that displacement is measured by one of: LVDT, strain gauge, capacitive plates, or piezoelectric crystal.

**Natural frequency:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$

**Damping ratio:**

$$\zeta = \frac{c}{2\sqrt{km}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Design Insight:** To extend the *flat* measurement range (bandwidth) of an accelerometer, you need **high $\omega_n$**, which means **stiff spring + light mass** — this is the design trade-off for MEMS accelerometers.

### 2.3.2 Piezoelectric Accelerometer

A **piezoelectric crystal** (e.g., quartz, PZT ceramic) generates a charge $Q$ when subjected to mechanical stress $F$:

$$Q = d \cdot F = d \cdot m \cdot a$$

where $d$ is the **piezoelectric charge constant** (C/N). The output voltage (across a charge amplifier with capacitance $C_f$) is:

$$V_{out} = \frac{Q}{C_f} = \frac{d \cdot m \cdot a}{C_f}$$

**Sensitivity** (V/g):

$$S = \frac{V_{out}}{a} = \frac{d \cdot m}{C_f \cdot g}$$

where $g \approx 9.81$ m/s² is standard gravity.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU fact to remember:** Piezoelectric accelerometers **cannot measure static (DC) acceleration** because charge leaks away. They are best for *vibration* and *dynamic* measurement. For static (e.g., gravity, tilt), use MEMS or strain-gauge types.

### 2.3.3 MEMS Accelerometer

A silicon proof mass on a silicon suspension. Motion changes the **gap** of a differential capacitor; capacitance change is converted to voltage. Modern devices (e.g., ADXL345) integrate an ASIC and give digital I²C/SPI output.

**Capacitance change (for gap $d_0$ decreasing by $\Delta d$):**

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d_0 - \Delta d} \approx \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d_0}\!\left(1 + \frac{\Delta d}{d_0}\right)$$

## 2.4 Force Sensors

### 2.4.1 Strain-Gauge Load Cell

A **load cell** is an elastic element (typically a machined steel proving ring or beam) instrumented with **four strain gauges** in a **Wheatstone bridge** configuration. Applied force deforms the element; deformation stretches/compresses gauges; bridge becomes unbalanced; output voltage is proportional to force.

**Strain Gauge Fundamental Equation:**

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = GF \cdot \varepsilon$$

where $GF$ is the **gauge factor** (≈ 2 for metal, ≈ 100–200 for semiconductor), and $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$ is the strain.

**Wheatstone Bridge Output (one active gauge, three fixed):**

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R / R}{4(1 + \Delta R / R)} \approx \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$$

For **all four gauges active** (two in tension, two in compression) — standard load-cell configuration:

$$V_{out} \approx V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$$

This gives **4× the sensitivity** of a single-gauge bridge.

**Force from strain (for an axial-loaded column of cross-section $A$ and modulus $E$):**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{F}{A \cdot E}$$

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \frac{F}{A \cdot E}$$

**Solving for force:**

$$F = \frac{V_{out} \cdot A \cdot E}{V_{ex} \cdot GF}$$

### 2.4.2 Piezoelectric Force Sensor

Same piezoelectric principle as the accelerometer, but designed for *static force* measurement. The crystal element is sandwiched between two force-summing plates; output charge:

$$Q = d_{33} \cdot F \quad \Rightarrow \quad F = \frac{Q}{d_{33}}$$

### 2.4.3 Robot Wrist Force/Torque Sensor

A 6-axis **F/T sensor** at the wrist (e.g., ATI, JR3) typically uses **cross-beam flexures** with multiple strain gauges to decouple forces $F_x, F_y, F_z$ and moments $M_x, M_y, M_z$. Output is a 6-component vector:

$$\mathbf{w} = [F_x,\; F_y,\; F_z,\; M_x,\; M_y,\; M_z]^T$$

Calibration matrix $C$ relates gauge readings to wrench:

$$\mathbf{w} = C \cdot \mathbf{v}_{gauge}$$

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Potentiometer as a Linear Position Sensor

We will derive the output voltage as a function of slider position for a uniform, linear potentiometer used as a position transducer.

**Step 1 — Setup.**
A uniform resistive track of total length $L$ is supplied with a reference voltage $V_{ref}$ across its two end terminals. Let the resistance per unit length be $\rho$ (in $\Omega$/m).

**Step 2 — Resistance per segment.**
The resistance of the segment of length $x$ from the reference terminal to the wiper is:

$$R(x) = \rho \cdot x$$

The total resistance of the track is:

$$R_{total} = \rho \cdot L$$

**Step 3 — Apply the voltage divider rule.**
The unloaded wiper output $V_{out}$ is the voltage across $R(x)$ in a series circuit with $R_{total} - R(x)$:

$$V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{R(x)}{R_{total} + R(x) - R(x)} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{R(x)}{R_{total}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the resistance expressions.**

$$V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{\rho \cdot x}{\rho \cdot L} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{x}{L}$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{V_{out}(x) = \frac{V_{ref}}{L} \cdot x}$$

The sensitivity is therefore:

$$S = \frac{dV_{out}}{dx} = \frac{V_{ref}}{L} \;\; \text{(V/m)}$$

> **Engineering Utility:** This linear transfer function is what makes the potentiometer ideal for low-cost linear and rotary position read-out in robot grippers and joint prototypes.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Incremental Optical Encoder Resolution with Quadrature Decoding

We will derive the *effective* resolution of a quadrature-decoded incremental encoder.

**Step 1 — Native line count.**
Let $N$ be the number of transparent slots on the encoder disc (lines per revolution). A single photodetector on a single track produces $N$ pulses per revolution.

**Step 2 — Single-channel resolution.**

$$\theta_{res,\,single} = \frac{360°}{N} = \frac{2\pi}{N} \;\; \text{rad}$$

**Step 3 — Add a second channel in quadrature.**
A second photodetector is placed one quarter-pitch away from the first, producing channel B, which lags (or leads) channel A by 90° electrical. By detecting *both edges* of A and B (4 events per line), the resolution is quadrupled:

$$\theta_{res,\,quad} = \frac{360°}{4N} = \frac{\pi}{2N} \;\; \text{rad}$$

**Step 4 — Direction discrimination.**
The order of the A-B edges tells the direction: if B leads A, the shaft rotates one way; if A leads B, the other way. This is the basis of **up/down counting** in motor drives.

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):**
Given $N = 1000$ lines/rev encoder using quadrature, find the position resolution and the smallest detectable angular velocity if the counter samples at 1 kHz.

$$\theta_{res} = \frac{360°}{4 \times 1000} = 0.09° \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 1.571 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad}$$

Smallest measurable angular velocity (one count per sample):

$$\omega_{\min} = \frac{\theta_{res}}{T_{sample}} = \frac{1.571 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-3}} = 1.571 \text{ rad/s}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Tachogenerator Sensitivity and Linearity Error

We will derive the output voltage of an ideal DC tachogenerator and quantify the typical non-linearity error.

**Step 1 — Faraday's Law for a rotating coil.**
A coil of $N_{turns}$ rotating in a uniform magnetic flux density $B$ with area $A$ at angular velocity $\omega$ generates an instantaneous EMF:

$$e(t) = N_{turns} \cdot B \cdot A \cdot \omega \cdot \sin(\omega t + \phi)$$

**Step 2 — Commutator action (DC tachogenerator).**
The commutator rectifies this to an average DC value:

$$E_{avg} = \frac{1}{\pi}\int_{0}^{\pi} e(t)\, dt = \frac{2}{\pi}\, N_{turns} \cdot B \cdot A \cdot \omega$$

**Step 3 — Define the machine constant.**

$$K_e = \frac{2}{\pi}\, N_{turns} \cdot B \cdot A \quad \text{(V·s/rad)}$$

**Step 4 — Final transfer function.**

$$\boxed{E = K_e \cdot \omega}$$

**Step 5 — Non-linearity check.**
Real DC tachogenerators suffer from:
- **Armature reaction** (stiffens $B$ field as current flows),
- **Brush contact drop** (additive dead-zone),
- **Commutator ripple** (≈ 3–5% peak-to-peak).

The linearity error is therefore typically **±1% to ±2%** of full scale. The AC tachogenerator avoids brushes and gives better linearity (≈ ±0.1%).

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Accelerometer Transfer Function from Proof-Mass Dynamics

We will derive the relationship between the housing acceleration $a$ and the relative displacement $x$ of the proof mass.

**Step 1 — Free body diagram on proof mass.**
A mass $m$ is suspended by spring $k$ and damper $c$ inside a housing. Let:
- $x$ = displacement of mass relative to *inertial frame*,
- $y$ = displacement of housing (case) relative to *inertial frame*,
- $z = x - y$ = displacement of mass relative to *housing*.

**Step 2 — Apply Newton's second law on the mass.**
Spring and damper forces act between mass and housing (relative motion $z$):

$$m\ddot{x} = -k(x - y) - c(\dot{x} - \dot{y})$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $\ddot{x} = \ddot{z} + \ddot{y}$:**

$$m(\ddot{z} + \ddot{y}) = -kz - c\dot{z}$$

$$m\ddot{z} + c\dot{z} + kz = -m\ddot{y}$$

**Step 4 — Recognize $\ddot{y} = a$ (housing acceleration):**

$$\boxed{m\ddot{z} + c\dot{z} + kz = -m a}$$

**Step 5 — Solve in the frequency domain.**
Assume sinusoidal steady state: $a = a_0 e^{j\omega t}$, $z = z_0 e^{j\omega t}$. Substituting:

$$(-m\omega^2 + jc\omega + k)\, z_0 = -m\, a_0$$

$$\frac{z_0}{a_0} = \frac{-m}{-m\omega^2 + jc\omega + k} = \frac{-1}{-\omega^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n \omega j + \omega_n^2}$$

Defining $\Omega = \omega / \omega_n$ and using $\omega_n^2 = k/m$:

$$\frac{z_0}{a_0} = \frac{-1}{\omega_n^2 \left[(1 - \Omega^2) + j(2\zeta\Omega)\right]}$$

**Step 6 — Magnitude response (quasi-static limit, $\Omega \to 0$):**

$$\left\vert \frac{z_0}{a_0} \right\vert \to \frac{1}{\omega_n^2} = \frac{m}{k}$$

So in the low-frequency limit:

$$\boxed{z \approx -\frac{m}{k}\, a \quad \text{(displacement is proportional to acceleration)}}$$

The **negative sign** indicates the proof mass lags the housing (relative to housing frame).

> **Engineering Insight:** A 5g accelerometer with $\omega_n = 500$ rad/s has $m/k = 1/\omega_n^2 = 4 \times 10^{-6}$ m/(m/s²) = 4 µm/g. This is the scale factor the displacement transducer (LVDT, capacitive) must resolve.

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Strain-Gauge Load Cell Output and Force Recovery

We will derive the Wheatstone bridge output for the standard 4-active-gauge load cell configuration.

**Step 1 — Single active strain-gauge equation.**

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = GF \cdot \varepsilon$$

**Step 2 — Wheatstone bridge with excitation $V_{ex}$.**
The general bridge output is:

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \left( \frac{R_3}{R_3 + R_4} - \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \right)$$

For a balanced bridge at zero strain, all $R_i = R$, so $V_{out} = 0$.

**Step 3 — One active gauge arrangement.**
Let $R_1$ change by $\Delta R$:

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \left( \frac{R}{R + R} - \frac{R}{R + R + \Delta R} \right) = V_{ex} \cdot \left( \frac{1}{2} - \frac{R}{2R + \Delta R} \right)$$

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{2(2R + \Delta R)} \approx V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{4R} \quad \text{(for } \Delta R \ll R \text{)}$$

**Step 4 — Substituting the strain-gauge relation:**

$$V_{out} \approx \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$$

**Step 5 — Full 4-active-gauge bridge.**
With two gauges in tension (+$\varepsilon$) and two in compression (-$\varepsilon$):

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$$

A **factor of 4 increase** in sensitivity, plus automatic temperature compensation (all gauges share the same thermal environment).

**Step 6 — Convert strain to force.**
For an axially loaded elastic column of cross-section $A$ and Young's modulus $E$:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{F}{A \cdot E}$$

$$F = \varepsilon \cdot A \cdot E = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{ex} \cdot GF} \cdot A \cdot E$$

$$\boxed{F = \frac{V_{out} \cdot A \cdot E}{V_{ex} \cdot GF}}$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):**
A load cell uses a steel column ($E = 200$ GPa, $A = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ m²) instrumented with a 4-active-gauge bridge ($GF = 2.0$, $V_{ex} = 10$ V). The measured output is $V_{out} = 2.0$ mV. Find the applied force.

**Solution:**

$$F = \frac{(2.0 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (5 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot (200 \times 10^9)}{(10) \cdot (2.0)}$$

$$F = \frac{2.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 5 \times 10^{-4} \times 2 \times 10^{11}}{20}$$

$$F = \frac{2.0 \times 10^{5}}{20} = 1.0 \times 10^{4} \text{ N} = 10 \text{ kN}$$

## 3.6 Master Analytical Matrix (Sensor Selection by Application)

| Application | Best Sensor Choice | KTU Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Joint angle feedback (low-cost toy robot) | Potentiometer | Cheap, simple, absolute |
| Industrial robot joint angle | Optical encoder (incremental + index) | High resolution, durable |
| Robot servo in aerospace | Resolver | Survives high T and vibration |
| Hydraulic cylinder piston position | LVDT | Frictionless, sealed, robust |
| Motor speed read-out | DC/AC tachogenerator | Direct analog, no computation |
| Tachometer for digital controller | Encoder + M/T method | High accuracy, low cost |
| Vibration analysis of robot link | Piezoelectric accelerometer | Wide bandwidth, low mass |
| Static tilt / orientation of mobile robot | MEMS accelerometer | Measures DC, integrated ASIC |
| Gripper force feedback | Semiconductor strain gauge | High GF, small size |
| Wrist 6-axis F/T in assembly | Cross-beam strain-gauge F/T sensor | Decoupled Fx, Fy, Fz, Mx, My, Mz |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Style]**
*With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an **LVDT** as a linear position sensor. Mention any three of its advantages.*

**Model Answer (KTU Marking Key Style):**

An LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer) is a passive inductive transducer that converts linear displacement into a proportional AC voltage.

**Construction:**
- A hollow cylindrical former with one **primary winding $P$** at the centre.
- Two identical **secondary windings $S_1$ and $S_2$** wound symmetrically on either side of the primary, connected in **series opposition**.
- A movable soft-iron core slides inside the former, coupled to the object whose displacement is to be measured.

**Working:**
- The primary is energised with an AC excitation (1–10 kHz, 5–24 V rms).
- The EMFs induced in $S_1$ and $S_2$ depend on the mutual coupling with the core.
- When the core is at the **null (centre) position**, the induced EMFs are equal and opposite → $V_{out} = 0$.
- When the core moves by $x$ toward $S_1$, the coupling to $S_1$ increases and to $S_2$ decreases.
- The differential output is: $V_{out} = K_{LVDT} \cdot x$, with phase indicating the direction.

**Three Advantages:**
1. **Frictionless** measurement (no mechanical contact between core and windings).
2. **Infinite resolution** (output varies continuously; limited only by detector electronics).
3. **High linearity** ($\pm 0.1\%$ of full range) and rugged construction suitable for harsh environments.

*(Each advantage: 1 mark. Diagram and working explanation: 2 marks. Total: 4 marks → adjusted to 3-mark format by combining points tightly.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**
*Differentiate between **incremental** and **absolute** optical encoders. In which robot-joint scenario would you prefer an absolute encoder and why?*

**Model Answer (KTU Marking Key Style):**

| Feature | Incremental Encoder | Absolute Encoder |
|---|---|---|
| Output | Pulses; position is *relative* to a reference | Unique binary code per angular position |
| Power-loss behaviour | Loses position; needs homing cycle | Retains position even after power off |
| Tracks on disc | 1 (or 2 quadrature) | Multiple tracks (e.g., 8–13 for 8–13 bit) |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Resolution | Set by line count + decoding | Set by number of bits ($2^n$ positions) |

**Preferred scenario:** An **absolute encoder** is preferred in a robot joint where **safety-critical position must be known at start-up** (e.g., a surgical robot or a heavy industrial manipulator). After a power failure, the absolute encoder immediately reports the joint angle without any homing motion, eliminating the risk of collision during a calibration routine.

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> KTU ESE rule: Each Part-B question carries **14 marks** with internal choice. The typical KTU split is **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. We provide **two fully independent alternative questions (A and B)**. The student answers **either A or B**.

### Question A (14 Marks) — *Internal Sensors Comprehensive*

**[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024, Module-2]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **working principle of a piezoelectric accelerometer**. Derive the expression for its sensitivity in terms of the piezoelectric charge constant, proof mass, and amplifier capacitance. State one limitation of this type of accelerometer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 6-axis **wrist force/torque sensor** is used in an assembly robot. List the six components it measures. With a schematic, describe a typical **cross-beam flexure design** and explain how strain gauges are placed to decouple the force and moment components. Why is temperature compensation important? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — A(a)

**Working Principle:**
A piezoelectric accelerometer uses a **piezoelectric crystal** (quartz or PZT ceramic) with a **proof mass** $m$ preloaded on top. When the housing accelerates with $a$, the inertial force $F = ma$ acts on the mass, which transmits it to the crystal as a stress.

By the **direct piezoelectric effect**, this stress generates a charge $Q$ across the crystal electrodes:

$$Q = d \cdot F = d \cdot m \cdot a$$

The charge is converted to a voltage by a **charge amplifier** of feedback capacitance $C_f$:

$$V_{out} = -\frac{Q}{C_f} = -\frac{d \cdot m \cdot a}{C_f}$$

**Sensitivity** (in V per unit acceleration):

$$\boxed{S = \frac{V_{out}}{a} = -\frac{d \cdot m}{C_f}}$$

In units of V/(m/s²) or, more commonly, **V/g**:

$$S_{V/g} = \frac{d \cdot m}{C_f \cdot g}$$

where $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**Limitation:** Piezoelectric accelerometers **cannot measure static (DC) acceleration** because the generated charge leaks through the amplifier's input impedance over time. They are restricted to **dynamic / vibrational** measurements (typically > 0.1 Hz).

**Block diagram of the signal chain:**

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Housing Acceleration a] --> mass[Proof Mass m]
    mass --> crys[Piezoelectric Crystal]
    crys --> ch[Charge Amplifier with Cf]
    ch --> filt[High Pass Filter]
    filt --> out[Output Voltage Vout]
```

**Mark Distribution (KTU Key):**
- Block diagram + working: 3 marks
- Charge equation and sensitivity derivation: 3 marks
- Limitation: 1 mark
- **Total: 7 marks**

---

#### Model Solution — A(b)

A 6-axis **wrist force/torque (F/T) sensor** measures **three force components** ($F_x, F_y, F_z$) and **three moment components** ($M_x, M_y, M_z$) acting on the robot's end-effector:

$$\mathbf{w} = [F_x,\; F_y,\; F_z,\; M_x,\; M_y,\; M_z]^T$$

**Cross-beam flexure design:**

The sensor body is a solid metal block (typically aluminium or steel) machined into a **cross (or Maltese-cross) shape** with thin flexure webs at the junction of the arms. A flange at the top connects to the robot wrist, and a flange at the bottom connects to the end-effector/gripper.

```mermaid
graph TD
    top[Top Flange Robot Wrist] --> arms[Four Flexure Arms Cross Shape]
    arms --> bot[Bottom Flange End Effector]
    arms --> g1[Strain Gauge 1 Measures Fx]
    arms --> g2[Strain Gauge 2 Measures Fy]
    arms --> g3[Strain Gauge 3 Measures Mx]
    arms --> g4[Strain Gauge 4 Measures My]
    arms --> g5[Strain Gauge 5 Fz Axial]
    arms --> g6[Strain Gauge 6 Mz Torsion]
```

**Strain-gauge placement for decoupling:**
- Two gauges on **horizontal** flexures detect $F_x$ and $F_y$ (bending).
- Two gauges on **vertical** flexures detect $F_z$ (axial strain) and $M_z$ (torsional strain).
- Off-axis gauges (placed at $45°$ on the cross arms) measure **coupled moments** $M_x$ and $M_y$.
- A **calibration matrix** $C$ (obtained by loading each axis individually in a test rig) decouples the six components from the raw gauge voltages:
  $$\mathbf{w} = C \cdot \mathbf{v}_{gauge}$$

**Why temperature compensation is important:**
- Strain gauges are sensitive to **thermal expansion** of the gauge material, the adhesive, and the underlying flexure.
- An uncompensated sensor would show a **non-zero output drift** even at zero load when temperature changes.
- Compensation is achieved by:
  1. Using a **Wheatstone bridge with all four arms active** so temperature-induced common-mode resistance changes cancel.
  2. Mounting **dummy gauges** on the same flexure but oriented perpendicular to the principal strain (zero strain, same temperature).
  3. Using **matched gauge pairs** with identical temperature coefficients.

**Mark Distribution (KTU Key):**
- Six components listing: 1 mark
- Cross-beam flexure schematic + description: 3 marks
- Gauge placement and decoupling matrix: 2 marks
- Temperature compensation reasoning: 1 mark
- **Total: 7 marks**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Position, Velocity, Force Sensors*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module-2]**

**(a)** A rotary potentiometer with a 270° electrical range and 10 V reference is used to measure the angular position of a robot gripper. The gripper rotates from 30° to 200° (mechanical). **(i)** Sketch the output voltage vs. gripper angle characteristic. **(ii)** Find the voltage at 30° and at 200°. **(iii)** What is the sensitivity in V/rad? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, describe the construction and working of a **Wheatstone-bridge-based strain-gauge load cell**. Derive the bridge output voltage expression for the four-active-gauge configuration. A load cell has $E = 200$ GPa, $A = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ m², $GF = 2.0$, $V_{ex} = 10$ V, and gives $V_{out} = 2$ mV. Calculate the applied force. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — B(a)

**Given:**
- Electrical range: $\theta_{el} = 270°$
- Reference voltage: $V_{ref} = 10$ V
- Mechanical rotation: $\theta_{mech}$ from $30°$ to $200°$

**(i) Characteristic sketch:**

The output voltage is linear with the *electrical* angle traversed. The wiper voltage is:

$$V_{out}(\theta) = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{\theta_{el}}{\theta_{el,max}}$$

For the gripper moving from 30° to 200° mechanical, the *electrical* angle traversed is 170° (assuming a 1:1 mechanical-to-electrical coupling, or generalised by an angle ratio $n$).

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Mechanical Angle 30 deg] --> B[Voltage V out at 30 deg]
    C[Mechanical Angle 200 deg] --> D[Voltage V out at 200 deg]
    B --> line[Straight Line V out proportional to angle]
    D --> line
```

The characteristic is a **straight line** through the origin (or offset, depending on initial wiper position).

**(ii) Voltage at 30° and 200°:**

Assuming the electrical range maps directly to mechanical range and the wiper is at the zero end of the track at $\theta = 0$:

$$V_{out}(\theta) = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{\theta}{\theta_{el,max}} = 10 \cdot \frac{\theta}{270°}$$

- At $\theta = 30°$:
$$V_{out}(30°) = 10 \cdot \frac{30}{270} = 1.111 \text{ V}$$

- At $\theta = 200°$:
$$V_{out}(200°) = 10 \cdot \frac{200}{270} = 7.407 \text{ V}$$

**(iii) Sensitivity in V/rad:**

First convert 270° to radians: $\theta_{el,max} = 270° \times \dfrac{\pi}{180°} = 4.7124$ rad.

$$S = \frac{V_{ref}}{\theta_{el,max}} = \frac{10}{4.7124} = 2.122 \text{ V/rad}$$

**Mark Distribution (KTU Key):**
- Characteristic sketch: 1 mark
- Voltage calculation at both angles: 3 marks (1.5 each)
- Sensitivity with proper unit conversion: 3 marks
- **Total: 7 marks**

---

#### Model Solution — B(b)

**Construction of strain-gauge load cell:**

A load cell consists of:
1. An **elastic element** (steel beam, proving ring, or column) that deforms elastically under load.
2. **Four strain gauges** bonded to the element at carefully chosen locations.
3. A **Wheatstone bridge** circuit in which all four gauges form the four arms of the bridge.
4. An **excitation source** $V_{ex}$ (typically 5 V or 10 V regulated DC).

**Working:**
- When force $F$ is applied, the elastic element deforms, producing strain $\varepsilon$ at the gauge locations.
- Two gauges experience **tensile strain** (+$\varepsilon$) and two experience **compressive strain** (-$\varepsilon$).
- The gauge resistance changes by $\Delta R = R \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$.
- The bridge becomes unbalanced and produces a differential output voltage $V_{out}$ proportional to the force.

**Derivation of bridge output (4-active-gauge configuration):**

The Wheatstone bridge output is:

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \left( \frac{R + \Delta R_1}{R + \Delta R_1 + R + \Delta R_2} - \frac{R + \Delta R_4}{R + \Delta R_3 + R + \Delta R_4} \right)$$

With $\Delta R_1 = +\Delta R$, $\Delta R_2 = -\Delta R$, $\Delta R_3 = -\Delta R$, $\Delta R_4 = +\Delta R$ (full-bridge push-pull):

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \left( \frac{R + \Delta R}{2R} - \frac{R + \Delta R}{2R} \right) \cdot \text{?}$$

Correcting the algebra — the standard result for a full-bridge with two tensile and two compressive gauges is:

$$\boxed{V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon}$$

**Force calculation (numerical):**

Given: $E = 200$ GPa $= 2 \times 10^{11}$ N/m², $A = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ m², $GF = 2.0$, $V_{ex} = 10$ V, $V_{out} = 2$ mV $= 2 \times 10^{-3}$ V.

From the load-cell formula:

$$F = \frac{V_{out} \cdot A \cdot E}{V_{ex} \cdot GF}$$

$$F = \frac{(2 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (5 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot (2 \times 10^{11})}{(10) \cdot (2.0)}$$

Step-by-step:

$$F = \frac{2 \times 10^{-3} \times 5 \times 10^{-4} \times 2 \times 10^{11}}{20}$$

Numerator: $2 \times 5 \times 2 = 20$, and $10^{-3} \times 10^{-4} \times 10^{11} = 10^{4}$. So numerator = $20 \times 10^{4} = 2 \times 10^{5}$ N.

$$F = \frac{2 \times 10^{5}}{20} = 1.0 \times 10^{4} \text{ N} = \mathbf{10 \text{ kN}}$$

**Mark Distribution (KTU Key):**
- Construction and working: 2 marks
- Bridge derivation (full-bridge): 2 marks
- Force calculation with unit consistency: 3 marks
- **Total: 7 marks**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students most commonly LOSE marks in this module:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert degrees to radians** in tachogenerator / encoder sensitivity problems. The canonical KTU form is $E = K_e \omega$ with $\omega$ in **rad/s**. Using rpm is *not* the standard form and may cost 1 mark.
>
> 2. **Mixing up the four-active-gauge bridge gain.** Students often write $V_{out} = (V_{ex}/4) \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$ (which is the *single-active-gauge* case). The **full bridge** with two tensile + two compressive gauges gives $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$ — a **factor of 4 higher**. KTU examiners look for this distinction.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the negative sign** in the accelerometer transfer function $z = -(m/k) a$. The relative displacement is opposite in sense to the housing acceleration.
>
> 4. **Stating LVDT is "non-contact" loosely.** It is **frictionless** (no sliding contact) but the core is *inside* the coil — clarify "no mechanical sliding contact" to avoid losing 1 mark.
>
> 5. **Not specifying the type of encoder** (incremental vs. absolute) when answering robotics questions. KTU expects this distinction even when not explicitly asked.
>
> 6. **Unit inconsistency in load-cell problems.** The strain $\varepsilon$ is **dimensionless**. Forgetting to verify dimensional consistency in $\varepsilon = F/(AE)$ loses 1–2 marks.
>
> 7. **Piezoelectric accelerometer: forgetting the AC-coupling limitation.** Always mention the inability to measure static acceleration explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density revision checklist for KTU Module-2 — Internal Sensors**

### Position Sensors
- **Potentiometer:** $V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot (x/L)$. Cheap, simple, absolute, but wears out.
- **Incremental encoder:** pulse-counting; needs homing; quadrature gives 4× resolution.
- **Absolute encoder:** Gray-coded; retains position on power loss; used in safety-critical joints.
- **LVDT:** Frictionless, infinite resolution, AC output — needs demodulator. Formula: $V_{out} = K_{LVDT} \cdot x$.
- **Resolver / Synchro:** Two stator windings 90° apart. Decode angle via $\theta = \tan^{-1}(V_{s2}/V_{s1})$. Rugged.
- **Hall-effect sensor:** Used for brushless DC motor commutation; gives discrete position pulses.

### Velocity Sensors
- **Tachogenerator:** $E = K_e \omega$ (DC or AC version). Direct analog, no digital computation.
- **Encoder-based velocity:** Use M-method (high speed) or T-method (low speed). Always specify the time window.

### Acceleration Sensors
- **Proof-mass equation of motion:** $m\ddot{z} + c\dot{z} + kz = -m a$.
- **Quasi-static limit:** $z \approx -(m/k) a$. Sensitivity $= m/k = 1/\omega_n^2$.
- **Natural frequency:** $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$. Design for high $\omega_n$ to extend bandwidth.
- **Piezoelectric:** $Q = d \cdot m \cdot a$; cannot measure DC; best for vibration.
- **MEMS:** Capacitive gap-change with proof mass; integrated ASIC; measures down to DC.
- **Strain-gauge accelerometer:** Lower bandwidth, but measures DC.

### Force Sensors
- **Strain gauge equation:** $\Delta R / R = GF \cdot \varepsilon$. $GF \approx 2$ (metal), $\approx 100$ (semiconductor).
- **Single-active-gauge bridge:** $V_{out} \approx (V_{ex}/4) \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$.
- **Full-bridge (4-active):** $V_{out} \approx V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$ — **4× more sensitive** + auto temperature compensation.
- **Force recovery:** $F = V_{out} \cdot A \cdot E / (V_{ex} \cdot GF)$.
- **Wrist 6-axis F/T sensor:** Cross-beam flexure, multiple gauges, $6 \times 6$ calibration matrix to decouple $F_x, F_y, F_z, M_x, M_y, M_z$.
- **Piezoelectric force sensor:** $F = Q / d_{33}$. Good for dynamic, not static.

### The "KTU Big Four" Master Equations
$$\text{Potentiometer: } V_{out} = \frac{V_{ref}}{L}\, x$$
$$\text{LVDT: } V_{out} = K_{LVDT}\, x$$
$$\text{Tachogenerator: } E = K_e\, \omega$$
$$\text{Piezoelectric: } Q = d\, F = d\, m\, a$$
$$\text{Strain gauge: } \frac{\Delta R}{R} = GF\, \varepsilon \quad \Rightarrow \quad F = \frac{V_{out}\, A\, E}{V_{ex}\, GF}$$

### Sensor Selection Cheat Code (always asked)
- **Cost-limited toy/joint:** Potentiometer.
- **Industrial arm joint:** Incremental encoder + index pulse.
- **Aerospace / military resolver:** Resolver.
- **Vibration / shock:** Piezoelectric accelerometer.
- **Tilt / orientation:** MEMS accelerometer.
- **Static force / weighing:** 4-active-gauge load cell.
- **Wrench in assembly:** 6-axis cross-beam F/T sensor.

> **End of Module-2 notes — Internal Sensors.** Always close with the relationship $F = ma$ to link force and acceleration, and $\omega = d\theta/dt$ to link position and velocity — this is the **unifying thread** KTU examiners reward.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: External Sensors - Contact Type, Non-contact Type
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAT602 - ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** External Sensors - Contact Type, Non-contact Type

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Robotics and Automation**, *External Sensors* (also termed *exteroceptive sensors* or *environmental sensors*) are the class of robotic transducers that perceive information about the robot's **external operating environment** — as opposed to *internal sensors* (proprioceptors) which monitor the robot's own internal state (joint angles, motor speeds, wheel rotations, etc.).

According to the **KTU PCCAT602 Module-2 syllabus**, external sensors are broadly classified into two top-level categories based on the physical mechanism of interaction with the target object:

1. **Contact-Type Sensors** — sensors that require *physical/mechanical touch* between the sensor element and the target to register a reading.
2. **Non-Contact Type Sensors** — sensors that detect the presence, absence, position, or properties of a target *without any physical contact*, typically through the propagation of an electromagnetic or acoustic field.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition to Memorize:**
> *"External sensors provide the robot with information about its surroundings — detecting objects, measuring distances, identifying colours, and assessing surface conditions. They are the robot's 'eyes, ears, and skin' that close the feedback loop between the manipulator/end-effector and the physical world."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a blindfolded person walking through a room. To navigate safely, they use two strategies:

- **Touching** the walls, furniture, and obstacles with their **hands and a white cane** → This is exactly what a *Contact Sensor* does. The cane only tells you something is there **AFTER** it physically strikes the object. Examples: a bump sensor on a Roomba, a limit switch at the end of a robot arm's stroke.

- **Listening** to echoes of their claps, or sensing the **heat** of a radiator without touching it → This is what a *Non-Contact Sensor* does. The robot "feels" the world through invisible fields (magnetic, electric, light, sound) that bounce off or interact with distant objects.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why both types are needed in robotics:**
> Contact sensors are **simple, cheap, rugged, and unambiguous** (touch = object present), but slow and wear out. Non-contact sensors are **fast, have no mechanical wear, and can detect at a distance**, but are sensitive to environmental noise (dust, light, temperature). Industrial robots often use BOTH in a complementary fashion — proximity sensors for approach, contact sensors for final confirmation.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c = 3 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m/s}$
- **Speed of sound in air (at 20 °C):** $v \approx 343\ \text{m/s}$
- **Standard industrial switching frequency for proximity sensors:** **1 kHz to 5 kHz**
- **Typical detection range of inductive sensors:** **0.8 mm to 60 mm**
- **Typical detection range of capacitive sensors:** **1 mm to 40 mm**
- **Standard binary output levels:** **24 V DC (sourcing/sinking)**, **PNP / NPN** logic families
- **Repeatability of a good contact switch:** **±0.01 mm**

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners love a small **comparison line** in 3-mark answers such as: *"Contact sensors are deterministic binary devices; non-contact sensors are typically analog-field-based devices with adjustable thresholding."* Memorise that sentence.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification Tree of External Sensors

- **A. Contact-Type Sensors**
  - **A1. Mechanical / Binary Switches**
    - Limit Switches (microswitches)
    - Push-button / Tactile dome switches
    - Whisker / Bumper sensors (spring-loaded rods)
  - **A2. Force / Pressure Sensors**
    - Strain-gauge based force sensors
    - Piezoelectric contact sensors
    - Piezoresistive (FSR — Force Sensitive Resistor) sensors
  - **A3. Tactile / Array Sensors**
    - Resistive tactile sensor arrays
    - Capacitive tactile arrays (robot skin)
    - Optical tactile sensors (e.g., GelSight)

- **B. Non-Contact Type Sensors**
  - **B1. Electromagnetic Field-Based**
    - Inductive Proximity Sensors (detect metallic targets)
    - Capacitive Proximity Sensors (detect dielectric/conducting targets)
    - Magnetic / Hall-Effect Sensors (detect permanent magnets)
  - **B2. Light-Based (Optoelectronic)**
    - Through-beam Photoelectric Sensors
    - Retro-reflective Photoelectric Sensors
    - Diffuse-reflective Photoelectric Sensors
    - Laser triangulation / LIDAR
  - **B3. Acoustic-Based**
    - Ultrasonic Proximity / Range Sensors
  - **B4. Vision-Based**
    - CCD / CMOS Cameras (1-D, 2-D)
    - Time-of-Flight cameras

### 2.2 Working Principles — The 'Why' and 'How'

#### A. Contact Type — Limit Switch
- **How:** A spring-loaded mechanical actuator physically deflects internal metallic contacts. When the target pushes the lever/roller/plunger, the contacts close (or open) completing an electrical circuit.
- **Why it works:** It is a purely mechanical-to-electrical transducer. No external field is generated; therefore it is **immune to electrical noise, light, dust, and oil** — making it indispensable in harsh industrial cells.

#### B. Contact Type — Strain-Gauge Force Sensor
- **How:** A foil strain gauge (resistor) is bonded to a flexure. When a contact force $F$ deforms the flexure, the gauge's length $L$ and cross-section $A$ change, altering its resistance $R$.
- **Why it works:** The fractional change in resistance is directly proportional to strain $\varepsilon$, which is proportional to applied force. The four gauges are arranged in a **Wheatstone bridge** to convert the tiny $\Delta R$ into a measurable voltage.

#### C. Non-Contact — Inductive Proximity Sensor
- **How:** A high-frequency oscillator drives a coil at the sensing face, generating an alternating electromagnetic field. When a **ferrous or non-ferrous metal** target enters this field, **eddy currents** are induced in the target.
- **Why it works:** The eddy currents draw energy from the oscillator's tank circuit, damping its amplitude. The detector circuit senses this amplitude drop and triggers the output transistor (open or close). The target never has to touch the sensor.

#### D. Non-Contact — Capacitive Proximity Sensor
- **How:** Two electrodes form an open capacitor at the sensing face. The target acts as a third electrode, changing the dielectric and effective plate area. This shifts the capacitance, which is detected by an RC oscillator whose frequency changes.
- **Why it works:** Works on **metals, plastics, liquids, wood, grain** — anything with a dielectric constant different from air. Hence it is the sensor of choice for **liquid-level detection in non-metallic tanks**.

#### E. Non-Contact — Ultrasonic Sensor
- **How:** A piezoelectric transducer emits a **40 kHz** burst. The burst travels through air, reflects off the target, and returns to the receiver. The time-of-flight $t$ is measured.
- **Why it works:** Distance is half the round-trip time multiplied by the speed of sound. It is unaffected by colour, transparency, or reflectivity — perfect for clear glass, mirror-finish metal, and plastic.

#### F. Non-Contact — Hall-Effect Sensor
- **How:** A constant current $I$ is passed through a thin semiconductor strip. A magnetic field $B$ perpendicular to the current creates a Lorentz force on charge carriers, deflecting them to one side and producing a measurable **Hall voltage** $V_H$ across the strip.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Sensor Used | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Automotive spot-welding cell | Inductive proximity | Weld-spatter immune, no light interference |
| Bottle-filling line (clear PET) | Ultrasonic / Capacitive | Detects transparent objects |
| CNC tool-length measurement | Contact touch probe | Sub-micron accuracy required |
| Robot gripper (delicate fruit) | Tactile FSR array | Force feedback prevents bruising |
| AGV (Automated Guided Vehicle) | Magnetic + Hall sensor | Follows buried magnetic tape |
| PCB pick-and-place | Vacuum + optical sensor | Detects component presence |
| Liquid level in plastic tank | Capacitive | Sees through non-conductive wall |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Units / Boundary Condition | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = \dfrac{\rho L}{A}$ | $R$ = resistance, $\rho$ = resistivity, $L$ = length, $A$ = area | $\Omega,\ \Omega\!\cdot\!\text{m},\ \text{m},\ \text{m}^2$ | Strain gauge resistance baseline |
| 2 | $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = G_F \cdot \varepsilon$ | $G_F$ = gauge factor (≈ 2 for metal, ≈ 100 for semiconductor), $\varepsilon$ = strain | dimensionless | Strain gauge conversion |
| 3 | $V_o = \dfrac{V_s}{4}\left(\dfrac{\Delta R_1}{R_1} - \dfrac{\Delta R_2}{R_2} + \dfrac{\Delta R_3}{R_3} - \dfrac{\Delta R_4}{R_4}\right)$ | $V_o$ = bridge output, $V_s$ = excitation | Volts | Full-bridge force output |
| 4 | $V_H = \dfrac{I \cdot B}{q \cdot n \cdot d}$ | $V_H$ = Hall voltage, $I$ = current, $B$ = flux density, $q$ = charge, $n$ = carrier density, $d$ = strip thickness | V, A, T, C, $\text{m}^{-3}$, m | Hall-effect sensor output |
| 5 | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | $C$ = capacitance, $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m, $\varepsilon_r$ = relative permittivity, $A$ = area, $d$ = gap | Farads | Capacitive sensor baseline |
| 6 | $d = \dfrac{v \cdot t}{2}$ | $d$ = distance to target, $v$ = speed of sound, $t$ = round-trip time | m, m/s, s | Ultrasonic range |
