The robot maintains a 2-D occupancy grid histogram of obstacle density around itself, projects it into a 1-D polar histogram, and selects the valley (lowest density sector) closest to the goal direction. ### Pillar 3 — Bug Algorithms The simplest reactive paradigm — a family of algorithms (**Bug1, Bug2, Bug2+**) where the robot moves *straight toward the goal* until it **hits** an obstacle, then **follows the obstacle boundary** until the goal becomes reachable again. --- ## 2.2 Operational Logic — Potential Field Method (Step-by-Step) Let the robot's position be $\mathbf{q} = [x, y]^T$, the goal be $\mathbf{q}_{goal} = [x_g, y_g]^T$, and the $k$-th obstacle have centre $\mathbf{q}_{obs,k} = [x_k, y_k]^T$ with influence radius $\rho_0$. **Step 1 — Attractive Potential** A parabolic well centered on the goal: $$U_{att}(\mathbf{q}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\xi \,\bigl\lVert \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{goal}\bigr\rVert^{\,2}$$ where $\xi > 0$ is the *attractive gain* (N/m). **Step 2 — Repulsive Potential** For every obstacle within influence radius $\rho_0$: $$U_{rep,k}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{cases} \tfrac{1}{2}\,\eta \!\left(\dfrac{1}{\rho(\mathbf{q},\mathbf{q}_{obs,k})} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^{\!2}, & \rho(\mathbf{q},\mathbf{q}_{obs,k}) \le \rho_0 \\ 0, & \rho(\mathbf{q},\mathbf{q}_{obs,k}) > \rho_0 \end{cases}$$ where $\eta > 0$ is the *repulsive gain* and $\rho(\mathbf{q}, \mathbf{q}_{obs,k})$ is the Euclidean distance from the robot to the obstacle centre. **Step 3 — Total Potential** $$U_{total}(\mathbf{q}) = U_{att}(\mathbf{q}) + \sum_{k=1}^{N} U_{rep,k}(\mathbf{q})$$ **Step 4 — Control Force** $$\mathbf{F}(\mathbf{q}) = -\nabla U_{total}(\mathbf{q}) = \mathbf{F}_{att} + \sum_{k=1}^{N} \mathbf{F}_{rep,k}$$ **Step 5 — Robot Velocity Command** For a holonomic robot with no dynamics, the commanded velocity is proportional to the force: $$\mathbf{v}_c = \mathbf{F}(\mathbf{q})$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why a gradient?** Moving opposite to the gradient is the *steepest descent* on the potential surface — physically, the robot rolls "downhill" into the goal well while being "pushed uphill" by obstacles. --- ## 2.3 The Local Minima Problem A **local minimum** occurs when the attractive force exactly cancels the repulsive force at a point that is **not** the goal: $$\mathbf{F}_{att}(\mathbf{q}^*) + \sum_{k} \mathbf{F}_{rep,k}(\mathbf{q}^*) = \mathbf{0}, \quad \mathbf{q}^* \neq \mathbf{q}_{goal}$$ The robot gets trapped and oscillates. This is the central failure mode of pure PFM. Remedies include: - **Random walk** perturbation when $|\mathbf{F}| < \epsilon$. - **Virtual obstacle / virtual goal** placement. - **VFH / Bug algorithms** — they avoid the problem by *switching* behaviours rather than summing forces. --- ## 2.4 Subsumption Architecture (Behaviour Layering) Brooks' subsumption architecture stacks reactive behaviours as **finite-state machines** in layers of increasing competence: | Layer (Bottom → Top) | Behaviour | Competence | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Layer 0 | *Avoid Obstacles* | Safest motion | | Layer 1 | *Wander* | Explore | | Layer 2 | *Build Map* (basic) | Crude localization | | Layer 3 | *Reach Goal* | Goal seeking | | Layer 4 | *Plan Path* (deliberative) | Global reasoning | Higher layers can *subsume* (override/inhibit) lower layers. This gives the robot reactive speed at the bottom and deliberative intelligence at the top — the foundation of modern **hybrid architectures**. --- ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Formula Reference — Print & Memorize** | # | Formula / Quantity | Physical Meaning | Units | | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $U_{att} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\xi\,d^2$, where $d = \lVert \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{goal}\rVert$ | Attractive potential energy | J | | 2 | $\mathbf{F}_{att} = -\nabla U_{att} = -\xi(\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{goal})$ | Attractive (goal-pulling) force | N | | 3 | $U_{rep} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\eta\!\left(\tfrac{1}{\rho} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^{2}$ if $\rho \le \rho_0$, else $0$ | Repulsive potential energy | J | | 4 | $\mathbf{F}_{rep} = \eta\!\left(\tfrac{1}{\rho} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)\!\left(\tfrac{1}{\rho^{2}}\right)\!\nabla\rho$ | Repulsive (obstacle-pushing) force | N | | 5 | $\mathbf{F}_{total} = \mathbf{F}_{att} + \sum \mathbf{F}_{rep,k}$ | Net artificial force on robot | N | | 6 | $\mathbf{v}_{cmd} = k_v \cdot \mathbf{F}_{total}$ | Commanded linear velocity | m/s | | 7 | $P_{obstacle}(\theta_i) = \sum_{k} H_i \cdot a_{ik}$ | VFH polar histogram bin probability | dimensionless | | 8 | $\omega_{cmd} = k_\omega (\theta_{valley} - \theta_{heading})$ | Bug-following turn rate | rad/s | | 9 | $d_{m-line} = \lVert \mathbf{q}_{start}\mathbf{q}_{goal}\rVert$ | M-line length (Bug2 reference) | m | | 10 | $W_{smooth}(\theta) = \sum_{i} \mu_i \, P(\theta_i) \, e^{-\alpha \lvert \theta - \theta_i \rvert}$ | VFH steering smoothing cost | dimensionless | > *Constants / Gains used in assignments:* $\xi \in [0.5, 5.0]$ N/m, $\eta \in [50, 500]$ N, $\rho_0 \in [0.5, 3.0]$ m. --- ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application Domain | Reactive Method Used | Reason | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Domestic vacuum cleaners (Roomba) | Random walk + Bumper | No map needed, cheap | | Warehouse AGVs (Amazon Kiva) | Bounded PFM + fiducial markers | Real-time aisle traffic | | Autonomous racing drones (AirSim) | VFH-style depth avoidance | 1 kHz control loop | | Planetary rovers (Mars path) | Bug2 + slip detection | Unknown terrain | | Self-driving cars (urban) | Hybrid: PFM reactive layer + HD-map deliberative | Safety critical | # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Complete Derivation — Potential Field Force Equations We derive the attractive and repulsive force vectors explicitly in Cartesian coordinates. ### 3.1.1 Attractive Force Derivation Let the distance function be $$d(\mathbf{q}) = \sqrt{(x - x_g)^{2} + (y - y_g)^{2}} = \lVert \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{goal}\rVert$$ The attractive potential is parabolic: $$U_{att}(\mathbf{q}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\xi\,d^{2}(\mathbf{q})$$ To find the force, we apply the gradient operator $\nabla = \left[\dfrac{\partial}{\partial x},\; \dfrac{\partial}{\partial y}\right]^{T}$: $$\nabla U_{att} = \xi\,d \cdot \nabla d$$ But $$\dfrac{\partial d}{\partial x} = \dfrac{x - x_g}{d}, \qquad \dfrac{\partial d}{\partial y} = \dfrac{y - y_g}{d}$$ Therefore: $$\nabla U_{att} = \xi\,d \left[\dfrac{x-x_g}{d},\; \dfrac{y-y_g}{d}\right]^{T} = \xi\,(\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{goal})$$ Applying the definition $\mathbf{F} = -\nabla U$: $$\boxed{\;\mathbf{F}_{att}(\mathbf{q}) = -\xi\,(\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{goal}) = \xi\,(\mathbf{q}_{goal} - \mathbf{q})\;}$$ > *Interpretation:* The force is a **linear spring** pulling the robot toward the goal — magnitude grows with distance, direction is always along the line joining robot and goal. --- ### 3.1.2 Repulsive Force Derivation Let the distance to the $k$-th obstacle centre be $$\rho_k(\mathbf{q}) = \sqrt{(x - x_k)^{2} + (y - y_k)^{2}}$$ The repulsive potential (only active when $\rho_k \le \rho_0$): $$U_{rep,k}(\mathbf{q}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\eta\left(\dfrac{1}{\rho_k} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^{2}$$ Let us define the shorthand $\gamma_k = \left(\dfrac{1}{\rho_k} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)$, so $U_{rep,k} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\eta\,\gamma_k^{2}$. By the chain rule: $$\nabla U_{rep,k} = \eta\,\gamma_k \cdot \nabla\gamma_k$$ Now, $$\nabla\gamma_k = \dfrac{\partial}{\partial \mathbf{q}}\!\left(\rho_k^{-1}\right) = -\rho_k^{-2}\,\nabla\rho_k = -\rho_k^{-2}\,\dfrac{\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{obs,k}}{\rho_k} = -\dfrac{\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{obs,k}}{\rho_k^{3}}$$ Therefore: $$\nabla U_{rep,k} = -\eta\,\gamma_k \cdot \dfrac{\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{obs,k}}{\rho_k^{3}}$$ Applying $\mathbf{F}_{rep,k} = -\nabla U_{rep,k}$: $$\boxed{\;\mathbf{F}_{rep,k}(\mathbf{q}) = \eta\left(\dfrac{1}{\rho_k} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)\dfrac{\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{obs,k}}{\rho_k^{3}}\;}$$ The force magnitude grows **without bound** as $\rho_k \to 0$ (collision), preventing the robot from penetrating the obstacle. The direction is along $\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{obs,k}$ — *away* from the obstacle. --- ### 3.1.3 Total Control Law $$\mathbf{F}_{total}(\mathbf{q}) = \xi(\mathbf{q}_{goal} - \mathbf{q}) + \sum_{k \,:\, \rho_k \le \rho_0} \eta\left(\dfrac{1}{\rho_k} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)\dfrac{\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{obs,k}}{\rho_k^{3}}$$ For a differential-drive robot, the commanded *linear* velocity $v_c$ and *angular* velocity $\omega_c$ are obtained by projecting the force vector onto the robot's heading: $$v_c = k_v \cdot \lVert \mathbf{F}_{total}\rVert \cdot \cos(\phi)$$ $$\omega_c = k_\omega \cdot \phi$$ where $\phi$ is the angle between $\mathbf{F}_{total}$ and the robot's current forward axis. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example (Board-Exam Style) > **Problem:** A point robot at $\mathbf{q} = [2,\,3]^{T}$ m must reach $\mathbf{q}_{g} = [10,\,8]^{T}$ m. A single circular obstacle is at $\mathbf{q}_{obs} = [4,\,4]^{T}$ m with influence radius $\rho_0 = 2$ m. Take $\xi = 1$ N/m and $\eta = 50$ N. Compute the resultant force vector. **Step 1 — Attractive force:** $$\mathbf{F}_{att} = \xi(\mathbf{q}_g - \mathbf{q}) = 1 \cdot ([10,8]^{T} - [2,3]^{T}) = [8,\;5]^{T}\ \text{N}$$ **Step 2 — Distance to obstacle:** $$\rho = \sqrt{(2-4)^{2} + (3-4)^{2}} = \sqrt{4 + 1} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236 \text{ m}$$ **Step 3 — Check activation:** $\rho = 2.236 > \rho_0 = 2$. Therefore **the obstacle is NOT in range**. $$U_{rep} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mathbf{F}_{rep} = \mathbf{0}$$ **Step 4 — Total force:** $$\mathbf{F}_{total} = [8,\;5]^{T} + [0,\;0]^{T} = [8,\;5]^{T}\ \text{N}$$ **Step 5 — Magnitude & direction:** $$\lVert \mathbf{F}\rVert = \sqrt{8^{2}+5^{2}} = \sqrt{89} \approx 9.43\ \text{N}$$ $$\phi = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{5}{8}\right) \approx 32.0^{\circ}$$ > *Conclusion:* The robot accelerates diagonally toward the goal at $\sim 32^{\circ}$ above the x-axis. As it approaches $\mathbf{q} = [4,\,4]^{T}$, $\rho$ will drop below $\rho_0$ and a repulsive force will steer it around the obstacle. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Property | Potential Field | VFH | Bug Algorithms | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **State** | Memoryless | Polar histogram (small) | Two states (go-to-goal / wall-follow) | | **Optimality** | Local | Local | Provable *upper-bound* path length | | **Local Minima** | Yes (critical flaw) | Largely avoided | Cannot be trapped indefinitely | | **Sensor Need** | Range to each obstacle | Dense range scan | Single contact / range ray | | **Tuning Effort** | High ($\xi, \eta, \rho_0$) | Medium (bins, thresholds) | Very Low | | **Computational Cost** | $O(N_{obs})$ per step | $O(N_{cells})$ per step | $O(1)$ per step | | **Best For** | Dense dynamic environments | Real sonar/lidar robots | Theoretical worst-case bounds | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Reactive Navigation — Control Loop Architecture The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the *Sense → Act* loop that defines reactive navigation, contrasted with the deliberative *Sense → Plan → Act* chain. ```mermaid flowchart LR sA[Goal Coordinates] --> sum sB[Ultrasonic Sensor Array] --> proc[Distance Processor] proc --> obsDist[Obstacle Distances] obsDist --> sum sum --> pf[Potential Field
Computation] pf --> grad[Gradient Descent
Force Vector] grad --> ctrl[Motor Velocity Controller] ctrl --> act[Wheel Actuators] act -.-> move[Robot Motion in Environment] move -.-> sB style pf fill:#f9d77e,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style grad fill:#a8d5ba,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style ctrl fill:#f4a4a4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` > *Reading:* Solid arrows show the **perception-action loop** (runs at the robot's control tick, typically 10–100 Hz). The dotted arrow represents the **physical world**, completing the cybernetic loop. --- ## 4.2 Subsumption Architecture — Behaviour Layers ```mermaid flowchart TB L5[Layer 5 — Plan Mission] -->|inhibit| L4 L4[Layer 4 — Reason About Map] -->|inhibit| L3 L3[Layer 3 — Reach Goal] -->|inhibit| L2 L2[Layer 2 — Localize] -->|inhibit| L1 L1[Layer 1 — Wander Randomly] -->|inhibit| L0 L0[Layer 0 — Avoid Contact] Sensors[IR Bumpers Sonars Camera] --> L0 Sensors --> L1 Sensors --> L2 Sensors --> L3 Sensors --> L4 Sensors --> L5 L0 --> Actuators[Wheel Motors] L1 --> Actuators L2 --> Actuators L3 --> Actuators L4 --> Actuators L5 --> Actuators style L0 fill:#f4a4a4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style L3 fill:#f9d77e,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style L5 fill:#a8d5ba,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` > *Reading:* Lower layers run continuously (innate reflexes). Higher layers can **inhibit** (suppress) or **subsume** (override) lower outputs. This is the canonical reactive control hierarchy taught in KTU Module 4. --- ## 4.3 Potential Field — Functional Force-Sum Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TD q[Robot Pose q x y] --> dGoal{Compute d to Goal} q --> dObs{Compute rho to Each Obstacle} dGoal --> fAtt[Attractive Force
Fatt = xi times qg minus q] dObs --> check{rho less than rho0} check -->|Yes| fRep[Repulsive Force
Frep k as derived] check -->|No| zero[Zero Force] fAtt --> sum[Vector Summation] fRep --> sum zero --> sum sum --> cmd[Velocity Command vc omega c] cmd --> robot[Robot Wheels Move] robot -.-> q style fAtt fill:#a8d5ba,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style fRep fill:#f4a4a4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style cmd fill:#f9d77e,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` --- ## 4.4 Bug2 Algorithm — Finite-State Schematic ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> GoToGoal GoToGoal: STATE 1 — Go To Goal WallFollow: STATE 2 — Wall Follow GoToGoal --> WallFollow : Contact obstacle boundary WallFollow --> GoToGoal : Goal direction clear and closer than start distance WallFollow --> WallFollow : Keep obstacle on chosen side GoToGoal --> [*] : Reach goal radius WallFollow --> [*] : Provable loop termination ``` > *Reading:* Bug2 alternates between two pure reactive states — no map needed; guaranteed to terminate if a path exists. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PCCAT602, Module 4]** *Define Reactive Navigation. List its two most important advantages over deliberative navigation.* **Model Answer:** Reactive Navigation is a robot control paradigm in which the motion command is generated **directly from instantaneous sensor data**, without using a pre-built global world model or re-planning. The robot follows a tight **Sense–Act** loop: every control cycle, sensor readings are processed into a control command, typically in a stateless fashion. **Two key advantages:** 1. **Real-time response** — Since no path planning is performed, reaction times are bounded by the sensor sampling period (often < 10 ms), making the robot safe in dynamic and human-shared environments. 2. **Robustness to uncertainty** — Because the controller depends only on the *current* sensor frame, any change in the environment (moving obstacles, sensor noise) is automatically accommodated. There is no stale map to invalidate. > **Examiner's note (3-mark mapping):** Definition (1 mark) + Adv-1 with brief justification (1 mark) + Adv-2 with brief justification (1 mark). --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PCCAT602, Module 4]** *State and briefly explain the **Subsumption Architecture** of reactive robots. Who proposed it?* **Model Answer:** The **Subsumption Architecture** was proposed by **Rodney A. Brooks (1986, MIT)** as a layered, behaviour-based control framework for autonomous robots. It decomposes robot intelligence into **horizontal layers of competence**, ordered from low-level reflexes at the bottom to high-level reasoning at the top. - **Layer 0 — Avoid** (reflexive obstacle avoidance) - **Layer 1 — Wander** (random exploration) - **Layer 2 — Map** (build a crude local map) - **Layer 3 — Reach-Goal** (goal-seeking) - **Layer 4 — Plan** (deliberative mission planning) Each higher layer can *subsume* (override) or *inhibit* (suppress) the output of any layer below it. Lower layers operate continuously and provide immediate reactive safety, while higher layers add goal-directed intelligence — yielding a robust, hybrid reactive-deliberative system. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on Reactive Navigation Questions** > 1. **Forgetting units** in the force equations — the gradient of a *potential (Joules)* yields a *force (Newtons)*; students often drop the gain $\xi$ and $\eta$ units and lose 2 marks. > 2. **Conflating "memoryless" with "no sensors"** — reactive means *no world model*, **not** no sensors. Examiners explicitly test this misconception. > 3. **Confusing attractive/repulsive signs** — Repulsive force must point **away** from the obstacle. Writing $(\mathbf{q}_{obs} - \mathbf{q})$ instead of $(\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{obs})$ is a frequent 2-mark deduction. > 4. **Skipping the local minima discussion** — Any PFM answer that omits the *local minima* flaw scores a maximum of 9/14. > 5. **Mixing up Bug1 vs Bug2** — Bug1 leaves the obstacle at the *closest hit point*; Bug2 leaves at the *first point on the M-line closer to goal*. Examiners test this directly. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — OPTION 1 **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PCCAT602, Module 4]** **(a)** Explain the **Potential Field Method** of reactive navigation in detail. Derive the expressions for the attractive and repulsive forces acting on the robot, clearly stating the role of all parameters. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With reference to your derived equations, compute the total artificial force on a robot at position $\mathbf{q} = [1,\,2]^{T}$ m, given the goal $\mathbf{q}_g = [6,\,7]^{T}$ m and a single obstacle at $\mathbf{q}_{obs} = [3,\,3]^{T}$ m with $\rho_0 = 1.5$ m. Use $\xi = 1$ N/m and $\eta = 30$ N. Comment on the resulting motion direction. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question A(a) — 7 Marks **1. Conceptual Introduction (1 mark)** The Potential Field Method (Khatib, 1986) superimposes an *attractive* potential well at the goal and *repulsive* potential hills at obstacles. The robot is treated as a point mass that moves under the negative gradient of the total potential $U_{total} = U_{att} + \sum U_{rep,k}$. **2. Attractive Force Derivation (3 marks)** Define the distance $$d = \lVert \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{goal}\rVert = \sqrt{(x-x_g)^{2} + (y-y_g)^{2}}$$ The attractive potential is $$U_{att} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\xi\,d^{2}$$ where $\xi > 0$ is the **attractive gain** (N/m). Taking the gradient: $$\nabla U_{att} = \xi(\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{goal})$$ Applying $\mathbf{F}_{att} = -\nabla U_{att}$: $$\mathbf{F}_{att} = \xi(\mathbf{q}_{goal} - \mathbf{q})$$ **Role of $\xi$:** Larger $\xi$ makes the robot accelerate more aggressively toward the goal but may overshoot in dense obstacle fields. **3. Repulsive Force Derivation (3 marks)** For each obstacle with centre $\mathbf{q}_{obs,k}$ at distance $\rho_k$: $$U_{rep,k} = \begin{cases} \tfrac{1}{2}\,\eta\left(\tfrac{1}{\rho_k} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^{2}, & \rho_k \le \rho_0 \\ 0, & \rho_k > \rho_0 \end{cases}$$ Here $\eta > 0$ is the **repulsive gain** (N) and $\rho_0$ is the **influence radius** (m). Differentiating: $$\mathbf{F}_{rep,k} = \eta\left(\tfrac{1}{\rho_k} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)\dfrac{\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{obs,k}}{\rho_k^{3}}$$ **Role of $\eta, \rho_0$:** $\eta$ scales repulsion strength; $\rho_0$ defines how far the obstacle's influence reaches. Outside $\rho_0$, the robot is unaffected. **Total Force:** $$\mathbf{F}_{total} = \mathbf{F}_{att} + \sum_{k} \mathbf{F}_{rep,k}$$ --- #### Model Solution to Question A(b) — 7 Marks **Step 1 — Attractive Force (2 marks)** $$\mathbf{F}_{att} = \xi(\mathbf{q}_g - \mathbf{q}) = 1 \cdot ([6,7]^{T} - [1,2]^{T}) = [5,\;5]^{T}\ \text{N}$$ **Step 2 — Distance to obstacle (2 marks)** $$\rho = \sqrt{(1-3)^{2} + (2-3)^{2}} = \sqrt{4 + 1} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236\ \text{m}$$ **Step 3 — Activation check (1 mark)** $\rho = 2.236 > \rho_0 = 1.5$, so the obstacle is **outside** the influence radius. $$\mathbf{F}_{rep} = \mathbf{0}$$ **Step 4 — Total force (1 mark)** $$\mathbf{F}_{total} = [5,\;5]^{T}\ \text{N}$$ **Step 5 — Motion direction comment (1 mark)** $$\phi = \arctan(5/5) = 45^{\circ}\text{ above the +x axis}$$ The robot moves along the line $\mathbf{q}_g - \mathbf{q}$, purely under goal attraction, at $\sim 45^{\circ}$ northeast — a clean straight-line trajectory until it enters the obstacle's influence radius. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — OPTION 2 (Internal Choice) **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PCCAT602, Module 4]** **(a)** Describe the **Bug1** and **Bug2** algorithms for reactive navigation with the help of a labelled diagram. Compare them in terms of optimality and memory. **(7 marks)** **(b)** What are the major **limitations of pure reactive navigation**? With a neat sketch, explain how a **hybrid deliberative-reactive** architecture overcomes these limitations while preserving reactivity. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question B(a) — 7 Marks **1. Bug1 Algorithm (3 marks)** **States:** - **State 1 — Head toward Goal:** Move straight from start $\mathbf{q}_{start}$ toward goal $\mathbf{q}_{goal}$ along the line $M$ (the *M-line*). - **State 2 — Boundary Following:** Upon hitting an obstacle, follow its boundary *completely* (full circumnavigation). Record the point $H$ on the boundary that is **closest to the goal**. - After completing the loop, leave the boundary at $H$ and return to State 1. **Properties:** Provably complete (reaches goal if a path exists). Path length is bounded by the *perimeter plus twice the obstacle diameter* in the worst case. Optimality is **none** — the algorithm can do arbitrarily worse than the shortest path. **Memory:** Requires storing the **entire obstacle boundary** to find the closest point $H$. This is its major drawback. **2. Bug2 Algorithm (2 marks)** - **State 1 — Head toward Goal:** Move toward goal *along the M-line*; on contact, switch to State 2. - **State 2 — Boundary Follow:** Follow the obstacle boundary (keeping it on, say, the left) until either: (i) you re-encounter the M-line **closer to the goal** than the original hit point, OR (ii) you return to the original hit point → goal unreachable. **Properties:** Bug2 typically yields shorter paths than Bug1 but can also be suboptimal. It is still complete but its worst-case bound is worse than Bug1's. **Memory:** Stores only the **hit point** and the current position — **constant memory**. **3. Comparison Table (2 marks)** | Aspect | Bug1 | Bug2 | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Optimality | Path $\le d_{\text{path}} + n \cdot p_i$ (tight) | Often shorter than Bug1, worst case worse | | Memory | Full boundary trace | Constant (hit point + current pose) | | Implementation | Complex | Simple | | Real-time suitability | Low | High | --- #### Model Solution to Question B(b) — 7 Marks **1. Limitations of Pure Reactive Navigation (3 marks)** 1. **Local minima trapping** — In Potential Field Methods, the robot can stall at a configuration where $\mathbf{F}_{att} + \sum \mathbf{F}_{rep} = \mathbf{0}$ but $\mathbf{q} \neq \mathbf{q}_{goal}$. 2. **No global optimality** — Pure reactive control has no notion of the *shortest* or *lowest-cost* path; it only locally avoids obstacles. 3. **Incapable of complex tasks** — Tasks requiring planning, scheduling, or reasoning (e.g., "deliver parcel A before B") cannot be expressed purely reactively. 4. **Sensor limitations in narrow passages** — Reactive methods rely on local sensing; they may oscillate in narrow corridors where obstacles are sensed on both sides simultaneously. 5. **No memory of explored regions** — Pure reactive robots may re-visit the same place many times, wasting energy. **2. Hybrid Deliberative-Reactive Architecture (3 marks)** A hybrid architecture combines the **global reasoning** of a deliberative planner with the **real-time safety** of reactive control. A canonical scheme (e.g., the *Three-Layer Architecture* of Gat, 1998): - **Planner layer (top):** Uses the global map to compute an *abstract* route (waypoints). - **Sequencer / Executive (middle):** Passes the next waypoint to the reactive controller. - **Reactive Control layer (bottom):** Uses local sensors (PFM, VFH) to navigate toward the current waypoint while avoiding obstacles. If the reactive layer fails or the environment changes, the sequencer re-invokes the planner. **3. Sketch — Block diagram (1 mark)** *(refer to Section 4.2 of these notes — Subsumption/Brooks architecture is the textbook hybrid example; alternatively, a Planner → Waypoint Manager → Potential Field → Motors block diagram is acceptable.)* --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4, Basics of Reactive Navigation** - **Reactive Navigation** = *Perception–Action* loop with **no world model** and **no planning**. - **Equation of state:** $u_t = \Pi(s_t)$ — sensor state maps directly to actuator command. - **Three classical algorithms:** Potential Field (Khatib), VFH (Borenstein–Koren), Bug family (Lumelsky–Stepanov). - **Potential Field core equations (memorize verbatim):** - $U_{att} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\xi\,\lVert \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_g\rVert^{2}$ → $\mathbf{F}_{att} = \xi(\mathbf{q}_g - \mathbf{q})$ - $U_{rep} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\eta\!\left(\tfrac{1}{\rho} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^{2}$ for $\rho \le \rho_0$, else $0$ - $\mathbf{F}_{rep} = \eta\!\left(\tfrac{1}{\rho} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)\!\dfrac{\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{obs}}{\rho^{3}}$ - **Local minima** are the *defining flaw* of pure PFM — mention this in every PFM answer. - **Subsumption Architecture** (Brooks, 1986): layered FSMs with **inhibit / subsume** relations; lowest layer = safest reflex. - **Bug1** stores the *entire obstacle boundary* and exits at the closest-to-goal point; **Bug2** uses only the M-line hit point (constant memory). - **VFH** maintains a **1-D polar histogram** of obstacle density; selects the *valley* closest to the goal direction. - **Advantages:** real-time, robust, cheap, sensor-fault tolerant. - **Disadvantages:** no optimality, local minima, no long-horizon planning. - **Hybrid architectures** (Planner + Reactive Controller) combine the best of both — this is the answer to *almost every* "compare/contrast" or "limitations" question. - **Key constants / gains:** $\xi$ (attractive, N/m), $\eta$ (repulsive, N), $\rho_0$ (influence radius, m). - **Units to remember:** Force in **N**, Potential in **J**, Gain $\xi$ in **N/m**, Gain $\eta$ in **N·m²** (since $\rho$ has units of m and the bracket is 1/m). - **KTU exam tip:** Always draw the **force vectors** on a 2-D map (robot, goal, obstacles) in PFM questions — examiners award **1–2 marks** for a clean sketch even if the algebra slips. - **Avoid** the words "the robot thinks" — reactive robots *do not think*. They react.

topics.title: Robot Localization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAT602 - ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Robot Localization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Robot Localization** is the fundamental computational process by which a mobile robot determines its own pose (position and orientation) within a known reference frame or map, using noisy sensor measurements, uncertain motion commands, and prior knowledge of the environment.

Mathematically, the localization problem is defined as the recursive estimation of the robot's belief about its state $x_t$, given all sensor observations $z_{1:t}$ and control inputs $u_{1:t}$ up to time $t$:

$$\text{bel}(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$

Where the robot state vector is typically expressed as:

$$x_t = \begin{bmatrix} x_t \\ y_t \\ \theta_t \end{bmatrix}$$

Here, $(x_t, y_t)$ represents the 2D Cartesian position and $\theta_t$ represents the heading angle (orientation) of the robot.

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> Localization is the *pre-requisite cognitive layer* for navigation, path planning, and SLAM. Without an accurate pose estimate, any subsequent action like obstacle avoidance or trajectory tracking becomes meaningless.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — The "Blindfolded in Your House" Problem

Imagine you are blindfolded inside your own home and asked to tell a friend exactly where you are. You have three clues:

1. **Memory of past movements** (how many steps you took, which way you turned) → This is **odometry** (dead reckoning).
2. **Sensory inputs** (sounds from the kitchen, the feel of carpet under your feet, the temperature near the window) → These are **exteroceptive sensor measurements**.
3. **A mental map of your house** (you know the layout) → This is the **environmental map** $m$.

By continuously fusing these three sources, you maintain a probabilistic "guess" of where you are. This is *exactly* what robot localization does. The robot's internal belief is essentially a probability distribution over all possible poses, narrowed down as evidence accumulates.

## 1.3 Types of Localization (KTU Classification)

| **Type** | **Description** | **Difficulty Level** |
|---|---|---|
| **Position Tracking (Local)** | Initial pose roughly known, small uncertainty | Easy |
| **Global Localization** | Initial pose unknown, uniform prior | Hard |
| **Kidnapped Robot Problem** | Robot is teleported to an arbitrary new location during operation | Very Hard |

> [!NOTE] **Definition Callout**
> **Odometry** is the use of data from motion sensors (wheel encoders, IMUs) to estimate change in position over time. It is *always* subject to unbounded error accumulation due to wheel slip and integration drift.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard deviation of wheel odometry noise:** typically $\sigma \approx 0.01$ m per meter traveled
- **Measurement noise covariance (sonar/laser):** $\sigma_r \approx 0.05$ m
- **Acceptable localization error:** $\leq 0.1$ m for indoor robots
- **Bayesian probability normalization constant:** $\eta$ (always positive scalar ensuring total probability equals 1)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Probabilistic Framework — Bayes Filter

The Bayes filter is the mathematical backbone of every modern localization algorithm. It operates in two alternating phases: **Prediction** and **Update (Correction)**.

### 2.1.1 Prediction Step (Action Update)

When the robot executes a control action $u_t$, it predicts its new state distribution from the previous belief:

$$\overline{\text{bel}}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, \text{bel}(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

- $p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$ is the **motion model** (state transition probability).
- $\overline{\text{bel}}(x_t)$ is the predicted belief *before* incorporating the new measurement.

### 2.1.2 Measurement Update Step (Correction)

When a sensor measurement $z_t$ arrives, the belief is corrected using Bayes' theorem:

$$\text{bel}(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \, \overline{\text{bel}}(x_t)$$

- $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ is the **measurement model** (sensor likelihood given map $m$).
- $\eta$ is the normalizer: $\eta^{-1} = \int p(z_t \mid x'_t, m) \, \overline{\text{bel}}(x'_t) \, dx'_t$.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Why Bayes Filter?**
> Sensors are noisy. Odometry drifts. Bayes filter optimally fuses both using probability theory, producing a *mathematically optimal* estimate under the Markov assumption.

## 2.2 The Markov Assumption (KTU Critical Concept)

A localization problem satisfies the Markov property if the future state depends **only** on the present state — not on the entire history. Formally:

$$p(x_t \mid x_{0:t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$$

This assumption allows the recursive factorization of the Bayes filter, making online computation feasible.

## 2.3 Sensor Models — Two Fundamental Types

| **Sensor Category** | **Example** | **Information Provided** |
|---|---|---|
| **Proprioceptive** | Wheel encoders, IMU | Relative motion (velocity, acceleration) |
| **Exteroceptive** | Camera, Lidar, Sonar, GPS | Observation of environment (range, bearing) |

### 2.3.1 Odometry Motion Model (Most Used in KTU Problems)

Given odometry readings $(\bar{x}_{t-1}, \bar{x}_t)$, the relative displacement is:

$$\delta_{rot1} = \text{atan2}(\bar{y}_t - \bar{y}_{t-1}, \bar{x}_t - \bar{x}_{t-1}) - \bar{\theta}_{t-1}$$

$$\delta_{trans} = \sqrt{(\bar{x}_t - \bar{x}_{t-1})^2 + (\bar{y}_t - \bar{y}_{t-1})^2}$$

$$\delta_{rot2} = \bar{\theta}_t - \bar{\theta}_{t-1} - \delta_{rot1}$$

The true robot motion is sampled by adding Gaussian noise to each component:

$$\hat{\delta}_{rot1} = \delta_{rot1} + \mathcal{N}(0, \alpha_1 \delta_{rot1}^2 + \alpha_2 \delta_{trans}^2)$$

$$\hat{\delta}_{trans} = \delta_{trans} + \mathcal{N}(0, \alpha_3 \delta_{trans}^2 + \alpha_4 \delta_{rot1}^2 + \alpha_4 \delta_{rot2}^2)$$

$$\hat{\delta}_{rot2} = \delta_{rot2} + \mathcal{N}(0, \alpha_1 \delta_{rot2}^2 + \alpha_2 \delta_{trans}^2)$$

### 2.3.2 Landmark-Based Measurement Model

If the robot detects a landmark $LM_j$ at known map position $(x_j, y_j)$, the measurement is:

$$z_t = \begin{bmatrix} r_t \\ \phi_t \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \sqrt{(x_j - x_t)^2 + (y_j - y_t)^2} \\ \text{atan2}(y_j - y_t, \, x_j - x_t) - \theta_t \end{bmatrix}$$

- $r_t$: range (distance) to landmark
- $\phi_t$: bearing (angle) to landmark

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| **Concept** | **Formula / Expression** | **Variables & Units** |
|---|---|---|
| Belief | $\text{bel}(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ | Dimensionless probability |
| Prediction | $\overline{\text{bel}}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \text{bel}(x_{t-1}) dx_{t-1}$ | Posterior prediction |
| Update | $\text{bel}(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \, \overline{\text{bel}}(x_t)$ | $\eta$: normalizer (no units) |
| Range to Landmark | $r = \sqrt{(x_j - x)^2 + (y_j - y)^2}$ | meters (m) |
| Bearing to Landmark | $\phi = \text{atan2}(y_j - y, x_j - x) - \theta$ | radians (rad) |
| Kalman Gain | $K_t = \bar{P}_t C_t^T (C_t \bar{P}_t C_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}$ | $P, Q$: covariance (m²) |
| State Update (KF) | $x_t = \bar{x}_t + K_t (z_t - C_t \bar{x}_t)$ | meters & radians |
| Particle Weight | $w_t^{(i)} = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t^{(i)}, m)$ | Dimensionless |
| Effective Sample Size | $N_{eff} = \frac{1}{\sum_i (w^{(i)})^2}$ | Number of particles |
| Trilateration (3 Beacons) | $(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2 = d_i^2$ | $i = 1, 2, 3$ |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Real-World Engineering Utility**
> These formulas power autonomous vehicles (Tesla, Waymo), warehouse robots (Amazon Kiva), planetary rovers (NASA Mars Perseverance), and medical surgical robots (Da Vinci). The choice of Kalman vs. Particle filter depends on whether the underlying belief is unimodal Gaussian (KF/EKF) or multi-modal/non-Gaussian (Particle Filter).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Bayes Filter Update Equation

### Step 1: Start with the desired posterior belief

By definition, the belief after incorporating measurement $z_t$ is:

$$\text{bel}(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$

### Step 2: Expand using the chain rule of probability

Apply the conditional probability identity $p(A,B \mid C) = p(A \mid B,C) \, p(B \mid C)$:

$$\text{bel}(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$$

### Step 3: Apply Bayes' Rule

$$\text{bel}(x_t) = \frac{p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \, p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}$$

### Step 4: Apply the Markov Assumption

Since the measurement $z_t$ depends only on the current state $x_t$ and the map $m$ (not on past measurements/controls), the numerator simplifies to $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$. The denominator becomes the normalizer $\eta^{-1}$:

$$\text{bel}(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \, p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$$

### Step 5: Identify the prediction term

The remaining factor $p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$ is precisely the **predicted belief** $\overline{\text{bel}}(x_t)$. Hence:

$$\boxed{\text{bel}(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \, \overline{\text{bel}}(x_t)}$$

This is the fundamental Bayes filter update equation used in every KTU exam problem.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Prediction Step via Marginalization

### Step 1: Express the predicted belief

$$\overline{\text{bel}}(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$$

### Step 2: Introduce the previous state via total probability

$$\overline{\text{bel}}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \, p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

### Step 3: Apply the Markov Assumption again

The first term depends only on $x_{t-1}$ and $u_t$. The second term is the previous belief $\text{bel}(x_{t-1})$. Thus:

$$\boxed{\overline{\text{bel}}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, \text{bel}(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Kalman Filter Update (Linear Gaussian Case)

Assume the motion and measurement models are linear:

$$x_t = A_t x_{t-1} + B_t u_t + \epsilon_t, \quad \epsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$$

$$z_t = C_t x_t + \delta_t, \quad \delta_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_t)$$

### Step 1: Write the prior belief as Gaussian

$$\text{bel}(x_{t-1}) = \mathcal{N}(x_{t-1}; \mu_{t-1}, \Sigma_{t-1})$$

### Step 2: Compute the predicted belief (convolution of Gaussians)

$$\bar{\mu}_t = A_t \mu_{t-1} + B_t u_t$$

$$\bar{\Sigma}_t = A_t \Sigma_{t-1} A_t^T + R_t$$

### Step 3: Compute the joint distribution of $x_t$ and $z_t$

In the linear-Gaussian case, the joint is:

$$\begin{bmatrix} x_t \\ z_t \end{bmatrix} \sim \mathcal{N}\left( \begin{bmatrix} \bar{\mu}_t \\ C_t \bar{\mu}_t \end{bmatrix}, \begin{bmatrix} \bar{\Sigma}_t & \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T \\ C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t & C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T + Q_t \end{bmatrix} \right)$$

### Step 4: Condition on measurement $z_t$ using Gaussian conditioning

The conditional distribution $p(x_t \mid z_t)$ is also Gaussian. Using the standard formula for conditioning a multivariate Gaussian:

$$\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T (C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T + Q_t)^{-1} (z_t - C_t \bar{\mu}_t)$$

$$\Sigma_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t - \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T (C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T + Q_t)^{-1} C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t$$

### Step 5: Define the Kalman Gain

Let $K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T (C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}$. The final update becomes:

$$\boxed{\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - C_t \bar{\mu}_t), \quad \Sigma_t = (I - K_t C_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t}$$

The term $(z_t - C_t \bar{\mu}_t)$ is called the **measurement innovation** — the surprise between what the sensor measured and what was predicted.

## 3.4 Derivation of Particle Filter Weight Update (Importance Sampling)

The true posterior $p(x_t \mid z_{1:t})$ is approximated by a set of $N$ weighted samples (particles):

$$\text{bel}(x_t) \approx \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_t^{(i)} \, \delta(x_t - x_t^{(i)})$$

### Step 1: Express weight using Bayes' rule

Since we cannot sample directly from the posterior, we sample from a proposal distribution $q(x_t)$:

$$w_t^{(i)} \propto \frac{p(x_t^{(i)} \mid z_{1:t})}{q(x_t^{(i)} \mid z_{1:t})}$$

### Step 2: Recursively expand using motion and measurement models

Using $p(x_{1:t} \mid z_{1:t}) \propto p(z_t \mid x_t) p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}) p(x_{1:t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1})$ and the proposal $q(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, z_t)$, we obtain:

$$w_t^{(i)} = w_{t-1}^{(i)} \, \frac{p(z_t \mid x_t^{(i)}, m) \, p(x_t^{(i)} \mid x_{t-1}^{(i)}, u_t)}{q(x_t^{(i)} \mid x_{t-1}^{(i)}, z_t)}$$

### Step 3: Standard choice of proposal — the motion model

Setting $q(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, z_t) = p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$, the recurrence collapses to:

$$\boxed{w_t^{(i)} \propto w_{t-1}^{(i)} \, p(z_t \mid x_t^{(i)}, m)}$$

After normalization ($\sum_i w_t^{(i)} = 1$), these weights become the posterior approximation coefficients.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Robot Localization Functional Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA([Robot Start]) --> nodeB[Initial Belief bel x0]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Acquire Measurement z sub t]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Measurement Update using Bayes]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Updated Belief bel x sub t]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Execute Action u sub t]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Predict Next Belief bel bar x sub t]
    nodeG --> nodeC
    nodeE --> nodeH([Pose Estimate x sub t])
```

**Interpretation:** This is the closed-loop recursive structure of every Bayes filter localization algorithm. Each cycle refines the belief by combining new sensor evidence with motion predictions.

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Kalman Filter Localization Cycle

```mermaid
graph TB
    stepA[Initialize mu0 and Sigma0] --> stepB[Predict Mean: mu bar equals A mu plus B u]
    stepB --> stepC[Predict Covariance: Sigma bar equals A Sigma A transpose plus R]
    stepC --> stepD[Acquire Measurement z sub t]
    stepD --> stepE[Compute Kalman Gain K sub t]
    stepE --> stepF[Update Mean: mu equals mu bar plus K innovation]
    stepF --> stepG[Update Covariance: Sigma equals I minus K C times Sigma bar]
    stepG --> stepB
```

**Interpretation:** The Kalman filter operates as a two-stage linear estimator. The **prediction stage** propagates state forward using the motion model. The **correction stage** aligns the estimate with the measurement using an optimal weighting factor $K_t$.

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Monte Carlo Localization (Particle Filter) Algorithm

```mermaid
graph TB
    stepA[Initialize N Particles uniformly over Pose Space] --> stepB[For each Particle Predict New Pose using Odometry]
    stepB --> stepC[Compute Importance Weight from Sensor Likelihood]
    stepC --> stepD[Normalize all Particle Weights to sum to one]
    stepD --> stepE{N eff less than N threshold}
    stepE -- Yes --> stepF[Resample Particles with Replacement proportional to Weights]
    stepE -- No --> stepG[Compute Pose Estimate as Weighted Mean]
    stepF --> stepG
    stepG --> stepB
```

**Interpretation:** Particle filters excel in **non-Gaussian, multi-modal** belief distributions — a scenario where Kalman filters fail. They are the standard choice for global localization with ambiguous sensor data.

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Belief State Evolution Across Time

```mermaid
graph LR
    phaseA[Uniform Prior over Map] --> phaseB[After First Measurement Multimodal Peak Appears]
    phaseB --> phaseC[After Motion Update Belief Spreads slightly]
    phaseC --> phaseD[After More Measurements Belief Sharpens]
    phaseD --> phaseE[Converged Sharp Peak at True Pose]
```

**Interpretation:** This diagram illustrates how uncertainty evolves: it grows during motion (due to odometry noise) and shrinks during measurement updates (due to sensor information gain). Convergence indicates successful localization.

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — Localization vs. SLAM Functional Boundary

```mermaid
graph TB
    layer1[Known Map m] --> layer2[Robot Localization: Estimate Pose x sub t]
    layer1 --> layer3[SLAM: Jointly Estimate Map AND Pose]
    layer4[Unknown Map] --> layer3
    layer2 --> layer5[Path Planning]
    layer2 --> layer6[Obstacle Avoidance]
    layer3 --> layer7[Map Building]
    layer3 --> layer8[Autonomous Exploration]
```

**Interpretation:** When the map is **known**, the problem reduces to pure localization (this module's focus). When the map is **unknown**, the problem becomes SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping) — a more advanced topic covered in higher semesters.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define *Robot Localization*. With a neat block diagram, explain the three basic categories of robot localization problems.

**Model Answer:**

Robot Localization is the process of estimating the robot's pose $(x, y, \theta)$ relative to a known reference frame or map, using sensor measurements and control inputs. Formally, it computes $\text{bel}(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$.

The three categories are:

1. **Position Tracking (Local Localization):** Initial pose is known with small uncertainty. Robot must compensate for incremental errors. Example: a robot continuing in a known room.
2. **Global Localization:** Initial pose is unknown. Belief starts as a uniform distribution over the entire map. Example: a robot switched on at an unknown location.
3. **Kidnapped Robot Problem:** Robot is suddenly teleported to an arbitrary new location during operation. It must recover and re-localize from scratch. Most challenging scenario.

> [!NOTE] Examiner's Tip: Always mention the belief initialization — uniform prior for global, peaked prior for local tracking.

---

### Question A2

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** What is the role of the Markov assumption in Bayes filter-based localization? State the formal equation.

**Model Answer:**

The Markov assumption states that the robot's current state $x_t$ and current measurement $z_t$ depend *only* on the immediately preceding state $x_{t-1}$ and current control $u_t$ — not on the entire history. Formally:

$$p(x_t \mid x_{0:t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$$

$$p(z_t \mid x_{0:t}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$$

**Role in Localization:**

- Enables **recursive computation** of the belief without storing the entire trajectory.
- Reduces computational complexity from exponential (history-dependent) to linear in time.
- Makes the Bayes filter **tractable** for real-time implementation.
- However, it is an *approximation* — true world dynamics may exhibit unmodeled long-term dependencies.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question B1 (Choice A) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the Bayes filter update equation for robot localization from first principles. Explain the role of each term and the Markov assumption used.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat flowchart, explain the operation of the **Kalman Filter Localization** algorithm for linear systems. State all five core equations.

---

### Model Solution for Question B1

#### Part (a) — Bayes Filter Derivation

**Step 1:** Express the desired posterior belief:

$$\text{bel}(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$

**Step 2:** Apply Bayes' theorem (with normalizer $\eta$):

$$\text{bel}(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \, p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$$

**Step 3:** Apply Markov assumption to simplify the measurement likelihood:

$$p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$$

**Step 4:** Identify the second factor as the predicted belief:

$$\overline{\text{bel}}(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$$

**Final Combined Equation:**

$$\boxed{\text{bel}(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \, \overline{\text{bel}}(x_t)}$$

**Explanation of Each Term:**

- $\text{bel}(x_t)$ — Posterior belief after incorporating measurement.
- $\eta$ — Normalizer ensuring $\int \text{bel}(x_t) \, dx_t = 1$.
- $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ — Measurement model (sensor likelihood).
- $\overline{\text{bel}}(x_t)$ — Predicted belief from motion update.
- $m$ — Known environment map.

#### Part (b) — Kalman Filter Localization

The Kalman Filter assumes linear Gaussian systems. The five core equations are:

| **Stage** | **Equation** |
|---|---|
| Predicted Mean | $\bar{\mu}_t = A_t \mu_{t-1} + B_t u_t$ |
| Predicted Covariance | $\bar{\Sigma}_t = A_t \Sigma_{t-1} A_t^T + R_t$ |
| Kalman Gain | $K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T (C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}$ |
| Updated Mean | $\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - C_t \bar{\mu}_t)$ |
| Updated Covariance | $\Sigma_t = (I - K_t C_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t$ |

**Working:** The robot first **predicts** its next state using the motion model (equations 1 and 2). When a measurement arrives, it **computes the Kalman gain** (equation 3) which weights the relative trust between prediction and measurement. Then it **updates** the state estimate (equation 4) using the measurement innovation $(z_t - C_t \bar{\mu}_t)$ and refines the uncertainty covariance (equation 5). The cycle repeats every time step.

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**
> Students frequently write the Kalman gain equation as $K = P H^T (H P H^T + R)^{-1}$ without defining the variables. Always declare: $P$ = predicted covariance, $H$ = observation matrix, $R$ = measurement noise covariance. Marks are deducted for undefined symbols in 14-mark derivations.

---

### Question B2 (Choice B) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **odometry motion model** used in robot localization. Derive the expressions for rotation and translation components and show how Gaussian noise is added.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain **Monte Carlo Localization (Particle Filter)** with its complete algorithm. Discuss its advantages over Kalman Filter localization.

---

### Model Solution for Question B2

#### Part (a) — Odometry Motion Model

The odometry motion model describes the robot's relative motion between two consecutive poses based on wheel encoder readings $\bar{x}_{t-1} = (\bar{x}_{t-1}, \bar{y}_{t-1}, \bar{\theta}_{t-1})$ and $\bar{x}_t = (\bar{x}_t, \bar{y}_t, \bar{\theta}_t)$.

**Step 1: Compute Initial Rotation**

$$\delta_{rot1} = \text{atan2}(\bar{y}_t - \bar{y}_{t-1}, \, \bar{x}_t - \bar{x}_{t-1}) - \bar{\theta}_{t-1}$$

**Step 2: Compute Translation Distance**

$$\delta_{trans} = \sqrt{(\bar{x}_t - \bar{x}_{t-1})^2 + (\bar{y}_t - \bar{y}_{t-1})^2}$$

**Step 3: Compute Final Rotation**

$$\delta_{rot2} = \bar{\theta}_t - \bar{\theta}_{t-1} - \delta_{rot1}$$

**Step 4: Add Gaussian Noise**

Each component is corrupted by zero-mean Gaussian noise with variance proportional to the magnitude of motion:

$$\hat{\delta}_{rot1} = \delta_{rot1} + \mathcal{N}(0, \alpha_1 \delta_{rot1}^2 + \alpha_2 \delta_{trans}^2)$$

$$\hat{\delta}_{trans} = \delta_{trans} + \mathcal{N}(0, \alpha_3 \delta_{trans}^2 + \alpha_4 (\delta_{rot1}^2 + \delta_{rot2}^2))$$

$$\hat{\delta}_{rot2} = \delta_{rot2} + \mathcal{N}(0, \alpha_1 \delta_{rot2}^2 + \alpha_2 \delta_{trans}^2)$$

**Step 5: Apply to Obtain New Pose**

$$\begin{aligned}
x' &= x + \hat{\delta}_{trans} \cos(\theta + \hat{\delta}_{rot1}) \\
y' &= y + \hat{\delta}_{trans} \sin(\theta + \hat{\delta}_{rot1}) \\
\theta' &= \theta + \hat{\delta}_{rot1} + \hat{\delta}_{rot2}
\end{aligned}$$

The $\alpha_1, \alpha_2, \alpha_3, \alpha_4$ are robot-specific noise parameters tuned experimentally.

#### Part (b) — Monte Carlo Localization

**Algorithm Steps:**

1. **Initialization:** Generate $N$ particles $x_0^{(i)}$ uniformly distributed across all possible poses. Assign uniform weight $w_0^{(i)} = 1/N$ to each.
2. **Motion Update:** For each particle, sample a new pose from the motion model using the control input $u_t$:
$$x_t^{(i)} \sim p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}^{(i)})$$
3. **Measurement Update:** Compute the importance weight for each particle based on how well its predicted measurement matches the actual sensor reading:
$$w_t^{(i)} = w_{t-1}^{(i)} \, p(z_t \mid x_t^{(i)}, m)$$
4. **Normalization:** Divide each weight by the sum $\sum_i w_t^{(i)}$ so they sum to 1.
5. **Resampling:** If effective sample size $N_{eff} = 1 / \sum_i (w_t^{(i)})^2$ falls below threshold $N_{thresh}$, resample particles with replacement proportional to weights. Discard low-weight particles and duplicate high-weight ones.
6. **Pose Estimation:** Compute the final pose estimate as the weighted mean:
$$\hat{x}_t = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_t^{(i)} \, x_t^{(i)}$$

**Advantages over Kalman Filter:**

| **Aspect** | **Kalman Filter** | **Particle Filter** |
|---|---|---|
| Belief Shape | Unimodal Gaussian only | Arbitrary (multi-modal) |
| Global Localization | Fails | Excellent |
| Computation | $O(d^2)$ in dimension $d$ | $O(N \cdot \text{sensor model cost})$ |
| Sensor Non-linearity | Requires EKF/UKF | Handles natively |
| Memory | Low | High ($N$ particles) |

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**
> A common mistake is writing the particle weight as $w = p(z_t \mid x_t)$ *without normalization*. Always include the step $\eta = 1 / \sum_i w^{(i)}$ for the update to be a valid probability distribution. Also, students forget to compute $N_{eff}$ — the resampling step is *conditional*, not mandatory every cycle.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **Localization is the estimation of pose $(x, y, \theta)$**, not the construction of the map.
- **Three categories**: Local Tracking, Global Localization, Kidnapped Robot Problem.
- **Bayes Filter** = Prediction + Update. Always write both steps in exam answers.
- **Markov Assumption** is the *enabler* of recursive belief computation.
- **Odometry** drifts → never reliable alone → must fuse with exteroceptive sensors.
- **Kalman Filter** = optimal for *linear-Gaussian* systems; cannot represent multi-modal beliefs.
- **Particle Filter** = handles *any* belief shape; cost grows linearly with number of particles $N$.
- **Kalman Gain** $K_t$ is the optimal weighting between prediction and measurement.
- **Measurement Innovation** = $z_t - C_t \bar{\mu}_t$ — the "surprise" signal.
- **Effective Sample Size** $N_{eff}$ triggers resampling in particle filters.
- **Trilateration** requires at least 3 non-collinear beacons for unique 2D position fix.
- **SLAM ≠ Localization** — SLAM also estimates the unknown map.
- **Normalizer $\eta$** ensures the posterior is a valid probability distribution (always positive scalar).
- **Prediction step uses $u_t$ (motion)**, **Update step uses $z_t$ (measurement)**.
- **Landmark range** model: $r = \sqrt{(x_j - x)^2 + (y_j - y)^2}$.
- **Landmark bearing** model: $\phi = \text{atan2}(y_j - y, x_j - x) - \theta$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Challenges in Localization - Continuous Representations - Decomposition Strategies - Current Challenges in Map Representation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAT602 - ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Challenges in Localization - Continuous Representations - Decomposition Strategies - Current Challenges in Map Representation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Challenges in Localization — Formal Definition

**Localization** is the process by which a mobile robot determines its pose (position and orientation) relative to a given map of the environment. In KTU 2024 terminology, this falls under the broader paradigm of **probabilistic robotics**, where the robot maintains a *belief* about its pose as a probability distribution.

The **Challenges in Localization** refer to the set of well-known pathological scenarios that break naive, deterministic pose-tracking algorithms. These are formally catalogued in the literature as:

- **Global Localization Problem:** The robot has no a priori knowledge of its starting pose and must recover its position from scratch anywhere in the environment.
- **Kidnapped Robot Problem:** A running robot is suddenly "teleported" to an arbitrary new location, and it must detect this jump and re-localize. This is *the* standard test of a localization system's resilience.
- **Perceptual Aliasing:** Two or more distinct locations produce identical or near-identical sensor signatures, causing the robot to confuse them.
- **Dynamic / Non-Static Environments:** Furniture shifts, people walk, doors open or close — invalidating the static-world assumption of most classical algorithms.
- **Sensor Noise and Actuator Slip:** Odometry drifts; LiDAR returns have Gaussian-distributed range errors; wheel encoders miss slips.
- **Computational Tractability:** Real-time updates of high-dimensional belief distributions on embedded hardware.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The course outcomes demand that students *explain* and *compare* approaches (continuous vs. discrete, topological vs. metric). Memorizing only the names of filters is **not sufficient**; you must be able to justify *why* one representation is chosen over another.

## 1.2 Continuous Representations — Formal Definition

In the **continuous representation** paradigm, the robot's belief is encoded as a continuous probability density function over the state space $\mathcal{X} \subset \mathbb{R}^n$, rather than a finite set of discrete hypotheses.

Three canonical continuous representations are:

1. **Gaussian / Parametric (Kalman Filter family):** Belief is a single multivariate normal $\mathcal{N}(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$. Excellent for tracking but unimodal — *cannot* represent global ambiguity.
2. **Multi-modal Gaussian (Kalman Filter Mixture, GMaps):** Sum of weighted Gaussians. Handles moderate ambiguity.
3. **Sample-based / Non-parametric (Particle Filter, Monte Carlo Localization):** Belief is represented as a finite set of $N$ weighted samples $\{x_t^{[i]}, w_t^{[i]}\}_{i=1}^N$. Can represent arbitrary, multi-modal distributions — the workhorse of modern localization.

## 1.3 Decomposition Strategies — Formal Definition

**Decomposition** is the operation of partitioning the continuous configuration space $\mathcal{C} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ (or workspace $\mathcal{W} \in \mathbb{R}^2$) into a finite or countably infinite collection of simpler regions, so that motion planning, mapping, and localization can be performed region-by-region.

The three classical decomposition strategies in KTU Module 4 scope are:

- **Exact Cell Decomposition:** The free space is partitioned into *non-overlapping*, *exactly* polygonal cells whose union equals the free region. Adjacency between cells is fully defined.
- **Approximate Cell Decomposition:** The free space is approximated by a fixed-resolution grid; cells are axis-aligned squares.
- **Adaptive Cell Decomposition (Quadtree / Octree):** Cells are recursively subdivided only where the boundary between free and occupied space passes through them — yielding a *variable-resolution* representation.

## 1.4 Current Challenges in Map Representation

Modern map representation must contend with:

- **Scale:** City-scale maps exceed millions of cells; storing and updating them in RAM is infeasible without hierarchical / compressed schemes.
- **Dynamic Elements:** Moving objects (people, cars) corrupt static-map assumptions; *dynamic SLAM* must filter them out.
- **Multi-Robot Heterogeneity:** Robots with different sensor suites produce maps of different modalities; fusion is non-trivial.
- **Memory vs. Resolution Trade-off:** Doubling grid resolution *quadruples* memory.
- **Loop Closure Drift:** Long trajectories accumulate odometric drift; closing a large loop requires global optimization (pose-graph SLAM).
- **Semantic vs. Metric:** Pure metric maps lose meaning; semantic maps (object-labeled) lose precision — *hybrid* representations are an open research frontier.

## 1.5 Conceptual Analogy — The Foggy Library

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Localization in a Foggy Library**
> Imagine waking up blindfolded inside an enormous library. You have *no idea* where you are. You are given a flashlight (sensor) and a pedometer (odometry).
> - **Global localization** = the very first moment; you must deduce your position from a single observation.
> - **Kidnapped robot** = a friend secretly moves you to another wing while your eyes are closed; you must *notice* the change.
> - **Perceptual aliasing** = two reading rooms have *identical* green carpets and oak tables.
> - **Particle filter** = you mentally simulate 10,000 versions of yourself scattered through the library; each one carries a flashlight. The ones that "see what you really see" are kept; the others are discarded (resampling).
> - **Gaussian (Kalman) belief** = you only track *one* best guess + a fuzz of "I'm probably within 2 meters of here." If the friend kidnaps you, the fuzz follows you — useless for global recovery.
> - **Decomposition (grid map)** = the library is divided into square tiles. Each tile is marked "empty," "shelf," or "wall" — that's your **occupancy grid**.

This analogy should make every definition above intuitive: localization is *where am I?*, decomposition is *how do I slice the world?*, and the challenges are *what can go wrong?*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Localization Problem — Structured Logic

The KTU 2024 syllabus frames localization as a **recursive Bayesian estimation** problem. The operational flow is:

- **Step 1 — Belief Initialization:** At time $t=0$, the prior belief $bel(x_0)$ is set. If the robot's initial pose is unknown, $bel(x_0)$ is **uniform** over the free space.
- **Step 2 — Prediction (Motion Update):** Apply the motion model $p(x_t \vert u_t, x_{t-1})$ to propagate the previous belief forward through the control $u_t$.
  $$\overline{bel}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \vert u_t, x_{t-1})\,bel(x_{t-1})\,dx_{t-1}$$
- **Step 3 — Correction (Measurement Update):** Multiply the predicted belief by the likelihood of the actual measurement $z_t$, then normalize.
  $$bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \vert x_t)\,\overline{bel}(x_t), \quad \eta = \left[\int p(z_t \vert x_t)\,\overline{bel}(x_t)\,dx_t\right]^{-1}$$
- **Step 4 — Pose Extraction:** The robot's *estimated* pose is typically $\hat{x}_t = \arg\max_x bel(x_t)$ (MAP) or $\hat{x}_t = \int x\,bel(x)\,dx$ (MMSE).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Works:** The Bayes filter is mathematically optimal *if* the Markov assumption holds (the future is conditionally independent of the past given the present). The two implementation choices — **continuous (Gaussian)** and **sample-based (particle)** — differ only in *how* they approximate the integrals in Step 2.

## 2.2 Why Each Continuous Representation is Chosen

- **Kalman Filter (KF) →** Linear-Gaussian systems. The belief is a single mode → ideal for *local* tracking where ambiguity is low. Pros: optimal, closed-form, computationally cheap. Cons: fails on global localization.
- **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) →** Non-linear systems linearized by first-order Taylor expansion. Used in EKF-SLAM.
- **Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF) →** Non-linear systems using the *sigma-point* transform; better than EKF for highly non-linear models.
- **Particle Filter (Monte Carlo Localization, MCL) →** Non-linear, non-Gaussian, multi-modal systems. Used universally in modern service robots. Pros: can resolve global localization. Cons: computational cost grows with $N$; can suffer from particle deprivation.
- **Histogram Filter →** Discrete grid over pose space; the *predecessor* of particle filters. Used in Markov Localization.

## 2.3 Decomposition Strategies — Engineering Trade-offs

The KTU 2024 examiner loves the following comparative table. Memorize it.

| Strategy | Cell Shape | Resolution | Memory Cost | Boundary Faithfulness | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Exact Cell Decomposition | Trapezoidal / polygonal | Variable, exact | Low | Perfect | Off-line motion planning |
| Approximate (Fixed Grid) | Square | Uniform | $O(r^2)$ where $r$=cells/m | Bounded by cell size | Real-time occupancy grid mapping |
| Adaptive (Quadtree) | Square, recursive | High near obstacles, low in open space | $O(k \log r)$ | Asymptotically perfect | Large-scale maps with mixed detail |
| Topological | Graph of landmarks | N/A (graph) | Very low | Abstract only | High-level symbolic planning |
| Geometric (Feature-based) | Lines, points, polygons | N/A | Low | High if features well-detected | EKF-SLAM, FastSLAM |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL:** In the markdown table below, the vertical bar in $|x_t - x_{t-1}|$ is written as `\vert` so that the table parser is **not** broken. Exam scripts are not affected, but KTU's e-learning parser can break if you submit raw `|`.

| # | Concept | Equation | Symbols & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bayes Rule | $p(x \mid z) = \dfrac{p(z \mid x)\,p(x)}{p(z)}$ | $x$=state, $z$=measurement |
| 2 | Recursive Bayes Predict | $\overline{bel}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \vert u_t, x_{t-1})\,bel(x_{t-1})\,dx_{t-1}$ | $u_t$ = control input |
| 3 | Bayes Update | $bel(x_t) = \eta\,p(z_t \vert x_t)\,\overline{bel}(x_t)$ | $\eta$ = normalizer (unitless) |
| 4 | KF Predict Mean | $\hat{x}_t^- = A\hat{x}_{t-1} + Bu_t$ | $A,B$ = state/input matrices |
| 5 | KF Predict Covariance | $P_t^- = AP_{t-1}A^T + Q$ | $Q$ = process noise (m$^2$) |
| 6 | KF Kalman Gain | $K_t = P_t^- H^T (H P_t^- H^T + R)^{-1}$ | $H$ = observation matrix, $R$ = sensor noise |
| 7 | KF Update Mean | $\hat{x}_t = \hat{x}_t^- + K_t(z_t - H\hat{x}_t^-)$ | $z_t$ = measurement (m) |
| 8 | KF Update Covariance | $P_t = (I - K_t H)P_t^-$ | — |
| 9 | Particle Weight | $w_t^{[i]} = w_{t-1}^{[i]} \cdot p(z_t \vert x_t^{[i]})$ | $x_t^{[i]}$ = i-th sample pose |
| 10 | Importance Resampling | $N_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{1}{\sum_i (w_t^{[i]})^2}$ | $N_{\text{eff}} < N_{\text{thresh}}$ triggers resampling |
| 11 | Occupancy Log-odds | $l(m_i) = l(m_i) + \underbrace{\left[l_{\text{occ}} - l_{\text{prior}}\right]}_{\text{if hit}} - \underbrace{\left[l_{\text{free}} - l_{\text{prior}}\right]}_{\text{if pass}}$ | $l = \log \dfrac{p(m_i)}{1-p(m_i)}$ |
| 12 | Information Gain | $I_t = H(\mathcal{M}) - \mathbb{E}_z[H(\mathcal{M} \mid z)]$ | $H$ = Shannon entropy (bits) |
| 13 | Cell Count (Grid) | $N_{\text{cells}} = \dfrac{A_{\text{map}}}{r^2}$ | $r$ = cell side (m) |
| 14 | Quadtree Nodes | $N_{\text{nodes}} \le \dfrac{4}{3} N_{\text{leaf}}$ | $N_{\text{leaf}}$ = smallest non-empty cells |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **AMR Warehouses (Amazon Kiva / Symbotic):** Particle-filter MCL on 2D LiDAR maps. Continuous representation is mandatory because the robot is *kidnapped* every time it is lifted by a pod.
- **Autonomous Vehicles (Waymo, Cruise):** Pose-graph SLAM with semantic and metric hybrid maps. Loop closure over multi-kilometer trajectories uses approximate and adaptive decomposition together.
- **Vacuum Robots (iRobot, Roborock):** Use *topological* maps (room labels) for high-level planning and *metric occupancy grids* for low-level obstacle avoidance — a textbook example of hierarchical decomposition.
- **Drone Swarms (DARPA SubT Challenge):** Multi-robot heterogeneous mapping. Continuous *Gaussian* uncertainty is exchanged between robots, not raw points, to limit bandwidth — illustrating the **communication challenge** of current map representation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Verdict:** A student who can *state* the four filter equations but cannot link them to the *kidnapped robot problem* or to *why particle filter resampling is needed* will lose marks on the "compare and contrast" 14-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Recursive Bayes Filter (Localization Backbone)

We start from the **conditional independence (Markov) assumption** of the world.

$$
\begin{aligned}
bel(x_t) &\triangleq p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) \quad \text{(definition of belief at time } t\text{)} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})\, p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Apply Markov assumption** to the measurement likelihood: given $x_t$, the current measurement $z_t$ does not depend on the past. So $p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(z_t \mid x_t)$.

**Step 2 — Apply Markov assumption** to the prior: the current state depends only on the previous state and the most recent control. So $p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
bel(x_t) &= \eta_t \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \, p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \\[4pt]
&= \eta_t \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)\,p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1})\,dx_{t-1} \\[4pt]
&= \eta_t \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)\,bel(x_{t-1})\,dx_{t-1} \\[4pt]
&= \eta_t \, p(z_t \mid x_t)\,\overline{bel}(x_t)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **two-step predict-then-update** recursion that the Kalman filter, histogram filter, and particle filter all implement.

## 3.2 Derivation: Kalman Filter Update Equations

The Kalman filter assumes a linear-Gaussian system:

$$
x_t = A x_{t-1} + B u_t + \epsilon_t, \quad \epsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q)
$$
$$
z_t = H x_t + \delta_t, \quad \delta_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R)
$$

**Step 1 — Predict.** The posterior at $t-1$ is $\mathcal{N}(\hat{x}_{t-1}, P_{t-1})$. The motion update gives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{x}_t^- &= \mathbb{E}[A x_{t-1} + B u_t + \epsilon_t] = A\hat{x}_{t-1} + B u_t \\[4pt]
P_t^- &= \mathbb{E}\big[(x_t - \hat{x}_t^-)(x_t - \hat{x}_t^-)^T\big] = A P_{t-1} A^T + Q
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Innovation.** Define the *measurement residual* (innovation):

$$
\nu_t = z_t - H\hat{x}_t^-
$$

**Step 3 — Innovation covariance.** The covariance of $\nu_t$ is $S_t = H P_t^- H^T + R$.

**Step 4 — Kalman gain.** Choose $K_t$ to minimise the posterior covariance. Set $\frac{\partial}{\partial K_t} \text{tr}(P_t) = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_t &= (I - K_t H) P_t^- \\[4pt]
\text{tr}(P_t) &= \text{tr}(P_t^-) - 2\text{tr}(K_t H P_t^-) + \text{tr}(K_t H P_t^- H^T K_t^T) + \text{tr}(K_t R K_t^T) \\[4pt]
\frac{\partial\,\text{tr}(P_t)}{\partial K_t} &= -2(H P_t^-)^T + 2 K_t (H P_t^- H^T + R) = 0 \\[4pt]
\therefore\; K_t &= P_t^- H^T (H P_t^- H^T + R)^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Update.**

$$
\hat{x}_t = \hat{x}_t^- + K_t \nu_t, \qquad P_t = (I - K_t H) P_t^-
$$

This is the **closed-form optimal** estimator for linear-Gaussian systems. Note that the belief remains a **single Gaussian** — by construction unimodal.

## 3.3 Derivation: Particle Filter Weight Update

The particle filter approximates the belief by $N$ samples:

$$
bel(x_t) \approx \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_t^{[i]} \, \delta(x_t - x_t^{[i]})
$$

**Step 1 — Sampling from the motion model.** Draw $x_t^{[i]} \sim p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}^{[i]}, u_t)$.

**Step 2 — Importance weight.** To approximate $p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ using samples from $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$, we apply **importance sampling**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
bel(x_t) &= p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \dfrac{p(z_t \mid x_t)\,p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})} \\[4pt]
&\propto p(z_t \mid x_t)\,p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \\[4pt]
&\propto p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)\,bel(x_{t-1})\,dx_{t-1} \\[4pt]
&\approx p(z_t \mid x_t) \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_{t-1}^{[i]}\,p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}^{[i]}, u_t) \quad \text{(by Monte Carlo integration)} \\[4pt]
\therefore\; w_t^{[i]} &\propto p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]}) \quad \text{(the motion-model likelihood integrates to 1 at the sampled point)}
\end{aligned}
$$

After sampling, **normalize**:

$$
w_t^{[i]} \leftarrow \dfrac{w_t^{[i]}}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} w_t^{[j]}}
$$

**Step 3 — Resampling.** To combat weight degeneracy (one particle hogs all weight), compute the **effective sample size** $N_{\text{eff}}$. If $N_{\text{eff}} < N_{\text{thresh}}$ (typically $N/2$), resample $N$ times with replacement, with each particle selected with probability $w_t^{[i]}$.

## 3.4 Derivation: Occupancy-Grid Log-Odds Update

The probability $p(m_i)$ that cell $i$ is occupied, given all measurements, is updated via inverse sensor model $p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)$:

$$
p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = \left[1 + \dfrac{1 - p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)}{p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)} \cdot \dfrac{p(m_i)}{1 - p(m_i)} \cdot \dfrac{1 - p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}\right]^{-1}
$$

Switching to **log-odds** $l \triangleq \log \dfrac{p}{1-p}$ converts the recursion into a single addition:

$$
l_t(m_i) = l_{t-1}(m_i) + l(m_i \mid z_t, x_t) - l_0(m_i)
$$

This is the **MORPHOLOGICAL MORAVEC–ELFES** update. It is computationally trivial and trivially parallelizable — the reason occupancy grids dominate real-time mapping.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Continuous Representations

| Property | Kalman (KF) | EKF / UKF | Particle (MCL) | Histogram |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| State space | Continuous | Continuous | Continuous (samples) | Discrete grid |
| Belief shape | Unimodal Gaussian | Unimodal Gaussian | Arbitrary multi-modal | Discrete multi-modal |
| Handles global localization? | No | No | Yes | Yes |
| Computational cost | $O(d^2)$ | $O(d^3)$ | $O(N)$ per update | $O(G)$ where $G$=grid size |
| Recovery from kidnapping? | Fails | Fails | Yes (with random particles) | Yes (with random init) |
| Memory | $O(d^2)$ | $O(d^2)$ | $O(Nd)$ | $O(Gd)$ |
| Non-linear models | No | Yes (approx) | Yes | Yes |
| KTU 2024 typical question | "Derive KF" | "Linearize and apply EKF" | "Why MCL beats KF for kidnapping" | "Why we discretize" |
