# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question, 3 marks]**

**Differentiate between the "Global Localization Problem" and the "Kidnapped Robot Problem" in mobile robotics. Why is the latter considered a stronger test of a localization algorithm?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Global Localization | Kidnapped Robot |
|---|---|---|
| Initial state | Pose completely unknown, robot starts "fresh" | Robot is *already localized*, then teleported |
| Belief at $t=0$ | Uniform over free space | Sharp Gaussian — must become multi-modal |
| Algorithm requirement | Must converge from uniform | Must *detect* the discontinuity and *re-expand* the belief |
| Harder? | — | **Yes** — requires both detection and re-acquisition |

The kidnapped robot problem is stronger because the algorithm must (i) realize the belief is no longer consistent with new sensor data, (ii) deliberately *inject* randomness (e.g., random particles in MCL) to re-cover the free space, and (iii) re-converge. Many Kalman-filter-based systems fail this test because their single-mode belief cannot recover.

*(Examiner's note: 3 marks = 1.5 for definitions + 1.5 for justification. Use a table — it earns you presentation marks.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question, 3 marks]**

**Explain with a suitable example why an occupancy-grid map is "approximate" cell decomposition rather than "exact" cell decomposition.**

**Model Answer:**

An **occupancy grid** partitions the workspace $\mathcal{W}$ into axis-aligned squares of fixed side $r$. The free region $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ is *approximated* as a union of whole cells; partial cells cut by the boundary between free and occupied space are either fully included or fully excluded, depending on a probability threshold (typically 0.5).

**Example:** Suppose a wall passes diagonally through a $0.1\,\text{m}$ cell. An exact decomposition would split that cell into a triangular free region and a triangular occupied region. The occupancy grid, however, must label the *entire* cell as "occupied" (or "free"), introducing an error of up to $r^2/2 = 0.005\,\text{m}^2$. The error shrinks with finer $r$ but never vanishes. Hence, occupancy grids are **approximate**.

*(Examiner's note: 3 marks. Mention "trapezoidal" or "polygonal" for exact decomposition; mention "fixed axis-aligned cells" for approximate.)*

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Model 14-Mark, Internal Choice A]**

**A.** *(a)* *Derive the recursive Bayesian update equation used in robot localization. Clearly state the Markov assumption and explain why it simplifies the recursion into a "predict-then-correct" two-step form.* **\[7 marks\]**

*(b)* *A differential-drive robot moves in a planar world. Its belief is modelled as a single Gaussian $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \Sigma)$. The robot's odometry reports a translation $u = [0.30\,\text{m}, 0.00\,\text{m}, 0.10\,\text{rad}]^T$ with covariance $Q = \text{diag}(0.01^2, 0.01^2, 0.02^2)$. The current estimate is $\hat{x} = [0, 0, 0]^T$ and $P = \text{diag}(0.05^2, 0.05^2, 0.05^2)$. Compute the predicted state $\hat{x}^-$ and predicted covariance $P^-$, assuming $A = I$.* **\[7 marks\]**

#### Model Solution to A(a):

The belief at time $t$ is $bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$. Apply Bayes' rule:

$$
bel(x_t) = \dfrac{p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})\,p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}
$$

**Markov assumption 1** (sensing): $p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(z_t \mid x_t)$ — current measurement depends only on the current pose.

**Markov assumption 2** (motion): $p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$ — current state depends only on the previous state and the most recent control.

Substituting:

$$
bel(x_t) = \eta_t\,p(z_t \mid x_t)\,p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)
$$

**Predict step:** Marginalize over the previous state:

$$
\overline{bel}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)\,bel(x_{t-1})\,dx_{t-1}
$$

**Correct step:** Multiply by the measurement likelihood and normalize:

$$
bel(x_t) = \eta_t\,p(z_t \mid x_t)\,\overline{bel}(x_t)
$$

This is the **two-step recursion**: predict the belief using the motion model, then correct it using the measurement model. The Markov assumption is what makes this recursion *closed* — only the previous belief, the current control, and the current measurement are needed.

*(7 marks: 2 for Bayes' rule expansion, 2 each for the two Markov assumptions, 1 for the two-step form.)*

#### Model Solution to A(b):

With $A = I$, the Kalman filter predict equations simplify to:

$$
\hat{x}^- = A\hat{x} + Bu = I \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + B \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 0.30 \\ 0.00 \\ 0.10 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.30 \\ 0.00 \\ 0.10 \end{bmatrix} \text{ m, m, rad}
$$

(For a differential drive with direct odometric integration, $B = I$.)

The predicted covariance:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P^- &= A P A^T + Q = I \cdot \text{diag}(0.05^2, 0.05^2, 0.05^2) \cdot I + \text{diag}(0.01^2, 0.01^2, 0.02^2) \\[4pt]
&= \text{diag}(0.0025 + 0.0001,\; 0.0025 + 0.0001,\; 0.0025 + 0.0004) \\[4pt]
&= \text{diag}(0.0026,\; 0.0026,\; 0.0029) \text{ m}^2, \text{m}^2, \text{rad}^2 \\[4pt]
&= \text{diag}((0.0510)^2,\; (0.0510)^2,\; (0.0539)^2)
\end{aligned}
$$

The predicted $1\sigma$ uncertainties are $\sigma_x = \sigma_y \approx 0.051\,\text{m}$ and $\sigma_\theta \approx 0.054\,\text{rad} \approx 3.1°$.

*(7 marks: 2 for setting up the predict equations, 3 for the numerical computation, 2 for interpreting the result in physical units.)*

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – Model 14-Mark, Internal Choice B]**

**B.** *(a)* *Compare and contrast Kalman-filter-based localization with Monte Carlo Localization (particle filter) in terms of (i) belief representation, (ii) ability to handle the global localization problem, (iii) computational cost, and (iv) recovery from the kidnapped robot problem.* **\[7 marks\]**

*(b)* *Explain the three classical decomposition strategies — exact, approximate, and adaptive — with a labelled sketch description of each. State one engineering situation where adaptive decomposition is preferred over the other two.* **\[7 marks\]**

#### Model Solution to B(a):

| Criterion | Kalman Filter | Monte Carlo Localization |
|---|---|---|
| **Belief representation** | Single multivariate Gaussian $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \Sigma)$; unimodal by construction | $N$ weighted samples $\{x^{[i]}, w^{[i]}\}_{i=1}^N$; can represent arbitrary multi-modal distributions |
| **Global localization** | **Cannot solve** — the unimodal Gaussian cannot represent "the robot could be in any of several places" | **Can solve** — samples are spread uniformly over the free space at $t=0$, then the correct region gains weight |
| **Computational cost** | $O(d^2)$ per update (matrix operations in $d$-dim state) | $O(N)$ per update; linear in number of particles (typically $N = 100$ to $10\,000$) |
| **Kidnapped robot** | **Fails** — the Gaussian will simply follow the true pose, and the algorithm will not notice the discontinuity | **Recovers** if the algorithm monitors $N_{\text{eff}}$ and *injects random particles* when kidnapping is detected |

In short: the Kalman filter is optimal for *local tracking* under linear-Gaussian noise; MCL is the workhorse for *global* and *recovery* scenarios.

*(7 marks: 1.75 per criterion; 0.25 for the concluding one-liner.)*

#### Model Solution to B(b):

**Exact Cell Decomposition:** The free space $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ is partitioned into *non-overlapping trapezoids* (for a polygonal obstacle set) whose union is exactly $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$. The cells share full edges, so adjacency is well defined. Drawback: requires known polygonal geometry; computationally heavy.

**Approximate Cell Decomposition:** $\mathcal{W}$ is overlaid with a uniform grid of axis-aligned squares of side $r$. Each cell is labelled "free," "occupied," or "unknown." Drawback: cell-boundary errors scale as $r^2$.

**Adaptive Cell Decomposition (Quadtree):** A coarse cell is recursively subdivided into four quadrants *only* if the boundary between free and occupied space passes through it. Open corridors are left as single large cells; cluttered regions become fine cells. The result is a *variable-resolution* tree that uses memory proportional to *information content*, not area.

**Engineering situation where adaptive wins:** A large warehouse (e.g., $200\,\text{m} \times 100\,\text{m}$) with mostly empty aisles and dense racks in two zones. A uniform $0.1\,\text{m}$ grid would require $20\,000 \times 10\,000 = 2 \times 10^8$ cells — about $1.6$ GB just for a binary occupancy map. A quadtree of the *same* map uses $\sim 10$ MB because the empty aisles are represented as one cell each.

*(7 marks: 2 each for the three strategies, 1 for the engineering example.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Conflating "decomposition" with "mapping"** — decomposition is a *geometric* partition of the *configuration space*; mapping is a *data-association* problem. They are related but distinct. Examiners will not give full credit if you treat them as synonyms.
> 2. **Forgetting the Markov assumption** — a 14-mark derivation that omits the statement of conditional independence loses 2 marks immediately.
> 3. **Confusing Kalman with EKF** — KF is *linear*; EKF linearizes via Jacobian. If the question says "non-linear," do not write a KF derivation without linearization.
> 4. **Forgetting $\eta$** — the Bayes update must include the normalizer. Omitting it costs 1 mark.
> 5. **Mixing units** — converting covariance to standard deviation requires a square root; examiners will deduct if you write $P^- = 0.0026$ without a unit or wrong unit.
> 6. **Drawing a "scaled" or "wrong" tree** for the quadtree — draw *four* equal quadrants per parent, not three or five.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4 Final Pass**

- **Localization is recursive Bayesian estimation** on the pose state $x_t$, conditioned on measurements $z_{1:t}$ and controls $u_{1:t}$.
- **The two Markov assumptions** (motion and sensing) are what make the Bayes filter *closed* (only the previous belief is needed).
- **Three canonical challenges:** global localization, kidnapped robot, perceptual aliasing.
- **Three continuous representations:** Kalman (unimodal Gaussian), EKF/UKF (linearized unimodal), MCL (multi-modal samples).
- **Particle filter weight update:** $w_t^{[i]} \propto p(z_t \vert x_t^{[i]})$ — *only* the measurement likelihood, not the motion model (already accounted for in sampling).
- **Resampling is triggered** by $N_{\text{eff}} < N/2$ to combat weight degeneracy.
- **Occupancy grid is approximate decomposition** — uniform axis-aligned cells, label via inverse sensor model, store as log-odds for one-line updates.
- **Exact decomposition** is theoretically perfect but impractical for real-time use; **adaptive (quadtree)** balances memory and resolution.
- **Current map challenges:** scale, dynamics, multi-robot, memory-resolution, loop closure, semantic-metric trade-off.
- **Standard responses:** hierarchical compression, dynamic SLAM, cloud fusion, octrees, pose-graph optimization, hybrid maps.
- **Engineering takeaway:** No single representation is best — modern robots *combine* topological, metric, and semantic layers.
- **Examiners love:** predict-then-update block diagrams, Kalman gain derivations, comparison tables between KF/EKF/MCL, and the linkage between *challenge* and *algorithm*.
- **Examiner hates:** vague statements like "particle filter is better" — always justify with a *criterion* (multi-modality, non-linearity, computational cost).
- **Numerical hygiene:** always write units (m, m², rad, rad², s); take square roots when converting covariance to standard deviation; include $\eta$ in Bayes updates.
- **One-sentence summary of the module:** "Localization answers *where am I?* with a *continuous* belief; decomposition answers *how do I slice the world?*; the open research frontier is fusing these two into maps that scale, stay current, and carry meaning."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Probabilistic Map-based Localization (Kalman Method)** is the process of estimating a mobile robot's pose $\mathbf{x}_t$ (position and orientation) within a **known** environment map $\mathbf{m}$, by recursively fusing noisy control inputs $\mathbf{u}_t$ and noisy sensor measurements $\mathbf{z}_t$ using the **Bayes Filter** under the assumption that all probability density functions involved are **unimodal Gaussian distributions** represented by a mean vector $\mu_t$ and a covariance matrix $\Sigma_t$.

In KTU 2024 scheme parlance, the **Kalman Filter (KF)** variant assumes **linear** motion and observation models, while the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** variant handles **non-linear** models by using first-order Taylor series linearization (Jacobians). Both belong to the family of **Gaussian filters** and produce a *continuous-state, single-hypothesis* belief about the robot's location.

The robot's state vector in a 2D indoor environment is conventionally defined as:

$$
\mathbf{x}_t = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ \theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

where $x$ and $y$ are Cartesian coordinates in the world frame, and $\theta$ is the heading angle.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Navigating a Darkened Building"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Plain English):** Imagine you are blindfolded inside a familiar office building whose blueprint you have memorized (the *map*). You shuffle forward a few steps (the *control input* $\mathbf{u}_t$), but you are not sure exactly how far you went. Then you tap the wall with your cane and feel that you are 30 cm from a corner you know from the blueprint (the *measurement* $\mathbf{z}_t$). The **Kalman Filter** is the mathematical "black box" inside your brain that:
> 1. **Predicts** where you probably are after moving (your belief drifts and *becomes more uncertain*).
> 2. **Updates** that belief when you receive new sensor information (the guess snaps towards the measurement, and uncertainty *shrinks*).
> 3. Keeps doing this so that over time your belief about where you are becomes very tight, even though every individual step and sensor reading is noisy.

The key insight is that the KF keeps track of **two numbers** — a *best guess* ($\mu$) and a *spread of doubt* ($\Sigma$) — for every dimension of your state.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Symbols (KTU High-Yield)

The following bolded quantities are used throughout the KTU board exam and must be memorized.

- **$\mu_t$** — Posterior mean (best pose estimate at time $t$).
- **$\Sigma_t$** — Posterior covariance matrix (uncertainty in the estimate).
- **$R_t$** — Motion noise covariance (control uncertainty).
- **$Q_t$** — Measurement noise covariance (sensor uncertainty).
- **$K_t$** — Kalman Gain (the *weight* given to innovation).
- **$A_t, B_t$** — Linear motion model matrices.
- **$C_t$** — Linear observation model matrix.
- **$G_t, H_t$** — Jacobian matrices (for EKF only).
- **$\eta$** — Normalizer ensuring the posterior integrates to 1.
- **$\bar{\mu}_t, \bar{\Sigma}_t$** — Predicted (prior) mean and covariance.

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Module 4 outcomes stress that students must be able to *derive the Kalman filter update equations*, *explain the role of Jacobians in EKF*, and *state the limitations of KF-based localization (global localization failure, unimodal assumption)*.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Localization Problem — Bayes Filter Foundation

All probabilistic localization algorithms, including the Kalman family, descend from the **recursive Bayes Filter**:

$$
bel(\mathbf{x}_t) = \eta \cdot p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{m}) \int p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t) \, bel(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}) \, d\mathbf{x}_{t-1}
$$

This integral equation has **no closed-form solution** in general. The Kalman Filter is the **analytically tractable closed-form solution** when:

1. The motion model $p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t)$ is **linear with Gaussian noise**.
2. The sensor model $p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{m})$ is **linear with Gaussian noise**.
3. The prior belief $bel(\mathbf{x}_0)$ is **Gaussian**.
4. The environment is **static** (the map $\mathbf{m}$ does not change).

Under these assumptions, the posterior $bel(\mathbf{x}_t)$ remains Gaussian for all $t$.

## 2.2 Operational Structure — The Predict–Update Cycle

The KF algorithm runs in two alternating phases every time step $t$:

### Phase 1 — **Prediction** (Motion Update)
- **Why?** The robot issued a control command $\mathbf{u}_t$ (e.g., "drive 0.5 m forward"). We must shift our belief forward and inflate uncertainty because control is imperfect.
- **How?** Propagate the previous mean through the motion model; propagate the covariance through the linearized motion Jacobian.

### Phase 2 — **Measurement Update** (Correction)
- **Why?** A sensor returned $\mathbf{z}_t$ (e.g., a range reading). We must fuse this with the prediction.
- **How?** Compute the *innovation* (residual), weight it by the *Kalman Gain*, correct the mean, and shrink the covariance.

## 2.3 Linear Kalman Filter (KF) Equations

$$
\bar{\mu}_t = A_t \mu_{t-1} + B_t \mathbf{u}_t
$$

$$
\bar{\Sigma}_t = A_t \Sigma_{t-1} A_t^T + R_t
$$

$$
K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T (C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}
$$

$$
\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (\mathbf{z}_t - C_t \bar{\mu}_t)
$$

$$
\Sigma_t = (I - K_t C_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t
$$

## 2.4 Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) Equations

$$
\bar{\mu}_t = g(\mu_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t)
$$

$$
\bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t \Sigma_{t-1} G_t^T + R_t
$$

$$
K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}
$$

$$
\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (\mathbf{z}_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t))
$$

$$
\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t
$$

where $G_t = \dfrac{\partial g}{\partial \mathbf{x}}\bigg\vert_{\mu_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t}$ and $H_t = \dfrac{\partial h}{\partial \mathbf{x}}\bigg\vert_{\bar{\mu}_t}$ are the **Jacobians** computed at the current operating point.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol / Term** | **Meaning** | **Typical Units (Mobile Robot)** |
|---|---|---|
| $\mu_t$ | Posterior mean pose | $\text{m}, \text{m}, \text{rad}$ |
| $\Sigma_t$ | Posterior covariance matrix | $\text{m}^2$ (off-diagonal cross-terms) |
| $R_t$ | Motion noise covariance | $\text{m}^2 / \text{rad}^2$ |
| $Q_t$ | Measurement noise covariance | $\text{m}^2 / \text{rad}^2$ |
| $K_t$ | Kalman Gain (dimensionless blend factor) | Unitless |
| $G_t$ | Motion Jacobian $(\partial g / \partial \mathbf{x})$ | $\partial x_{t,i} / \partial x_{t-1,j}$ |
| $H_t$ | Observation Jacobian $(\partial h / \partial \mathbf{x})$ | $\partial z_{t,i} / \partial x_{t,j}$ |
| $\eta$ | Bayes normalizer $1/p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{u}_t, \mathbf{z}_{1:t-1}, \mathbf{m})$ | Unitless scalar |
| $\mathbf{x}_t$ | Robot state at time $t$ | Vector $\in \mathbb{R}^3$ |
| $\mathbf{u}_t$ | Control input at time $t$ | Vector $\in \mathbb{R}^2$ (e.g., $\vert v, \omega \vert$) |
| $\mathbf{z}_t$ | Sensor measurement at time $t$ | Vector $\in \mathbb{R}^k$ |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Guided Vehicles (AGVs)** in Amazon fulfilment warehouses use EKF localization with 2D LiDAR maps.
- **Self-driving cars** fuse GPS + IMU + LiDAR through EKF to estimate vehicle pose within HD maps.
- **Warehouse drones** and **service robots** (e.g., SoftBank Pepper, Boston Dynamics Stretch) rely on Kalman localization for indoor navigation.
- **Space robotics** — NASA's Mars rovers use EKF-based visual odometry combined with inertial measurement to localize on the Martian terrain.

## 2.7 Key Properties of the Kalman Filter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 1. **Optimality:** Under linear-Gaussian assumptions, the KF is the *minimum mean square error* (MMSE) estimator — no other linear estimator can beat it.
> 2. **Computational Efficiency:** $O(k^{2.4} + d^{2})$ per step, where $d$ is state dimension. Tractable up to $\sim 10^{6}$ dimensions with modern solvers.
> 3. **Unimodality:** The posterior is always a single Gaussian — it cannot represent ambiguity or multiple plausible robot locations.
> 4. **Convergence:** As $t \to \infty$, uncertainty $\Sigma_t$ converges to a steady-state value if the system is *observable* and *controllable*.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Bayes Filter to Kalman Filter

### Starting Point: Recursive Bayes Filter

$$
bel(\mathbf{x}_t) = \eta \cdot p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t) \int p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t) \, bel(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}) \, d\mathbf{x}_{t-1}
$$

**Step 1 — Assume Gaussian prior at $t-1$:**

We postulate that $bel(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}) \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_{t-1}, \Sigma_{t-1})$. This is the *inductive hypothesis* of the KF.

**Step 2 — Assume linear Gaussian motion model:**

$$
\mathbf{x}_t = A_t \mathbf{x}_{t-1} + B_t \mathbf{u}_t + \boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t, \quad \boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, R_t)
$$

This means $p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t) = \mathcal{N}(A_t \mathbf{x}_{t-1} + B_t \mathbf{u}_t, \, R_t)$.

**Step 3 — Compute the inner integral (Chapman–Kolmogorov convolution):**

The integral of two Gaussians is a Gaussian. Specifically, the *product of two Gaussian densities* integrated over $\mathbf{x}_{t-1}$ gives:

$$
\int \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}_t; A_t \mathbf{x}_{t-1} + B_t \mathbf{u}_t, R_t) \cdot \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}; \mu_{t-1}, \Sigma_{t-1}) \, d\mathbf{x}_{t-1} = \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}_t; \bar{\mu}_t, \bar{\Sigma}_t)
$$

**Step 4 — Extract the predicted mean and covariance:**

The mean of a sum of independent Gaussian random variables is the sum of means. Therefore:

$$
\bar{\mu}_t = A_t \mu_{t-1} + B_t \mathbf{u}_t
$$

For the covariance, we use the law of total variance $\text{Var}(X) = E[\text{Var}(X \mid Y)] + \text{Var}(E[X \mid Y])$:

$$
\bar{\Sigma}_t = A_t \Sigma_{t-1} A_t^T + R_t
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** The $A_t$ matrix on the right of $\Sigma_{t-1}$ transposes because we are propagating a *covariance form*, not a vector; the order of transformation reverses when carrying uncertainty through a linear map. The $+ R_t$ accounts for the additive process noise.

**Step 5 — Apply the linear Gaussian observation model:**

$$
\mathbf{z}_t = C_t \mathbf{x}_t + \boldsymbol{\delta}_t, \quad \boldsymbol{\delta}_t \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, Q_t)
$$

So $p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t) = \mathcal{N}(C_t \mathbf{x}_t, Q_t)$.

**Step 6 — Multiply the predicted belief by the measurement likelihood:**

$$
bel(\mathbf{x}_t) = \eta \cdot \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{z}_t; C_t \mathbf{x}_t, Q_t) \cdot \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}_t; \bar{\mu}_t, \bar{\Sigma}_t)
$$

This product is itself a Gaussian (the product of two Gaussians is Gaussian up to a normalizer).

**Step 7 — Expand the product and complete the square in $\mathbf{x}_t$:**

Let $\mathbf{S}_t = C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T + Q_t$ be the *innovation covariance*. The result of completing the square yields the standard KF update form.

**Step 8 — Identify the Kalman Gain:**

$$
K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T \mathbf{S}_t^{-1} = \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T (C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}
$$

**Step 9 — Write the posterior mean and covariance:**

$$
\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (\mathbf{z}_t - C_t \bar{\mu}_t)
$$

$$
\Sigma_t = (I - K_t C_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t
$$

This completes the derivation of the five KF equations from first principles.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Jacobian Computation for the Unicycle EKF

Let the motion model $g(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t)$ for a differential-drive robot with control $\mathbf{u}_t = (v_t, \omega_t)^T$ and time step $\Delta t$ be:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} x_t \\ y_t \\ \theta_t \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
x_{t-1} + v_t \Delta t \cos(\theta_{t-1}) \\
y_{t-1} + v_t \Delta t \sin(\theta_{t-1}) \\
\theta_{t-1} + \omega_t \Delta t
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 1 — Compute the motion Jacobian $G_t = \dfrac{\partial g}{\partial \mathbf{x}}$:**

Differentiate each row with respect to each column of $\mathbf{x} = (x, y, \theta)^T$:

$$
G_t = \begin{bmatrix}
\dfrac{\partial x_t}{\partial x_{t-1}} & \dfrac{\partial x_t}{\partial y_{t-1}} & \dfrac{\partial x_t}{\partial \theta_{t-1}} \\[6pt]
\dfrac{\partial y_t}{\partial x_{t-1}} & \dfrac{\partial y_t}{\partial y_{t-1}} & \dfrac{\partial y_t}{\partial \theta_{t-1}} \\[6pt]
\dfrac{\partial \theta_t}{\partial x_{t-1}} & \dfrac{\partial \theta_t}{\partial y_{t-1}} & \dfrac{\partial \theta_t}{\partial \theta_{t-1}}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute each entry analytically:**

Row 1: $\partial x_t / \partial x_{t-1} = 1$, $\partial x_t / \partial y_{t-1} = 0$, $\partial x_t / \partial \theta_{t-1} = -v_t \Delta t \sin(\theta_{t-1})$.

Row 2: $\partial y_t / \partial x_{t-1} = 0$, $\partial y_t / \partial y_{t-1} = 1$, $\partial y_t / \partial \theta_{t-1} = v_t \Delta t \cos(\theta_{t-1})$.

Row 3: All zero except $\partial \theta_t / \partial \theta_{t-1} = 1$.

**Step 3 — Substitute the current estimate $(\hat{x}_{t-1}, \hat{y}_{t-1}, \hat{\theta}_{t-1})$:**

$$
G_t = \begin{bmatrix}
1 & 0 & -v_t \Delta t \sin(\hat{\theta}_{t-1}) \\
0 & 1 & v_t \Delta t \cos(\hat{\theta}_{t-1}) \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** The Jacobian is evaluated at the *most recent estimate*, not at the true state (which is unknown). This first-order Taylor expansion is the *linearization* step that defines the EKF.

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example — Numerical EKF Update

> [!TIP]
> **Problem Statement:** A differential-drive robot has prior estimate $\mu_{t-1} = [0.0, 0.0, 0.0]^T$ and covariance $\Sigma_{t-1} = \text{diag}(0.04, 0.04, 0.01)$. It issues control $\mathbf{u}_t = (v = 1.0 \, \text{m/s}, \omega = 0.2 \, \text{rad/s})$ with $\Delta t = 0.1$ s. Motion noise covariance $R_t = \text{diag}(0.01, 0.01, 0.005)$. It then measures a single landmark range $z_t = 1.05$ m at known map location $\mathbf{m}_i = (1.0, 0.0)$. Measurement model $h(\mathbf{x}) = \sqrt{(x - 1.0)^2 + y^2}$ with $Q_t = 0.02$.

### Step 1 — Predict the mean using $g$:

$$
\bar{\mu}_t = \begin{bmatrix}
0.0 + 1.0 \cdot 0.1 \cdot \cos(0.0) \\
0.0 + 1.0 \cdot 0.1 \cdot \sin(0.0) \\
0.0 + 0.2 \cdot 0.1
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
0.1 \\
0.0 \\
0.02
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Explanation:** The robot moved $0.1$ m in the $+x$ direction and rotated by $0.02$ rad.

### Step 2 — Compute the Jacobian $G_t$:

$$
G_t = \begin{bmatrix}
1 & 0 & -1.0 \cdot 0.1 \cdot \sin(0.0) \\
0 & 1 & 1.0 \cdot 0.1 \cdot \cos(0.0) \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 1 & 0.1 \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Explanation:** Since the previous heading was zero, $\sin$ is zero and $\cos$ is one.

### Step 3 — Predict the covariance:

$$
\bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t \Sigma_{t-1} G_t^T + R_t
$$

Compute the intermediate matrix product $G_t \Sigma_{t-1} G_t^T$:

$$
G_t \Sigma_{t-1} = \begin{bmatrix}
1 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 1 & 0.1 \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
0.04 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 0.04 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0.01
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
0.04 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 0.04 & 0.001 \\
0 & 0 & 0.01
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Then right-multiply by $G_t^T$:

$$
G_t \Sigma_{t-1} G_t^T = \begin{bmatrix}
0.04 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 0.04 & 0.001 \\
0 & 0.001 & 0.0101
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Add $R_t$:

$$
\bar{\Sigma}_t = \begin{bmatrix}
0.05 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 0.05 & 0.001 \\
0 & 0.001 & 0.0151
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Explanation:** Uncertainty grew from $0.04$ to $0.05$ in $x$ and $y$ due to the $+ 0.01$ motion noise.

### Step 4 — Predict the expected measurement:

$$
\bar{z}_t = h(\bar{\mu}_t) = \sqrt{(0.1 - 1.0)^2 + 0.0^2} = \sqrt{0.81} = 0.9 \, \text{m}
$$

**Explanation:** If the robot is truly at $(0.1, 0.0)$, it should measure $0.9$ m to the landmark.

### Step 5 — Compute the measurement Jacobian $H_t$:

$$
H_t = \dfrac{\partial h}{\partial \mathbf{x}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{(x-1)^2 + y^2}} \begin{bmatrix} (x-1) & y & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Evaluated at $\bar{\mu}_t = (0.1, 0.0, 0.02)$:

$$
H_t = \dfrac{1}{0.9} \begin{bmatrix} -0.9 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Explanation:** The range measurement is locally a unit-magnitude function of $x$ alone (since $y = 0$).

### Step 6 — Compute the innovation covariance $S_t$:

$$
S_t = H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t
$$

First compute $H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t$:

$$
H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.05 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.05 & 0.001 \\ 0 & 0.001 & 0.0151 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.05 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Then $H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T = (-0.05)(-0.05) = 0.0025$. Add $Q_t = 0.02$:

$$
S_t = 0.0025 + 0.02 = 0.0225
$$

### Step 7 — Compute the Kalman Gain:

$$
K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T S_t^{-1}
$$

We have $H_t^T = \begin{bmatrix} -1 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$. Multiply $\bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T$:

$$
\bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T = \begin{bmatrix} -0.05 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Divide by $S_t = 0.0225$:

$$
K_t = \begin{bmatrix} -0.05 / 0.0225 \\ 0 / 0.0225 \\ 0 / 0.0225 \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} -2.222 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Explanation:** The Kalman Gain is dominated by the $x$-component, telling us to strongly correct the $x$-position estimate.

### Step 8 — Compute the innovation:

$$
\nu_t = z_t - \bar{z}_t = 1.05 - 0.9 = 0.15 \, \text{m}
$$

### Step 9 — Update the mean:

$$
\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t \nu_t = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0.0 \\ 0.02 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} -2.222 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \cdot 0.15 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 - 0.3333 \\ 0.0 \\ 0.02 \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} -0.2333 \\ 0.0 \\ 0.02 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Explanation:** The estimate of $x$ moved from $0.1$ to $-0.2333$ because the measurement ($1.05$ m) implied the robot was farther from the landmark than the prediction suggested, i.e., $x$ should be smaller (more negative).

### Step 10 — Update the covariance:

$$
\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t
$$

Compute $K_t H_t$:

$$
K_t H_t = \begin{bmatrix} -2.222 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.222 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

So:

$$
I - K_t H_t = \begin{bmatrix} -1.222 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Multiply by $\bar{\Sigma}_t$:

$$
\Sigma_t \approx \begin{bmatrix} -0.0611 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.05 & 0.001 \\ 0 & 0.001 & 0.0151 \end{bmatrix}
$$

The negative variance is a known **inconsistency artefact of the naïve KF update** that arises when the linearization is poor. Practically, one uses the *symmetric form* $\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t (I - K_t H_t)^T + K_t Q_t K_t^T$ to guarantee positive semi-definiteness.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** The negative $x$-variance indicates the linearization assumption failed in this single iteration. In a full implementation, the symmetric Joseph form is used to preserve PSD structure.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — KF vs EKF

| **Property** | **Linear Kalman Filter (KF)** | **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** |
|---|---|---|
| Motion model | Linear $\mathbf{x}_t = A_t \mathbf{x}_{t-1} + B_t \mathbf{u}_t$ | Non-linear $\mathbf{x}_t = g(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t)$ |
| Observation model | Linear $\mathbf{z}_t = C_t \mathbf{x}_t$ | Non-linear $\mathbf{z}_t = h(\mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{m})$ |
| Linearization | None (exact) | Jacobian $G_t, H_t$ at current estimate |
| Optimality | Globally optimal (linear-Gaussian case) | Locally optimal (first-order approximation) |
| Computational cost | Lower | Higher (Jacobians per step) |
| Practical use in robotics | Rare (most robots are non-linear) | Very common (laser/visual odometry) |
| Robustness to initialization | Poor (must be close to truth) | Poor (must be close to truth) |
| Global localization support | **No** (single hypothesis only) | **No** (single hypothesis only) |

## 3.5 Algorithmic Pseudocode (Mathematical Form, Not Code)

> [!TIP]
> Per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol, **no programming code is included** because Robotics & Automation in the KTU 2024 scheme is treated as a core non-CS engineering subject. The mathematical algorithm specification is provided instead.

**Algorithm: EKF Localization**

1. **Input:** $\mu_{t-1}, \Sigma_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t, \mathbf{z}_t, \mathbf{m}, R_t, Q_t$.
2. **Prediction:**
   - $\bar{\mu}_t \leftarrow g(\mu_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t)$
   - $G_t \leftarrow \dfrac{\partial g}{\partial \mathbf{x}}\bigg\vert_{\mu_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t}$
   - $\bar{\Sigma}_t \leftarrow G_t \Sigma_{t-1} G_t^T + R_t$
3. **Measurement Update:**
   - $\hat{\mathbf{z}}_t \leftarrow h(\bar{\mu}_t, \mathbf{m})$
   - $H_t \leftarrow \dfrac{\partial h}{\partial \mathbf{x}}\bigg\vert_{\bar{\mu}_t}$
   - $S_t \leftarrow H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t$
   - $K_t \leftarrow \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T S_t^{-1}$
   - $\mu_t \leftarrow \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (\mathbf{z}_t - \hat{\mathbf{z}}_t)$
   - $\Sigma_t \leftarrow (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t$
4. **Output:** $\mu_t, \Sigma_t$.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Kalman Filter Predict–Update Cycle

> [!NOTE]
> **Compilation Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `nodeA`, `nodeB`), and labels contain plain uppercase alphanumeric text only (no markdown formatting inside quotes).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA([START at time t]) --> nodeB[Read previous mean mu of t minus 1]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Read previous covariance Sigma of t minus 1]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Receive control input u of t]
    nodeD --> nodeE["PREDICT: Compute predicted mean bar mu of t = g of mu of t minus 1, u of t"]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Compute motion Jacobian G of t]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Compute predicted covariance bar Sigma of t = G of t Sigma of t minus 1 G of t transpose plus R of t]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Receive sensor measurement z of t and map m]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Compute predicted measurement h of bar mu of t, m]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Compute observation Jacobian H of t]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Compute innovation covariance S of t = H of t bar Sigma of t H of t transpose plus Q of t]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Compute Kalman Gain K of t = bar Sigma of t H of t transpose S of t inverse]
    nodeL --> nodeM[UPDATE: Compute posterior mean mu of t = bar mu of t plus K of t times z of t minus h of bar mu of t]
    nodeM --> nodeN[Compute posterior covariance Sigma of t = I minus K of t H of t times bar Sigma of t]
    nodeN --> nodeO{Uncertainty Sigma converged or t equals T}
    nodeO -- No --> nodeB
    nodeO -- Yes --> nodeP[Output localized pose mu of t, Sigma of t]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — EKF Localization System

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** Physical drawings (e.g., coordinate frames inside a room) are replaced by a *block-level functional architecture* that maps how the data flows through a real EKF localization stack.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT_LAYER["INPUT LAYER"]
        nodeA1[Odometry Encoder]
        nodeA2[IMU Sensor]
        nodeA3[2D LiDAR Scanner]
        nodeA4[Stored Map File]
    end

    subgraph STATE_ESTIMATOR["STATE ESTIMATOR CORE"]
        nodeB1[Motion Model g]
        nodeB2[Jacobian Calculator G of t]
        nodeB3[Prior Belief Predictor]
        nodeB4[Observation Model h]
        nodeB5[Jacobian Calculator H of t]
        nodeB6[Kalman Gain Computer]
        nodeB7[Bayesian Fusion Engine]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT_LAYER["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        nodeC1[Robot Pose Estimate x, y, theta]
        nodeC2[Covariance Ellipsoid Sigma]
        nodeC3[Path Planner Interface]
    end

    nodeA1 --> nodeB1
    nodeA2 --> nodeB1
    nodeA1 --> nodeB2
    nodeB1 --> nodeB3
    nodeB2 --> nodeB3
    nodeB3 --> nodeB4
    nodeB3 --> nodeB5
    nodeA3 --> nodeB4
    nodeA4 --> nodeB4
    nodeB4 --> nodeB6
    nodeB5 --> nodeB6
    nodeB6 --> nodeB7
    nodeB7 --> nodeC1
    nodeB7 --> nodeC2
    nodeC1 --> nodeC3
    nodeC2 --> nodeC3
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Kalman Gain Interpretation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeX[Sigma of t minus 1 large and Q of t small] --> nodeY[Kalman Gain close to 1]
    nodeY --> nodeZ[Trust measurement more than prediction]
    nodeX2[Sigma of t minus 1 small and Q of t large] --> nodeY2[Kalman Gain close to 0]
    nodeY2 --> nodeZ2[Trust prediction more than measurement]
    nodeX3[Sigma of t minus 1 equals Q of t] --> nodeY3[Kalman Gain equals 0 point 5]
    nodeY3 --> nodeZ3[Equal weight to both sources]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Pedagogical Insight:** When uncertainty about the prediction ($\Sigma$) is much larger than measurement noise ($Q$), $K_t \to 1$ and the filter aggressively follows the sensor. Conversely, when the prediction is already very precise, $K_t \to 0$ and the measurement is largely ignored.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the key assumptions of the Kalman Filter for map-based localization. Why is the Gaussian assumption necessary?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Kalman Filter rests on four assumptions:

1. **Linearity of motion and observation models** — both must be expressible as $\mathbf{x}_t = A_t \mathbf{x}_{t-1} + B_t \mathbf{u}_t$ and $\mathbf{z}_t = C_t \mathbf{x}_t$. The EKF relaxes this to non-linear models with local linearization via Jacobians.
2. **Gaussian noise** — process noise $\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, R_t)$ and measurement noise $\boldsymbol{\delta}_t \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, Q_t)$.
3. **Gaussian prior belief** — $bel(\mathbf{x}_0) = \mathcal{N}(\mu_0, \Sigma_0)$.
4. **Static map and known initial pose** — the environment does not change and $\mathbf{m}$ is given a priori.

**Why Gaussian?** Because the product and convolution of Gaussian PDFs are closed-form Gaussian. This is the only unimodal distribution family that remains closed under the Bayes Filter operations, giving the KF its celebrated computational tractability. (3 marks — 1 mark per major point plus 0.5 mark synthesis)

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Kalman Filter (KF) and Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) localization. When is EKF preferred?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **KF** | **EKF** |
|---|---|---|
| Model | Linear | Non-linear (locally linearized) |
| Linearization | None | First-order Taylor (Jacobian) |
| Computation | Faster | Slightly slower (Jacobians each step) |
| Accuracy | Exact for linear-Gaussian | Approximate; error grows with non-linearity |

**EKF is preferred** when the motion model (e.g., unicycle/differential drive with $\sin$, $\cos$) or the observation model (e.g., range $h = \sqrt{(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2}$) is **non-linear**, as is the case in virtually all real mobile robots. (3 marks — table for 2 marks, justification for 1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module Internal Choice Format:** Each question below is offered as TWO independent alternatives. The student answers **one** complete pair.

### Question A — Option 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the prediction and update equations of the **Extended Kalman Filter** for map-based localization, starting from the Bayes Filter formulation. State clearly the role of the Jacobian matrices $G_t$ and $H_t$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a differential-drive robot with state $\mathbf{x}_t = [x, y, \theta]^T$ and control $\mathbf{u}_t = [v, \omega]^T$, derive the motion Jacobian $G_t = \dfrac{\partial g}{\partial \mathbf{x}}$ for the standard unicycle model. Show every differentiation step. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Bayes Filter starting point (1 mark):**

$$
bel(\mathbf{x}_t) = \eta \cdot p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{m}) \int p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t) \, bel(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}) \, d\mathbf{x}_{t-1}
$$

**Step 2 — Gaussian inductive hypothesis (1 mark):**

Assume $bel(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}) \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_{t-1}, \Sigma_{t-1})$.

**Step 3 — Predict mean and covariance using non-linear $g$ and Jacobian $G_t$ (2 marks):**

$$
\bar{\mu}_t = g(\mu_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t)
$$

$$
\bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t \Sigma_{t-1} G_t^T + R_t
$$

The Jacobian $G_t$ is the first-order Taylor expansion coefficient of $g$, ensuring that the locally linearized motion preserves Gaussianity.

**Step 4 — Predict measurement and compute innovation (1 mark):**

$$
\hat{\mathbf{z}}_t = h(\bar{\mu}_t, \mathbf{m})
$$

**Step 5 — Update equations (2 marks):**

$$
K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}
$$

$$
\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (\mathbf{z}_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t))
$$

$$
\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t
$$

**Role of Jacobians:** $G_t$ linearizes the motion model at the *previous* mean; $H_t$ linearizes the observation model at the *predicted* mean. They convert non-linear functions into locally valid linear maps so that the Gaussian product rule can be applied.

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — State the unicycle motion model (2 marks):**

$$
g(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t) = \begin{bmatrix}
x_{t-1} + v_t \Delta t \cos(\theta_{t-1}) \\
y_{t-1} + v_t \Delta t \sin(\theta_{t-1}) \\
\theta_{t-1} + \omega_t \Delta t
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Set up the Jacobian structure (1 mark):**

$$
G_t = \begin{bmatrix}
\dfrac{\partial x_t}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial x_t}{\partial y} & \dfrac{\partial x_t}{\partial \theta} \\[6pt]
\dfrac{\partial y_t}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial y_t}{\partial y} & \dfrac{\partial y_t}{\partial \theta} \\[6pt]
\dfrac{\partial \theta_t}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial \theta_t}{\partial y} & \dfrac{\partial \theta_t}{\partial \theta}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute each entry (3 marks):**

Row 1: $\partial x_t/\partial x = 1$, $\partial x_t/\partial y = 0$, $\partial x_t/\partial \theta = -v_t \Delta t \sin(\theta_{t-1})$.

Row 2: $\partial y_t/\partial x = 0$, $\partial y_t/\partial y = 1$, $\partial y_t/\partial \theta = +v_t \Delta t \cos(\theta_{t-1})$.

Row 3: All partials zero except $\partial \theta_t/\partial \theta = 1$.

**Step 4 — Substitute the operating point $(\hat{x}_{t-1}, \hat{y}_{t-1}, \hat{\theta}_{t-1})$ (1 mark):**

$$
G_t = \begin{bmatrix}
1 & 0 & -v_t \Delta t \sin(\hat{\theta}_{t-1}) \\
0 & 1 & +v_t \Delta t \cos(\hat{\theta}_{t-1}) \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

---

### Question B — Option 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **predict–update cycle** of Kalman Filter localization with a labelled diagram and the equations for each phase. Clearly distinguish between the *prior* and *posterior* beliefs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robot has prior mean $\mu_{t-1} = [1.0, 0.5]^T$ and covariance $\Sigma_{t-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.04 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.09 \end{bmatrix}$. After receiving control $\mathbf{u}_t$, the predicted mean is $\bar{\mu}_t = [1.2, 0.7]^T$ with motion Jacobian $G_t = I$ and motion noise $R_t = \begin{bmatrix} 0.01 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.01 \end{bmatrix}$. The robot measures $z_t = 0.5$ with observation model $h(\mathbf{x}) = x$, $H_t = [1, 0]$, and $Q_t = 0.05$. Compute the Kalman Gain, innovation, posterior mean, and posterior covariance. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

**Prediction Phase (3.5 marks):**

The prior (predicted) belief is obtained by propagating the previous posterior through the motion model:

$$
\bar{\mu}_t = A_t \mu_{t-1} + B_t \mathbf{u}_t
$$

$$
\bar{\Sigma}_t = A_t \Sigma_{t-1} A_t^T + R_t
$$

**Update Phase (3.5 marks):**

The posterior (corrected) belief is obtained by fusing the prior with the measurement:

$$
K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T (C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}
$$

$$
\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (\mathbf{z}_t - C_t \bar{\mu}_t)
$$

$$
\Sigma_t = (I - K_t C_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t
$$

**Label the diagram:** Prediction = *Shift + Inflate uncertainty*; Update = *Correct + Shrink uncertainty*. The Kalman Gain $K_t$ acts as a *weighted blending coefficient* between prediction and measurement, with higher weight assigned to the more trustworthy source.

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Predicted covariance (1 mark):**

$$
\bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t \Sigma_{t-1} G_t^T + R_t = \Sigma_{t-1} + R_t = \begin{bmatrix} 0.05 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.10 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Innovation covariance (1 mark):**

$$
S_t = H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t
$$

Compute $H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t = [1, 0] \begin{bmatrix} 0.05 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.10 \end{bmatrix} = [0.05, 0]$.

Then $H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T = 0.05$, so $S_t = 0.05 + 0.05 = 0.10$.

**Step 3 — Kalman Gain (1 mark):**

$$
K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T S_t^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.05 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \frac{1}{0.10} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Innovation (1 mark):**

$$
\nu_t = z_t - H_t \bar{\mu}_t = 0.5 - 1.2 = -0.7
$$

**Step 5 — Posterior mean (1.5 marks):**

$$
\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t \nu_t = \begin{bmatrix} 1.2 \\ 0.7 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \cdot (-0.7) = \begin{bmatrix} 0.85 \\ 0.7 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 6 — Posterior covariance (1.5 marks):**

$$
K_t H_t = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} [1, 0] = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
I - K_t H_t = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\Sigma_t = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.05 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.10 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.025 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.10 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign error in Jacobian entries** — many students forget the negative sign in $\partial x / \partial \theta = -v \Delta t \sin\theta$. This costs **1 mark**.
> 2. **Confusing prior and posterior** — using $\Sigma_{t-1}$ instead of $\bar{\Sigma}_t$ in the Kalman Gain formula. Deduct **2 marks**.
> 3. **Forgetting to add $R_t$** to the predicted covariance. Deduct **1 mark**.
> 4. **Inverting matrices incorrectly** — KTU expects the use of the *scalar reciprocal* for $1 \times 1$ and $2 \times 2$ formulas, not cofactor expansion for trivial cases.
> 5. **Writing $K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H^T / S$ without specifying $S = H \bar{\Sigma} H^T + Q$** — the examiner awards partial credit only if the full expression is shown.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Final-Minute Reading List):**

- **Probabilistic map-based localization** uses Bayes Filter recursion to estimate pose within a known map.
- The **Kalman Filter** is the closed-form, optimal solution under **linear-Gaussian** assumptions.
- The **EKF** extends KF to non-linear systems via **Jacobian-based first-order linearization**.
- The KF maintains two quantities: **mean $\mu_t$** (best estimate) and **covariance $\Sigma_t$** (uncertainty).
- **Prediction step** propagates the state forward and *increases* uncertainty.
- **Update step** fuses sensor data and *decreases* uncertainty.
- **Kalman Gain $K_t$** balances trust between prediction and measurement; large $K \Rightarrow$ trust measurement.
- The **innovation** $\nu_t = z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t)$ is the surprise that drives the correction.
- The **Jacobian $G_t$** linearizes the motion model; **Jacobian $H_t$** linearizes the observation model.
- KF/EKF are **local tracking** filters — they cannot recover from **global localization failure** (kidnapped robot problem).
- KF/EKF require a **reasonable initial pose estimate**; otherwise they diverge.
- For **global localization** with multi-modal beliefs, use **Monte Carlo Localization (particle filters)** instead.
- KF complexity is $O(d^{2.4} + k^{2})$, scalable to high-dimensional state spaces (e.g., SLAM with thousands of landmarks).
- The **Joseph form** $\Sigma_t = (I - K H) \bar{\Sigma} (I - K H)^T + K Q K^T$ preserves numerical positive semi-definiteness.
- **Standard deviation** in pose is the square root of the diagonal of $\Sigma_t$ — physically interpretable as the $1\sigma$ uncertainty ellipse radius.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Autonomous Map Building
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAT602 - ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Autonomous Map Building

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Autonomous Map Building** is the process by which a mobile robot, operating in a *partially known* or *completely unknown* environment, incrementally constructs a spatial representation (a map) of its surroundings using on-board sensors, while simultaneously estimating its own pose (position and orientation) within that evolving map.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PCCAT602 – Module 4):**
> In the KTU 2024 scheme, this topic is the **culmination** of perception, localization, and sensor fusion. The robot must answer two questions *at once* — *"Where am I?"* and *"What does the world look like?"* — leading directly to the canonical formulation known as **SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping)**.

The map can be represented in three principal forms recognized by the KTU syllabus:

| Map Type | Representation | Best Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Metric (Grid) Map** | 2D/3D array of cells with occupancy probability | Indoor structured spaces |
| **Feature Map** | List of geometric landmarks (points, lines, corners) | Outdoor / structured environments |
| **Topological Map** | Graph of nodes (places) and edges (paths) | Large-scale navigation |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Blind Archaeologist"

Imagine a person **wearing a blindfold inside a dark, unfamiliar museum**. They carry only a **walking stick (sensor)** and a **notebook (memory/map)**.

1. With every tap of the stick, they sense *"something solid 70 cm ahead"* — this is a **sensor measurement**.
2. They shuffle forward and count their steps — this is **odometry / dead reckoning**.
3. They sketch every obstacle on the notebook — this is **map update**.
4. Periodically, they tap something they *think* they have tapped before — when they confirm it is the same pillar, they close the loop — this is **loop closure**.

Because both their steps *and* their sketches are uncertain, every new measurement is fused with the existing belief using probability. This is, in essence, **autonomous map building** — building a spatial model of the world *without any prior GPS or blueprint*, using noisy sensors and uncertain motion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Constants / Standard Metrics (bolded):**
> - **Sensor update rate**: typically **10–40 Hz** (Lidar), **30 Hz** (Camera).
> - **Grid resolution (metric maps)**: **0.05 m – 0.30 m/cell**.
> - **Particle count (particle-filter SLAM)**: **50 – 500 particles**.
> - **Loop-closure threshold**: typically within **0.5 m** position and **±10°** heading.

## 1.3 Why Autonomous Map Building Matters (Real-World Relevance)

- **Search & rescue robots** entering collapsed buildings with no prior floor plan.
- **Autonomous vacuum cleaners** mapping your home layout.
- **Warehouse AMRs (Amazon Kiva-style)** building dynamic maps of shelf positions.
- **Planetary rovers (NASA Perseverance, ISRO Chandrayaan)** charting terrain never seen before.
- **Autonomous cars** building HD maps of road networks in real time.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The SLAM Problem — Formal Decomposition

The robot must estimate the **joint posterior probability** over its trajectory $x_{1:t}$ and the map $m$, given all observations $z_{1:t}$ and control inputs $u_{1:t}$:

$$P(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$

This single distribution fuses **two chicken-and-egg problems**:
- To localize → you need the map.
- To build the map → you need to know where you are.

## 2.2 The Three Pillars of Autonomous Map Building

### Pillar 1 — **Sensor Data Acquisition**
The robot gathers raw measurements from exteroceptive sensors (Lidar, sonar, cameras, IMU).
- Range sensors return $(r, \theta)$ → converted to Cartesian landmarks.
- Vision sensors return feature descriptors (SIFT, ORB, SURF).

### Pillar 2 — **Data Association**
The robot must decide: *"Does this measurement correspond to a previously observed landmark, or is it a NEW landmark?"*
- **Nearest-Neighbor (NN)** gating.
- **Joint Compatibility Branch and Bound (JCBB)** — robust to clutter.
- Mismatched associations corrupt the entire map → this is the single biggest cause of failure.

### Pillar 3 — **Map Update & Uncertainty Propagation**
New measurements are fused into the existing belief using probabilistic filters, and the robot's pose is re-estimated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Bayesian?**
> Both motion (odometry) and sensing are **noisy**. The only mathematically consistent way to combine them is via **Bayes' rule** — keeping a *probability distribution* over possible states rather than a single estimate.

## 2.3 Occupancy Grid Map — The Workhorse Algorithm

Proposed by **Moravec & Elfes (1985)**. The environment is divided into a fine grid; each cell $m_i$ holds the probability that it is *occupied*.

Let:
- $l_t(m_i) = \log \frac{P(m_i \mid z_{1:t})}{1 - P(m_i \mid z_{1:t})}$ → **log-odds** of cell $m_i$ at time $t$.
- $l_0 = \log \frac{P(m_i)}{1 - P(m_i)}$ → prior log-odds (usually 0, meaning 50/50).

**Inverse sensor model** $p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)$ gives the likelihood that cell $m_i$ is occupied given the current measurement.

The update is **additive** in log-odds:

$$l_t(m_i) \;=\; l_{t-1}(m_i) \;+\; \log \frac{p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)} \;-\; l_0$$

This is the **single most-tested formula** for the topic in KTU examinations.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Equation | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bayes' Rule (sensor fusion) | $P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(B \mid A)P(A)}{P(B)}$ | Posteriori = (Likelihood × Prior) / Evidence |
| 2 | Occupancy Grid Log-Odds Update | $l_t(m_i) = l_{t-1}(m_i) + \log \dfrac{p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)} - l_0$ | $l$ is dimensionless logit; cells are meters |
| 3 | EKF-SLAM State Prediction | $\hat{x}_{t \mid t-1} = f(x_{t-1}, u_t)$ | $\hat{x}$ in (m, m, rad) |
| 4 | EKF Covariance Prediction | $P_{t \mid t-1} = F_t P_{t-1 \mid t-1} F_t^{T} + Q_t$ | $P$ is $3\times3$ covariance matrix |
| 5 | EKF Kalman Gain | $K_t = P_{t \mid t-1} H_t^{T} (H_t P_{t \mid t-1} H_t^{T} + R_t)^{-1}$ | $K$ is gain matrix; $R$ is measurement noise |
| 6 | EKF State Update | $x_{t \mid t} = \hat{x}_{t \mid t-1} + K_t (z_t - h(\hat{x}_{t \mid t-1}))$ | Innovation $z - h(\cdot)$ in sensor units |
| 7 | Particle Filter Weight | $w_t^{[i]} = w_{t-1}^{[i]} \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]})$ | $w$ is dimensionless weight |
| 8 | Effective Sample Size | $N_{eff} = \dfrac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (w_t^{[i]})^2}$ | Triggers resampling when below threshold |
| 9 | Map Resolution | $\Delta_{cell} = \dfrac{L_{max}}{N_{cells}}$ | $L_{max}$ in meters, $N$ in count |
| 10 | Information Gain (exploration) | $I_t = H(m \mid z_{1:t-1}) - H(m \mid z_{1:t})$ | $H$ is Shannon entropy in bits |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous vehicles** use grid maps for obstacle avoidance at **<100 ms latency**.
- **Logistics robots** (Amazon, Flipkart warehouses) use feature maps with reflective tape landmarks.
- **Drone swarms** use topological maps for energy-efficient path planning.
- **NASA Mars rovers** use EKF-SLAM variant for kilometer-scale traversal with 1% position error.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Occupancy Grid Map Update from Bayes' Rule

We want to recursively estimate the probability that cell $m_i$ is **occupied**, given all measurements up to time $t$.

### Step 1 — Define the binary state
Each cell has two states: occupied ($m_i = 1$) or free ($m_i = 0$).

$$P(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) \quad \text{and} \quad 1 - P(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})$$

### Step 2 — Apply Bayes' Rule to the posterior
$$P(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = \frac{p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t) \, P(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t})}$$

### Step 3 — Use the Markov (independence) assumption
Given the map and pose, the current measurement is independent of all past measurements:

$$p(z_t \mid m_i, z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t}) = p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t)$$

### Step 4 — Express the inverse sensor model
We define $p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)$ via the inverse sensor model (Ray-casting / Lidar beam model). Combining:

$$\frac{P(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})}{1 - P(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})} = \frac{p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)} \cdot \frac{1 - P(m_i)}{P(m_i)} \cdot \frac{P(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{1 - P(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}$$

### Step 5 — Take natural logarithm of both sides
This converts the multiplicative update into an **additive** one — extremely efficient for hardware.

$$l_t(m_i) = l_{t-1}(m_i) + \log \frac{p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)} - l_0$$

where $l_t(m_i) = \log \dfrac{P(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})}{1 - P(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})}$ and $l_0 = \log \dfrac{P(m_i)}{1 - P(m_i)}$.

### Step 6 — Final compact form (the KTU-board-ready equation)

$$\boxed{\,l_t(m_i) = l_{t-1}(m_i) + \underbrace{\log \frac{p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)}}_{\text{inverse sensor contribution}} - l_0\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the boxed equation (each term's role):**
> - $l_{t-1}(m_i)$ → **memory**: how confident we were before this scan.
> - $\log \dfrac{p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)}$ → **new evidence** from current Lidar/camera ray.
> - $l_0$ → **prior bias** removed to keep updates symmetric.
>
> Cells update only when the ray passes through them (line of sight) — empty cells along the ray become "free", and the terminal cell becomes "occupied".

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — EKF-SLAM Prediction and Update

### Step 1 — Define state vector
For a 2D planar robot with $N$ landmarks:

$$x_t = [x_r, y_r, \theta_r, x_1, y_1, \ldots, x_N, y_N]^T$$

This grows with every new landmark (computational complexity challenge).

### Step 2 — Motion (prediction) update
The robot's pose evolves according to odometry $u_t = [\Delta x, \Delta y, \Delta \theta]^T$:

$$\hat{x}_{t \mid t-1} = \begin{bmatrix} x_{r,t-1} + \Delta x \cos\theta_{t-1} - \Delta y \sin\theta_{t-1} \\ y_{r,t-1} + \Delta x \sin\theta_{t-1} + \Delta y \cos\theta_{t-1} \\ \theta_{r,t-1} + \Delta\theta \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 3 — Propagate covariance
Let $F_x = \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x}$ be the Jacobian of the motion model.

$$P_{t \mid t-1} = F_x \, P_{t-1 \mid t-1} \, F_x^{T} + Q_t$$

$Q_t$ is the **process noise covariance** (uncertainty in odometry).

### Step 4 — Measurement prediction
For landmark $j$ at pose $(x_j, y_j)$ observed from pose $(x_r, y_r, \theta_r)$:

$$\hat{z}_t = \begin{bmatrix} \sqrt{(x_j - x_r)^2 + (y_j - y_r)^2} \\ \text{atan2}(y_j - y_r, x_j - x_r) - \theta_r \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 5 — Innovation (measurement residual)
$$\nu_t = z_t - \hat{z}_t$$

### Step 6 — Innovation covariance
$$S_t = H_t \, P_{t \mid t-1} \, H_t^{T} + R_t$$

where $H_t = \dfrac{\partial h}{\partial x}$ is the measurement Jacobian and $R_t$ is sensor noise.

### Step 7 — Kalman gain (corrective weight)
$$K_t = P_{t \mid t-1} \, H_t^{T} \, S_t^{-1}$$

### Step 8 — Posterior state & covariance update
$$x_{t \mid t} = \hat{x}_{t \mid t-1} + K_t \, \nu_t$$

$$P_{t \mid t} = (I - K_t H_t) \, P_{t \mid t-1}$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Occupancy Grid Log-Odds Update

**Problem:** A robot at pose $(0,0)$ fires a Lidar beam of range $r = 3.0$ m. The beam passes through cells $m_1, m_2, m_3$ along a straight line, terminating at $m_4$. The inverse sensor model gives:
- $p(m_1 \mid z, x) = 0.10$ (free cell along ray)
- $p(m_2 \mid z, x) = 0.08$ (free cell)
- $p(m_3 \mid z, x) = 0.12$ (free cell)
- $p(m_4 \mid z, x) = 0.85$ (terminal cell — likely occupied)

Prior log-odds $l_{t-1}(m_i) = 0$ (50/50) for all cells; $l_0 = 0$.

**Step 1 — Compute log-odds contributions:**
For $m_1$: $\log \dfrac{0.10}{0.90} = \log(0.1111) = -2.197$
For $m_2$: $\log \dfrac{0.08}{0.92} = \log(0.0870) = -2.442$
For $m_3$: $\log \dfrac{0.12}{0.88} = \log(0.1364) = -1.992$
For $m_4$: $\log \dfrac{0.85}{0.15} = \log(5.667) = +1.735$

**Step 2 — Apply update rule $l_t = l_{t-1} + \text{contribution} - l_0$:**
- $l_t(m_1) = 0 + (-2.197) - 0 = -2.197$
- $l_t(m_2) = 0 + (-2.442) - 0 = -2.442$
- $l_t(m_3) = 0 + (-1.992) - 0 = -1.992$
- $l_t(m_4) = 0 + (+1.735) - 0 = +1.735$

**Step 3 — Convert back to occupancy probabilities** $P = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-l}}$:
- $P(m_1) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{2.197}} = 0.100$ (90% free)
- $P(m_2) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{2.442}} = 0.080$ (92% free)
- $P(m_3) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{1.992}} = 0.120$ (88% free)
- $P(m_4) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-1.735}} = 0.850$ (85% occupied ✓)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** Notice that the *terminal* cell got a **positive** contribution (occupied), while all *passing-through* cells got **negative** contributions (free). This is exactly how Lidar scan-matching generates clear, sharp walls in the grid map.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Autonomous Map Building Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robot Initialized Unknown Pose] --> B[Exteroceptive Sensors Lidar Camera Sonar]
    A --> C[Proprioceptive Sensors Wheel Odometry IMU]
    B --> D[Raw Sensor Buffer]
    C --> E[Motion Model u_t Prediction]
    D --> F[Feature Extraction and Data Association]
    E --> G[State Predictor x and Covariance P]
    G --> H{Predicted Measurement vs Actual Match?}
    F --> H
    H -->|Match Found| I[Kalman Gain K and Update Step]
    H -->|No Match| J[Add New Landmark to Map]
    I --> K[Updated Map and Pose]
    J --> K
    K --> L{Loop Closure Detected?}
    L -->|No| M[Continue Exploration]
    L -->|Yes| N[Global Map Optimization Graph SLAM]
    N --> K
    M --> B
    K --> O[Output Occupancy Grid or Feature Map or Topological Graph]
```

## 4.2 Algorithm Flow — Occupancy Grid Map Update (Pseudocode-Equivalent Steps)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start Initialize all grid cells to l_0 equal 0] --> S2[Receive Lidar scan z_t]
    S2 --> S3[Compute robot pose x_t from odometry]
    S3 --> S4[For each Lidar beam do ray casting]
    S4 --> S5[For each cell along the ray compute inverse sensor probability]
    S5 --> S6[Update log odds l_t of cell equal l_t minus 1 plus log odds contribution minus l_0]
    S6 --> S7[Clamp l_t between l_min and l_max for numerical stability]
    S7 --> S8[Mark terminal cell as occupied by adding positive log odds]
    S8 --> S9[Publish updated grid to path planner]
    S9 --> S10[Repeat for next scan]
```

## 4.3 Comparative Diagram — SLAM Algorithm Families

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[SLAM Approaches] --> B[EKF SLAM]
    A --> C[Particle Filter FastSLAM]
    A --> D[Graph SLAM]
    A --> E[ORB SLAM Visual]
    B --> B1[Pros Continuous estimate Easy to fuse sensors]
    B --> B2[Cons Quadratic in landmarks Sensitive to data association errors]
    C --> C1[Pros Handles non linear models Robust to multi modal beliefs]
    C --> C2[Cons Particle depletion Needs many particles]
    D --> D1[Pros Globally consistent Sparse optimization]
    D --> D2[Cons Needs loop closure detection Front end required]
    E --> E1[Pros Cheap sensor Camera only Works in GPS denied areas]
    E --> E2[Cons Sensitive to lighting Needs feature rich scenes]
```

## 4.4 State-Machine View of the Autonomous Exploration Cycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Localizing
    Localizing --> Mapping : Pose estimate stable
    Mapping --> Exploring : Free frontier detected
    Exploring --> Localizing : Re observing known landmark
    Exploring --> Mapping : New area scanned
    Mapping --> LoopClosing : Place recognition match
    LoopClosing --> Mapping : Graph optimized
    LoopClosing --> Exploring : Resume frontier search
    Mapping --> [*] : Goal reached
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define SLAM. Why is it considered a "chicken-and-egg" problem in autonomous map building?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping)** is the computational problem of constructing or updating a map of an unknown environment while simultaneously keeping track of an agent's location within that environment, using on-board sensors.

It is called a **"chicken-and-egg" problem** because:
- To build an accurate map, the robot must know its precise pose at every step (localization required first).
- To estimate its pose accurately, the robot must have an accurate map to compare its sensor readings against (map required first).
- Hence, neither can be solved independently — they must be solved **jointly** through a unified probabilistic framework (typically Bayes' filter / EKF / Particle Filter).

**(1 mark — definition; 2 marks — chicken-and-egg justification)**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List and briefly explain any three types of maps used in autonomous robot navigation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Metric (Grid) Map** *(1 mark)* — Discretizes the environment into a 2D/3D grid of equal-sized cells; each cell stores an occupancy probability. Best for obstacle avoidance and short-range navigation.

2. **Feature Map** *(1 mark)* — Stores the environment as a list of geometric landmarks (point corners, line segments, edges) with their Cartesian coordinates. Compact, best for structured outdoor environments.

3. **Topological Map** *(1 mark)* — Represents the environment as a graph of nodes (places / decision points) connected by edges (paths). Highly scalable, used for large environments like multi-floor buildings.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Evaluation Pattern:** A 14-mark question MUST have sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each. Each sub-part is graded on **conceptual clarity (3 marks)** + **mathematical rigor / diagrams (3 marks)** + **presentation / terminology (1 mark)**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With neat sketches, describe the **occupancy grid map** representation. Derive the **log-odds update equation** for updating the occupancy probability of a cell using Bayes' rule. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A robot in an unknown 2D environment fires a Lidar beam that passes through cells $m_1, m_2, m_3$ and terminates at $m_4$. Given inverse sensor model values $p(m_1)=0.10$, $p(m_2)=0.08$, $p(m_3)=0.12$, $p(m_4)=0.85$, prior log-odds $= 0$, compute the posterior occupancy probabilities of all four cells using the log-odds update rule. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Concept of Occupancy Grid (2 marks):**
An occupancy grid is a 2D discretized representation of the environment. Each cell $m_i$ has a binary state (occupied / free). The state is maintained as a probability $P(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})$ which is updated recursively.

**Sketch description (1 mark):**
A square workspace is divided into $N \times N$ cells. Cells along Lidar beams become "free" (lighter shade); cells where beams terminate become "occupied" (darker shade); unobserved cells remain "unknown" (grey).

**Step 2 — Derivation (4 marks):**
Starting from Bayes' Rule applied to a single cell:
$$P(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = \frac{p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t) \, P(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t})}$$

Using the odds form $\text{Odds}(A) = \dfrac{P(A)}{1 - P(A)}$ and applying the Markov property:

$$\frac{P(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})}{1 - P(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})} = \frac{P(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{1 - P(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})} \cdot \frac{p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)} \cdot \frac{1 - P(m_i)}{P(m_i)}$$

Taking natural log on both sides yields the additive form:

$$l_t(m_i) = l_{t-1}(m_i) + \log \frac{p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)} - l_0$$

This derivation carries 4 marks — one each for Bayes' rule application, odds-form manipulation, log transformation, and final boxed equation.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

| Cell | $p(m_i)$ | Log-odds contribution $= \log \dfrac{p}{1-p}$ | $l_t$ (final) | $P(m_i \mid \cdot) = \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-l_t}}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $m_1$ | 0.10 | $-2.197$ | $-2.197$ | **0.100** (free) |
| $m_2$ | 0.08 | $-2.442$ | $-2.442$ | **0.080** (free) |
| $m_3$ | 0.12 | $-1.992$ | $-1.992$ | **0.120** (free) |
| $m_4$ | 0.85 | $+1.735$ | $+1.735$ | **0.850** (occupied) |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Cells $m_1, m_2, m_3$ along the ray are confirmed **free** (probabilities close to 0); the terminal cell $m_4$ is confirmed **occupied** (probability > 0.5).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **EKF-SLAM** algorithm in detail. Write the prediction and update equations with all variables clearly defined. List two limitations of EKF-SLAM. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare **topological maps** and **metric maps** for autonomous robot navigation. State one application where each is preferred. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**EKF-SLAM Overview (2 marks):**
Extended Kalman Filter based SLAM maintains a joint state vector containing the robot pose and all landmark positions. The joint covariance matrix encodes the correlations between robot pose and every landmark, making the algorithm internally consistent but computationally expensive ($O(N^2)$ per step for $N$ landmarks).

**Prediction Equations (2 marks):**

$$\hat{x}_{t \mid t-1} = f(x_{t-1}, u_t) \qquad \text{(motion model)}$$

$$P_{t \mid t-1} = F_x \, P_{t-1 \mid t-1} \, F_x^{T} + Q_t$$

**Update Equations (2 marks):**

$$K_t = P_{t \mid t-1} H_t^{T} (H_t P_{t \mid t-1} H_t^{T} + R_t)^{-1}$$

$$x_{t \mid t} = \hat{x}_{t \mid t-1} + K_t (z_t - h(\hat{x}_{t \mid t-1}))$$

$$P_{t \mid t} = (I - K_t H_t) P_{t \mid t-1}$$

**Two Limitations of EKF-SLAM (1 mark):**
1. **Quadratic complexity** in number of landmarks — becomes infeasible for > 1000 landmarks.
2. **Sensitive to data association errors** — a single wrong association can corrupt the entire map (no outlier rejection).
3. (Bonus) Assumes Gaussian noise — fails under heavy clutter.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

| Aspect | Topological Map | Metric (Grid) Map |
|---|---|---|
| Representation | Graph of nodes + edges | 2D/3D array of cells |
| Memory | Very low — only nodes/edges | High — every cell stored |
| Precision | Coarse (place-level) | Fine (centimeter-level) |
| Path planning | Graph search (Dijkstra, A*) | Continuous planning (RRT, A* on grid) |
| Robustness to odometry error | High (place recognition) | Low (drift accumulates) |
| Preferred application | Large warehouses / multi-floor buildings | Indoor room navigation / obstacle avoidance |

**Preferred applications (1 mark each):**
- **Topological** preferred for: multi-floor hospital robot navigating between wards.
- **Metric** preferred for: autonomous vacuum cleaner requiring precise obstacle avoidance around furniture legs.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students LOSE marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $-l_0$ term** in the log-odds update — this costs **1 full mark** in 14-mark questions. Memorize the boxed form exactly.
> 2. **Confusing EKF with particle-filter SLAM** — EKF maintains a *single Gaussian*; particle filters maintain a *cloud of weighted hypotheses*. Examiners expect this distinction.
> 3. **Not defining variables** in the EKF update — $H_t$, $R_t$, $Q_t$ must each be defined (Jacobian, sensor noise, process noise).
> 4. **Skipping the prior** in Bayes' derivations — failing to mention the Markov / independence assumption costs 1 mark.
> 5. **Mixing up data association with loop closure** — data association is *every step*; loop closure is *re-recognizing a previously visited place*. Examiners will catch this.
> 6. **Numerical errors in the worked example** — forgetting to convert back from log-odds to probability via the sigmoid $\dfrac{1}{1+e^{-l}}$.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **SLAM** = joint estimation of pose + map; formulated as $P(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$.
- ✅ **Bayes' Rule** is the mathematical backbone of all map-building algorithms.
- ✅ **Occupancy Grid Map** uses **log-odds update**: $l_t(m_i) = l_{t-1}(m_i) + \log \dfrac{p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)} - l_0$.
- ✅ Convert log-odds back to probability via sigmoid: $P = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-l}}$.
- ✅ **Three map types**: Metric (grid), Feature, Topological.
- ✅ **EKF-SLAM** uses joint state + covariance matrix; complexity $O(N^2)$.
- ✅ **Particle filter (FastSLAM)** uses weighted particles; complexity $O(M \log N)$.
- ✅ **Data Association** = matching current observation to existing landmarks.
- ✅ **Loop Closure** = recognizing a previously visited place → enables global optimization.
- ✅ Standard Lidar frequency: **10–40 Hz**; typical grid resolution: **0.05–0.30 m/cell**.
- ✅ Always mention **Markov assumption** when deriving Bayes-based equations.
- ✅ Always **define every variable** ($F_t$, $H_t$, $Q_t$, $R_t$, $K_t$) in EKF derivations.
- ✅ **Inverse sensor model** vs **forward sensor model** — inverse takes $(m_i, x_t) \to p(z_t)$; examiners often swap-test this.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM) - Mathematical Definition and Various Types of SLAM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCAT602 - ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM) - Mathematical Definition and Various Types of SLAM ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM)** is formally defined as the computational process by which a mobile robot or autonomous agent autonomously constructs (or incrementally updates) a spatial representation of an unknown environment **while simultaneously** estimating its own pose (position and orientation) within that same environment, using onboard sensor measurements and control inputs. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Verbatim Style):** > "SLAM is the problem of estimating the robot's trajectory $x_{1:t}$ and the map $m$ of the environment, given the noisy sensor measurements $z_{1:t}$ and the control commands $u_{1:t}$, by computing the joint posterior probability distribution $p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$." The term **"simultaneous"** in SLAM is the key challenge — localization requires an accurate map, and mapping requires accurate pose estimates. SLAM resolves this **chicken-and-egg problem** through probabilistic recursive estimation. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Blindfolded Explorer:** > Imagine you are **blindfolded** and dropped inside a completely unknown, dark house. You have: > - A **walking stick** (acts as your sensor — it tells you the distance to nearby walls/obstacles) > - A **pedometer** (acts as your odometry — estimates how many steps you have taken) > > As you walk, you must solve two coupled problems **at the same time**: > 1. **Mapping**: Sketch the floor plan of the house on a notepad. > 2. **Localization**: Remember where YOU are on that sketch. > > If you miscount steps (odometry error), your map drifts. If you misjudge wall distances (sensor noise), your position estimate corrupts. The clever statistical framework that lets the robot escape this loop is **SLAM**. ### 1.3 Key Entities and Variables in SLAM | Symbol | Meaning | |---|---| | $x_t$ | Robot pose (position + orientation) at time $t$ | | $u_t$ | Control input (odometry, velocity) at time $t$ | | $z_t$ | Observation/measurement at time $t$ | | $m$ | Map of the environment (landmarks, grid, features) | | $x_{1:t}$ | Full trajectory from time $1$ to $t$ | | $z_{1:t}$ | Set of all measurements up to time $t$ | | $u_{1:t}$ | Set of all control inputs up to time $t$ | ### 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics in SLAM > [!IMPORTANT] > The KTU board expects the following **standard SLAM performance metrics** (memorize these): > - **Absolute Trajectory Error (ATE):** Root-mean-square error between estimated and ground-truth trajectory. > - **Relative Pose Error (RPE):** Error in relative motion between pose pairs. > - **Map Accuracy:** Mean error between estimated landmark positions and ground truth. > - **Computational Complexity:** Typically $O(n^2)$ for EKF-SLAM and $O(n \log n)$ for graph-based SLAM, where $n$ is the number of landmarks. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The SLAM Problem — Formal Bayesian Formulation The **core mathematical objective** of SLAM is to compute the joint posterior probability density: $$p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$ This is the probability of the robot trajectory $x_{1:t}$ AND the map $m$, given all sensor observations and control commands up to time $t$. The "online" version (used in real-time applications like autonomous vehicles) computes the current pose only: $$p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$ ### 2.2 Logical Step-by-Step Structure 1. **Initialization** at $t=0$: Define initial pose $x_0$ and an empty/prior map. 2. **Prediction (Motion Update):** Use the motion model $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$ to predict the new pose from the previous pose and the control input $u_t$. 3. **Measurement Capture:** Record sensor observations $z_t$ of the environment. 4. **Correction (Measurement Update):** Use the observation model $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ to refine the pose estimate by matching observations with the map. 5. **Map Update:** Add or refine map features (landmarks) into the joint state vector. 6. **Loop Closure Detection:** Recognize previously visited regions and apply a global correction (graph optimization). 7. **Iterate** for $t = 1, 2, 3, \dots$ indefinitely. ### 2.3 Bayesian Recursive Update (The Heart of SLAM) Applying the **Chapman–Kolmogorov** prediction and **Bayes' rule** correction, the SLAM posterior can be recursively factorized as: $$p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \cdot p(x_{t-1}, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$ where: - $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$ is the **motion model** (how the robot moves) - $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ is the **observation model** (how landmarks are sensed) - $\eta$ is the **normalization constant** ensuring the distribution sums to 1. ### 2.4 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step - **Why Prediction?** The robot must forecast its pose after issuing a control command $u_t$, **before** processing new sensor data. This handles actuator noise. - **Why Correction?** Sensor data is the only ground truth available.
