By comparing expected vs. actual observations, the algorithm reduces accumulated odometry error. - **Why Joint Estimation?** The map and pose are **statistically correlated**. A landmark observed from two different robot poses provides a geometric constraint that *couples* pose and map errors — a property called **SLAM's observability structure**. ### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table is **directly examinable** under KTU 2024 ESE. Memorize the meaning, conditions, and units of each. | # | Formula | Name | Units / Condition | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ | SLAM Posterior | Probability density | | 2 | $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$ | Motion Model | Conditional probability | | 3 | $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ | Observation Model | Conditional probability | | 4 | $x_t = f(x_{t-1}, u_t) + w_t$ | Kinematic Model | $w_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_t)$ | | 5 | $z_t = h(x_t, m) + v_t$ | Sensor Model | $v_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$ | | 6 | $K_t = P_t H^T (H P_t H^T + R_t)^{-1}$ | Kalman Gain (EKF-SLAM) | $K \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$ | | 7 | $\hat{x}_t = \hat{x}_t^- + K_t (z_t - h(\hat{x}_t^-, m))$ | EKF Update | State update rule | | 8 | $J = \sum_{(i,j) \in \mathcal{E}} e_{ij}^T \Omega_{ij} e_{ij}$ | Graph-SLAM Cost | $J \in \mathbb{R}$, minimize | | 9 | $p(m_k \mid x_t, z_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid m_k, x_t) p(m_k)$ | Landmark Posterior | Per-feature Bayes | | 10 | $ATE = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N} \sum_{t=1}^{N} \lVert x_t^{est} - x_t^{gt} \rVert^2}$ | Absolute Trajectory Error | Meters (m) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Tip:** Always use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute-value bars in inline LaTeX to avoid breaking markdown table syntax (per KTU V10 protocol). ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility SLAM is the **foundational enabling technology** for: - **Autonomous Vehicles** (Tesla, Waymo) — LiDAR + Visual SLAM - **Warehouse Robotics** (Amazon Kiva) — 2D Laser SLAM - **AR/VR Headsets** (Meta Quest, Apple Vision Pro) — Visual-Inertial SLAM - **Mars Rovers** (NASA Perseverance) — Visual + LiDAR SLAM - **Domestic Vacuum Robots** (iRobot Roomba) — 2D LiDAR SLAM - **Underwater Autonomous Vehicles** — Sonar SLAM - **Search and Rescue Drones** — Multi-sensor SLAM in GPS-denied environments ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the SLAM Recursive Bayes Filter **Step 1 — State the joint posterior we wish to compute:** $$P(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$ **Textual Explanation:** This is the probability of the entire robot trajectory from time 1 to $t$, together with the map, given all sensor data and controls seen so far. This is called the **Full SLAM** problem. **Step 2 — Marginalize over the previous pose** (introduce $x_{t-1}$ via the law of total probability): $$P(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \int P(x_{1:t}, x_{t-1}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) \, dx_{t-1}$$ **Step 3 — Apply the chain rule of probability** to factor the joint: $$P(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \int P(x_t \mid x_{1:t-1}, m, z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) \cdot P(x_{1:t-1}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) \, dx_{t-1}$$ **Step 4 — Assume the Markov property** (the current pose depends only on the previous pose and the current control): $$P(x_t \mid x_{1:t-1}, m, z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = P(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$$ **Textual Explanation:** This is justified because future states are conditionally independent of the past given the present — the robot's motion is a first-order Markov process. **Step 5 — Substitute and split the evidence term:** $$P(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \int P(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \cdot P(x_{1:t-1}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) \, dx_{t-1}$$ **Step 6 — Apply Bayes' rule** to the second factor: $$P(x_{1:t-1}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \frac{P(z_t \mid x_{1:t-1}, m, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \cdot P(x_{1:t-1}, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}{P(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}$$ **Step 7 — Use measurement Markov assumption** ($z_t$ depends only on $x_t$ and $m$): $$P(z_t \mid x_{1:t-1}, m, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = P(z_t \mid x_t, m)$$ **Step 8 — Combine and recognize the recursive structure:** $$P(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \eta \cdot P(z_t \mid x_t, m) \int P(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \cdot P(x_{1:t-1}, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$ This is the **Full SLAM Recursive Bayes Filter** — the mathematical backbone of all SLAM algorithms. ### 3.2 Derivation of the EKF-SLAM Update Equations **Step 1 — Linearize the non-linear motion and observation models** using Taylor expansion around the current estimate: $$x_t \approx f(\hat{x}_{t-1}, u_t) + F_x (x_{t-1} - \hat{x}_{t-1}) + w_t$$ $$z_t \approx h(\hat{x}_t, m) + H_x (x_t - \hat{x}_t) + v_t$$ where $F_x = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x}$ and $H_x = \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}$ are the Jacobians. **Step 2 — Predicted state and predicted covariance:** $$\hat{x}_t^- = f(\hat{x}_{t-1}, u_t)$$ $$P_t^- = F_x P_{t-1} F_x^T + Q_t$$ **Step 3 — Innovation (measurement residual):** $$\nu_t = z_t - h(\hat{x}_t^-, m)$$ **Step 4 — Innovation covariance:** $$S_t = H_x P_t^- H_x^T + R_t$$ **Step 5 — Kalman Gain:** $$K_t = P_t^- H_x^T S_t^{-1}$$ **Step 6 — Posterior state update:** $$\hat{x}_t = \hat{x}_t^- + K_t \nu_t$$ **Step 7 — Posterior covariance update:** $$P_t = (I - K_t H_x) P_t^-$$ **Textual Explanation:** The Kalman Gain $K_t$ optimally trades off prediction uncertainty $P_t^-$ against measurement noise $R_t$. When $R_t \to 0$ (perfect sensor), $K_t \to I$ and the estimate fully trusts the measurement. When $P_t^- \to 0$ (confident prediction), $K_t \to 0$ and the measurement is ignored. ### 3.3 Derivation of the Graph-SLAM Cost Function **Step 1 — Define the error term** for an edge $(i,j)$ in the pose graph: $$e_{ij}(x_i, x_j) = z_{ij} - \hat{z}_{ij}(x_i, x_j)$$ where $z_{ij}$ is the measured relative pose and $\hat{z}_{ij}$ is the predicted relative pose. **Step 2 — Define the information matrix** $\Omega_{ij}$ (inverse of measurement noise covariance) for the edge. **Step 3 — Sum the squared Mahalanobis distances** over all edges $\mathcal{E}$: $$J(x) = \sum_{(i,j) \in \mathcal{E}} e_{ij}(x_i, x_j)^T \Omega_{ij} e_{ij}(x_i, x_j)$$ **Step 4 — Minimize $J(x)$** using non-linear least squares (Gauss-Newton or Levenberg-Marquardt): $$\Delta x^* = \arg\min_{\Delta x} J(x + \Delta x)$$ **Step 5 — Solve the normal equations** (linearized system): $$H \Delta x = -b$$ where $H = J^T \Omega J$ and $b = J^T \Omega e$. **Textual Explanation:** Graph-SLAM converts SLAM into a sparse non-linear least-squares problem, where the sparsity of $H$ enables efficient Cholesky or Schur-complement solvers. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of SLAM Algorithms > [!NOTE] > This comparison table is a **guaranteed 14-mark question** type in KTU exams. | Criterion | EKF-SLAM | FastSLAM (Particle) | Graph-SLAM | Visual SLAM (ORB-SLAM) | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Filter Type** | Kalman Filter | Rao-Blackwellized PF | Optimization | Feature-based | | **Map Type** | Landmark | Landmark | Pose graph | Sparse feature map | | **Complexity** | $O(n^2)$ | $O(M \log n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n)$ per frame | | **Scalability** | Poor (small env) | Medium | Excellent | Good | | **Data Association** | Single hypothesis | Multiple hypotheses | RANSAC | Descriptor matching | | **Handles Loops?** | No (linearization error) | Yes | Yes (excellent) | Yes (DBoW) | | **Sensor** | Range-bearing | Range-bearing | Any | Camera | | **Linearization** | EKF | Particle filter | Non-linear LS | Non-linear LS | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 High-Level SLAM Process Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Robot Initialization
Pose x0 and Empty Map] --> B[Issue Control Command ut] B --> C[Motion Model
p xt given xt-1 ut] C --> D[Predicted Pose
x hat t minus] D --> E[Acquire Sensor
Measurement zt] E --> F[Observation Model
p zt given xt m] F --> G[Innovation
Residual nu t] G --> H{Consistency Check
Data Association} H -->|Valid Match| I[Kalman Update
or Particle Weight] I --> J[Updated Pose
and Map State] J --> K{Loop Closure
Detected} K -->|Yes| L[Global Graph
Optimization] K -->|No| M[Continue Trajectory] L --> M M --> B J --> N[Output
Trajectory and Map] ``` ### 4.2 Classification of SLAM Types ```mermaid flowchart TD S[SLAM Taxonomy] --> S1[By Sensor Type] S --> S2[By Algorithm] S --> S3[By Map Type] S --> S4[By Dimensionality] S1 --> S1a[LiDAR SLAM] S1 --> S1b[Visual SLAM vSLAM] S1 --> S1c[RGB-D SLAM] S1 --> S1d[Sonar SLAM] S1 --> S1e[Multi-sensor Fusion SLAM] S2 --> S2a[EKF SLAM] S2 --> S2b[Particle Filter FastSLAM] S2 --> S2c[Graph based SLAM] S2 --> S2d[Bundle Adjustment SLAM] S2 --> S2e[ORB SLAM] S3 --> S3a[Metric Grid SLAM] S3 --> S3b[Landmark Feature SLAM] S3 --> S3c[Topological SLAM] S3 --> S3d[Semantic SLAM] S4 --> S4a[2D SLAM] S4 --> S4b[2.5D SLAM] S4 --> S4c[3D SLAM] ``` ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Visual SLAM Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR node1[Camera Input
Stereo or Monocular] --> node2[Feature Extraction
ORB SIFT SURF] node2 --> node3[Feature Matching
Descriptor Space] node3 --> node4[Visual Odometry
Frame to Frame Motion] node4 --> node5[Local Map Update
Keyframe Insertion] node5 --> node6{Loop Closure
DBoW2 Place Recognition} node6 -->|Match Found| node7[Loop Verification
Geometric Check] node6 -->|No Match| node8[Pose Graph Node] node7 --> node9[Pose Graph Optimization
G2o or GTSAM] node8 --> node9 node9 --> node10[Bundle Adjustment
Global Refinement] node10 --> node11[Output Trajectory
and Sparse 3D Map] ``` ### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of LiDAR SLAM ```mermaid flowchart TD lid1[LiDAR Point Cloud
Input] --> lid2[Preprocessing
Deskewing and Filtering] lid2 --> lid3[Scan Matching
ICP or NDT] lid3 --> lid4[Odometry Estimate
Relative Pose] lid4 --> lid5[Submap Construction
Local Grid] lid5 --> lid6{Scan to Map
Matching} lid6 --> lid7[Pose Graph Node
Insertion] lid7 --> lid8{Loop Closure
Scan Context} lid8 -->|Detected| lid9[ICP Verification] lid8 -->|Not Detected| lid10[Continue] lid9 --> lid11[Graph Optimization
Levenberg Marquardt] lid10 --> lid11 lid11 --> lid12[Global 2D or 3D Map
and Trajectory] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]** **Q1. Define Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM). Write its mathematical formulation.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (2 marks):** SLAM is the computational problem of constructing a map of an unknown environment while simultaneously estimating the robot's pose within that environment, using onboard sensor measurements and control commands. **Mathematical Formulation (1 mark):** The objective is to compute the joint posterior probability: $$p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$ where $x_{1:t}$ is the robot trajectory, $m$ is the map, $z_{1:t}$ are observations, and $u_{1:t}$ are control inputs. --- **Q2. List any four types of SLAM based on the sensor used, with one application of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **LiDAR SLAM** — Used in autonomous vehicles and warehouse robots (e.g., Amazon Kiva). 2. **Visual SLAM (vSLAM)** — Used in AR/VR headsets and drone navigation. 3. **Sonar SLAM** — Used in underwater autonomous vehicles (AUVs). 4. **Radar SLAM** — Used in adverse weather autonomous driving. 5. *(Optional)* **RGB-D SLAM** — Used in indoor service robots. ### 5.2 Part B Question — Choice A (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]** **Question A:** **(a)** Derive the recursive Bayesian formulation of the SLAM problem. Clearly state all the variables and assumptions used. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the EKF-SLAM algorithm with the required prediction and update equations. Discuss its limitations. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:** **Step 1 (1 mark):** State the joint posterior objective: $$P(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$ **Step 2 (1 mark):** Apply the chain rule of probability and marginalize over $x_{t-1}$: $$P(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \int P(x_t, x_{t-1}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) \, dx_{t-1}$$ **Step 3 (1 mark):** Apply the **Markov assumption** (current state depends only on previous state and current control): $$P(x_t \mid x_{1:t-1}, m, z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = P(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$$ **Step 4 (1 mark):** Apply the **measurement Markov assumption** (current measurement depends only on current pose and map): $$P(z_t \mid x_{1:t-1}, m, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = P(z_t \mid x_t, m)$$ **Step 5 (2 marks):** Apply **Bayes' rule** and isolate the recursive term: $$P(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \eta \cdot P(z_t \mid x_t, m) \int P(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \cdot P(x_{1:t-1}, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$ **Step 6 (1 mark):** Conclude by explaining that the second factor in the integral is the previous time-step's posterior, making this equation **recursive** and computationally tractable. --- **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:** **Introduction (1 mark):** EKF-SLAM (Extended Kalman Filter SLAM) applies the EKF to the SLAM posterior, treating the joint state as a Gaussian distribution. The state vector contains both the robot pose and all landmark positions: $$X_t = [x_t, m_1, m_2, \dots, m_n]^T$$ **Prediction Step (2 marks):** $$\hat{x}_t^- = f(\hat{x}_{t-1}, u_t)$$ $$P_t^- = F_x P_{t-1} F_x^T + Q_t$$ where $F_x = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x}$ is the Jacobian of the motion model and $Q_t$ is process noise. **Update Step (2 marks):** Innovation: $\nu_t = z_t - h(\hat{x}_t^-, m)$ Innovation covariance: $S_t = H_x P_t^- H_x^T + R_t$ Kalman Gain: $K_t = P_t^- H_x^T S_t^{-1}$ State update: $\hat{x}_t = \hat{x}_t^- + K_t \nu_t$ Covariance update: $P_t = (I - K_t H_x) P_t^-$ **Limitations (2 marks):** 1. **Quadratic Complexity $O(n^2)$** — Infeasible for large environments. 2. **Single Hypothesis Data Association** — Fails in cluttered or ambiguous scenes. 3. **Linearization Errors** — First-order Taylor approximation causes inconsistency in long trajectories. 4. **Gaussian Assumption** — Cannot represent multi-modal posteriors. ### 5.3 Part B Question — Choice B (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam - Sample Model Paper Style]** **Question B:** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the different types of SLAM based on sensors, algorithms, and map representation. Give two examples for each category. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare and contrast EKF-SLAM, FastSLAM, and Graph-SLAM in terms of complexity, scalability, and loop-closure handling. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:** **Classification (6 marks — 2 marks per category):** **1. Based on Sensor (2 marks):** - **LiDAR SLAM** — 2D (Hector SLAM) and 3D (LOAM, LIO-SAM). Used in autonomous cars and warehouses. - **Visual SLAM** — Monocular (ORB-SLAM), Stereo. Used in drones and AR. - **RGB-D SLAM** — Kinect-based indoor mapping (RTAB-Map). - **Sonar SLAM** — Underwater applications (e.g., AUVs in oceanic surveys). **2. Based on Algorithm (2 marks):** - **Kalman Filter based** — EKF-SLAM, UKF-SLAM. - **Particle Filter based** — FastSLAM 1.0, FastSLAM 2.0. - **Graph Optimization based** — Pose graph SLAM (g2o, GTSAM). - **Direct/Feature based** — DSO, LSD-SLAM, ORB-SLAM. **3. Based on Map (2 marks):** - **Metric / Occupancy Grid** — Hector SLAM, Cartographer. - **Landmark / Feature Map** — EKF-SLAM, FastSLAM. - **Topological Map** — Representing places as nodes connected by edges. - **Semantic Map** — Object-labeled maps (e.g., door, chair) for human-robot interaction. **Conclusion (1 mark):** Modern systems often combine multiple categories (e.g., Visual-Inertial SLAM with semantic mapping). --- **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:** | Parameter | EKF-SLAM | FastSLAM | Graph-SLAM | |---|---|---|---| | **Algorithmic Basis** | Extended Kalman Filter | Rao-Blackwellized Particle Filter | Non-linear Least Squares | | **Computational Complexity** | $O(n^2)$ per step | $O(M \log n)$ per step | $O(n \log n)$ for full optimization | | **Scalability** | Poor beyond 50–100 landmarks | Moderate (depends on particles $M$) | Excellent (sparse graphs) | | **Map Type** | Landmark list | Landmark list | Pose graph with landmarks | | **Loop Closure** | Cannot correct — drifts | Possible but particle-depleting | Excellent — global optimization | | **Data Association** | Single hypothesis (greedy) | Multi-hypothesis (per particle) | RANSAC-based geometric verification | | **Uncertainty Handling** | Gaussian only | Multi-modal posterior | Gaussian + robust kernels | | **Use Case** | Small-scale mapping | Medium environments with ambiguity | Large-scale (city-level) | **Key Discussion Points (3 marks):** 1. **EKF-SLAM** is the historical baseline but suffers from quadratic cost and linearization drift. 2. **FastSLAM** decouples the SLAM posterior into a product of $M$ independent landmark EKFs conditioned on a particle, yielding $O(M \log n)$ complexity. 3. **Graph-SLAM** defers computation to a backend optimization, allowing **loop closures** to retroactively correct the entire trajectory — making it the modern standard (used in Google's Cartographer and OpenStreetMap pipelines). ### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 ESE:** > 1. **Writing "SLAM is a robot that maps and localizes"** — This is incorrect. SLAM is an *algorithm/problem*, not a robot. Always say *"SLAM is the computational problem/method..."* — **(Lose 0.5–1 mark)** > 2. **Confusing $p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ with the motion model** — The full posterior contains the motion model and observation model *inside* it after Bayes expansion. **(Lose 1–2 marks)** > 3. **Forgetting the Markov assumption** in the Bayesian derivation — This is a mandatory justification step. **(Lose 1 mark)** > 4. **Mixing up EKF-SLAM and FastSLAM complexities** — EKF-SLAM is $O(n^2)$; FastSLAM is $O(M \log n)$. Many students write $O(n^2)$ for both. **(Lose 0.5 mark)** > 5. **Not distinguishing "Online SLAM" vs "Full SLAM"** — Online SLAM estimates only $p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$, while Full SLAM estimates the entire trajectory $p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$. **(Lose 1 mark)** ### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Exam Prep):** - ✅ **SLAM = Joint estimation of map + pose** — the chicken-and-egg problem. - ✅ **Mathematical objective** is the joint posterior $p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$. - ✅ **Recursion** comes from the Markov + Bayes + Marginalization trio. - ✅ **Two core models:** Motion model $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$ and Observation model $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$. - ✅ **Online SLAM** = current pose only; **Full SLAM** = entire trajectory. - ✅ **EKF-SLAM complexity** is $O(n^2)$ — quadratic in number of landmarks. - ✅ **FastSLAM complexity** is $O(M \log n)$ — $M$ particles, $n$ landmarks. - ✅ **Graph-SLAM** uses pose graph + non-linear least squares — modern standard. - ✅ **Loop closure** is the key feature that distinguishes good SLAM from drifting SLAM. - ✅ **Four sensor types:** LiDAR, Camera (Visual), Sonar, RGB-D. - ✅ **Four map types:** Metric grid, Landmark feature, Topological, Semantic. - ✅ **Kalman Gain** $K_t$ trades off prediction uncertainty vs. sensor noise. - ✅ **First EKF-SLAM implementation** was by Smith, Self, and Cheeseman (1990) — historical fact for viva. - ✅ **ORB-SLAM, Cartographer, LOAM, LIO-SAM** are the modern state-of-the-art SLAM systems to remember. - ✅ **No programming code** in KTU exams for this topic — pure math + diagrams.

topics.title: Path Planning - Graph Search - Deterministic Graph Search - Breadth First Search - Depth First Search - Dijkstra's Algorithm - A* - D* Algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAT602 - ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Path Planning - Graph Search - Deterministic Graph Search - Breadth First Search - Depth First Search - Dijkstra's Algorithm - A* - D* Algorithms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 4: Path Planning & Deterministic Graph Search

## 1.1 What is Path Planning?

**Path Planning** is the computational problem of finding a geometric, collision-free trajectory that takes a mobile robot (or manipulator) from an initial configuration to a desired goal configuration while optimizing a chosen criterion — usually path length, time, energy, or safety margin.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, path planning sits at the intersection of **kinematics**, **artificial intelligence**, and **control theory**. The configuration space (C-space) of the robot is the universe in which planning occurs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Module 4 Focus**
> Path planning is divided into two broad families:
> - **Graph Search (Discrete Planning)** — uses a discretized representation.
> - **Sampling-Based Planning (Continuous Planning)** — e.g., RRT, PRM.
> Module 4 of PCCAT602 concentrates on **Graph Search** algorithms.

## 1.2 Graph Search — The Big Picture

A **graph** $G = (V, E)$ is a mathematical abstraction where:
- $V$ = set of **vertices / nodes** (representing configurations / cells / waypoints).
- $E$ = set of **edges** (representing valid transitions between configurations).

Each edge may carry a **cost** $c(u,v) \geq 0$ (e.g., distance, time, energy).

**Graph search** = systematically traversing this graph to discover a path from a **start node** $s$ to a **goal node** $g_{\text{goal}}$.

### 🎯 Intuitive Analogy

> Imagine you are inside a large shopping mall and want to reach the food court. The mall map is the **graph**, stores are **nodes**, and corridors connecting stores are **edges**. Walking down a corridor has a "cost" (length or crowd density). A graph search algorithm is like a smart GPS that decides *which corridor to try next* to reach your destination quickly.

## 1.3 Deterministic vs. Non-Deterministic (Stochastic) Search

| Feature | Deterministic Search | Stochastic Search |
|---|---|---|
| Tie-breaking rule | Fixed / predefined | Random (e.g., coin toss) |
| Repeatability | Identical output every run | Different output each run |
| Examples | BFS, DFS, Dijkstra, A*, D* | Randomized DFS, RRT, Genetic Algorithms |

A **Deterministic Graph Search** algorithm produces the **same path every time** for the same input graph — crucial in industrial robotics where reproducibility is a hard requirement.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Deterministic Graph Search):**
> A graph traversal procedure in which the choice of the next node to expand is uniquely determined by the current state of the search (open list, closed list, costs, and heuristics) — without any element of randomness.

## 1.4 Roadmap of the Topic

We will study **five foundational deterministic graph search algorithms**, in increasing order of intelligence:

1. **Breadth-First Search (BFS)** — level-by-level expansion.
2. **Depth-First Search (DFS)** — deepest expansion first.
3. **Dijkstra's Algorithm** — uniform-cost shortest path.
4. **A* (A-Star) Algorithm** — Dijkstra + heuristic guidance.
5. **D* (Dynamic A*) Algorithm** — incremental replanning for unknown / changing environments.

---

## 🧠 Why Should a Robotics Engineer Care?

In **industrial automation**, a mobile robot transporting goods in a warehouse must repeatedly replan as shelves move. In **autonomous vehicles**, sudden obstacles force replanning at millisecond timescales. Understanding deterministic graph search gives you the mathematical and algorithmic foundation to build such systems reliably.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Common Terminology Across All Algorithms

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $s$ | Start node |
| $g_{\text{goal}}$ | Goal node |
| $g(n)$ | **Actual cost** from start $s$ to node $n$ along the best known path |
| $h(n)$ | **Heuristic estimate** of cost from node $n$ to goal $g_{\text{goal}}$ |
| $f(n)$ | **Evaluation function** $= g(n) + h(n)$ |
| $c(u,v)$ | Cost of traversing edge from $u$ to $v$ |
| $d(u,v)$ | Shortest-path distance from $u$ to $v$ |
| Open List | Set of nodes discovered but not yet expanded (frontier) |
| Closed List | Set of nodes already expanded (explored) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **Dijkstra's** algorithm, $h(n) = 0$ for all nodes, so $f(n) = g(n)$.
> In **A***, $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ — the heuristic guides the search.

---

## 2.2 Breadth-First Search (BFS)

### Definition
**BFS** explores all nodes at the current depth before moving to any node at the next depth level. It uses a **FIFO Queue**.

### Properties
- **Complete:** Yes (finds goal if reachable).
- **Optimal:** Only if all edges have **uniform cost** $c(u,v) = 1$.
- **Time Complexity:** $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$.
- **Space Complexity:** $O(\vert V \vert)$ (worst case — entire frontier in memory).
- **Data Structure:** FIFO Queue (First-In First-Out).

### Algorithm (Formal Pseudocode)

```
BFS(s, goal):
    1. Create empty queue Q, push s into Q
    2. Mark s as visited
    3. While Q is not empty:
         node ← Q.pop_front()
         If node == goal: return RECONSTRUCT_PATH()
         For each neighbor v of node:
             If v not visited:
                 Mark v as visited
                 parent[v] ← node
                 Q.push_back(v)
    4. Return FAILURE
```

### Intuition (Lakhs-of-Students Friendly)
> Think of ripples expanding in a pond when you drop a stone. The first ripple is the start node. The search expands outward in concentric rings — every node at distance 1 is explored before any node at distance 2, and so on.

---

## 2.3 Depth-First Search (DFS)

### Definition
**DFS** explores as deep as possible along each branch before backtracking. It uses a **LIFO Stack** (or recursion).

### Properties
- **Complete:** Yes (in finite graphs).
- **Optimal:** **No** — does not guarantee shortest path.
- **Time Complexity:** $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$.
- **Space Complexity:** $O(\vert V \vert)$ in worst case, but typically $O(d)$ where $d$ = max depth.
- **Data Structure:** LIFO Stack (Last-In First-Out).

### Algorithm

```
DFS(s, goal):
    1. Create empty stack S, push s onto S
    2. Mark s as visited
    3. While S is not empty:
         node ← S.pop()
         If node == goal: return RECONSTRUCT_PATH()
         For each neighbor v of node:
             If v not visited:
                 Mark v as visited
                 parent[v] ← node
                 S.push(v)
    4. Return FAILURE
```

### Intuition
> You are in a maze. You always take the leftmost unexplored path until you hit a dead-end. Then you backtrack to the last junction and try the next branch. DFS is exactly this strategy.

---

## 2.4 Dijkstra's Algorithm

### Definition
**Dijkstra's Algorithm** finds the **shortest path** from a source node $s$ to **all other nodes** in a graph with **non-negative edge weights**. It is the foundation upon which A* is built.

### Properties
- **Complete:** Yes.
- **Optimal:** Yes (for non-negative weights).
- **Time Complexity:** $O(\vert V \vert^2)$ with array; $O((\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert) \log \vert V \vert)$ with binary heap.
- **Space Complexity:** $O(\vert V \vert)$.
- **Data Structure:** Priority Queue (min-heap) keyed by $g(n)$.

### Algorithm

```
Dijkstra(s, goal):
    1. For all n in V: g(n) ← ∞, parent[n] ← NULL
    2. g(s) ← 0
    3. Insert all nodes into priority queue OPEN, keyed by g(n)
    4. While OPEN is not empty:
         u ← node in OPEN with minimum g(u)
         Remove u from OPEN, add u to CLOSED
         If u == goal: return RECONSTRUCT_PATH()
         For each neighbor v of u:
             If v in CLOSED: continue
             tentative_g ← g(u) + c(u,v)
             If tentative_g < g(v):
                 g(v) ← tentative_g
                 parent[v] ← u
                 Update v in OPEN with new key
    5. Return FAILURE
```

### Relaxation Step
The line `If tentative_g < g(v)` is the **relaxation operation** — it updates the shortest known path if a better one is found.

### Intuition
> Like water spreading from a source, with elevation as cost. Water reaches the lowest-cost neighbor first, then propagates outward. The order in which nodes are "finalized" depends entirely on accumulated cost $g(n)$.

---

## 2.5 A* (A-Star) Algorithm

### Definition
**A*** is **Dijkstra + Heuristic**. It adds a heuristic $h(n)$ that estimates the cost-to-goal, biasing the search toward the goal and dramatically reducing the number of expanded nodes.

### The Master Formula

$$f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$$

where:
- $g(n)$ = known cost from $s$ to $n$.
- $h(n)$ = estimated cost from $n$ to $g_{\text{goal}}$.
- $f(n)$ = estimated cost of the **cheapest solution passing through $n$**.

### Admissibility & Consistency

| Property | Definition | Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| **Admissible Heuristic** | $h(n) \leq d(n, g_{\text{goal}})$ (never overestimates) | Guarantees **optimal path** |
| **Consistent (Monotone) Heuristic** | $h(u) \leq c(u,v) + h(v)$ for all edges $(u,v)$ | Guarantees $g$-values never need re-opening |

Common heuristics in robotics:
- **Euclidean Distance:** $h(n) = \sqrt{(x_n - x_g)^2 + (y_n - y_g)^2}$ — admissible.
- **Manhattan Distance:** $h(n) = \vert x_n - x_g \vert + \vert y_n - y_g \vert$ — admissible for 4-connected grids.
- **Chebyshev Distance:** $h(n) = \max(\vert x_n - x_g \vert, \vert y_n - y_g \vert)$ — admissible for 8-connected grids.

### Properties
- **Complete:** Yes (in finite graphs with bounded costs).
- **Optimal:** Yes (with admissible heuristic).
- **Time Complexity:** $O(\vert V \vert \log \vert V \vert)$ with binary heap — typically much less than Dijkstra because $h(n)$ prunes nodes.
- **Space Complexity:** $O(\vert V \vert)$.

### Algorithm

```
A_Star(s, goal):
    1. For all n in V: g(n) ← ∞, f(n) ← ∞, parent[n] ← NULL
    2. g(s) ← 0, f(s) ← h(s)
    3. Insert s into priority queue OPEN keyed by f
    4. While OPEN is not empty:
         u ← node in OPEN with minimum f(u)
         If u == goal: return RECONSTRUCT_PATH()
         Remove u from OPEN, add u to CLOSED
         For each neighbor v of u:
             If v in CLOSED: continue
             tentative_g ← g(u) + c(u,v)
             If tentative_g < g(v):
                 g(v) ← tentative_g
                 f(v) ← g(v) + h(v)
                 parent[v] ← u
                 Insert/Update v in OPEN
    5. Return FAILURE
```

---

## 2.6 D* (Dynamic A*) Algorithm

### Definition
**D*** is an **incremental, dynamic replanning** version of A*. Instead of replanning from scratch when the environment changes, D* reuses previous search information and locally repairs the path. Originally proposed by Anthony Stentz (1994).

### Why D* Matters in Robotics
- Sensor information may reveal new obstacles **after** initial planning.
- Replanning from scratch (running A* again) is computationally expensive.
- D* modifies only the affected portion of the path — much faster.

### Key Idea
D* propagates cost changes **from the goal backward through the graph**, updating only nodes whose $g$-values are affected. The state of each node is one of:
- **NEW** — never been in the open list.
- **OPEN** — currently in the open list.
- **CLOSED** — already expanded.
- **RAISE** — $g$-value increased (new obstacle detected).
- **LOWER** — $g$-value decreased (new shorter path found).

### Process
1. Run A* from goal to start (note the **reverse direction**).
2. Robot begins executing the path.
3. When an obstacle is detected, run **PROCESS-STATE** to propagate cost changes.
4. Path is repaired locally.

> [!NOTE]
> **D* Lite (Koenig & Likhachev, 2002)** is a simplified, more popular successor of D* that uses the same incremental search ideas but with cleaner implementation.

### Properties
- **Complete:** Yes.
- **Optimal:** Yes (after convergence).
- **Time Complexity:** Same as A* in the worst case; far better for incremental updates.

---

## 2.7 📊 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | BFS Cost | $f(n) = d(s,n)$ | Uniform edge weights |
| 2 | DFS Cost | No cost function | Uses backtracking |
| 3 | Dijkstra Evaluation | $f(n) = g(n)$ | $h(n) = 0$ |
| 4 | A* Evaluation | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | Core formula |
| 5 | Admissibility | $h(n) \leq d(n, g_{\text{goal}})$ | Guarantees optimality |
| 6 | Consistency | $h(u) \leq c(u,v) + h(v)$ | Triangle inequality |
| 7 | Euclidean Heuristic | $h(n) = \sqrt{(x_n - x_g)^2 + (y_n - y_g)^2}$ | 2D admissible |
| 8 | Manhattan Heuristic | $h(n) = \vert x_n - x_g \vert + \vert y_n - y_g \vert$ | 4-connected grid |
| 9 | Chebyshev Heuristic | $h(n) = \max(\vert x_n - x_g \vert, \vert y_n - y_g \vert)$ | 8-connected grid |
| 10 | BFS Complexity | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | Time |
| 11 | Dijkstra Complexity | $O((\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert) \log \vert V \vert)$ | With binary heap |
| 12 | A* Complexity | $O(\vert V \vert \log \vert V \vert)$ | Worst case |
| 13 | Relaxation | If $g(u) + c(u,v) < g(v)$, then $g(v) \leftarrow g(u) + c(u,v)$ | Core update rule |
| 14 | Triangle Inequality | $d(u,w) \leq d(u,v) + d(v,w)$ | Foundation of heuristics |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Algorithm | Industry Application |
|---|---|
| **BFS** | Shortest path in unweighted social networks, web crawlers, broadcasting. |
| **DFS** | Topological sorting, cycle detection, maze exploration, garbage collection. |
| **Dijkstra** | GPS routing (without traffic), network routing (OSPF protocol). |
| **A*** | Video game pathfinding, robot navigation in known maps, Google Maps base routing. |
| **D*** | Mars Rover navigation (NASA), autonomous cars in dynamic traffic, warehouse robots. |

# 🔢 Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples

To make every algorithm **concrete and traceable**, we will use a **single sample graph** throughout this section. This is the same pattern KTU examiners expect you to follow in your answer sheets.

## 3.1 The Reference Graph

Consider the following weighted, undirected graph $G$ with **6 nodes** $V = \{S, A, B, C, D, G\}$ where:
- $S$ = Start node
- $G$ = Goal node

### Edge Weights

| Edge | Cost | Edge | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| $(S, A)$ | 1 | $(B, C)$ | 1 |
| $(S, B)$ | 4 | $(B, D)$ | 4 |
| $(A, B)$ | 2 | $(C, D)$ | 3 |
| $(A, C)$ | 5 | $(C, G)$ | 2 |
| — | — | $(D, G)$ | 1 |

### Heuristic Values $h(n)$ (Euclidean-style, admissible)

| Node | S | A | B | C | D | G |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $h(n)$ | 7 | 6 | 4 | 2 | 2 | 0 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Verify admissibility: every $h(n)$ above is **less than or equal to** the true shortest distance from $n$ to $G$. This makes it a valid admissible heuristic for A*.

---

## 3.2 BFS Trace (Uniform Cost = 1)

> [!NOTE]
> BFS ignores weights — every edge has cost 1. The shortest path from $S$ to $G$ in BFS is by minimum **number of edges**, not minimum weight.

### Step-by-Step Trace

| Step | Open Queue (FIFO) | Visited | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $[S]$ | $\{S\}$ | Pop $S$, expand |
| 2 | $[A, B]$ | $\{S, A, B\}$ | Pop $A$, no new neighbors (B already visited) |
| 3 | $[B, C]$ | $\{S, A, B, C\}$ | Pop $B$, expand to $D$ |
| 4 | $[C, D]$ | $\{S, A, B, C, D\}$ | Pop $C$, expand to $G$ |
| 5 | $[D, G]$ | $\{S, A, B, C, D, G\}$ | Pop $D$, $G$ already found |

**BFS Path Found:** $S \rightarrow A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C \rightarrow G$ (4 edges).

> ⚠️ This is **NOT** the minimum-weight path — it is the minimum-**hop** path. BFS gives the correct shortest path only when edge weights are uniform.

---

## 3.3 DFS Trace

### Step-by-Step Trace (using a stack, neighbors in alphabetical order)

| Step | Stack (LIFO) | Visited | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $[S]$ | $\{S\}$ | Pop $S$, expand |
| 2 | $[B, A]$ | $\{S, A, B\}$ | Push neighbors alphabetically: A then B |
| 3 | $[C, A]$ | $\{S, A, B, C\}$ | Pop $B$, push $C, D$ (A already visited) |
| 4 | $[D, A]$ | $\{S, A, B, C, D\}$ | Pop $C$, $D$ already in stack |
| 5 | $[A]$ | $\{S, A, B, C, D\}$ | Pop $D$, expand to $G$ |
| 6 | $[G]$ | $\{S, A, B, C, D, G\}$ | Pop $A$, no new |
| 7 | $[\ ]$ | $\{S, A, B, C, D, G\}$ | Pop $G$ → **GOAL FOUND** |

**DFS Path Found:** $S \rightarrow B \rightarrow C \rightarrow D \rightarrow G$ (4 edges, weight = $4+1+3+1 = 9$).

**DFS is NOT optimal** — it found a longer path than necessary.

---

## 3.4 Dijkstra's Algorithm — Exhaustive Trace

We maintain:
- $g(n)$ = best known cost from $S$ to $n$.
- Open list = priority queue keyed by $g$.
- Closed list = finalized nodes.

### Trace Table

| Step | Pop (min $g$) | Neighbors Examined | Updated $g$ Values | Open State (after update) | Closed |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $S$ (g=0) | $A$ → $g=0+1=1$; $B$ → $g=0+4=4$ | $g(A)=1$, $g(B)=4$ | $\{A:1, B:4\}$ | $\{S\}$ |
| 2 | $A$ (g=1) | $B$ → $1+2=3 < 4$ ✓; $C$ → $1+5=6$ | $g(B)=3$, $g(C)=6$ | $\{B:3, C:6\}$ | $\{S,A\}$ |
| 3 | $B$ (g=3) | $C$ → $3+1=4 < 6$ ✓; $D$ → $3+4=7$ | $g(C)=4$, $g(D)=7$ | $\{C:4, D:7\}$ | $\{S,A,B\}$ |
| 4 | $C$ (g=4) | $D$ → $4+3=7 = 7$ no change; $G$ → $4+2=6$ | $g(G)=6$ | $\{D:7, G:6\}$ | $\{S,A,B,C\}$ |
| 5 | $G$ (g=6) | — | — | $\{D:7\}$ | $\{S,A,B,C,G\}$ |

**GOAL POPPED at step 5** with $g(G) = 6$.

### Reconstructing the Path

Following `parent[]` pointers backward from $G$:
- $G$ ← $C$ ← $B$ ← $A$ ← $S$

**Dijkstra's Optimal Path:** $\boxed{S \rightarrow A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C \rightarrow G}$
**Total Cost:** $g(G) = 1 + 2 + 1 + 2 = 6$.

---

## 3.5 A* Algorithm — Exhaustive Trace

We maintain $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$.

### Trace Table

| Step | Pop (min $f$) | $g$, $h$, $f$ of Popped | Neighbors | New $g$ | New $f$ | Open After |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $S$ | $g=0, h=7, f=7$ | $A$: $g=1, f=1+6=7$; $B$: $g=4, f=4+4=8$ | $g(A)=1, g(B)=4$ | $f(A)=7, f(B)=8$ | $\{A:7, B:8\}$ |
| 2 | $A$ | $g=1, h=6, f=7$ | $B$: $1+2=3 < 4$ → $g=3$; $C$: $1+5=6$ | $g(B)=3, g(C)=6$ | $f(B)=7, f(C)=8$ | $\{B:7, C:8\}$ |
| 3 | $B$ | $g=3, h=4, f=7$ | $C$: $3+1=4 < 6$ → $g=4$; $D$: $3+4=7$ | $g(C)=4, g(D)=7$ | $f(C)=6, f(D)=9$ | $\{C:6, D:9\}$ |
| 4 | $C$ | $g=4, h=2, f=6$ | $D$: $4+3=7 = 7$ no change; $G$: $4+2=6$ | $g(G)=6$ | $f(G)=6$ | $\{G:6, D:9\}$ |
| 5 | $G$ | $g=6, h=0, f=6$ | — | — | — | — |

**GOAL POPPED at step 5** with $f(G) = g(G) + h(G) = 6 + 0 = 6$.

### Reconstructing the Path
- $G$ ← $C$ ← $B$ ← $A$ ← $S$

**A* Optimal Path:** $\boxed{S \rightarrow A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C \rightarrow G}$
**Total Cost:** $6$.

### Why A* Is Smarter Than Dijkstra Here

| Metric | Dijkstra | A* |
|---|---|---|
| Nodes expanded to reach $G$ | 5 (S, A, B, C, G) | 5 (S, A, B, C, G) |
| Decision basis | $g$ only | $g + h$ |
| Ties at $f=7$ | — | $A$ and $B$ both $f=7$, alphabetical break |

In larger graphs, A* **drastically outperforms** Dijkstra because $h(n)$ prunes nodes whose $f$-value already exceeds the best known goal cost.

---

## 3.6 D* — Incremental Replanning Demonstration

Suppose after the A* plan $S \rightarrow A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C \rightarrow G$ is generated, the robot moves to $B$ and its sensors detect that **edge $(B, C)$ has become blocked** (e.g., a new obstacle).

### Step 1 — Initial State
- Robot is at $B$.
- Path so far executed: $S \rightarrow A \rightarrow B$.
- Remaining planned path: $B \rightarrow C \rightarrow G$.

### Step 2 — Detect Cost Change
- $c(B, C)$ changes from $1$ to $\infty$.
- The $g$-value of $C$ (and all nodes that depended on going through $C$) is now invalid.

### Step 3 — Local Repair (RAISE state)
- Mark $C$ as **RAISE** (its $g$-value must be increased).
- Examine alternative neighbors of $B$: $D$ (cost $4$).
- Set $g(C) = \infty$ initially; later find $g(C)$ via $D$: $g(C) = g(D) + c(D, C)$.

### Step 4 — Backward Propagation
- Propagate changes backward through $g$.
- Find new optimal: $g(C) = 7$ (via $D$), then $g(G) = 8$ (via $D$).
- Updated path from $B$: $B \rightarrow D \rightarrow G$.

### Step 5 — New Path
$$\text{New path from } B: \quad B \rightarrow D \rightarrow G$$

> [!TIP]
> The beauty of D* is that it does **not** re-plan from $S$ — only the affected downstream portion is repaired. In a real warehouse robot operating at 1 m/s, this saves critical milliseconds.

### D* Search Direction — Important Distinction

> [!WARNING]
> Unlike A*, **D* plans from goal back to start** so that, when the robot moves forward, the cost changes (which occur near the robot) can be propagated forward using already-known information. This is a fundamental conceptual difference. Examiners love testing this!

---

## 3.7 Comparative Summary Table (Essential for KTU Answers)

| Criterion | BFS | DFS | Dijkstra | A* | D* |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Edge cost | Uniform only | Ignored | Non-negative | Non-negative | Non-negative |
| Optimal? | Yes (uniform) | No | Yes | Yes (admissible $h$) | Yes |
| Complete? | Yes | Yes (finite) | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Uses heuristic? | No | No | No | Yes | Yes |
| Direction | Start → Goal | Start → Goal | Start → Goal | Start → Goal | Goal → Start |
| Dynamic env. | No | No | No | No | **Yes** |
| Time (worst) | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | $O((V+E)\log V)$ | $O(V \log V)$ | $O(V \log V)$ + replan |
| Space | $O(V)$ | $O(V)$ | $O(V)$ | $O(V)$ | $O(V)$ |
| Data Structure | FIFO Queue | LIFO Stack | Priority Queue | Priority Queue | Priority Queue |

    S ---|4| B((B))

```

**Reading the diagram:**
- 🟢 **Green** = Start node $S$.
- 🔴 **Pink** = Goal node $G$.

---

## 4.2 BFS — Level-by-Level Expansion Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Initialize: push S to Queue]) --> Check{Queue empty?}
    Check -- No --> Pop[Pop front node n]
    Pop --> GoalCheck{n == goal?}
    GoalCheck -- Yes --> Return([Reconstruct path and return])
    GoalCheck -- No --> Visit[Mark n visited]
    Visit --> Neighbors[For each neighbor v of n]
    Neighbors --> Seen{v already visited?}
    Seen -- No --> Mark[Mark v visited, set parent]
    Mark --> Push[Push v to Queue]
    Push --> Check
    Seen -- Yes --> Check
    Check -- Yes --> Fail([Return FAILURE])

    style Start fill:#90EE90
    style Return fill:#FFB6C1
    style Fail fill:#FFB6C1
```

---

## 4.3 DFS — Stack-Based Backtracking Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Initialize: push S to Stack]) --> Check{Stack empty?}
    Check -- No --> Pop[Pop top node n]
    Pop --> GoalCheck{n == goal?}
    GoalCheck -- Yes --> Return([Reconstruct path and return])
    GoalCheck -- No --> Visit[Mark n visited]
    Visit --> Neighbors[For each neighbor v of n]
    Neighbors --> Seen{v already visited?}
    Seen -- No --> Mark[Mark v visited, set parent]
    Mark --> Push[Push v to Stack]
    Push --> Check
    Seen -- Yes --> Check
    Check -- Yes --> Fail([Return FAILURE])

    style Start fill:#90EE90
    style Return fill:#FFB6C1
    style Fail fill:#FFB6C1
```

> [!NOTE]
> Visually, BFS and DFS flows look identical — the only difference is **Queue vs. Stack** (FIFO vs. LIFO). Examiners frequently test this distinction.

---

## 4.4 A* Algorithm — Architectural Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A([Initialize OPEN with start s]) --> B[Compute f(s)=g(s)+h(s)]
    B --> C{OPEN empty?}
    C -- Yes --> F([Return FAILURE])
    C -- No --> D[Pop node n with minimum f n]
    D --> E{n == goal?}
    E -- Yes --> P([Reconstruct optimal path])
    E -- No --> G[Move n to CLOSED]
    G --> H[For each neighbor v of n]
    H --> I{v in CLOSED?}
    I -- Yes --> H
    I -- No --> J[tentative g = g n + cost n to v]
    J --> K{tentative g < g v?}
    K -- No --> H
    K -- Yes --> L[Update g v and f v]
    L --> M[Set parent of v to n]
    M --> N[Insert or Update v in OPEN]
    N --> H
    H --> C

    style A fill:#90EE90
    style P fill:#FFB6C1
    style F fill:#FFB6C1
```

---

## 4.5 D* — Dynamic Replanning Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Init([Initial Plan: A* from Goal to Start]) --> Exec[Robot executes path forward]
    Exec --> Sense{Sensor detects change?}
    Sense -- No --> Exec
    Sense -- Yes --> Update[Update edge costs in map]
    Update --> Mark[Mark affected nodes RAISE or LOWER]
    Mark --> Process[Call PROCESS-STATE repeatedly]
    Process --> Repair{Local repair needed?}
    Repair -- Yes --> Replan[Update g-values and parent pointers]
    Replan --> Process
    Repair -- No --> NewPath([Updated optimal path ready])
    NewPath --> Exec

    style Init fill:#90EE90
    style NewPath fill:#FFB6C1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **loop** in this diagram captures the essence of D* — the robot keeps executing and replanning **continuously** as new sensor data arrives. This is exactly how the Mars rovers navigate unknown terrain.

---

## 4.6 Algorithm Decision Tree — Choosing the Right Search

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Edge weights uniform?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Need shortest path?}
    Q1 -- No --> Q3{Have admissible heuristic?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> A1([Use BFS])
    Q2 -- No --> A2([Use DFS])
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q4{Environment changes at runtime?}
    Q3 -- No --> A3([Use Dijkstra])
    Q4 -- Yes --> A4([Use D Star])
    Q4 -- No --> A5([Use A Star])

    style A1 fill:#90EE90
    style A2 fill:#90EE90
    style A3 fill:#90EE90
    style A4 fill:#FFB6C1
    style A5 fill:#FFB6C1
```
