# 🎓 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]`
**Define deterministic graph search. How does it differ from stochastic search algorithms?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **deterministic graph search** is a path-planning procedure in which the choice of the next node to expand is uniquely determined by the current state of the search (open list, closed list, accumulated costs, and heuristic values). The algorithm produces **identical results on every run** for the same input graph.

In contrast, **stochastic (non-deterministic) search algorithms** use randomized tie-breaking rules or probabilistic decisions (e.g., RRT, Genetic Algorithms). Two runs on the same input may yield different paths.

**Key distinctions:**
- Deterministic → **reproducible**, **certifiable** (industrial requirement).
- Stochastic → **faster exploration** of high-dimensional spaces (research / exploration robots).

> [!NOTE]
> **Cognitive Level:** L2 (Understand). **CO Mapping:** CO4 — Apply path planning techniques.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]`
**State and explain the admissibility condition for a heuristic function used in the A* algorithm.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A heuristic function $h(n)$ is **admissible** if, for every node $n$ in the search graph:

$$h(n) \leq d(n, g_{\text{goal}})$$

where $d(n, g_{\text{goal}})$ is the **true shortest distance** from $n$ to the goal.

In other words, $h(n)$ must **never overestimate** the actual remaining cost to the goal.

**Significance:** When $h(n)$ is admissible, A* is guaranteed to return an **optimal (minimum-cost) path**. Common admissible heuristics include Euclidean distance, Manhattan distance, and Chebyshev distance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cognitive Level:** L2 (Understand).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]` — 14 Marks

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **A* algorithm** for path planning in robotics. Define the evaluation function and explain the role of the heuristic. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Apply the **A* algorithm** on the graph shown below. Show every step, including the values of $g(n)$, $h(n)$, and $f(n)$. Determine the optimal path from $S$ to $G$. **(7 marks)**

| Edge | Cost | Edge | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| $S \rightarrow A$ | 1 | $B \rightarrow D$ | 4 |
| $S \rightarrow B$ | 4 | $C \rightarrow D$ | 3 |
| $A \rightarrow B$ | 2 | $C \rightarrow G$ | 2 |
| $A \rightarrow C$ | 5 | $D \rightarrow G$ | 1 |
| $B \rightarrow C$ | 1 | | |

Heuristic: $h(S)=7, h(A)=6, h(B)=4, h(C)=2, h(D)=2, h(G)=0$.

#### ✅ Model Solution

**(a) Explanation (7 marks):**

The **A* algorithm** is a best-first search algorithm that finds the least-cost path from a start node $s$ to a goal node $g_{\text{goal}}$ in a weighted graph. It combines the strengths of **Dijkstra's algorithm** (which guarantees optimality) with the goal-directed efficiency of a heuristic.

**Evaluation function:**

$$f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$$

where:
- $g(n)$ is the **actual cost** from $s$ to $n$ along the best discovered path.
- $h(n)$ is the **heuristic estimate** of cost from $n$ to $g_{\text{goal}}$.
- $f(n)$ is the **estimated total cost** of the cheapest solution passing through $n$.

**Role of the heuristic:**
- If $h(n) = 0$, A* degenerates to Dijkstra (uniform exploration in all directions).
- If $h(n)$ is **admissible** (never overestimates true cost), A* guarantees **optimality**.
- A well-designed heuristic drastically reduces the number of nodes expanded, focusing search toward the goal.

**Algorithm steps (high-level):**
1. Initialize $g(s) = 0$, $f(s) = h(s)$, insert $s$ into OPEN.
2. While OPEN is not empty: pop node $n$ with minimum $f(n)$.
3. If $n$ = goal → return reconstructed path.
4. Otherwise expand neighbors using the **relaxation step**:

$$\text{If } g(n) + c(n,v) < g(v) \text{, then } g(v) \leftarrow g(n) + c(n,v), \; f(v) \leftarrow g(v) + h(v)$$

5. Insert updated neighbors into OPEN keyed by $f$.

**(b) Step-by-Step Trace (7 marks):**

| Step | Pop $n$ | $g(n), h(n), f(n)$ | Neighbors & New $g$ | Open after update |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $S$ | $0, 7, 7$ | $A: g=1, f=7$; $B: g=4, f=8$ | $\{A:7, B:8\}$ |
| 2 | $A$ | $1, 6, 7$ | $B: g=3, f=7$; $C: g=6, f=8$ | $\{B:7, C:8\}$ |
| 3 | $B$ | $3, 4, 7$ | $C: g=4, f=6$; $D: g=7, f=9$ | $\{C:6, D:9\}$ |
| 4 | $C$ | $4, 2, 6$ | $D: g=7$ (no change); $G: g=6, f=6$ | $\{G:6, D:9\}$ |
| 5 | $G$ | $6, 0, 6$ | GOAL REACHED | — |

**Optimal Path:** $S \rightarrow A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C \rightarrow G$
**Total Cost:** $g(G) = 1 + 2 + 1 + 2 = 6$.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]` — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Compare and contrast **Dijkstra's algorithm** and **A* algorithm**. Discuss their optimality, completeness, time complexity, and use of heuristics. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **D* algorithm**. Why is it preferred over A* in dynamic environments? Illustrate with a scenario where replanning is needed. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution

**(a) Comparison (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Dijkstra's Algorithm | A* Algorithm |
|---|---|---|
| Evaluation function | $f(n) = g(n)$ | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ |
| Heuristic used | No ($h(n) = 0$) | Yes |
| Optimality | Yes (non-negative weights) | Yes (admissible $h$) |
| Completeness | Yes | Yes |
| Time complexity | $O((V+E)\log V)$ with heap | $O(V\log V)$ worst, but faster in practice |
| Search direction | Uniform in all directions | Goal-directed (focused) |
| Number of nodes expanded | Many — explores entire reachable space | Few — heuristic prunes irrelevant branches |
| Suitable for | Static, known environments | Static, known environments with heuristic available |
| Disadvantage | Slow on large graphs | Depends on heuristic quality |

**Conclusion:** A* is essentially Dijkstra **plus a heuristic**. With a good heuristic, A* expands **far fewer nodes** while still guaranteeing optimality — making it the preferred choice for large graphs.

**(b) D* Algorithm (7 marks):**

**D* (Dynamic A*)**, proposed by Anthony Stentz (1994), is an **incremental heuristic search algorithm** designed for environments where **cost changes are discovered during execution**.

**Key Concepts:**
- D* plans from **goal → start** (reverse of A*), so that cost changes near the robot (forward direction) can be propagated efficiently.
- Each node has a **state tag**: NEW, OPEN, CLOSED, RAISE, or LOWER.
- When the robot senses a change, only the affected portion of the search tree is repaired.

**Why D* is preferred over A* in dynamic environments:**

| Aspect | A* | D* |
|---|---|---|
| Replanning from scratch | Yes (wasteful) | No (incremental) |
| Reuse of previous search | No | Yes |
| Speed after first plan | Slow | Fast |
| Sensor integration | Not native | Built-in |

**Scenario:** A warehouse robot has planned path $S \rightarrow A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C \rightarrow G$. While moving, it detects a **newly placed obstacle** between $B$ and $C$. With A*, it must throw away the entire plan and re-plan from scratch. With D*, it **only re-plans the downstream portion** from $B$ onward, finding new path $B \rightarrow D \rightarrow G$. This is computationally far cheaper — critical in real-time robotics.

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> Many students write "D* plans from start to goal" — this is **WRONG**. D* plans from **goal back to start** so that forward propagation of cost changes during robot motion is efficient. Also, students often confuse D* with D* Lite — note that **D* Lite (Koenig & Likhachev, 2002)** is a modern, simpler successor of the original D* and is the more commonly used variant in practice.

---

## 5.3 🧾 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist (High Density)**

- ✅ **BFS** = FIFO queue, finds shortest path only for **uniform edge costs**.
- ✅ **DFS** = LIFO stack, **not optimal**, useful for cycle detection / topological sort.
- ✅ **Dijkstra** = priority queue on $g(n)$, optimal for non-negative weights, **no heuristic**.
- ✅ **A*** = priority queue on $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$, optimal when $h$ is admissible.
- ✅ **Admissible heuristic** = $h(n) \leq d(n, g_{\text{goal}})$. Never overestimates.
- ✅ **Consistent heuristic** = $h(u) \leq c(u,v) + h(v)$. Stronger than admissible.
- ✅ **Euclidean / Manhattan / Chebyshev** are admissible heuristics for grid worlds.
- ✅ **D*** = incremental replanning, plans **goal → start**, propagates cost changes forward.
- ✅ **D* Lite** is the modern successor of D*, used in most contemporary robotic systems.
- ✅ **Relaxation** = the core update operation: improve $g(v)$ if a shorter path is found.
- ✅ BFS and DFS differ only in **data structure**: Queue vs. Stack.
- ✅ A* degenerates to Dijkstra when $h(n) = 0$ for all $n$.
- ✅ A* degenerates to BFS (uniform-cost) when all $c(u,v) = 1$ and $h = 0$.
- ✅ In **Mars Rover navigation**, D* / D* Lite is the standard algorithm.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule of Graph Search (memorize this):**
> *"An algorithm is only as good as its heuristic. A well-designed admissible heuristic is the difference between searching 10,000 nodes and 100."*

---

### 📚 Further Reading (Standard References)

- **Hart, P. E., Nilsson, N. J., & Raphael, B. (1968).** "A Formal Basis for the Heuristic Determination of Minimum Cost Paths." *IEEE Transactions on Systems Science and Cybernetics.* — The original A* paper.
- **Stentz, A. (1994).** "Optimal and Efficient Path Planning for Partially-Known Environments." *Proceedings of the IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation.* — The original D* paper.
- **Koenig, S., & Likhachev, M. (2002).** "D* Lite." *Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence.* — The widely-used D* Lite paper.
- **Russell, S., & Norvig, P.** *Artificial Intelligence: A Modern Approach (AIMA)*, Chapters 3 & 4. — The standard AI textbook covering search algorithms in depth.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Potential Field Based Path Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAT602 - ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Potential Field Based Path Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Potential Field Based Path Planning** is a reactive, configuration-space (C-space) motion planning technique in which the robot is treated as a point particle moving under the influence of an **Artificial Potential Field (APF)**. The field is constructed as the superposition (sum) of:

1. An **Attractive Potential Field** $U_{att}(q)$ — generated by the goal configuration, which pulls the robot toward the target.
2. A **Repulsive Potential Field** $U_{rep}(q)$ — generated by obstacles, which pushes the robot away from collisions.

The total potential $U(q) = U_{att}(q) + U_{rep}(q)$ defines a scalar energy landscape over the robot's configuration space, and the robot descends the **negative gradient** of this field to reach the goal. The method was originally proposed by **Oussama Khatib (1986)** and is a cornerstone of real-time obstacle avoidance in modern robotics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic is a direct application of gradient-based optimization in C-space. The examiner expects both the *physical intuition* and the *vector calculus formulation* — i.e., $F = -\nabla U$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — A Rolling Ball in a Hilly Landscape**
> Imagine a bowling ball placed on a stretched rubber sheet. The **goal** is a deep valley (gravity wells the ball toward it), and each **obstacle** is a stiff tent-pole pushing upward from beneath the sheet (creating repulsive "hills"). The ball naturally rolls downhill, avoiding the hills and ending in the valley.
>
> - The **slope of the sheet** at any point = the **force vector** acting on the robot.
> - The **height of the sheet** = the **potential energy** $U(q)$.
> - The ball stops only when it reaches the goal (the global minimum).
>
> This is precisely how APF works: $U$ is the height map, and $-\nabla U$ is the direction the robot moves.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $\xi$ (xi) | Attractive gain (scaling constant) | N/m or unitless |
| $\eta$ (eta) | Repulsive gain (scaling constant) | N·m² or unitless |
| $\rho(q, q_{goal})$ | Euclidean distance from robot to goal | m |
| $\rho(q, q_{obs})$ | Euclidean distance from robot to obstacle | m |
| $\rho_0$ | Distance of influence of an obstacle | m |
| $q = (x, y)$ | Robot configuration in 2D plane | m, m |

> [!TIP]
> The trade-off between $\xi$ and $\eta$ directly controls path shape: a **high $\xi$** gives a straight, fast approach to the goal, while a **high $\eta$** gives a cautious, wide berth around obstacles.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step by Step

### Step 1: Define the Configuration Space
The robot operates in a 2D (or n-D) C-space where each point $q = (x, y)$ represents a unique pose.

### Step 2: Construct the Attractive Potential
A bowl-shaped function with its minimum at the goal. Two common forms exist:
- **Quadratic potential** (good near goal, steep far away)
- **Conic potential** (constant magnitude of attractive force regardless of distance — preferred for large workspaces).

### Step 3: Construct the Repulsive Potential
A function that grows to infinity (or a large value) as the robot approaches an obstacle, and is **zero** beyond the distance of influence $\rho_0$.

### Step 4: Superpose (Add) the Fields
Because potentials are scalars, they add algebraically — this is the **superposition principle**, which makes APF computationally efficient.

### Step 5: Compute the Force (Negative Gradient)
The resultant force on the robot is:
$$F(q) = -\nabla U(q) = -\nabla U_{att}(q) - \nabla U_{rep}(q)$$
This force vector dictates both the **direction** and the **speed** of the robot's next move.

### Step 6: Integrate to Update Position
Using simple discrete-time integration (Euler method):
$$q_{k+1} = q_k + \alpha \cdot F(q_k)$$
where $\alpha$ is a step-size parameter.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this works for real-time control:** Each step requires only local distance computations to nearby obstacles — no global map search is needed, making APF ideal for dynamic environments.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Conditions / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $U_{att}(q) = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\xi\,\rho^2(q, q_{goal})$ | Quadratic attractive potential | Smooth, $\nabla$ is linear in distance |
| 2 | $U_{att}(q) = \xi\,\rho(q, q_{goal})$ | Conic attractive potential | Constant force magnitude for $\rho > d$ |
| 3 | $F_{att}(q) = -\nabla U_{att} = -\xi\,(q - q_{goal})$ | Attractive force (quadratic case) | Points directly toward goal |
| 4 | $U_{rep}(q) = \begin{cases} \tfrac{1}{2}\,\eta\!\left(\tfrac{1}{\rho(q,q_{obs})} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^{\!2}, & \rho \le \rho_0 \\ 0, & \rho > \rho_0 \end{cases}$ | Repulsive potential (Khatib form) | Zero beyond $\rho_0$; $\to \infty$ at obstacle |
| 5 | $F_{rep}(q) = \begin{cases} \eta\!\left(\tfrac{1}{\rho} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)\tfrac{1}{\rho^2}\,\hat{e}_{obs}, & \rho \le \rho_0 \\ 0, & \rho > \rho_0 \end{cases}$ | Repulsive force | $\hat{e}_{obs}$ = unit vector from obstacle to robot |
| 6 | $U(q) = U_{att}(q) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} U_{rep,i}(q)$ | Total potential | Sum over all $n$ obstacles |
| 7 | $F(q) = -\nabla U(q)$ | Resultant generalized force | Used as motion command |
| 8 | $q_{k+1} = q_k + \alpha F(q_k)$ | Discrete motion update | $\alpha$ = step size, time step $\Delta t = 1$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-Symbol Note for KTU Examiners:** When copying these formulas into an answer script, write absolute value as $\vert \cdot \vert$, **never** as $\vert \cdot \vert$ in a markdown table, to avoid formatting breakage in digital answer sheets.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Use of APF |
|---|---|
| **Mobile robots (AGVs)** | Real-time obstacle avoidance in warehouses |
| **Robotic manipulators** | Online trajectory correction in cluttered cells |
| **Autonomous vehicles** | Lane-keeping + dynamic obstacle avoidance |
| **Drone swarms** | Decentralized collision-free flight |
| **Surgical robots** | Safe motion around anatomical obstacles |
| **Underwater ROVs** | Reactive navigation in unknown currents |

## 2.4 Advantages and Limitations

**Advantages:**
- Mathematically simple and computationally cheap ($O(n)$ per step where $n$ = number of obstacles).
- Naturally produces smooth trajectories.
- Easily handles dynamic / moving obstacles.

**Limitations (HIGH-YIELD for KTU):**
1. **Local Minima Problem** — robot gets trapped where $F_{att} = -F_{rep}$ but $q \neq q_{goal}$ (e.g., in a narrow corridor between two obstacles).
2. **Goal-Non-Reachable with Obstacles Nearby (GNRON)** — when the goal is inside the obstacle's repulsive field.
3. **Oscillations in narrow passages** — robot zig-zags between opposing repulsive forces.
4. **No consideration of robot dimensions** (point-mass assumption).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Attractive Force (Quadratic Potential)

**Given:**
$$U_{att}(q) = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\xi\,\rho^2(q, q_{goal}) \quad \text{where} \quad \rho^2 = (x - x_{goal})^2 + (y - y_{goal})^2$$

**Step 1 — Write the potential in coordinates:**
$$U_{att}(x, y) = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\xi\!\left[(x - x_{goal})^2 + (y - y_{goal})^2\right]$$

**Step 2 — Compute the partial derivative with respect to $x$:**
$$\frac{\partial U_{att}}{\partial x} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\xi \cdot 2(x - x_{goal}) = \xi\,(x - x_{goal})$$

**Step 3 — Compute the partial derivative with respect to $y$:**
$$\frac{\partial U_{att}}{\partial y} = \xi\,(y - y_{goal})$$

**Step 4 — Form the gradient vector:**
$$\nabla U_{att} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial U_{att}}{\partial x} \\[6pt] \dfrac{\partial U_{att}}{\partial y} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \xi(x - x_{goal}) \\[6pt] \xi(y - y_{goal}) \end{bmatrix} = \xi\,(q - q_{goal})$$

**Step 5 — Apply the gradient-descent rule $F = -\nabla U$:**
$$F_{att} = -\nabla U_{att} = -\xi\,(q - q_{goal})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The attractive force is a linear "spring" pulling the robot toward the goal with strength proportional to the displacement — analogous to **Hooke's Law** for a virtual spring of stiffness $\xi$.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Repulsive Force (Khatib's Form)

**Given:**
$$U_{rep}(q) = \begin{cases} \tfrac{1}{2}\,\eta\!\left(\dfrac{1}{\rho(q,q_{obs})} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^{\!2}, & \rho \le \rho_0 \\ 0, & \rho > \rho_0 \end{cases}$$

Let $\rho \equiv \rho(q, q_{obs})$ for brevity, and define $f(\rho) = \dfrac{1}{\rho} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0}$.

**Step 1 — Differentiate $U_{rep}$ using the chain rule:**
$$\frac{\partial U_{rep}}{\partial \rho} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\eta \cdot 2 f(\rho) \cdot \frac{df}{d\rho} = \eta\, f(\rho) \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{\rho^2}\right) = -\eta\!\left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)\!\frac{1}{\rho^2}$$

**Step 2 — Express $\partial\rho/\partial x$ and $\partial\rho/\partial y$:**
Since $\rho = \sqrt{(x - x_{obs})^2 + (y - y_{obs})^2}$,
$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial x} = \frac{x - x_{obs}}{\rho}, \qquad \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial y} = \frac{y - y_{obs}}{\rho}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the chain rule for the total gradient:**
$$\frac{\partial U_{rep}}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial U_{rep}}{\partial \rho}\cdot\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial x} = -\eta\!\left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)\!\frac{1}{\rho^2}\cdot\frac{x - x_{obs}}{\rho}$$

**Step 4 — Form the full gradient (in vector form):**
$$\nabla U_{rep} = -\eta\!\left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)\!\frac{1}{\rho^2}\cdot\hat{e}_{obs}$$
where $\hat{e}_{obs} = \dfrac{q - q_{obs}}{\rho}$ is the unit vector pointing **from obstacle to robot**.

**Step 5 — Apply $F_{rep} = -\nabla U_{rep}$:**
$$F_{rep} = \eta\!\left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)\!\frac{1}{\rho^2}\cdot\hat{e}_{obs} \quad \text{for} \quad \rho \le \rho_0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity checks:**
> - At $\rho = \rho_0$: $F_{rep} = 0$ (force vanishes at boundary of influence).
> - As $\rho \to 0$ (collision): $F_{rep} \to \infty$ (infinite push-away — robust safety).
> - Direction $\hat{e}_{obs}$: always points from obstacle outward, ensuring the robot is **pushed away** from the obstacle.

## 3.3 Local Minima — Mathematical Condition

A **local minimum** occurs when:
$$\nabla U(q^*) = 0 \quad \text{but} \quad q^* \neq q_{goal}$$

Expanding:
$$F_{att}(q^*) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} F_{rep,i}(q^*) = 0 \quad \text{with} \quad q^* \neq q_{goal}$$

**Classical example — symmetric trap between two obstacles:**
- Two obstacles equidistant from the line joining start and goal.
- Robot reaches midpoint where $F_{att}$ points toward goal and the two $F_{rep}$ vectors cancel horizontally but oppose $F_{att}$ vertically.
- Net force = 0 → robot stalls.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A common 7-mark question is to *sketch a configuration showing the local minimum* and explain why gradient descent fails. Memorize the symmetric two-obstacle case.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Summary of the Full Algorithm

| Step | Operation | Mathematical Expression | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sense | $q = (x,y)$ from odometry | Current pose |
| 2 | Compute attractive | $F_{att} = -\xi(q - q_{goal})$ | Goal seeking |
| 3 | Compute repulsive | $F_{rep,i} = \eta(\tfrac{1}{\rho_i} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0})\tfrac{1}{\rho_i^2}\hat{e}_{obs,i}$ | Obstacle avoidance |
| 4 | Sum forces | $F = F_{att} + \sum F_{rep,i}$ | Combine effects |
| 5 | Normalize direction | $\hat{F} = F / \vert F \vert$ | Unit direction |
| 6 | Update pose | $q_{k+1} = q_k + \alpha\,\hat{F}$ | Motion |
| 7 | Check goal | If $\rho(q, q_{goal}) < \epsilon$, STOP | Termination |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of APF Path Planning

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Robot at q_start] --> B[Read current configuration q]
    B --> C[Compute distance to goal: rho_goal]
    C --> D[Compute Attractive Force F_att]
    D --> E[For each obstacle i: compute rho_i]
    E --> F[Compute Repulsive Force F_rep_i]
    F --> G{Sum all forces F_total = F_att + Sigma F_rep_i}
    G --> H[Compute unit direction F_total / norm F_total]
    H --> I[Update configuration: q_new = q + alpha times F_total]
    I --> J{Distance to goal less than epsilon}
    J -- No --> B
    J -- Yes --> K[Goal Reached: Path Complete]
    
    style A fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style K fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style G fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style J fill:#d62728,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Potential Field Conceptual Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IN[Inputs]
        QG[Goal Configuration q_goal]
        OB[Obstacle Positions q_obs]
        QS[Start Configuration q_start]
    end
    
    subgraph APF[Artificial Potential Field Engine]
        UA[Attractive Potential Module: U_att = 0.5 xi rho^2]
        UR[Repulsive Potential Module: U_rep = 0.5 eta 1/rho minus 1/rho_0 squared]
        SUM[Superposition: U_total = U_att plus U_rep]
        GRAD[Gradient Engine: F equals minus Grad U]
    end
    
    subgraph OUT[Outputs]
        FNET[Resultant Force Vector F]
        PATH[Robot Trajectory Path]
        VM[Velocity Command v equals F]
    end
    
    QS --> UA
    QG --> UA
    OB --> UR
    UA --> SUM
    UR --> SUM
    SUM --> GRAD
    GRAD --> FNET
    FNET --> VM
    VM --> PATH
    
    style UA fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style UR fill:#d62728,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style SUM fill:#9467bd,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style GRAD fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Force Decomposition in C-Space

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Robot at position q] --> FA[Attractive Force F_att: directed toward goal]
    R --> FR[Repulsive Force F_rep: directed away from obstacle]
    FA --> NET[Net Force F_net equals F_att plus F_rep]
    FR --> NET
    NET --> DIR[Motion Direction: Unit vector F_net divided by magnitude]
    DIR --> MOVE[Robot moves by step alpha in direction]
    MOVE --> R
    
    style R fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style NET fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style DIR fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Each box represents a functional block. The colored inputs (blue, red, purple, green) represent the four major computational stages of the APF method. The flow is cyclic because the robot continuously re-evaluates forces at each new configuration.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define Artificial Potential Field. State any two limitations.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An Artificial Potential Field (APF) is a scalar function $U(q)$ defined over the robot's configuration space, constructed as the sum of an attractive potential $U_{att}$ (from the goal) and a repulsive potential $U_{rep}$ (from obstacles). The robot moves along the negative gradient $-\nabla U$ to reach the goal.

**Two limitations (1½ marks each):**
1. **Local minima problem** — the robot can get stuck at configurations where $\nabla U = 0$ but $q \neq q_{goal}$.
2. **GNRON (Goal Non-Reachable with Obstacles Nearby)** — the goal itself may lie inside the repulsive region, making it unreachable.

---

### **Q2. Write the expression for attractive and repulsive potentials used in APF.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Attractive potential (quadratic form):** $U_{att} = \dfrac{1}{2}\xi\,\rho^2(q, q_{goal})$, force $F_{att} = -\xi(q - q_{goal})$.

**Repulsive potential (Khatib form):**

$$U_{rep}(q) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{2}\,\eta\!\left(\dfrac{1}{\rho(q,q_{obs})} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^{\!2}, & \rho \le \rho_0 \\[4pt] 0, & \rho > \rho_0 \end{cases}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`

#### **(a)** Explain the Artificial Potential Field (APF) method for path planning. Derive the expressions for attractive and repulsive forces. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (2 marks):**
The APF method, introduced by Khatib (1986), treats the robot as a point in a C-space where an artificial potential energy landscape is constructed. The goal creates a "well" that attracts the robot, while each obstacle creates a "hill" that repels it. The total potential is:
$$U(q) = U_{att}(q) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} U_{rep,i}(q)$$

**Attractive Force Derivation (2 marks):**
$$U_{att} = \tfrac{1}{2}\xi\,\rho^2 \implies F_{att} = -\nabla U_{att} = -\xi(q - q_{goal})$$
This is a virtual spring force with stiffness $\xi$.

**Repulsive Force Derivation (3 marks):**
$$U_{rep} = \tfrac{1}{2}\eta\!\left(\tfrac{1}{\rho} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^{\!2}$$
Differentiating:
$$F_{rep} = \eta\!\left(\tfrac{1}{\rho} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)\!\frac{1}{\rho^2}\hat{e}_{obs}, \quad \text{for } \rho \le \rho_0$$

#### **(b)** Discuss the local minima problem in APF. Sketch a configuration where it occurs and suggest two remedies. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Explanation of Local Minima (3 marks):**
A local minimum occurs when:
$$F_{att}(q^*) + \sum_{i} F_{rep,i}(q^*) = 0, \quad q^* \neq q_{goal}$$

**Sketch / Configuration (2 marks):**
Two obstacles placed symmetrically on either side of the straight line from start to goal. At the midpoint, the horizontal components of repulsive forces cancel, while the vertical components exactly balance the attractive force → robot stalls.

**Remedies (2 marks):**
1. **Random-walk / perturbation** — add a small random force when $|\nabla U| < \epsilon$ to escape the trap.
2. **Modified potential functions** — use harmonic functions (e.g., Navigation Functions by Koditschek & Rimon) that are guaranteed to have a single global minimum at the goal.
3. **Virtual obstacle / virtual goal** — temporarily insert a virtual repulsive source to push the robot out.
4. **Hybrid global + local planning** — use a global planner (e.g., A*) for coarse path and APF for local refinement.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]`

#### **(a)** A mobile robot is at configuration $q = (0, 0)$ and the goal is at $q_{goal} = (10, 0)$. An obstacle is located at $q_{obs} = (5, 0)$ with distance of influence $\rho_0 = 3$ m. Take $\xi = 1$, $\eta = 10$. Compute the net force on the robot at $q = (0, 0)$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Attractive force (2 marks):**
$$\rho_{goal} = \sqrt{(0-10)^2 + 0^2} = 10 \text{ m}$$
$$F_{att} = -\xi(q - q_{goal}) = -1\!\left[(0-10),\, 0\right] = (10, 0) \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Repulsive force (3 marks):**
$$\rho_{obs} = \sqrt{(0-5)^2 + 0^2} = 5 \text{ m}$$
Since $\rho_{obs} = 5 > \rho_0 = 3$, the robot is **outside** the obstacle's influence region, so:
$$F_{rep} = 0 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Net force (2 marks):**
$$F_{net} = F_{att} + F_{rep} = (10, 0) \text{ N}$$

**Conclusion:** The robot will move in the +x direction with a force of 10 N (toward the goal), and the obstacle does not influence it at this configuration.

---

#### **(b)** Repeat the calculation in (a) when the robot is at $q = (4, 0)$ and discuss the result. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Attractive force (2 marks):**
$$F_{att} = -\xi(q - q_{goal}) = -1\!\left[(4-10),\, 0\right] = (6, 0) \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Repulsive force (3 marks):**
$$\rho_{obs} = \sqrt{(4-5)^2 + 0^2} = 1 \text{ m}$$
Since $\rho_{obs} = 1 \le \rho_0 = 3$, the repulsive force is **active**.

Unit vector from obstacle to robot:
$$\hat{e}_{obs} = \frac{q - q_{obs}}{\rho} = \frac{(-1, 0)}{1} = (-1, 0)$$

Magnitude factor:
$$\eta\!\left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)\frac{1}{\rho^2} = 10 \times \left(1 - \tfrac{1}{3}\right) \times \frac{1}{1} = 10 \times \tfrac{2}{3} = \tfrac{20}{3}$$

Therefore:
$$F_{rep} = \tfrac{20}{3} \cdot (-1, 0) = \left(-\tfrac{20}{3},\, 0\right) \approx (-6.67, 0) \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Net force (2 marks):**
$$F_{net} = (6, 0) + (-6.67, 0) = (-0.67, 0) \text{ N}$$

**Discussion:** The net force is **negative** in the x-direction, meaning the repulsive force is now stronger than the attractive force. The robot will be **pushed back** away from the obstacle (in the $-x$ direction), demonstrating the obstacle-avoidance behavior. This is correct APF behavior, but note that if the robot were exactly at $q = (4.4, 0)$, the forces would balance, creating a local minimum where the robot stalls just before the obstacle.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the superposition principle** — writing the force as $U_{att} \cdot U_{rep}$ instead of $U_{att} + U_{rep}$. (–2 marks)
> 2. **Using $\nabla U$ instead of $-\nabla U$** for the force direction. The robot moves **downhill**, so the sign is critical. (–1 to –2 marks)
> 3. **Omitting the distance-of-influence condition** $\rho_0$ in the repulsive formula. The piecewise definition is mandatory. (–1 mark)
> 4. **Not mentioning the local minima problem** when asked about limitations — this is a KTU-favorite 2–3 mark sub-question. (–2 marks)
> 5. **Confusing attractive and repulsive directions** — $F_{att}$ points *toward* the goal, $F_{rep}$ points *away from* the obstacle. (–1 mark)
> 6. **Skipping units** in numerical problems — KTU examiners deduct ½ mark for missing units in physical quantities.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Potential Field Path Planning:**

- [x] **APF** = Attractive potential (goal) + Repulsive potential (obstacles).
- [x] **Force direction:** $F = -\nabla U$ (always moves toward lower potential).
- [x] **Attractive potential (quadratic):** $U_{att} = \tfrac{1}{2}\xi\rho^2$; force $F_{att} = -\xi(q - q_{goal})$.
- [x] **Attractive potential (conic):** $U_{att} = \xi\rho$ for $\rho > d$ (constant force magnitude).
- [x] **Repulsive potential (Khatib):** $U_{rep} = \tfrac{1}{2}\eta(\tfrac{1}{\rho} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0})^2$ valid only for $\rho \le \rho_0$, else 0.
- [x] **Repulsive force direction:** points **from obstacle outward** along unit vector $\hat{e}_{obs} = (q - q_{obs})/\rho$.
- [x] **Repulsive force magnitude:** grows as $1/\rho^3$ near the obstacle (very steep safety barrier).
- [x] **Total potential:** scalar sum (superposition); **Total force:** vector sum.
- [x] **Local minimum condition:** $\nabla U(q^*) = 0$ at $q^* \neq q_{goal}$.
- [x] **Classic local minima scenarios:** symmetric two-obstacle trap, obstacle in front of goal, narrow corridor.
- [x] **Remedies:** random walk, virtual obstacles, navigation functions, hybrid A* + APF.
- [x] **GNRON problem:** goal inside repulsive region → use modified repulsive potential $U_{rep} = \tfrac{1}{2}\eta(\tfrac{1}{\rho} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0})^2 \rho^2_{goal}$ to ensure $F_{rep} \to 0$ as robot approaches goal.
- [x] **Computational cost:** $O(n)$ per step, where $n$ = number of obstacles — extremely efficient.
- [x] **Best suited for:** real-time dynamic obstacle avoidance, not for global path optimality.
- [x] **Originator:** Oussama Khatib, 1986.
- [x] **KTU-favorite 7-marker:** "Explain APF and derive the forces" — must include both the math AND the local minima discussion.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Obstacle Avoidance - Bug Algorithm - Vector Field Histogram - Dynamic Window Approaches
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAT602 - ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Obstacle Avoidance - Bug Algorithm - Vector Field Histogram - Dynamic Window Approaches

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🧭 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Obstacle Avoidance — Formal Definition

**Obstacle Avoidance** is a fundamental capability in mobile robotics wherein a robot dynamically alters its trajectory in real time to prevent collisions with static or dynamic obstacles while continuing to progress toward a designated goal. According to the KTU 2024 Robotics syllabus, obstacle avoidance algorithms operate in the local perception-action loop, using sensor data (range finders, LIDAR, sonar, depth cameras) to compute a safe steering command at every control cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Examiner-Verbatim):** *Obstacle avoidance is the process of computing, at each sampling instant, a collision-free velocity command $(v, \omega)$ that steers the robot from its current configuration toward the goal while respecting the kinematic and dynamic constraints of the platform.*

The three classical paradigms discussed in this module are:

1. **Bug Algorithms** — A *deliberative-reactive* family of path planners that mimic the navigational instincts of a blind insect.
2. **Vector Field Histogram (VFH)** — A *grid-based reactive* method that compresses a local occupancy grid into a polar histogram to select a steering direction.
3. **Dynamic Window Approach (DWA)** — A *velocity-space sampling* method that searches the space of admissible $(v, \omega)$ pairs for the one that maximizes an objective function.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogies

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — Bug Algorithm: The Blind Man in a Room**
> Imagine a person blindfolded inside an unfamiliar room, holding a white cane. To reach the opposite door, the person first walks straight toward the door. When the cane touches a wall, he follows the wall with his left hand until he has circumnavigated it, and then resumes walking straight toward the door. This is essentially **Bug1/Bug2** behaviour.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 — VFH: The Mountain Climber Reading a Compass**
> A mountaineer with a circular compass marked with 72 sectors looks at the surrounding mountain peaks. Each sector's "needle deflection" tells him where the densest obstacles lie. He picks the sector with the lowest deflection (clearest path) that is closest to the direction of the summit (goal). This is **VFH**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 3 — DWA: The Driver on a Slippery Road**
> A driver cannot instantly steer or brake. Within the *physically reachable* velocity–steering combinations in the next second, he evaluates (a) alignment with the road, (b) clearance from kerbs, and (c) speed preference. He chooses the *feasible and optimal* combination. This is **DWA**.

## 1.3 Why These Three Methods?

| Property | Bug Algorithm | VFH | DWA |
|----------|---------------|-----|-----|
| Sensor Requirement | Contact / Range | Range Scan (LIDAR/Sonar) | Range Scan + Odometry |
| Computational Cost | Very Low | Low | Moderate |
| Optimality Guarantee | Bounded suboptimality | Heuristic | Locally optimal |
| Dynamic Obstacles | Limited (Bug2 variants) | Moderate | Excellent |
| Kinematic Constraint | Not modelled | Not modelled | Explicitly modelled |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric Used Across All Three:** *Performance is measured by **Path Length Ratio** $L_{\text{ratio}} = L_{\text{actual}} / L_{\text{optimal}}$ and **Clearance** $d_{\min}$ (in **meters**) from the nearest obstacle.*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📐 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Bug Algorithm — Family Tree

The Bug family is among the earliest reactive planners. It assumes a *point robot* with a *perfect tactile sensor* (or short-range range sensor) and *perfect localization* to the goal.

### 2.1.1 Bug 0 (The Simplest Bug)

**Behavioural States:**
- **State S1 — Move to Goal:** Drive straight toward the goal.
- **State S2 — Follow Boundary:** Upon contact with an obstacle, traverse its perimeter.
- **Switching Rule:** Leave boundary when the goal-direction line from current point is *clear* of the obstacle.

**Pseudocode Logic (logic only, no code per protocol):**
1. From start $q_{\text{start}}$, compute heading $\theta = \text{atan2}(y_g - y, x_g - x)$.
2. Move with linear velocity $v$ until either: (a) goal reached, or (b) obstacle encountered.
3. On contact, switch to boundary-following; record the *hit point* $q_{\text{hit}}$.
4. On boundary, compute at each step the angle $\alpha = \angle(q, q_g)$ between current heading and goal direction.
5. If the line segment $\overline{q, q_g}$ is unobstructed (no obstacle cells intersect it), depart the boundary and resume State S1.

### 2.1.2 Bug 1

**Distinguishing Feature:** Upon hitting an obstacle, Bug1 traverses the *entire* obstacle boundary to find the *closest point to the goal*. This point is called the **leave point** $q_{\text{leave}}$.

**Performance Bound:**

$$
L_{\text{Bug1}} \leq L_{\text{opt}} + \frac{n}{2} \cdot P_i
$$

where $n$ is the number of obstacles and $P_i$ is the perimeter of the $i$-th obstacle. This is a **2-optimality** result under bounded-perimeter assumptions.

### 2.1.3 Bug 2

**Distinguishing Feature:** Instead of circumnavigating, Bug2 follows a *m-line* (the straight line from start to goal). It leaves the boundary the first time it re-encounters a point on the m-line that is *closer* to the goal than the hit point.

**Performance Bound:**

$$
L_{\text{Bug2}} \leq L_{\text{opt}} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i
$$

which is a *looser* bound than Bug1 in the worst case but produces shorter paths in practice because it does not always circumnavigate.

## 2.2 Vector Field Histogram (VFH)

VFH, introduced by **Borenstein and Koren (1991)**, compresses a 2-D Cartesian occupancy grid into a 1-D **polar histogram** of obstacle density vs. angle.

### 2.2.1 Algorithm Stages

**Stage 1 — Cartesian Grid Update:**
Each cell $C_{ij}$ has certainty value $c_{ij} \in [0, 1]$ updated by Bayesian or ad-hoc rules from sensor readings.

**Stage 2 — Polar Histogram Construction:**
Divide the $360°$ field into $n$ angular sectors (typically $n = 72$, i.e., $5°$ per sector). For each sector $k$:

$$
h_k = \sum_{i,j} c_{ij} \cdot \frac{1}{d^2_{ij}}
$$

where $d_{ij}$ is the distance from cell $C_{ij}$ to the robot. The $1/d^2$ weighting makes *near* obstacles dominate.

**Stage 3 — Smoothing:**
Apply a weighted circular moving average across adjacent sectors to reduce discretization noise:

$$
h'_k = \frac{h_{k-a} + 2h_{k-a+1} + \cdots + 2h_{k+a-1} + h_{k+a}}{2a + 1}
$$

where $a$ is the smoothing radius (typically $a = 2$ or $a = 5$).

**Stage 4 — Thresholding:**
Define a threshold $h_{\text{thr}}$ and a robot radius $r$. Find the set of *candidate valleys* $V$ — angular ranges where $h'_k < h_{\text{thr}}$.

**Stage 5 — Selection of Steering Direction:**
Among all valleys $V$, pick the candidate direction $\theta_{\text{steer}}$ that minimizes the cost function:

$$
\theta_{\text{steer}} = \arg\min_{\theta_k \in V} \left\{ \mu_1 \cdot \Delta(\theta_k, \theta_g) + \mu_2 \cdot \Delta(\theta_k, \theta_{p-1}) + \mu_3 \cdot \frac{1}{\min h'_k} \right\}
$$

where $\Delta(\cdot, \cdot)$ is the angular difference (modulo $2\pi$), $\theta_g$ is the goal direction, $\theta_{p-1}$ is the previous steering direction, and $\mu_1, \mu_2, \mu_3$ are tunable weights.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **VFH+ Enhancement:** VFH+ accounts for robot *width* and *kinematics* by inflating obstacles and using a *threshold curve* that depends on the robot's trajectory geometry. VFH* adds *look-ahead verification* (A* on local grid) to escape local minima.

## 2.3 Dynamic Window Approach (DWA)

DWA, introduced by **Fox, Burgard and Thrun (1997)**, operates in the *velocity space* $(v, \omega)$ of a differential-drive robot.

### 2.3.1 Three Constraints Defining the Search Space

**1. Circular Velocities (kinematic reachability):**

$$
V_s = \{ (v, \omega) \;\vert\; v \in [v_{\min}, v_{\max}] \text{ and } \omega \in [\omega_{\min}, \omega_{\max}] \}
$$

**2. Admissible Velocities (collision-free in next cycle):**

$$
V_a = \left\{ (v, \omega) \;\vert\; v \leq \sqrt{2 \cdot d(v, \omega) \cdot \dot{v}_b} \text{ and } \omega \leq \sqrt{2 \cdot d(v, \omega) \cdot \dot{\omega}_b} \right\}
$$

where $d(v, \omega)$ is the distance to the closest obstacle along the predicted arc, and $\dot{v}_b, \dot{\omega}_b$ are maximum decelerations (braking limits).

**3. Dynamic Window (physically reachable in one timestep $\Delta t$):**

$$
V_d = \left\{ (v, \omega) \;\vert\; v \in [v_c - \dot{v} \cdot \Delta t,\; v_c + \dot{v} \cdot \Delta t] \text{ and } \omega \in [\omega_c - \dot{\omega} \cdot \Delta t,\; \omega_c + \dot{\omega} \cdot \Delta t] \right\}
$$

where $(v_c, \omega_c)$ is the current velocity.

**The final search space:**

$$
V_r = V_s \cap V_a \cap V_d
$$

### 2.3.2 Objective Function (Maximization)

$$
G(v, \omega) = \sigma \left[ \alpha \cdot \text{heading}(v, \omega) + \beta \cdot \text{clearance}(v, \omega) + \gamma \cdot \text{velocity}(v, \omega) \right]
$$

where $\sigma$ is a normalization function (e.g., $1 / (s + x_i)$, $s$ = small constant) and $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ are weights.

**Sub-objectives:**
- $\text{heading}(v, \omega) = \pi - \vert \theta_g - \theta_{\text{predicted}} \vert$ (alignment with goal)
- $\text{clearance}(v, \omega) = d(v, \omega)$ (distance to nearest obstacle)
- $\text{velocity}(v, \omega) = v$ (forward speed preference)

**Optimal velocity command:**

$$
(v^*, \omega^*) = \arg\max_{(v, \omega) \in V_r} G(v, \omega)
$$

## 2.4 📋 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units / Range | Used In |
|---|--------------------|------------|---------------|---------|
| 1 | Bug1 Path Bound | $L_{\text{Bug1}} \leq L_{\text{opt}} + \tfrac{n}{2} P_i$ | meters | Bug1 |
| 2 | Bug2 Path Bound | $L_{\text{Bug2}} \leq L_{\text{opt}} + \sum P_i$ | meters | Bug2 |
| 3 | VFH Polar Cell Value | $h_k = \sum_{i,j} c_{ij} / d_{ij}^{\,2}$ | dimensionless | VFH |
| 4 | VFH Smoothed Value | $h'_k = \tfrac{1}{2a+1}\sum_{j=-a}^{a} h_{k+j}$ | dimensionless | VFH |
| 5 | VFH Steering Cost | $\min_{\theta_k \in V} [\mu_1 \Delta\theta + \mu_2 \Delta\theta_{p-1} + \mu_3 / h'_k]$ | degrees | VFH |
| 6 | DWA Search Space | $V_r = V_s \cap V_a \cap V_d$ | $(v, \omega)$ pairs | DWA |
| 7 | DWA Admissible Velocity | $v \leq \sqrt{2\, d\, \dot{v}_b}$ | m/s | DWA |
| 8 | DWA Dynamic Window | $v \in [v_c - \dot{v}\Delta t,\; v_c + \dot{v}\Delta t]$ | m/s | DWA |
| 9 | DWA Objective | $G = \sigma[\alpha \text{hdg} + \beta \text{clr} + \gamma v]$ | normalized | DWA |
| 10 | Predicted Trajectory Heading | $\theta_{\text{pred}} = \theta_c + \omega \Delta t$ | rad | DWA |
| 11 | Angular Difference (mod $2\pi$) | $\Delta\theta = \text{atan2}(\sin(\theta_k - \theta_g),\; \cos(\theta_k - \theta_g))$ | rad | VFH/DWA |
| 12 | Robot Curvature Radius | $R = v / \omega$ | meters | DWA |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** VFH is deployed in industrial AGVs and vacuum robots (e.g., Roomba's predecessor, the BSaR driver). DWA is the default local planner in the **ROS Navigation Stack** (`base_local_planner` / `dwa_local_planner`). Bug algorithms remain pedagogical benchmarks and are used in low-cost micro-robotics where computing power is constrained.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: DWA Admissible Velocity Constraint

**Statement:** Show that for a circular differential-drive robot, the maximum safe linear velocity at the next control cycle is:

$$
v_{\text{adm}} = \sqrt{2 \cdot d \cdot \dot{v}_b}
$$

where $d$ is the clearance along the trajectory and $\dot{v}_b$ is the maximum braking deceleration.

**Derivation:**

We model braking as uniform deceleration. The kinematic equation under constant deceleration $\dot{v}_b$ from initial speed $v_0$ to rest over a stopping distance $d_s$ is:

$$
0 = v_0^{\,2} - 2 \dot{v}_b \, d_s
$$

(using the standard relation $v_f^{\,2} = v_0^{\,2} + 2 a d$, with $v_f = 0$ and $a = -\dot{v}_b$).

For *collision-free* motion, the stopping distance $d_s$ must be **less than or equal to** the available clearance $d$:

$$
d_s \leq d
$$

Substituting the braking equation solved for $d_s = v_0^{\,2} / (2 \dot{v}_b)$:

$$
\frac{v_0^{\,2}}{2 \dot{v}_b} \leq d
$$

Rearranging for $v_0$:

$$
v_0^{\,2} \leq 2 d \, \dot{v}_b
$$

Taking the positive square root (velocity is non-negative):

$$
v_0 \leq \sqrt{2 \, d \, \dot{v}_b}
$$

Therefore, the maximum admissible linear velocity is:

$$
v_{\text{adm}} = \sqrt{2 \, d \, \dot{v}_b}
$$

The analogous angular admissible velocity is:

$$
\omega_{\text{adm}} = \sqrt{2 \, d \, \dot{\omega}_b}
$$

**Interpretation:** The faster the robot can brake (high $\dot{v}_b$), or the more clearance (large $d$), the higher the admissible speed.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: VFH Polar Histogram Cell Value

**Statement:** Derive the formula for the $k$-th sector of the VFH polar histogram given an occupancy grid cell $C_{ij}$ at distance $d_{ij}$ and certainty $c_{ij}$.

**Derivation:**

The robot is at the origin. Each grid cell $C_{ij}$ has polar coordinates $(d_{ij}, \phi_{ij})$ where:

$$
\phi_{ij} = \text{atan2}(y_j - y_r,\; x_i - x_r)
$$

The angular width of sector $k$ is:

$$
\Delta\phi = \frac{2\pi}{n} = \frac{2\pi}{72} = 5° \quad \text{(for } n = 72\text{)}
$$

A cell $C_{ij}$ belongs to sector $k$ if:

$$
k \Delta\phi \leq \phi_{ij} < (k+1) \Delta\phi
$$

The "obstacle density" of sector $k$ aggregates contributions from all cells in that sector. To penalize *near* obstacles more than *far* ones, the $1/d^2$ inverse-square weighting is applied:

$$
h_k = \sum_{(i,j) \in \text{sector } k} \frac{c_{ij}}{d_{ij}^{\,2}}
$$

For a single cell at distance $d$ with certainty $c = 1$:

$$
h_k^{(1)} = \frac{1}{d^2}
$$

**Numerical example:** A cell at $d = 1$ m contributes $h = 1.0$; at $d = 2$ m, $h = 0.25$; at $d = 3$ m, $h = 0.111$. The contribution drops off rapidly with distance, concentrating VFH's attention on *imminent* obstacles.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: DWA Heading Objective

**Statement:** Derive the heading component $\text{heading}(v, \omega)$ of the DWA objective.

**Derivation:**

The robot's predicted position after a short time $\Delta t$ under velocity $(v, \omega)$ is, for a differential-drive robot:

$$
x(t+\Delta t) = x + v \Delta t \cos(\theta_c + \tfrac{1}{2}\omega \Delta t)
$$

$$
y(t+\Delta t) = y + v \Delta t \sin(\theta_c + \tfrac{1}{2}\omega \Delta t)
$$

The heading of the predicted trajectory at the end of the interval is:

$$
\theta_{\text{pred}} = \theta_c + \omega \Delta t
$$

The *target heading* $\theta_g$ toward the goal is:

$$
\theta_g = \text{atan2}(y_g - y,\; x_g - x)
$$

The angular deviation is:

$$
\Delta\theta = \theta_g - \theta_{\text{pred}} = \theta_g - \theta_c - \omega \Delta t
$$

The heading objective should be maximized when the robot points *directly at* the goal ($\Delta\theta = 0$) and minimized when perpendicular ($\vert \Delta\theta \vert = \pi/2$) or away ($\vert \Delta\theta \vert = \pi$). The natural form is:

$$
\text{heading}(v, \omega) = \pi - \vert \Delta\theta \vert
$$

This yields $\text{heading} = \pi$ when perfectly aligned and $\text{heading} = 0$ when directly opposite the goal.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Criterion | Bug 1 | Bug 2 | VFH | DWA |
|-----------|-------|-------|-----|-----|
| **Type** | Deliberative-reactive | Deliberative-reactive | Reactive (grid-based) | Reactive (velocity-based) |
| **Sensor Data Used** | Contact + Goal | Contact + Goal | 2-D Range Scan | 2-D Range Scan + Odometry |
| **Computational Complexity** | $O(P)$ (perimeter) | $O(P)$ | $O(n \cdot N_{\text{cells}})$ | $O(N_v \cdot N_\omega)$ |
| **Accounts for Dynamics** | No | No | No | Yes (explicit) |
| **Optimality** | Bounded 2× in worst case | Bounded but looser | Heuristic (no guarantee) | Locally optimal |
| **Handles Narrow Passages** | Poorly | Poorly | Moderate | Good |
| **Real-Time Feasibility** | High | High | High | Moderate (sampling required) |
| **Common Platform Use** | Educational | Educational | Industrial AGVs | ROS default planner |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** For *static* environments, Bug or VFH suffices. For *dynamic* environments with moving obstacles, DWA is the industry default. For *computationally constrained* platforms (8-bit microcontrollers), Bug2 is preferred.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bug 2 Algorithm — State Transition Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start at q_start] --> B{Goal Reached?}
    B -- No --> C[Compute m-line heading to goal]
    C --> D[Move along m-line toward goal]
    D --> E{Obstacle Detected?}
    E -- No --> D
    E -- Yes --> F[Record hit point q_hit]
    F --> G[Follow obstacle boundary]
    G --> H{Encounter m-line again?}
    H -- No --> G
    H -- Yes --> I{Point on m-line closer to goal than q_hit?}
    I -- No --> G
    I -- Yes --> J[Record leave point q_leave]
    J --> K[Resume motion along m-line]
    K --> B
    B -- Yes --> L[Stop: Goal Achieved]
```

## 4.2 VFH — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Sensor Scan LIDAR or Sonar] --> B[Cartesian Occupancy Grid Update]
    B --> C[Cell Certainty c_ij]
    C --> D[Polar Histogram Builder with inverse-square weighting]
    D --> E[Smoothing Stage circular moving average]
    E --> F[Thresholding h_k vs h_thr]
    F --> G[Candidate Valley Set V]
    G --> H[Cost Evaluator against goal and previous heading]
    H --> I[Selected Steering Direction theta_steer]
    I --> J[Low-level Motor Command v and omega]
```

## 4.3 DWA — Velocity Space Search Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Current Velocity v_c omega_c] --> B[Compute V_s circular velocities]
    A --> C[Compute V_d dynamic window reachable in Delta t]
    C --> D[Predict trajectories in V_d]
    D --> E[Raycast for collision detection]
    E --> F[Compute V_a admissible velocities]
    B --> G[Intersect V_s and V_a and V_d = V_r]
    F --> G
    C --> G
    G --> H[Sample N_v by N_omega grid]
    H --> I[Evaluate objective G = heading + clearance + velocity]
    I --> J[Select arg max G]
    J --> K[Publish v* and omega* to robot base]
```

## 4.4 Comparative Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Environment with Obstacles] --> B[Sensor Module LIDAR or Sonar or Contact]
    B --> C{Algorithm Family}
    C -- Deliberative Reactive --> D1[Bug 1 or Bug 2]
    C -- Grid Based --> D2[VFH or VFH+ or VFH*]
    C -- Velocity Based --> D3[DWA]
    D1 --> E[Steering Command v omega]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Mobile Robot Actuators]
    F --> G[Updated Robot Pose]
    G --> A
```

## 4.5 Dynamic Window Sampling Pattern (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Full Velocity Space V_s rectangle] --> B[Apply Admissible Velocities V_a curved boundary]
    B --> C[Apply Dynamic Window V_d small box around v_c omega_c]
    C --> D[Resulting Search Region V_r elliptical intersection]
    D --> E[Discrete Sampling Grid points]
    E --> F[Trajectory Prediction for each sample]
    F --> G[Objective G Evaluation]
    G --> H[arg max G chosen as v* omega*]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🎯 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the Bug Algorithm and distinguish between Bug1 and Bug2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Bug Algorithm is a class of local path-planning algorithms for a mobile robot with a point model and perfect localization. The robot alternates between *motion-to-goal* and *boundary-following* behaviours.

- **Bug 1:** Upon hitting an obstacle, it fully circumnavigates the obstacle to find the boundary point closest to the goal, then departs. Bound: $L_{\text{Bug1}} \leq L_{\text{opt}} + (n/2)\sum P_i$.
- **Bug 2:** Follows the m-line (start-goal straight line) and leaves the obstacle boundary as soon as it re-encounters a point on the m-line that is closer to the goal than the hit point. Bound: $L_{\text{Bug2}} \leq L_{\text{opt}} + \sum P_i$.

**Key Distinction:** Bug1 always finds the optimal leave point on the obstacle; Bug2 is greedy and may produce longer paths but is computationally cheaper.

---

### Question 2: What is the Vector Field Histogram (VFH)? State its three main processing stages. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Vector Field Histogram (VFH) is a real-time obstacle avoidance method that converts a 2-D Cartesian occupancy grid into a 1-D polar histogram of obstacle density versus angle, and selects a steering direction from low-density valleys closest to the goal.

**Three main stages:**
1. **Grid Update** — Update cell certainty values $c_{ij}$ from sensor readings.
2. **Polar Histogram Construction** — Compute sector densities $h_k = \sum c_{ij}/d_{ij}^2$.
3. **Steering Selection** — Identify candidate valleys below threshold $h_{\text{thr}}$ and choose direction minimizing weighted cost (goal alignment + smoothness + safety).

---

## 📚 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks split into two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. The student chooses **one** of the two alternatives offered.

---

### ❓ Question A — (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat flow diagram, explain the Dynamic Window Approach (DWA) for obstacle avoidance in a mobile robot. Define the admissible velocity constraint and derive it.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A differential-drive robot has current velocity $v_c = 0.5$ m/s, $\omega_c = 0.2$ rad/s. The maximum accelerations are $\dot{v} = 0.5$ m/s² and $\dot{\omega} = 1.0$ rad/s². The control cycle is $\Delta t = 0.1$ s. The clearance to the nearest obstacle along the predicted trajectory is $d = 0.8$ m and the maximum braking decelerations are $\dot{v}_b = 1.0$ m/s² and $\dot{\omega}_b = 2.0$ rad/s². Compute the dynamic window $V_d$ and the admissible velocity limits.

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Explanation with flow diagram and derivation (7 marks):**

**DWA Flow Diagram (refer to Section 4.3):** The DWA works as follows:
1. From the current velocity $(v_c, \omega_c)$, compute the dynamic window $V_d$ — the set of velocities reachable in the next time step $\Delta t$.
2. From the obstacle map, compute the admissible velocity set $V_a$ — those velocities from which the robot can brake to a stop before collision.
3. Intersect $V_d$, $V_a$ with the full velocity space $V_s$ to obtain the *reachable, admissible* set $V_r$.
4. Sample $V_r$ discretely; for each $(v, \omega)$ candidate, predict the trajectory (arc of radius $R = v/\omega$) over a short horizon.
5. Evaluate the objective $G(v, \omega) = \sigma[\alpha \text{heading} + \beta \text{clearance} + \gamma \text{velocity}]$.
6. Select the $(v^*, \omega^*) = \arg\max G(v, \omega)$ and publish to the robot base.

**Derivation of the admissible velocity constraint (already derived in Section 3.1):**

Braking kinematics: $0 = v_0^2 - 2 \dot{v}_b d_s$, hence $d_s = v_0^2 / (2 \dot{v}_b)$.

For collision avoidance: $d_s \leq d$, so $v_0^2 \leq 2 d \dot{v}_b$, giving $v_{\text{adm}} = \sqrt{2 d \dot{v}_b}$.

**Marks distribution (KTU typical):** 2 marks for diagram, 3 marks for stepwise explanation, 2 marks for derivation.

**(b) Numerical computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Compute Dynamic Window $V_d$.**

Linear velocity range:

$$
v_{\min} = v_c - \dot{v} \cdot \Delta t = 0.5 - 0.5 \times 0.1 = 0.45 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
v_{\max} = v_c + \dot{v} \cdot \Delta t = 0.5 + 0.5 \times 0.1 = 0.55 \text{ m/s}
$$

So $v \in [0.45,\; 0.55]$ m/s.

Angular velocity range:

$$
\omega_{\min} = \omega_c - \dot{\omega} \cdot \Delta t = 0.2 - 1.0 \times 0.1 = 0.1 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
\omega_{\max} = \omega_c + \dot{\omega} \cdot \Delta t = 0.2 + 1.0 \times 0.1 = 0.3 \text{ rad/s}
$$

So $\omega \in [0.1,\; 0.3]$ rad/s.

**Step 2: Compute Admissible Velocities $V_a$.**

$$
v_{\text{adm}} = \sqrt{2 \cdot d \cdot \dot{v}_b} = \sqrt{2 \times 0.8 \times 1.0} = \sqrt{1.6} \approx 1.265 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
\omega_{\text{adm}} = \sqrt{2 \cdot d \cdot \dot{\omega}_b} = \sqrt{2 \times 0.8 \times 2.0} = \sqrt{3.2} \approx 1.789 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 3: Compute $V_r = V_s \cap V_a \cap V_d$.**

Since $V_d \subset V_a$ in this case (because $0.55 < 1.265$ and $0.3 < 1.789$), the dynamic window is the binding constraint.

$$
\boxed{V_r = \left\{ (v, \omega) \;\vert\; v \in [0.45,\; 0.55] \text{ m/s},\; \omega \in [0.1,\; 0.3] \text{ rad/s} \right\}}
$$

The robot can therefore adjust its speed by ±0.05 m/s and its angular rate by ±0.1 rad/s in the next cycle while remaining safe.

---

### ❓ Question B — (14 Marks) [Alternative]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With suitable diagrams, describe the Vector Field Histogram (VFH) method. Derive the polar histogram cell value formula.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 72-sector VFH is constructed from a $40 \times 40$ grid (each cell 0.1 m × 0.1 m). Three occupied cells exist:
- Cell A: at polar coordinates $(d, \phi) = (1 \text{ m},\ 30°)$, certainty $c = 1.0$.
- Cell B: at $(2 \text{ m},\ 32°)$, certainty $c = 0.8$.
- Cell C: at $(1.5 \text{ m},\ 150°)$, certainty $c = 0.9$.

Compute the raw polar histogram values for sectors containing these cells (assume each cell maps to exactly one $5°$ sector) and identify the candidate valleys if $h_{\text{thr}} = 0.5$.

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

**(a) VFH description with diagrams (7 marks):**

*(Refer to Section 4.2 block diagram and Section 2.2 for full description.)*

The VFH method proceeds through these stages:
1. **Cartesian Grid Update** — Maintain a 2-D certainty grid $C_{ij}$ updated by sensor readings (typically LIDAR or sonar arrays).
2. **Polar Histogram Computation** — For each of $n$ angular sectors (commonly 72, i.e., 5° each), compute the obstacle density:

$$
h_k = \sum_{i,j \in \text{sector } k} \frac{c_{ij}}{d_{ij}^{\,2}}
$$

The inverse-square weighting ensures that nearby obstacles dominate the histogram.

3. **Smoothing** — Apply circular moving average with radius $a$ (e.g., $a = 5$):

$$
h'_k = \frac{1}{2a+1} \sum_{j=-a}^{a} h_{k+j}
$$

4. **Thresholding** — Compare $h'_k$ to $h_{\text{thr}}$; sectors below form candidate valleys $V$.
5. **Direction Selection** — Choose $\theta_{\text{steer}}$ that minimizes weighted cost of angular deviation from goal, deviation from previous direction, and proximity to dense obstacles.

**Derivation of $h_k$:** (Refer to Section 3.2) Each cell in sector $k$ contributes $c_{ij}/d_{ij}^2$; the sector value is the sum over all such cells. The $1/d^2$ factor makes $h$ sensitive to *imminent* obstacles.

**Marks split:** 2 for diagram, 3 for description, 2 for derivation.

**(b) Numerical computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Map cells to sectors.**

With $n = 72$ sectors, sector $k$ spans angles $[k \cdot 5°,\ (k+1) \cdot 5°)$. So $\phi = 30°$ maps to sector $k_A = 6$; $\phi = 32°$ maps to sector $k_B = 6$; $\phi = 150°$ maps to sector $k_C = 30$.

**Step 2: Compute sector values using $h_k = c/d^2$.**

Sector 6 (containing cells A and B):

$$
h_6 = \frac{c_A}{d_A^{\,2}} + \frac{c_B}{d_B^{\,2}} = \frac{1.0}{1^2} + \frac{0.8}{2^2} = 1.0 + 0.2 = 1.2
$$

Sector 30 (containing cell C):

$$
h_{30} = \frac{c_C}{d_C^{\,2}} = \frac{0.9}{1.5^2} = \frac{0.9}{2.25} = 0.4
$$

All other sectors: $h_k = 0$.

**Step 3: Apply threshold $h_{\text{thr}} = 0.5$.**

- Sector 6: $h_6 = 1.2 > 0.5$ → **blocked** (dense obstacle region near 30°).
- Sector 30: $h_{30} = 0.4 < 0.5$ → **candidate valley** (free near 150°).
- Sectors 0–5 and 7–29 and 31–71: $h = 0 < 0.5$ → **all candidate valleys**.

**Step 4: Identify candidate valley set $V$.**

$$
V = \{0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 7, 8, \ldots, 29, 30, 31, \ldots, 71\} \setminus \{6\}
$$

The robot has $71$ of $72$ sectors free, with the obstacle concentrated around 30°–35°. The chosen steering direction is the valley closest to the goal (and aligned with previous heading, depending on cost weights).

**Interpretation:** A goal at, say, $\theta_g = 90°$ (sector 18) would lead the robot to choose sector 18 directly, since it is a free valley aligned with the goal.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in Obstacle Avoidance Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $\frac{1}{d^2}$ weighting** in VFH. Students often write $h_k = \sum c_{ij}$ — this is WRONG. Always include the inverse-square distance term.
>
> 2. **Confusing $V_s$, $V_a$, $V_d$ in DWA.** $V_s$ = full kinematic range, $V_a$ = braking-safe, $V_d$ = one-cycle reachable. The final search space is the **intersection** $V_r = V_s \cap V_a \cap V_d$.
>
> 3. **Sign error in admissible velocity formula.** The formula is $v \leq \sqrt{2 d \dot{v}_b}$, NOT $v \leq 2 d \dot{v}_b$. The square root is critical.
>
> 4. **Omitting units.** KTU examiners explicitly deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit. Always state m/s, rad/s, m/s², etc.
>
> 5. **Not sketching the m-line in Bug2 questions.** The m-line is the straight line from start to goal — examiners expect to see it drawn.
>
> 6. **In numerical answers, not showing intermediate steps.** Compute $v_{\min}, v_{\max}$ explicitly; do not jump to the final window.
>
> 7. **Mixing degrees and radians.** VFH sectors are usually in degrees; DWA angles in radians. Convert carefully: $1$ rad $= 57.296°$.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ **Bug Algorithm:** Point robot, two behaviours (move-to-goal, boundary-following). **Bug1** = full circumnavigation, **Bug2** = m-line-based.
- ⭐ **Bug1 bound:** $L_{\text{Bug1}} \leq L_{\text{opt}} + \frac{n}{2} \sum P_i$ (tight, 2-optimal).
- ⭐ **Bug2 bound:** $L_{\text{Bug2}} \leq L_{\text{opt}} + \sum P_i$ (looser, but practical).
- ⭐ **VFH Stages:** Grid Update → Polar Histogram ($h_k = c_{ij}/d^2$) → Smoothing → Threshold → Direction Selection.
- ⭐ **VFH Key Parameters:** $n = 72$ sectors, $a \in [2, 5]$ smoothing radius, $h_{\text{thr}}$ typically $0.3$–$0.6$.
- ⭐ **VFH+** adds kinematic and robot-width awareness; **VFH\*** adds look-ahead verification.
- ⭐ **DWA Search Space:** $V_r = V_s \cap V_a \cap V_d$ — three constraints intersection.
- ⭐ **DWA Admissible Velocity:** $v \leq \sqrt{2 d \dot{v}_b}$ (linear), $\omega \leq \sqrt{2 d \dot{\omega}_b}$ (angular).
- ⭐ **DWA Dynamic Window:** $v \in [v_c - \dot{v}\Delta t, v_c + \dot{v}\Delta t]$ (one-cycle reachable).
- ⭐ **DWA Objective:** $G = \sigma[\alpha \text{heading} + \beta \text{clearance} + \gamma \text{velocity}]$.
- ⭐ **Heading sub-objective:** $\text{heading} = \pi - \vert \theta_g - \theta_{\text{pred}} \vert$ (max = $\pi$ when aligned).
- ⭐ **Trajectory radius of curvature:** $R = v / \omega$ for differential drive.
- ⭐ **Industry Deployment:** DWA = ROS default local planner; VFH = industrial AGVs; Bug = educational/embedded.
- ⭐ **Computational complexity:** Bug $< $ VFH $< $ DWA (in typical sensing setups).
- ⭐ **Path optimality:** Bug1 (bounded), VFH (heuristic), DWA (locally optimal).
- ⭐ **Real-time guarantee:** Bug and VFH are deterministic; DWA is sampling-based with bounded sample budget.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Tip for KTU Exam:** When asked to "compare and contrast," always tabulate. When asked to "derive," show every algebraic step with units. When asked to "explain with diagram," draw the m-line (Bug), the polar histogram (VFH), or the velocity search space (DWA). Visual answers consistently earn 1–2 extra marks in KTU valuation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Navigation Architectures - Modularity for Code Reuse and Sharing - Control Localization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAT602 - ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Navigation Architectures - Modularity for Code Reuse and Sharing - Control Localization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Navigation Architectures — Formal Definition

A **Navigation Architecture** in mobile robotics is a structured software and hardware framework that organizes the decision-making and control flow of an autonomous agent as it moves through an environment. It defines how perception, localization, mapping, path planning, and motion control modules interact temporally and logically.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, the navigation architecture is responsible for:
1. Acquiring sensor data
2. Building or maintaining a world model
3. Localizing the robot within that model
4. Planning a route from start to goal
5. Executing motion commands while avoiding obstacles

### Three Classical Paradigms

| Architecture Type | Also Known As | Core Idea |
|---|---|---|
| **Deliberative** | Sense–Plan–Act (SPA) | Think first, then act using a complete world model |
| **Reactive** | Behavior-Based | Respond instantly to sensor stimuli, no world model |
| **Hybrid** | Three-Layer / Layered | Combines deliberative reasoning with reactive control |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The hybrid (three-layered) architecture is the most asked topic in KTU 2024 Scheme examinations, particularly relating to **CLARAty** (Coupled-Layer Architecture for Robot Autonomy) developed by NASA JPL.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Self-Driving Taxi

Imagine you are a **taxi driver in an unfamiliar city**:

- **Deliberative (Sense-Plan-Act):** You look at a paper map (world model), plan the entire 20 km route on paper, and then start driving along that fixed plan. If the road is blocked, you must stop and replan.
- **Reactive (Behavior-Based):** You don't use any map. You simply follow other cars (follow behavior), stop when you see a red light (stop behavior), and swerve around obstacles (avoid behavior). You react second-by-second.
- **Hybrid (Three-Layered):** You glance at a map (deliberative layer) for the high-level route, then drive while constantly watching the road (control/executive layer), and your foot instantly hits the brake when a pedestrian jumps in (reactive layer).

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Lock-In:** Pure deliberative = Slow but smart. Pure reactive = Fast but dumb. Hybrid = Smart **and** fast — this is why every real autonomous vehicle uses it.

## 1.3 Modularity for Code Reuse and Sharing — Formal Definition

**Modularity** is the software-engineering principle of decomposing a robotic system into independent, self-contained, and interchangeable software units (modules), each with a **standardized interface** that enables reuse across different robots and applications.

In KTU 2024 terms, modular robotics software design involves:

- **Abstraction layers** that hide hardware specifics
- **Message-passing interfaces** (publishers/subscribers, client/server)
- **Reusable libraries** (drivers, planners, filters)
- **Hardware abstraction** so that the same planning code can run on a wheeled robot, a drone, or a humanoid

### Real-World Analogy — The Smartphone App

Think of your smartphone apps:
- The **camera app**, **WhatsApp**, and **Google Maps** all share the **GPS module** without knowing how GPS works internally.
- They use standardized APIs (the "interface") to ask "where am I?".
- The GPS module is **modular** — swap it for a better chip, and all apps still work.

**Robot Operating System (ROS)** is the engineering realization of this principle. Each ROS **node** (e.g., `/lidar_publisher`, `/path_planner`) is an independent process that exchanges data over named **topics** using a standard message type.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Metric:** Modular systems typically achieve **60–80% code reuse** across robot platforms, drastically reducing development time.

## 1.4 Control Localization — Formal Definition

**Localization** is the computational process of estimating the **pose** (position $(x, y, z)$ + orientation $(\phi, \theta, \psi)$) of a robot relative to a reference frame, using sensor measurements and probabilistic inference.

The KTU 2024 syllabus classifies localization into three increasing levels of difficulty:

| Localization Level | Definition | Difficulty |
|---|---|---|
| **Position Tracking** | Initial pose known; estimate small incremental motion | ★☆☆ |
| **Global Localization** | Pose completely unknown; robot must determine where it is | ★★☆ |
| **Kidnapped Robot Problem** | Robot is teleported mid-operation; must recover | ★★★ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (for 3-mark answers):** Localization is the problem of determining a robot's pose from sensor data, formally expressed as the posterior probability $P(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$, where $x_t$ is the pose, $z$ are measurements, and $u$ are control actions.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Lost Tourist

Imagine waking up blindfolded in **Mumbai**. You have a **smartphone** (sensors) that gives you:
- GPS coordinates (global, but noisy indoors)
- Wi-Fi signal strength
- Step count (odometry)

**Localization** is your brain fusing all these imperfect clues to conclude: *"I am likely at Gateway of India."*

Each clue alone is unreliable — together they triangulate your position. This is precisely what **Kalman Filters** and **Particle Filters** do mathematically.

---

## 1.5 Key Terminology Master List

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Pose** | Robot's full configuration: $(x, y, \theta)$ in 2D |
| **Odometry** | Estimation of pose from wheel/IMU integration |
| **Beacon** | Known landmark used for absolute reference |
| **SLAM** | Simultaneous Localization and Mapping |
| **Belief** | Probability distribution over robot poses |
| **Markov Assumption** | Future state depends only on current state |
| **Sensor Fusion** | Combining multiple noisy sensors to reduce uncertainty |
| **Covariance** | Measure of uncertainty in the estimate |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Analysis of Navigation Architectures

### 2.1.1 Deliberative (Sense–Plan–Act) Architecture

**Operational Flow:**

1. **Sense Phase:** All sensors are read; a complete global world model $M$ is constructed.
2. **Plan Phase:** A global path $\pi^*$ from start $x_s$ to goal $x_g$ is computed using search algorithms (A*, Dijkstra, RRT*).
3. **Act Phase:** The robot executes the planned trajectory using a low-level controller.

**Strengths:**
- Globally optimal paths can be computed
- Reasoning about future states is possible
- Easy to formalize and verify

**Weaknesses:**
- Computationally expensive
- Brittle in dynamic environments (the world model becomes stale)
- Slow reaction time (sense → plan latency)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse the *deliberative* layer with the *path planning* step. The deliberative layer is the **entire cognitive layer**, of which path planning is just one function.

### 2.1.2 Reactive (Behavior-Based) Architecture

**Operational Flow:**

1. Sensors → Direct mapping to motor commands
2. **Behaviors** are small, parallel processes (e.g., *avoid-obstacle*, *go-to-goal*)
3. Behaviors compete or cooperate via **vector summation** or **subsumption**

**Foundational Work:** Rodney Brooks' **Subsumption Architecture (1986)** — behaviors are layered, with higher layers subsuming (overriding) lower ones.

**Strengths:**
- Real-time response (milliseconds)
- No world model required (robust to dynamic environments)
- Inherently parallel

**Weaknesses:**
- Cannot reason about long-term goals
- Local minima traps (deadlock loops)
- Emergent behavior is hard to predict

### 2.1.3 Hybrid (Three-Layer) Architecture

The **three layers** are:

| Layer | Function | Time Scale | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Deliberative (Top)** | Mission planning, reasoning | Minutes to hours | "Go to room B" |
| **Executive (Middle)** | Sequence tasks, monitor progress | Seconds to minutes | "Traverse corridor" |
| **Reactive (Bottom)** | Real-time motor control | Milliseconds | "Avoid obstacle" |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CLARAty Architecture (NASA JPL):** Deliberative ↔ Executive ↙ Reactive Control. The middle executive layer translates abstract plans into executable behaviors. This is a guaranteed 14-marker question in KTU 2024 ESE.

## 2.2 Deep Theoretical Analysis of Modularity

### 2.2.1 The Three Pillars of Modular Robotics Software

1. **Information Hiding:** Each module exposes only its **interface** (function signatures, message types), not its implementation.
2. **Loose Coupling:** Modules depend on each other only through the interface, not internal logic. Changing one module does not break others.
3. **High Cohesion:** Each module has a single, well-defined responsibility (Single Responsibility Principle).

### 2.2.2 ROS as the Industry-Standard Modular Framework

**Robot Operating System (ROS)** implements modularity through:

- **Nodes:** Independent executable processes
- **Topics:** Named buses for asynchronous publish/subscribe messaging
- **Services:** Synchronous request/reply RPC calls
- **Messages:** Strongly-typed data structures (`.msg` files)
- **Launch files:** XML-based composition of multiple nodes
- **Packages:** Versioned, reusable code bundles

**Communication Pattern:**

```
Sensor Driver Node → /scan topic → Localization Node → /pose topic → Planner Node → /cmd_vel → Motor Node
```

### 2.2.3 Reusability Metrics

A well-designed modular robotic system enables:

| Reuse Level | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Code Reuse** | Same source compiles on different robots | Path planner used on TurtleBot and Husky |
| **Component Reuse** | Same compiled binary on different robots | Pre-built ROS package |
| **Architectural Reuse** | Same design pattern across projects | Three-layer on any mobile platform |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Code reuse in robotics reduces development time by approximately **70%** when transitioning from one robot platform to another within the same family.

## 2.3 Deep Theoretical Analysis of Localization

### 2.3.1 Probabilistic Foundation — Bayes Filter

Localization is fundamentally a **Bayesian inference** problem. The recursive Bayes filter estimates the posterior belief $bel(x_t)$ over the robot's pose $x_t$.

**Two-Step Recursion:**

1. **Prediction (Motion Update):** Propagate belief forward using control input $u_t$ and the motion model.
2. **Correction (Measurement Update):** Refine belief using sensor observation $z_t$.

### 2.3.2 Localization Algorithms Classification

| Algorithm | Type | Belief Representation | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Kalman Filter (KF)** | Parametric | Single Gaussian | Linear systems |
| **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** | Parametric | Single Gaussian (linearized) | Mildly nonlinear |
| **Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF)** | Parametric | Sigma points | Highly nonlinear |
| **Particle Filter (Monte Carlo)** | Non-parametric | Set of weighted samples | Multimodal (global) |
| **Histogram Filter** | Discrete grid | Probability grid | Small discrete spaces |

### 2.3.3 Odometry-Based Localization

**Dead Reckoning** uses the kinematic model to estimate pose from wheel encoders:

- For a **differential drive robot** with wheel velocities $v_L$ and $v_R$ and wheel separation $b$:
  - Linear velocity: $v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2}$
  - Angular velocity: $\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{b}$

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** Odometry errors are **unbounded** and accumulate without limit. A 1% distance error becomes a 10 m error after 1 km. Always combine odometry with absolute sensors (GPS, beacons) for bounded error.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $bel(x_t) = P(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ | Posterior belief over pose | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\bar{bel}(x_t) = \int P(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$ | Prediction step | dimensionless |
| 3 | $bel(x_t) = \eta \, P(z_t \mid x_t) \, \bar{bel}(x_t)$ | Correction step ($\eta$ is normalizer) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2}$ | Differential drive linear velocity | m/s |
| 5 | $\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{b}$ | Differential drive angular velocity | rad/s |
| 6 | $x_{t} = x_{t-1} + v \Delta t \cos\theta_{t-1}$ | Position update (x) | m |
| 7 | $y_{t} = y_{t-1} + v \Delta t \sin\theta_{t-1}$ | Position update (y) | m |
| 8 | $\theta_{t} = \theta_{t-1} + \omega \Delta t$ | Heading update | rad |
| 9 | $\sigma^2_{accumulated} = n \cdot \sigma^2_{step}$ | Odometry error growth | m² |
| 10 | $K_t = \Sigma_t H^T (H \Sigma_t H^T + Q)^{-1}$ | Kalman Gain | dimensionless |
| 11 | $P(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$ | Motion model probability | dimensionless |
| 12 | $P(z_t \mid x_t)$ | Measurement model (sensor) | dimensionless |
| 13 | $\eta = 1 / \int P(z_t \mid x_t') \bar{bel}(x_t') dx_t'$ | Bayes normalizer | dimensionless |
| 14 | $N_{particles} \approx O(1000 \text{ to } 100000)$ | Particle filter count | particles |
| 15 | $\text{Code Reuse \%} = \frac{\text{Reused Lines}}{\text{Total Lines}} \times 100$ | Modularity metric | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark-Allocation Tip:** Equations 1–3, 6–8, and 10 are the **most frequently asked** derivation starters in KTU 2024 ESE. Memorize the *Bayes filter two-step recursion* in full.
